Contaminant Name: 1,2,3-Trichloropropane | Description: 1,2,3-Trichloropropane is synthetic, chlorinated hydrocarbon. At room temperature, it is a colorless to straw-colored liquid [2412 and 2414]. It is manufactured for use as an industrial solvent and as a cleaning and degreasing agent [2412]. It is also a by-product of the production of other chlorinated compounds, including epichlorohydrin, and has been used as an intermediate in the production or synthesis of other chemicals such as polymers, polysulfides, and hexafluoropropylene [2412 and 2415]. It has been found as an impurity in soil fumigants [2412]. 1,2,3-Tricholoropropane production levels and use information is limited [2412].
1,2,3-Trichloropropane can enter the environment through industrial activity, including releases from manufacturers who make or use 1,2,3-trichloropropane, releases from disposal sites such as landfills or hazardous waste sites, agricultural application of fumigants, and from chemical spills. Annual releases reported to the Toxics Release Inventory ranged from more than 98,000 pounds to less than 1,500 pounds between 1998 and 2013, primarily released via fugitive air emissions, stack emissions, or landfill disposal [2416].
1,2,3-Trichloropropane is moderately volatile from aqueous phase [2415]. Therefore, releases to surface water or surface soils can evaporate. In the air, 1,2,3-trichloropropane is oxidized by hydroxyl radicals; with a predicted half-life ranging from 27 to 46 days depending on modeling assumptions. Subsurface releases can result in groundwater contamination because breakdown in or evaporation from groundwater is minimal. Although reductive dehalogenation might occur in anaerobic sediments, 1,2,3-trichloropropane was not readily biodegradable in tests under aerobic conditions [2415]. Measured bioconcentration factors indicate no significant bioaccumulation potential [2415].
Exposure routes for 1,2,3-trichloropropane include inhalation, ingestion, and dermal contact [2414]. USEPA has classified 1,2 3-trichloropropaneis as "likely to be carcinogenic to humans" under the 2005 "Guidelines for Carcinogenic Risk Assessment", based on tumors in animal studies including tumors of the oral cavity, forestomach, pancreas, kidney, preputial gland, clitoral gland, mammary gland, and Zymbal's gland in rats, and the oral cavity, forestomach, liver, Harderian gland, and uterus in mice [2417]. USEPA has also established a chronic oral reference dose (RfD) of 4 x 10-3 milligrams per kilogram per day (mg/kg/day) and a chronic inhalation reference concentration (RfC) of 3 x 10-4 milligrams per cubic meter (mg/m3) to reduce the risk of chronic exposure health effects such as liver and kidney damage and reduced body weight [2417, 2412]. As of 2015, 1,2,3-trichloropropane is not regulated in drinking water, but there is a 1-day health advisory of 0.6 milligrams per liter (mg/L) and a 10-day health advisory of 0.6 mg/L for TCP in drinking water for a 10 kilogram (kg) child [2412].

Contaminant Name: 1,2,3-Trichloropropane | Function: Aeration and Air Stripping | Details: Aeration processes, in general, rely on the diffusion of contaminants from the treated water to non-contaminated air. These technologies enhance the natural tendency of a volatile organic compound to migrate from a liquid phase to a gas phase in order to achieve equilibrium by increasing air-to-water contact. Because the volatility of 1,2,3-trichloropropane is relatively low, aeration processes must have high air-to-water ratios to achieve contaminant removal.

Contaminant Name: 1,2,3-Trichloropropane | Function: Granular Activated Carbon | Details: GAC is a porous adsorptive media with extremely high internal surface area. GACs are manufactured from a variety of raw materials with porous structures including bituminous coal, lignite coal, peat, wood, and coconut shells. These raw materials are used in a number of manufacturing processes that create and/or enlarge pores in the carbonized materials to create a porous structure with a large surface area per unit mass.
When water containing 1,2,3-trichloropropane is treated with GAC, it passes through treatment columns or beds containing GAC. 1,2,3-Trichloropropane, along with many other organics, is separated from the water most often by adsorption to the surfaces in the pores of the GAC. Treatment capacity for this VOC may vary depending on the properties of the GAC, which vary with the type of raw material and the manufacturing process.
When a GAC bed is no longer able to adsorb a sufficient quantity of 1,2,3-trichloropropae molecules, the concentration in the effluent begins to rise. When this concentration reaches an unacceptable level, the carbon is considered “spent” and must be replaced by virgin GAC or reactivated GAC. Reactivation (which is commonly termed “regeneration” in the drinking water industry) is a high temperature and pressure process that removes organic compounds from adsorption sites on GAC so that it can be reused.
GAC beds typically also require periodic backwash to prevent headloss or biomass accumulation. The backwash process must be designed to remove accumulated solids, while preventing the spent carbon at the top of the column from mixing with the unspent carbon at the bottom, creating a mixed bed and the possibility of “leakage” of the target contaminant.

Contaminant Name: 1,2,3-Trichloropropane | Overall: EPA has not established best available treatment technologies for 1,2,3-trichloropropane because it is not regulated in drinking water. Remediation technologies available to treat TCP contamination in groundwater and soil include granular activated carbon (GAC), dechlorination by hydrogen release compound (HRC®), reductive dechlorination by zero valent zinc and others 2413. USEPA 2413 also identifies several bench-scale tests of treatment: ultraviolet radiation and chemical oxidation with potassium permanganate 2419 chemical oxidation with Fentons reagent 2420 TCP degradation in water using advanced oxidation processes involving ozone and hydrogen peroxide 2418 limited degradation of TCP in saturated soil and groundwater using zero-valent iron 2420 effective reduction using granular zero valent zinc for remediation of TCP in groundwater, 2421 and the use of genetically engineered strains of Rhodococcus for the complete biodegradation of TCP under aerobic conditions 2420.
Information on contaminant removal is available for the following technologies:

Contaminant Name: 1,2-Dibromoethane | Description: Ethylene dibromide (EDB) is a colorless, heavy liquid that has a mildly sweet, chloroform-like odor [2600,2601]. Prior to the phaseout of leaded gasoline in the United States, EDB was primarily used as an anti-knock compound. Its use as a fumigant for citrus, grain, and soil was discontinued in 1984. It is currently used as a solvent for resins, gums, and waxes; as a chemical intermediate in the synthesis of dyes and pharmaceuticals; and as a precursor in the synthesis of vinyl chloride [2600]. It continues to be used as an anti-knock additive in aviation fuels [2601]. 
EDB is released during the use, storage, and transport of leaded gasoline, as well as during any spills; from its former use as a pesticide; and wastewater and emissions from processes and waste waters of the chemical industries that use it. When soil and climatic conditions are favorable, EDB may get into drinking water by runoff into surface water, or by leaching into groundwater [2601]. Also, EDB appears to be formed naturally by microalgae growth and has been detected in ocean waters and air [2600].
EDB exhibits low-to-moderate soil adsorption and, therefore, will leach quickly into groundwater. It is very stable towards hydrolysis and is more likely to undergo aerobic biodegradation in the soil rather than abiotic degradation. In aquatic environments, the primary removal process for EDB is evaporation: the volatilization half-life from a typical lake or river is 1–5 days. Biodegradation of EDB in groundwater can be slow (with half-lives in months), and biotic hydrolysis only readily occurs in the presence of a natural catalyst such as hydrogen sulfide. In the ambient atmosphere, EDB exists as a vapor, and, although it undergoes degradation in a reaction with photochemically produced hydroxyl radicals, it is likely to be persistent [2600].
EDB is highly toxic to humans, and acute exposure to high concentrations of EDB can cause collapse or death. Based on human and animal studies, EDB absorption into the body occurs rapidly through the oral, dermal, and inhalation routes. Animal studies have shown long term exposure may cause damage to the liver, kidneys and testes by inhalation, ingestion, or dermal contact. EDB is a human and animal reproductive toxicant and has been demonstrated to adversely affect reproductive health. EDB is classified as likely to be carcinogenic to humans via both the oral and inhalation routes of exposure based on strong evidence of carcinogenicity in animals and inconclusive evidence of carcinogenicity in an exposed human population [2600]. The maximum contaminant level goal (MCLG) for EDB is zero. EPA has set an enforceable maximum contaminant level (MCL) for EDB at 0.00005 mg/L or 50 ppt [2601].
Contaminant Name: 1,2-Dibromoethane | CAS Number: 106-93-4 | DTXSID: DTXSID3020415 | Synonyms: EDB, Ethylene bromide, Ethylene dibromide, Glycol bromide | Contaminant Type: Chemical

Contaminant Name: 1,2-Dibromoethane | Function: Adsorptive Media | Details: A pilot scale study using a cast iron-based media found removals from 39 to 73 percent, but the highest removals were achieved using empty bed contact times (EBCTs) much higher than typical in full-scale drinking water applications [2491]. A bench scale study using a micelle–clay complex formed from the interaction of the organic cation ODTMA with montmorillonite achieved 91 percent removal, but also with a relatively high EBCT (greater than 30 minutes) [2492].

Contaminant Name: 1,2-Dibromoethane | Function: Aeration and Air Stripping | Details: A pilot study found removals of 26 to 50 percent by packed tower aeration using relatively low air-to-water ratios (7:1 to 21:1). At a higher air-to-water ratio (92:1), the study found that the tower removed ethylene dibromide to below the detection limit, but the detection limit was not quantified [2493].

Contaminant Name: 1,2-Dibromoethane | Function: Granular Activated Carbon | Details: One study reported aggregate results of a program in Florida that installed more than 800 point of entry GAC filters. With media replacement every six months, only 89 of the filters at 53 installations experienced breakthrough (defined as detectable ethylene dibromide above an analytical limit of 0.02 ug/L). Many of these results were breakthrough in the lead filter of a series; only 53 breakthroughs at 40 installations reached the final filter. Thus, the majority of installations studied achieved removals of up to greater than 99 percent [2490]. A study of a small (1 cubic foot) commercial GAC filter installed in the pump house of a private well showed similarly high removals. For 11 months, the filter removed ethylene dibromide to below an analytical limit of 0.01 ug/L (greater than 99 percent removal). Although ethylene dibromide was detected after 12 months, removal at that time was still greater than 98 percent [2494].

Contaminant Name: 1,2-Dibromoethane | Overall: Studies have examined the removal of ethylene dibromide using GAC, packed tower aeration, and adsoprtive media. GAC removed greater than 99 percent of the contaminant to below detectable limits for periods of 6 to 11 months. Removal of greater than 50 percent of ethylene dibromide by aeration, on the other hand, required relatively high air-to-water ratios. The adsorptive media tested also required relatively high empty bed contact times (EBCTs) to achieve high removals.

Contaminant Name: 1,4-dioxane | Description: 1,4-dioxane is a volatile organic compound (VOC) that is produced commercially through the dehydration and ring closure of diethylene glycol.  Historically, 1,4-dioxane was used as a stabilizer for the solvent 1,1,1-trichloroethane.  With legislation in the 1990s aimed to phase out 1,1,1-trichloroethane (Clean Air Act, Montreal Protocol), production of 1,4-dioxane as a stabilizer has decreased.  Today, 1,4-dioxane is primarily used as a solvent for celluloses, organic products, lacquers, paints, cosmetics, deodorants, emulsions, and adhesives [2062].
In 1992, of the 1.13 million pounds of 1,4-dioxane released into the U.S. environment, roughly 60 percent was released into the atmosphere, 40 percent into surface waters, and less than one percent onto land.  1,4-dioxane's low soil-sorption coefficient, complete miscibility with water, and resistance to biodegradation contribute to the ability of 1,4-dioxane to leach into and remain in groundwater.  1,4-dioxane has been detected in surface and groundwater samples, in wastewater treatment plant effluents, and in 31 of the 1,689 sites on EPA's National Priorities List [2051].
The EPA classifies 1,4-dioxane as a Group B2 probable human carcinogen by all routes of exposure.  Inhalation is the most common route of exposure; however, exposure may occur through ingestion of contaminated food and water or dermal contact.  Exposure to 1,4-dioxane in tap water through inhalation during showering or other indoor activities can result in higher exposure compared to ingestion of drinking water [2062].  Acute side effects include irritation of the eyes, nose, throat and lungs; possible drowsiness; vertigo; headache; and anorexia.  Human and animal studies identify the liver and kidneys as the target organs for 1,4-dioxane toxicity following short-term exposure to high doses of 1,4-dioxane (regardless of the route of exposure) [2062].  Chronic exposure may result in dermatitis or possible liver and kidney damage.  The reproductive effects are unknown [2051].
As a solvent, 1,4-dioxane can be regulated as a hazardous waste; however, no federal drinking water standards have been established.  The EPA classifies 1,4-dioxane as an emerging contaminant and includes it in the Third Drinking Water Contaminant Candidate List (CCL3).  It is also a proposed contaminant for third Unregulated Contaminant Monitoring Rule program (UCMR 3).  State regulators use drinking water action levels and health advisories to establish site cleanup goals.  In September 2004, Colorado became the first state to establish an enforceable cleanup standard for 1,4-dioxane in groundwater and surface water. This standard is being phased in and requires facilities to have met a 6.1 μg/L limit by March 2005 and a 3.2 μg/L limit by March 2012 [2051].
Pump and treat remediation is the primary method used when remediating sites contaminated with 1,4-dioxane.  Advanced oxidation processes involving hydrogen peroxide with ultraviolet (UV) light or ozone are used to breakdown 1,4-dioxane [2051].  Some of the degradates include aldehydes, organic acids, and the mono- and diformate esters of 1,2-ethanediol [2070].  Other remediation treatment technologies include photocatalysis and soil vapor extraction [2051].  14-dioxane is not well controlled by adsorption onto activated carbon or by air stripping.  it is not well degraded by typical soil microorganisms [2142].
Date of literature search: April 2022

Contaminant Name: 1,4-dioxane | Function: Adsorptive Media | Details: Removal of 1,4-dioxane from water using adsorptive media was found to be ineffective and not feasible for full-scale drinking water treatment. One bench-scale study [2050] evaluated the feasibility of using swellable organically modified silica (SOMS) to remove 1,4-dioxane and reported 18 to 49 percent removal.

Contaminant Name: 1,4-dioxane | Function: Aeration and Air Stripping | Details: Based on one study [2067], removal of 1,4-dioxane from water via aeration was found to be ineffective (0 to 3 percent removal). The tests were performed at room temperature for 144 minutes with a maximum air to water ratio of 80:1.

Contaminant Name: 1,4-dioxane | Function: Biological Filtration | Details: Removal of 1,4-dioxane by biological filtration was examined in two bench-scale studies [2098, 2731] and one full-scale study [2733]. In the first bench study, approximately 93 to 97 percent contaminant removal was achieved with a microbial consortium enriched from a contaminated aquifer [2098]. The study was carried out in a continuous-flow attached-growth reactor in the presence of tetrahydrofuran at a high temperature (35 ºC) with long filter run times (384 to 600 hours), which are atypical conditions for drinking water treatment [2098]. In another bench-scale study [2731], approximately 94 to 96 percent contaminant removal was achieved by treating contaminated groundwater in two different types of reactors (JOB5 and CB1190) with granular activated carbon filter media. This study employed 120 hours of detention time, also atypical of drinking water treatment. A full-scale study using granular activated carbon media to support biological growth [2733] found only 28 percent contaminant removal in secondary effluent from a reclamation plant. Thus, biological filtration may not be suitable for 1,4-dioxane in full-scale water treatment applications.

Contaminant Name: 1,4-dioxane | Function: Biological Treatment | Details: Biological degradation of 1,4-dioxane was reported to be effective in laboratory studies (up to 94 percent removal). The best-characterized aerobic bacteria strain is a monooxygenases-expressing strain, Pseudonocardia dioxanivorans CB1190. CB1190 was reported to effectively degrade 1,4-dioxane by more than 80 percent in several days at room temperature [2079, 2039]. Another species, Flavobacterium, was found to co-metabolize 1,4-dioxane with presence of tetrahydrofuran [2107]. Flavobacterium degraded 1,4-dioxane up to 94 percent in two to three weeks at room temperature in the presence of tetrahydrofuran. Biological treatment may not be feasible for full-scale water treatment due to a number of considerations including reliability, robustness, and sustainability issues.

Contaminant Name: 1,4-dioxane | Function: Chlorine | Details: Based on two studies [2059, 2067], removal of 1,4-dioxane from water via chlorination was found to be ineffective (0 percent removal) at room temperature, with high doses of chlorine. One study [2059] was able to achieve 1,4-dioxane removal greater than 80 percent with pH 6, 75-degree Celsius water, and high doses of chlorine (1,000 mg/L). These conditions are not feasible for full-scale drinking water treatment.

Contaminant Name: 1,4-dioxane | Function: Conventional Treatment | Details: Removal of 1,4-dioxane from water by coagulation and sedimentation was found to be ineffective (0 percent removal) based on data available from one bench-scale study [2067] involving an elevated ferric chloride dose (55 mg/L) and a 1.4 hour detention time.
Contaminant Name: 1,4-dioxane | Function: GAC Isotherm | Details: One study reported a GAC isotherm for a bituminous carbon material with lab water [2731].

Contaminant Name: 1,4-dioxane | Function: Granular Activated Carbon | Details: Although GAC adsorption was found to be relatively effective for the removal of 1,4-dioxane at the bench scale (67 to 87 percent) [2067, 2731]., pilot- and full-scale studies showed much lower effectiveness. One pilot-scale study reported 18 percent removal of 1,4-dioxane using a peat-based GAC material [2110]. The study also found that 56 percent of the contaminant was removed using GAC downstream of reverse osmosis. Two full-scale studies displayed poor removal of 1,4-dioxane, achieving a range of less than 0 to 27 percent [2722, 2732]. These low percent removals are indicative of early 1,4-dioxane breakthrough through the GAC..

Contaminant Name: 1,4-dioxane | Function: Hydrogen Peroxide | Details: Bench scale studies have found the use of hydrogen peroxide alone was found to be ineffective in removing 1,4-dioxane (0 to 18 percent) [2059,2479]. Results of several bench and pilot-scale studies [2032, 2045, 2046, 2059] found that adding ferrous iron (Fenton’s reagent) increased removal efficiencies. Between 4 and greater than 99 percent removal was achieved, generally with the following parameters enhancing removal: increased reaction time, decreased pH, increased hydrogen peroxide dose, and increased iron dose. However, effective removal generally requires high iron and/or hydrogen peroxide doses, long reaction times, and/or high temperature, which are beyond typical conditions for drinking water treatment.
However, it is believed that complete mineralization of 1,4-dioxane is not achieved with Fenton's reagent according to bench-scale studies. Rather, the contaminant is broken down into low molecular weight organic aldehydes and acids which are resistant to further chemical oxidation.

Contaminant Name: 1,4-dioxane | Function: Membrane Filtration | Details: In a full-scale facility treating surface water combined with groundwater using ultrafiltration, the technology was found to be ineffective at 1,4-dioxane removal [2722]. The study showed a range of 0 to 33 percent removal.

Contaminant Name: 1,4-dioxane | Function: Membrane Separation | Details: Full-scale studies with RO membranes reported removals ranging between 69 [2733] and 85 percent [2722]. Bench and pilot-scale studies demonstrated that the small molecular size of this contaminant is an important consideration when selecting a particular membrane for treatment. A bench-scale study using flat sheet RO membranes found only 2 percent removal [2054], while a pilot study with spiral wound desalination type RO membranes found up to 96 percent removal [2110]. A bench-scale study with NF membranes achieved a maximum of 48 percent removal [2089].

Contaminant Name: 1,4-dioxane | Function: Other Treatment | Details: Three other treatment technologies were reported effective for 1,4-dioxane degradation at bench- or pilot-scale studies, including gamma irradiation, corona discharge, photocatalysis, and sonication (sonolysis). A gamma irradiation dose of 750 Gy decomposed 1,4-dioxane by more than 80 percent with an initial concentration of 10 mg/L [2100]. Corona discharge, an emerging advanced oxidation process, was reported to effectively degrade 1,4-dioxane from 18 ug/L by greater than 90 percent in a pilot study [2102]. Sonication was found effective for 1,4-dioxane removal depending on contact time, frequency, and power. For 100 mg/L of 1,4-dioxane, a degradation efficiency of 78 percent was achieved in 200 minutes with an ultrasonic frequency of 20 kHz and 300W of power [2032]. A study using a continuous flow reactor and the same frequency (20kHz), however, resulted in minimal degradation (12 percent) [2724]. Ultrasonic frequencies and sparging gas were reported to affect the destruction efficiency [2090, 2036]. Catalyst (titanium dioxide) and oxidants (ferrous, iron and persulfate) generally improve the overall degradation efficiency [2082, 2032]. Combination of sonication with photocatalysis (UV + TiO2) generated significantly higher degradation efficiencies [2035]. Photocatalysis using visible light and TiO2 alone has shown to be effective at degrading 1,4-dioxane in bench-scale studies (up to >99 percent removal) [2723, 2726, 2728]. These technologies may not be suitable for full-scale drinking water treatment. Additionally, one bench-scale study found titanium dioxide alone ineffective for removing 1,4-dioxane (2 percent removal) [2055].

Contaminant Name: 1,4-dioxane | Overall: Several treatment processes are considered effective for the removal of 1,4-dioxane, some of which achieve greater than 99 percent removal under specific conditions.
Advanced oxidation processes such as ozone combined with hydrogen peroxide, ultraviolet light combined with titanium dioxide, ultraviolet light combined with peroxide, hydrogen peroxide combined with ferrous iron (Fenton's reaction), and other advanced oxidation processes are considered to be the most effective treatment processes for 1,4-dioxane removal, routinely achieving greater than 99 percent reduction.  Membrane separation using reverse osmosis (up to 96 percent removal) was also shown to be effective at removing 1,4-dioxane at the bench-scale level.  Under specific conditions, adsorptive media (18 to 49 percent removal with a swellable organically modified silica) and granular activated carbon (only 18 percent removal in column studies) were able to remove some 1,4-dioxane.  These processes may not be feasible for full-scale water treatment applications.  Other atypical water treatment technologies (biological treatment and biofiltration, both with added microbes, corona discharge, gamma irradiation, and sonication) also showed promise at removing 1,4-dioxane.
Based on limited studies, the following processes were determined to be ineffective at removing 1,4-dioxane, often removing less than one percent of the contaminant: aeration, chlorine disinfection, permanganate addition, conventional treatment, hydrogen peroxide addition, ozonation, powdered activated carbon, and UV irradiation alone. 
Research was identified evaluating the following treatment technologies for the removal of 1,4-dioxane:
Adsorptive Media, Aeration and Air Stripping, Biological Filtration, Biological Treatment, Chlorine, Conventional Treatment, Granular Activated Carbon, Hydrogen Peroxide, Membrane Separation, Other Treatment, Ozone, Ozone Hydrogen Peroxide, Permanganate, Powdered Activated Carbon, Ultraviolet Irradiation, Ultraviolet Irradiation  Ozone, and Ultraviolet Irradiation Hydrogen Peroxide.

Contaminant Name: 1,4-dioxane | Function: Ozone | Details: Bench scale studies have found ozone alone to be relatively ineffective (2 to approximately 45 percent removal) when treating water with high 1,4-dioxane concentrations (55 to 500 milligrams per liter). These studies also employed higher ozone doses than those typically encountered at water treatment facilities [2044, 2054]. Bench scale studies treating water with low 1,4-dioxane concentrations (3.8 to 20 micrograms per liter) achieved somewhat higher removals (22 to 78 percent) using more typical ozone doses [2478, 2479].
Thirty-five to greater than 99 percent removal of 1,4-dioxane was achieved when ozone was used concurrently with novel processes. One bench-scale study [2054] investigating ozonation combined with electrolysis found that removal of 1,4-dioxane increased with an increase in electrolytic current. Another bench-scale study [2044] achieved removals of 39 to 52 percent using ozone in the presence of a palladium catalyst. Finally, a pilot Hydro-Non-Thermal-Plasma system emitting a corona discharge in conjunction with ozone removed 51 to 94 percent, depending on the frequency of operation and flow-rate [2102].
While no significant toxic byproducts were detected in the effluent of the pilot plasma system, bench-scale studies investigating the use of ozone, as well as ozone in combination with a palladium catalyst, found only partial oxidation of 1,4-dioxane into oxidation intermediates.

Contaminant Name: 1,4-dioxane | Function: Ozone and Hydrogen Peroxide | Details: Similar to other advanced oxidation technologies that create hydroxyl radicals, ozone combined with hydrogen peroxide has been shown to be effective for 1,4-dioxane removal (8 to greater than 99 percent removal). The removal efficiency depends on ozone dose, hydrogen peroxide dose, contact time, 1,4-dioxane concentration and competing compounds, such as bicarbonate alkalinity and organic chemicals. However, most ozone and hydrogen peroxide doses tested were higher than is typical for drinking water treatment.

Contaminant Name: 1,4-dioxane | Function: Permanganate | Details: Removal of 1,4-dioxane from water using permanganate was found to be ineffective (0 percent removal) based on data available from one study [2067] at 25 degrees Celsius. The study showed that 1,4-dioxane was not removed even with high doses of permanganate (100 mg/L) and long detention times (180 minutes).

Contaminant Name: 1,4-dioxane | Function: Powdered Activated Carbon | Details: Removal of 1,4-dioxane from water using powdered activated carbon was found to be ineffective (0 percent removal) based on data available from one study [2067]. The study showed that, even with high doses of powdered activated carbon and long detention times, 1,4-dioxane was not removed.

Contaminant Name: 1,4-dioxane | Function: Ultraviolet Irradiation | Details: Bench-scale studies [2064, 2065, 2086, 2108, 2475, 2477, 2479, 2725, 2727, 2729] indicate that ultraviolet (UV) irradiation alone is ineffective for the removal of 1,4-dioxane (0 to 51 percent photodegradation). It was also found that conversion of the contaminant to CO2 was insignificant. Although one pilot-scale study found a maximum degradation of 90 percent, the greatest effectiveness was achieved by using two reactors in series and lowering the flow rate to 20 percent of the maximum studied [2720]. The resulting contact time was not reported, so it is unclear if this result reflects typical drinking water treatment conditions. With a single reactor at the maximum flow rate, 1,4-dioxane degradation was only 24 percent.

The addition of ferrous ions enhanced removal in bench-scale studies, achieving 25 to 89 percent removal due to the formation of hydroxyl radicals [2046,2065]. However, the iron dose (5 g/L) and many UV reaction times tested were not representative of drinking water treatment conditions. 
The use of the photocatalyst TiO2 was found to effectively remove 1,4-dioxane in bench-scale studies [2034, 2038, 2048, 2055, 2086, 2091, 2101, 2105, 2108, 2723, 2726, 2730]. Approximately 30 to 100 percent removal was achieved, and generally, removal was enhanced with increased time, UV intensity, and TiO2 dose (up to a point). However, many UV reaction times tested were excessive and not representative of drinking water treatment conditions [2035, 2065, 2092, 2097, 2104, 2109]. Also, several bench-scale studies found that the use of UV/TiO2 was able to completely mineralize the contaminant to CO2.

Contaminant Name: 1,4-dioxane | Function: Ultraviolet Irradiation and Hydrogen Peroxide | Details: Similar to other advanced oxidation technologies that create hydroxyl radicals, ultraviolet irradiation combined with hydrogen peroxide was found to be effective at removing 1,4-dioxane (up to greater than 99 percent removal). Data suggest that the removal efficiency depends on UV dose, hydrogen peroxide dose, contact time, 1,4-dioxane concentration, and hydroxyl radical scavenger (e.g., bicarbonate alkalinity). Many early studies treated water with high 1,4-dioxane concentrations (4 to 450 milligrams per liter) and/or employed hydrogen peroxide doses and UV reaction times greater than typical of drinking water treatment [2033, 2034, 2046, 2075, 2085, 2108]. More recent studies, however, have achieved high removals (up to greater than 99 percent) using more typical hydrogen peroxide doses and UV reaction times [2479, 2480, 2481, 2482, 2721]. These studies include those treating water with low 1,4-dioxane concentrations (4 to 20 micrograms per liter) [2479, 2481, 2723, 2725, 2727, 2730]. 
 Fenton and ferrioxalate were reported to improve 1,4-dioxane degradation rate by UV and hydrogen peroxide. These studies employed relatively high iron doses (30 milligrams per liter and greater) [2046,2085].

Contaminant Name: 1,4-dioxane | Function: Ultraviolet Irradiation and Ozone | Details: Similar to other advanced oxidation technologies that create hydroxyl radicals, ultraviolet irradiation combined with ozone was found to be of varying effectiveness at removing 1,4-dioxane (7.6 to greater than 98 percent removal). Data suggest that the removal efficiency depends on UV dose, ozone dose, contact time, and initial 1,4-dioxane concentration. Most of the studies treated water with high 1,4-dioxane concentrations (55 to 500 milligrams per liter) and employed ozone doses and/or UV reaction times greater than typical of drinking water treatment [2475, 2476, 2477]. One bench scale study, however, achieved 78 percent removal of a low initial 1,4-dioxane concentration (20 micrograms per liter) using more typical ozone dose and UV reaction time [2479].

Contaminant Name: 17a-ethynyl estradiol | Description: 17a-ethynyl estradiol is a synthetic chemical that is an ovulation inhibitor. It is a principal component in the oral contraceptive pill, is used in hormone replacement therapy, and is a prescription pharmaceutical product. In the technical literature it is oftentimes referred to as EE2. 
It is an endocrine disrupting compound (EDC) with potential adverse effects in humans, animals and fish. [578] It is termed an estrogenic compound as it can block or mimic natural estrogen. It is one of the most potent estrogenic compounds. [578, 579] Links exist between EDCs like EE2 and reproductive failures and abnormalities in fish. [578] Concentrations as low as 2 ng/L could induce measurable changes in fish reproduction, and such concentrations are typical of those effluent from municipal waste water treatment plants. [578]
Residuals of EE2 passing the body are not completely removed during municipal waste water treatment and carry over into receiving streams that may serve as sources for drinking water treatment. [579]
EE2 is not regulated by USEPA in drinking water.  It is on Contaminat Candidate List 3.
The analytical methods for EE2 are varied, as are their detection levels. A study of European waste water effluents reported a mean of 1.4 ng/L and a maximum of 8.9 ng/L. [579] A study of four municipal waste water treatment effluents in the US reported an EE2 range 0.14 to 2.0 ng/L. [580] A study of European surface waters reported a mean of 0.8 ng/L and a maximum of 5.1 ng/L. [579] In surface waters in the Netherlands, EE2 was detected at three of 11 locations with detects in the 0.3 to 4.3 ng/L range. [581] It was not detected at 0.05 ng/L in two US surface waters. [580] In a survey of pharmaceuticals in US streams by the USGS, EE2 had a 5.7 percent frequency of detection with a median of 0.094 ug/L and a maximum of 0.237 ug/L. The USGS study was biased toward streams susceptible to contamination. [582, 583]
Date of Literature Search: March 2007

Contaminant Name: 17a-ethynyl estradiol | Function: Adsorptive Media | Details: Removal of 17a-ethynyl estradiol (EE2) by adsorptive media can be very effective and is dependent on the pH, the contaminant properties (pKa and hydrophobicity) and the surface charge of the media. One equilibrium sorption study showed no significant sorption of EE2 onto the positively charged alumina or the negatively charged silica; linear sorption was observed for EE2 onto Porapak, a hydrophobic organic media. The linear sorption coefficient to Porapak was 4.4E-3 L/m2.

Contaminant Name: 17a-ethynyl estradiol | Function: Chlorine | Details: The oxidation of 17a-ethynyl estradiol (EE2) from water by chlorine oxidation can be effective and is dependent on the initial contaminant dose, chlorine dose, contact time and pH. One study presented the EE2 half life values of 73.2 minutes, 14.6 minutes and 7.3 minutes for chlorine doses of 0.1 mg/L, 0.5 mg/L and 1.0 mg/L. Another study showed that oxidation of EE2 by chlorine is rapid and near completion in the following conditions: pH of 5.5, 3.8 mg/L chlorine dose and 24 hour contact time.

Contaminant Name: 17a-ethynyl estradiol | Function: Chlorine Dioxide | Details: Oxidation of 17a-ethynyl estradiol (EE2) by chlorine dioxide can be very effective at low CT values and is dependent on the initial contaminant dose, chlorine dioxide dose, contact time and pH. In one study, the reaction of EE2 was too fast to be detected after 5 minutes of contact time with surface water. The study also reported that a dose of 0.1 mg/L was sufficient to oxidize 97% of EE2 in groundwater.

Contaminant Name: 17a-ethynyl estradiol | Function: Conventional Treatment | Details: Removal of 17a-ethynyl estradiol from water by coagulation/clarification was found to be ineffective. Less than 20% removal was reported in three studies. For some studies, 20% removal was not considered significant considering the analytical errors of the experiments. No difference was shown based on the type of coagulant used for aluminum sulfate, ferric chloride or ferric sulfate. One study showed better removal (20%) with polyaluminum chloride in comparison to ferric sulfate (

Contaminant Name: 17a-ethynyl estradiol | Function: Granular Activated Carbon | Details: Removal of 17a-ethynyl estradiol with GAC in one study was very effective (greater than 99 percent removal, but bed life not reported).

Contaminant Name: 17a-ethynyl estradiol | Function: Membrane Separation | Details: Removal of 17a-ethynyl estradiol with nanofiltration membrane can be very effective (24.3 to greater than 99 percent removal) and is dependent on the membrane composition, membrane permeability and the feed pressure. Lower feed pressures showed greater removal of EE2.

Contaminant Name: 17a-ethynyl estradiol | Overall: The following processes are considered effective for the removal or oxidation of 17a-ethynyl estradiol (EE2): chlorine dioxide (97 to greater than 99 percent), ozone (greater than 95 percent removal), ultraviolet irradiation (up to 99 percent) with titanium dioxide photocatalysis, ultraviolet irradiation coupled with hydrogen peroxide (greater than 99 percent), membrane separation using nanofiltration (up to 99 percent), powdered activated carbon (50 to greater than 99 percent), and adsorptive media that is hydrophobic. Scant data for GAC indicate its effectiveness but GAC bed life was not reported. Performance of these technologies can vary based on water quality and treatment characteristics.
Chlorine provided about 50 percent oxidation employing CT values near 7 mg min/L.
Based on the available literature, conventional treatment (less than 20 percent removal), precipitative softening (less than 10 percent removal) are not considered effective for the removal of EE2.
Studies were identified evaluating the following treatment technologies for the removal of 17-a-ethynyl estradiol:

Contaminant Name: 17a-ethynyl estradiol | Function: Ozone | Details: Ozone is effective for the oxidation of 17a-ethynyl estradiol (EE2) in drinking water applications and typical CT values. EE2 is oxidized by ozone quickly and at efficiencies of greater than 95% under water treatment conditions. Studies have identified the oxidation products formed during the ozonation of EE2. One study reported the slow reappearance of 0.1-0.2% of the initial EE2 concentration after ozonation.

Contaminant Name: 17a-ethynyl estradiol | Function: Powdered Activated Carbon | Details: Removal of 17a-ethynyl estradiol with PAC can be very effective (50 to greater than 99 percent removal) and is dependent on PAC dose and type. Studies examined six different PACs. Removal in laboratory water was greater than in natural waters. Greater PAC doses increased the removal of EE2.

Contaminant Name: 17a-ethynyl estradiol | Function: Precipitative Softening | Details: Removal of 17a-ethynyl estradiol (EE2) from water by precipitative softening was found to be minimally effective based on data available from one study. This particular study measured EE2 concentrations spiked in Passaic River water and after softening at pH 11.3. Removal of EE2 was minimal (

Contaminant Name: 17a-ethynyl estradiol | Function: Ultraviolet Irradiation | Details: Oxidation of 17a-ethynyl estradiol (EE2) from water by ultraviolet radiation can be effective depending on the lamp type, dose and contact time. Studies showed that control with UV alone varies between less than 10% and 100%. Complete oxidation was shown in one study only after 120 minutes of UVA exposure. Medium pressure lamps showed more control in comparison to low pressure lamps. The addition of H2O2 or photocatalysis with TiO2 increased the rate of oxidation in comparison to UV alone. Transformation products are unknown.

Contaminant Name: 17a-ethynyl estradiol | Function: Ultraviolet Irradiation and Hydrogen Peroxide | Details: Greater oxidation of 17a-ethynyl estradiol (EE2) was observed with the addition of H2O2 in comparison to UV alone. In one study, near complete contaminant removal was observed with a H2O2 dose of 5mg/L and less than 500 mJ/cm2. Medium pressure lamps showed slightly more removal in comparison to low pressure lamps. An increase in H2O2 concentration increased the rate of oxidation. Transformation products are unknown.

Contaminant Name: 4-Nonylphenol | Description: 4-Nonylphenol is used in a variety of pesticides and consumer products and is a common biodegradation product of nonylphenol ethoxylates used in detergents. [1537] 4-Nonylphenol mimics estrogen and is therefore an endocrine-disrupting compound. [1539, 1540]
4-Nonylphenol is commonly used as a raw material for making detergents, pesticides, anti-oxidants in plastics and rubbers, as a supplemental agent/stabilizer of polyvinyl chloride, as well as other uses. [1537, 1543] 4-nonylphenol in the aqueous environment originates predominantly from the incomplete biotransformation of nonylphenol polyethoxylate (NPEO) nonionic surfactants of various ethoxylate chain lengths. [1537, 1539, 1541] The major source of 4-nonylphenol in the environment is from treated wastewater effluent and land application of biosolids. 
4-Nonylphenol is hydrophobic, relatively insoluble in water, and can persist and accumulate in sediments and sludge. [1540] It may take months or years to degrade in the environment. 
4-Nonylphenol can bind to estrogen receptors and elicit estrogenic action in vivo and in vitro. [1539, 1541] 4-nonylphenol is reported to have a greater affinity for estrogen receptors in fish compared with humans. [1541] Tests in rats for reproductive effects of 4-nonylphenol over three generations confirmed both male (decreased epididymal sperm density and testicular spermatid head counts) and female (increased estrus cycle length and decreased ovarian weights) reproductive changes. [1541] 4-Nonylphenol can cause breast cancer cells to increase in number. [1547] Human exposure to 4-nonylphenol has not yet been evaluated. [1537] Exposure to 4-nonylphenol inhibits seed germination in some plants and growth of certain aquatic plants and algae. [1547]
There are no existing regulations for 4-nonylphenol in drinking water; nor is the compound listed as a candidate for future regulatory decision-making. 
Residues of 4-nonylphenol have been reported in river water, groundwater adjacent to a contaminated river, seawater, tap water, sediments, and fish tissues. [1541, 1546] Concentrations of 4-nonylphenol in surface waters are in the range of 0.11 to 180 micrograms per liter. [1537] 
Date of last literature review: August, 2009.

Contaminant Name: 4-Nonylphenol | Function: Biological Treatment | Details: One study cited reduction of 4-nonylphenol by using biosorption and integrated sonochemical Fenton reaction followed by biosorption treatment. Sonochemical Fenton degradation experiments were conducted at 300 kHz in homemade reactor. Biosorption treatment experiments were conducted using P. lilacinus fungal strains. Both the methods were effective in removing 4-nonylphenol, with biosorption removing 59 to 61 percent in seven days and integrated sonochemical Fenton reaction followed by biosorption achieved up to 88 percent removal.  Sonochemical Fenton reaction removed 66 percent of 4-nonylphenol in the first hour. No hazardous byproducts were detected in the experiments.

Contaminant Name: 4-Nonylphenol | Function: Chlorine | Details: Free chlorine can remove 65 to 100 percent of 4-nonylphenol after a contact time of 10 to 60 minutes.  However, reduction in 4-nonylphenol concentrations may not be complete, resulting in halogenated transformation byproducts.

Contaminant Name: 4-Nonylphenol | Function: Conventional Treatment | Details: Removal of 4-nonylphenol from water by coagulation, clarification, and filtration was found to be ineffective.  Less than 33 percent removal was reported in multiple samples collected over period of three months in one study.  Another study reported no removal of 4-nonylphenol by conventional treatment.  Aluminum sulfate coagulant showed better removal (10 to 33 percent) compared to ferric chloride (0 percent).

Contaminant Name: 4-Nonylphenol | Function: GAC Isotherm | Details: Based on isotherm studies, adsorption of 4-nonylphenol from water to granular activated carbon (GAC) can be very effective. One study reported coal-based GAC was more effective than other types due to its large pore volume. Another study showed that the presence of dissolved humic acids (DHA) can lower 4-nonylphenol adsorption onto GAC. Adsorption capacity decreased with duration of operation; the extent depended on carbon type and years of the operation.

Contaminant Name: 4-Nonylphenol | Function: Granular Activated Carbon | Details: Removal of 4-nonylphenol from water by granular activated carbon (GAC) can be very effective at lower concentrations (up to 100 percent). Adsorption capacity was shown to reduce with years of operation; the extent of the reduction depended on carbon type and years of operation. The presence of dissolved humic acids (DHA) was shown to adversely impact the adsorption capacity of GAC.

Contaminant Name: 4-Nonylphenol | Function: Membrane Separation | Details: Removal of 4-nonylphenol from water by membrane separation (specifically nanofiltration membranes) can be very effective (up to 100 percent).  For loose nanofiltration membranes, the retention time has a significant influence on 4-nonylphenol removal.

Contaminant Name: 4-Nonylphenol | Function: Other Treatment | Details: Two papers investigated the removal of 4-nonylphenol from water by electrochemical techniques. A potential difference of 0.7 V can achieve 38 to 100 percent removal of 4-nonylphenol. Percent removal is a function of the initial concentration and the reaction time. A reaction time of 10 min achieved 100 percent removal when the initial concentration was 1.1 mg/L 4-nonylphenol. At an initial concentration of 22 mg/L, a reaction time of 60 min achieved 65 percent removal.

Contaminant Name: 4-Nonylphenol | Overall: The following treatment technologies are effective in removing 4-nonylphenol from water: free chlorine (65 to 100 percent), granular activated carbon (up to 90 percent), ozone (37 to 93 percent), ozone combined with peroxide (55 to 90 percent), biological treatment in conjunction with sonochemical treatment (up to 88 percent), and biological treatment (59 to 61 percent). Conventional treatment has shown mixed results. One study reported 10 to 33 percent removal with aluminum sulfate as a coagulant and another study reported no removal when ferric chloride was used. Electrochemical oxidation of 4-nonylphenol has been effective (up to 100 percent) at milligram per liter concentrations.
Research has identified evaluation of the following treatment technologies for the removal of 4-Nonylphenol:

Contaminant Name: 4-Nonylphenol | Function: Ozone | Details: Ozone is effective for oxidizing 4-nonylphenol in drinking water applications. Ozone quickly reacts with 4-nonylphenol. The maximum removal of 4-nonylphenol occurred in the first 30 seconds after ozone was added. Removal efficiency ranged from 37 to 93 percent at an ozone dose of 1 to 5 mg/L.

Contaminant Name: 4-Nonylphenol | Function: Ozone and Hydrogen Peroxide | Details: One paper investigated the removal of 4-nonylphenol from water by ozone combined with hydrogen peroxide. The combination of ozone and peroxide can achieve 55 to 90 percent 4-nonylphenol removal, with higher removal associated with longer ozone contact times.  Varying the ozone to peroxide ratio appeared to have no impact on the 4-nonylphenol removal. This technology does not appear to be more effective than removal by ozone alone.

Contaminant Name: Acetochlor | Description: Acetochlor is a synthetic organic compound of the chloroacetanilide class of herbicides. Its IUPAC name is 2-chloro-N-(ethoxymethyl)-N-(2-ethyl-6-methylphenyl)acetamide. It is primarily used for selective pre- and early post-emergent control of broadleaf weeds and annual grasses for corn. Acetochlor was granted a conditional registration by USEPA in 1994 in an effort to significantly reduce the total use of corn herbicides in the United States, including alachlor, metolachlor, atrazine, and 2,4-D. [1441, 1444] In the United States, an estimated 32.3 million pounds of acetochlor is applied to field corn annually. [1442]
Acetochlor enters the water through accidental spills, spraying, leaching from fields via run off, and leaching of groundwater. Peak concentrations of the herbicide are detected in surface waters during the spring, as a result of runoff.
In addition to the goal of reduced herbicide use, the USEPA required an extensive surface water monitoring program intended to limit potential risks to human health and the environment. In the event the time-weighted annual mean concentration (AMC) of acetochlor exceeds 2ug/L, the Acetochlor Registration Partnership (ARP), consisting of Monsato and Dow AgroSciences, would suspend sales. [1444] This threshold was selected by USEPA as a conservative limit based on the maximum contaminant level in effect for alachlor. [1444]
Studies indicate that acetochlor and its degradates, acetochlor ethanesulfonic acid (ESA) and acetochlor oxanilic acid (OXA), are moderately persistent and moderately to very mobile in soil, depending on the characteristics of the soil where it is applied. As a result, there is a relatively high potential for acetochlor residues to reach ground and surface water. Acetochlor does not appear to pose a significant risk of concentrating through the food chain. [1441]
Acetochlor is not regulated by USEPA in drinking water, but is an unregulated chemical drinking water contaminant listed on the USEPA's Contaminant Candidate Lists 1, 2 and 3. [620] USEPA has classified acetochlor as “likely to be carcinogenic to humans” based on tumor incidence in animals, and has determined that acetochlor and alachlor share a common mechanism of toxicity for formation of nasal tumors in rats. [1442, 1444] Both degradates, however, appear to be less toxic than parent compounds in all species tested. [1444]
Date of Literature Search: December 2009

Contaminant Name: Acetochlor | Function: Adsorptive Media | Details: Removal of acetochlor by adsorptive media can be effective and is dependent on the contaminant properties (chemical structure, hydrophobicity) and the surface charge of the media. One study showed a removal range of 52 to 93 percent, based on varying concentrations of herbicide, clay, and organic cation surfactant. The same study reported acetochlor removal of greater than 99 percent in a column filter comprised of layers of organoclay complex and quartz sand. Another bench-scale study found acetochlor removal in the range of 11 to 79 percent, depending on the type of organic cation and the level of saturation of surfactant on the clay surface.
March 2009

Contaminant Name: Acetochlor | Function: Biological Treatment | Details: Removal of acetochlor by biological treatment was found to be quite effective. One bench-scale study reported removal of between 84 and greater than 99 percent by mixed microbial cultures, primarily comprised of strains belonging to Pseudomonas and Bacillus. Another bench-scale study performed with biofilms colonized on polycarbonate film bands placed in surface water spiked with acetochlor was less effective at removing the herbicide, resulting in 26 percent removal.
March 2009

Contaminant Name: Acetochlor | Function: Chlorine | Details: Chlorine is ineffective in removing acetochlor. Based on data from two studies, acetochlor was removed from -1 to 23 percent. Results from one study showed that acetochlor’s degradates that lacked the acetanilide functional group were removed completly, suggesting this functional group provides some level of protection against oxidation from free chlorine.
March 2009

Contaminant Name: Acetochlor | Function: Conventional Treatment | Details: Removal of acetochlor by conventional treatment was found to be ineffective. Three studies, one bench-scale and two full-scale, reported between 4 and 23 percent removal.
March 2009

Contaminant Name: Acetochlor | Function: GAC Isotherm | Details: An activated carbon isotherm study showed a relatively high adsorption capacity for acetochlor by GAC. The study also showed that acetochlor had a higher adsorption affinity than its degradates, implying that more water-soluble compounds sorb less to activated carbon.
March 2009

Contaminant Name: Acetochlor | Function: Granular Activated Carbon | Details: GAC adsorption was effective for the removal of acetochlor. Based on data available from two full-scale studies, GAC achieved between 59 and 63 percent removal. Activated carbon treatment was found to be more effective for acetochlor than for its degradates, suggested by one study to be due to the more polar nature of the degradates.
March 2009

Contaminant Name: Acetochlor | Function: Other Treatment | Details: Bank filtration was effective in the removal of acetochlor, resulting in eliminating between 72 and 95 percent of the contaminant. It should be noted that the mean residence time for the bank filtration process was estimated at 6 days, suggesting other processes could have contributed to the herbicide removal (e.g. biological treatment).

Anodic Fenton Treatment, a process not typically seen in water treatment applications, was found to be effective in removing acetochlor. A study showed almost 98 percent removal in three minutes using a glass reactor consisting of two half-cells connected by an anion membrane.
March 2009

Contaminant Name: Acetochlor | Overall: The following processes were found to be effective for the removal of acetochlor: granular activated carbon (59 to 63 percent), ozone (56 to 68 percent), powdered activated carbon (43 to 99 percent), ultraviolet irradiation (44 to 97 percent), and ultraviolet irradiation hydrogen peroxide (70 percent removal).
Processes ineffective for the removal of acetochlor were chlorine (0 to 23 percent) and conventional treatment (4 to 23 percent).
Other treatments employing processes not typical to drinking water treatment were suggestive of acetochlor removal. They were: organoclay complexes, biological treatment, and anodic fenton treatment.
Research was identified evaluating the following treatment technologies for the removal of acetochlor:

Contaminant Name: Acetochlor | Function: Ozone | Details: Ozone is effective for the removal of acetochlor in drinking water applications. One full-scale study demonstrated the removal of a small concentration of acetochlor to below detection limits by ozone doses typically required for disinfection. Three studies, reporting both bench and full-scale data, found removals between 56 and 68 percent.
June 2009

Contaminant Name: Acetochlor | Function: Powdered Activated Carbon | Details: Removal of acetochlor with PAC is effective (43 to 99 percent removal) under a range of water quality parameters and PAC doses in bench and full-scale studies performed with several surface waters. Increased removal was achieved with higher PAC doses and more contact time. Removal was suggested to be impeded by decreased pH and increased NOM.
June 2009

Contaminant Name: Acetochlor | Function: Ultraviolet Irradiation | Details: Data are suggestive that ultraviolet (UV) irradiation is effective for the removal of acetochlor. Based on data from one study, a range of 77 to 97 percent photodegradation was observed with the addition of 1 to 10 mg/L of humic acid. The control scenario, with no humic acid added, resulted in 71 percent removal. The author suggested that humic acids (as well as fulvic acids and some inorganic ions) could sensitize photodegradation up to a certain concentration before having a counteractive effect by absorbing/blocking the light energy. However, another study found that the most effective scenario was the control scenario, removing 62 percent of the acetochlor present, with increasing amounts of humic acids reducing the effects of UV. 20 mg/L of humic acid resulted in 44 percent removal.
April 2009

Contaminant Name: Acetochlor | Function: Ultraviolet Irradiation and Hydrogen Peroxide | Details: Results of one study suggested that the presence of hydrogen peroxide increased the removal of acetochlor when irradiated with ultraviolet light in comparison to UV treatment alone. The addition of 10 mg/L of hydrogen peroxide increased acetochlor removal from 62 to 70 percent. This is likely due to the increased formation of hydroxyl radicals which are a strong oxidizer.
March 2009

Contaminant Name: Acetochlor Degradates | Description: Acetochlor is a synthetic organic compound of the chloroacetanilide class of herbicides, primarily used for selective pre- and early post-emergent control of broadleaf weeds and annual grasses for corn. Its IUPAC name is 2-chloro-N-(ethoxymethyl)-N-(2-ethyl-6-methylphenyl)-acetamide [1444]. Acetochlor is not currently regulated by USEPA in drinking water, but was listed on the Contaminant Candidate Lists 1, 2 and 3 [529, 859]. USEPA has classified acetochlor as “likely to be carcinogenic to humans” based on tumor incidence in animals [1444].
In the environment, acetochlor generates several degradation products that can be divided into two categories: ionic degradates, which include acetochlor ethanesulfonic acid (ESA), acetochlor oxanilic acid (OXA) and acetochlor sulfinylacetic acid, and neutral degradates, which mainly include hydroxyacetochlor, deschloroacetochlor, 2-chloro-2’-ethyl-6’-methylacetanilide, 2-hydroxy-2’-ethyl-6’-methylacetanilide, 2’-ethyl-6’-methylacetanilide, 2-ethyl-6-methylaniline [1820]. The principal means of degradation is microbial. 
Some research states that metabolites of herbicides may be more important environmental contaminants than the parent compounds, and that they are typically encountered at concentrations much higher than the parent herbicides [1446, 1820]. Other research states that some neutral degradates appear to be more toxic than their ionic counterparts [1450].
Although environmental degradates of the chloroacetamide herbicides are not currently regulated in the United States, the CCL3 includes acetochlor ESA and acetochlor OXA for which drinking water standards may be established in the future [859].
Acetochlor ESA is 2-[(2-ethyl-6-methylphenyl)(ethoxymethyl)amino]-2-oxoethanesulfonic acid.  Acetochlor OXA is 2-[(2-ethyl-6-methylphenyl)(ethoxymethyl)amino]-2-oxoacetic acid. 
Reregistration documents for acetochlor state that ESA and OXA are found frequently in source and drinking waters.  In surface waters, their concentrations are in the same order of magnitude as the parent acetochlor.  In ground waters, their concetrations may exceed those of the parent.  ESA and OXA are not likely to be carcinogenic and are significantly less toxic than the parent.

Contaminant Name: Acetochlor Degradates | Function: Biological Treatment | Details: Removal of the acetochlor degradate 2-ethyl-6-methylaniline by biological treatment was found to be quite effective in a bench-scale study. A mixed microbial culture primarily comprised of strains belonging to Kocuria and Bacillus was able to remove 99 percent of the contaminant. Over a nine day study of the biotransformation of the parent compound acetochlor, the concentration of 2-ethyl-6-methylaniline increased steadily until day four, where it was almost completely removed in 24 hours by the mixed culture.

Contaminant Name: Acetochlor Degradates | Function: Chlorine | Details: Chlorine is ineffective in removing the following acetochlor degradates: ESA, OXA, hydroxyacetochlor, deschloroacetochlor, 2-chloro-2'-ethyl-6'-methylacetanilide, 2-hydroxy-2'-ethyl-6'-methylacetanilide, and 2'-ethyl-6'-methylacetanilide. Based on data from three studies, these contaminants were removed from -76 to 16 percent when only chlorine was used, or when chlorine was used concurrently with conventional treatment.  Results from one study showed that degradates lacking the acetanilide functional group (2-ethyl-6-methylaniline) were removed completely, suggesting this functional group provides some level of protection against oxidation from free chlorine.

Contaminant Name: Acetochlor Degradates | Function: Conventional Treatment | Details: Removal of the following acetochlor degradates by conventional treatment was found to be ineffective: ESA, OXA, hydroxyacetochlor, deschloroacetochlor, 2-chloro-2'-ethyl-6'-methylacetanilide, 2-hydroxy-2'-ethyl-6'-methylacetanilide, 2'-ethyl-6'-methylacetanilide, and 2-ethyl-6-methylaniline. Three studies, one bench-scale and two full-scale, reported between -76 and 16 percent removal when conventional treatment was used, or when conventional treatment was used concurrently with chlorine.

Contaminant Name: Acetochlor Degradates | Function: GAC Isotherm | Details: An activated carbon isotherm study showed that acetochlor degradates vary considerably in their affinity for PAC.  A commercially available PAC was used in this study rather ground GAC. 
Hydroxyacetochlor and deschloroacetochlor had the highest adsorption capacities, followed by 2-chloro-2'-ethyl-6'-methylacetanilide and 2-ethyl-6-methylaniline.  Lowest capacities were observed for 2'-ethyl-6'-methylacetanilide and 2-hydroxy-2'-ethyl-6'-methylacetanilide. 
The approximate order of the study also showed that acetochlor had a higher adsorption affinity than its degradates, and that more water-soluble compounds sorb less to activated carbon.

Contaminant Name: Acetochlor Degradates | Function: Granular Activated Carbon | Details: GAC adsorption was somewhat effective for the removal of the following acetochlor degradates: ESA, OXA, hydroxyacetochlor, deschloroacetochlor, 2-chloro-2'-ethyl-6'-methylacetanilide, 2-hydroxy-2'-ethyl-6'-methylacetanilide, and 2'-ethyl-6'-methylacetanilide.  Based on data available from two full-scale studies, GAC typically achieved between 27 and 52 percent removal when employed concurrently with conventional treatment. Activated carbon treatment was found to be more effective for acetochlor than for its degradates.

Contaminant Name: Acetochlor Degradates | Function: Other Treatment | Details: Based on two years of data from a full scale study, bank filtration was found to be ineffective in the removal of acetochlor degradates, eliminating between -26 and 30 percent of acetochlor ESA and OXA. The study found that the parent acetamides are generally less stable or are better adsorbed to sediments than are their degradates.

Contaminant Name: Acetochlor Degradates | Overall: The following processes were found to be somewhat effective for the removal of ESA, OXA, hydroxyacetochlor, deschloroacetochlor, 2-chloro-2'-ethyl-6'-methylacetanilide, 2-hydroxy-2'-ethyl-6'-methylacetanilide, and 2'-ethyl-6'-methylacetanilide: granular activated carbon (27 to 52 percent) and ozone (55 to 73 percent). Powdered activated carbon was found to be somewhat effective in removing OXA and ESA (typically 20 to 80 percent). The use of chlorine and ozone were quite effective in removing 2-ethyl-6-methylaniline (100 percent).
Processes ineffective for the removal of ESA and OXA were chlorine (-76 to 16 percent), conventional treatment (-76 to 16 percent), and bank filtration (-26 to 30 percent). Chlorine (3 to 6 percent) and conventional treatment (4 to 6 percent) were also found to be ineffective in removing hydroxyacetochlor, deschloroacetochlor, 2-chloro-2'-ethyl-6'-methylacetanilide, 2-hydroxy-2'-ethyl-6'-methylacetanilide, and 2'-ethyl-6'-methylacetanilide.
Biological treatment, which is not a typical drinking water treatment process, was suggestive of 2-ethyl-6-methylaniline removal. 
Research was identified evaluating the following treatment technologies for the removal of acetochlor degradates:

Contaminant Name: Acetochlor Degradates | Function: Ozone | Details: Ozone is effective for the removal of  acetochlor degradates in drinking water applications (ESA, OXA, hydroxyacetochlor, deschloroacetochlor, 2-chloro-2'-ethyl-6'-methylacetanilide, 2-hydroxy-2'-ethyl-6'-methylacetanilide, 2'-ethyl-6'-methylacetanilide, and 2-ethyl-6-methylaniline). Two studies, one bench-scale and one full-scale, found typical removals between 55 and 100 percent. Studies stated that due to the cleaving of double bonds and the opening of aromatic rings, alcohols, carbonyls, and carboxylic acids are created during ozonation.

Contaminant Name: Acetochlor Degradates | Function: Powdered Activated Carbon | Details: Removal of acetochlor degradates ESA and OXA with PAC is effective (typically about 20 to 80 percent removal) under a range of water quality parameters and PAC doses in bench and full-scale studies performed with several surface waters. Increased removal was achieved with higher doses and increased contact time. Removal was suggested to be impeded by decreased pH and increased NOM.

Contaminant Name: Alachlor | Function: Adsorptive Media | Details: Adsorption can be very effective (up to greater than 99 percent removal) for alachlor removal. Alachlor removal by adsorptive media is largely dependent on the initial concentration, adsorbent type, and water chemistry (pH and competing anions).
High removal (85 to 99.5) can be achieved by adsorptive media made from clay-micelle and clay-vesicle systems in bench scale studies. The clay complexes are formed using different surfactants which have the affinity to adsorb alachlor.

Contaminant Name: Alachlor | Function: Biological Filtration | Details: Biological filtration using biologically-active carbon (BAC) was found to be highly effective in the removal of alachlor (70 to 100 percent) based on data available from one study.

Contaminant Name: Alachlor | Function: Chlorine | Details: Chlorine was not particularly effective for the removal of alachlor (30 percent) in bench-scale and full-scale studies with surface water. Sequential oxidation by chlorine following conventional treatment did not result in any greater increased in removal compared to chlorination only.  Higher removal percentages (up to 80 percent) were observed when chlorine was used in following conventional treatment and PAC addition.  The increased removal is predominantly attributed to the addition of PAC.

Contaminant Name: Alachlor | Function: Chlorine Dioxide | Details: Oxidation of alachlor by chlorine dioxide in lab-modified and surface water was studied in one bench-scale experiment. The results indicated alachlor was not effectively removed by chlorine dioxide (

Contaminant Name: Alachlor | Function: Conventional Treatment | Details: Removal of alachlor from water by conventional treatment alone is not very effective (less than 40 percent). Conventional treatment alone may be suitable for applications where influent alachlor concentrations are slightly above the maximum contaminant limit and minimial removal is required. Higher removal rates (80 to 90 percent) can achieved by addition of other concurrent or sequential treatment processes (e.g., ozone or PAC)

Contaminant Name: Alachlor | Function: GAC Isotherm | Details: Studies evaluating the adsorption capacity of GAC in distilled water, ground water and river water found that the Freundlich K values varied significantly depending on the source water.
The greatest adsorption capacity was achieved with filtered ground water . The lowest adsorption capacity, on the other hand, was found with filtered surface water.

Contaminant Name: Alachlor | Function: Granular Activated Carbon | Details: Granular activated carbon (GAC) has been identified as a best available technology (BAT) for the removal of alachlor.  Removal of alachlor from water by tailored or modified GAC can be very effective (almost 100 percent). Tailored or modified GAC pre-loaded bioactive adsorbers also demonstrated effective adsorption of alachlor.
The effectiveness of GAC for alachlor removal from water in practical applications is adversely affected by backgorund organics compounds, such as humic acids[727].

Contaminant Name: Alachlor | Function: Hydrogen Peroxide | Details: Based on available data, the addition of hydrogen peroxide alone does not result in the removal of alachlor from water.   However, chemicals such as copper oxide nanoparticles are efficient catalysts for the degradation of alachlor using hydrogen peroxide. It was also discovered that there is an apparent optimum hydrogen peroxide dose to achieve maximum removal, and addition of excess  hydrogen peroxide results in a decrease in the removal of alachlor [706].

Contaminant Name: Alachlor | Function: Membrane Separation | Details: Reverse osmosis (RO) has been demonstrated to be effective for the removal of alachlor (71 to 99 percent).  Multiple membrane materials have been shown to be effective, including thin film composites, nylon amide and cellulose acetate,  in single-pass RO units treating surface water. In a pilot-scale study, 100 percent removal was observed when ground water spiked with alachlor was treated using a thin film composite membrane [728].
Nanofiltration (NF) membranes were also able reject 100 percent of the influent alachlor at low pressures (50 to 200 psi).  The critical factor in determining the effectiveness of RO and NF for the removal of alachlor is the molecular weight cut-off (MWCO) of the membrane relative to the molecular weight of alachlor (269.8).

Contaminant Name: Alachlor | Overall: The following processes are considered effective for the removal of alachlor: adsorptive media using clay complexes (up to greater than 99 percent removal), granular activated carbon (up to 100 percent removal), biological filtration (70 to 100 percent), membrane separation using reverse osmosis (71 to 98 percent removal), ozone (69 to 97 percent), ozone  hydrogen peroxide, powdered activated carbon (50 to 75 percent), ultraviolet irradiation, ultraviolet irradiation hydrogen peroxide, and ultraviolet irradiation ozone. The level of control for granular activated carbon is consistent with the designation as Best Available Technology for alachlor removal. Performance of these technologies can vary based on water quality and treatment characteristics.
Conventional treatment, chlorine, chlorine dioxide, and permanganate were not shown to be effective for the removal of alachlor.
Research was identified evaluating the following treatment technologies for the removal of Alachlor:

Contaminant Name: Alachlor | Function: Ozone | Details: Effective removal (69 to 97 percent) of alachlor by ozone was observed during bench- and pilot-scale studies utilizing both ground and surface waters. The ozone dose needed to achieve these removal percentage is very high (7 to 28 mg/L) compared to typical doses used in drinking water treatment [721][728].
Moderate removal (70 percent) is achieved at low ozone doses (4.3 mg/L). Higher removal (80 to 90 percent) can be achieved when ozone application is combined alum and/or PAC addition [723].
The effect of ozonation can be enhanced by the use of metal catalysts such as Fe+2, Mn+2, and Cu/Al2O3 [716]. Alachlor degradation was observed to increase as the pH increased.

Contaminant Name: Alachlor | Function: Ozone and Hydrogen Peroxide | Details: Hydrogen peroxide can enhance the removal of alachlor by ozone within limitations.  It was observed that above a certain ozone-to-hydrogen peroxide ratio, the hydrogen peroxide acts as hydroxyl ion scavenger rather than a hydroxyl ion generator [720]. Determination of the optimum ratio between these oxidants is site-specific and the most important criterion for this treatment technology.

Contaminant Name: Alachlor | Function: Permanganate | Details: Experiments investigating the removal of alachlor by permanganate indicated no reduction in influent concentration. Based on the data available from one study, permanagate has no impact on alachlor concentrations in water under the conditions evaluated.

Contaminant Name: Alachlor | Function: Powdered Activated Carbon | Details: Application of PAC alone results in moderate alachlor removal (50 to 75 percent). Very effective alachlor removal  can be achieved  when PAC is used with ozone and/or alum and/or chlorine.

Contaminant Name: Alachlor | Function: Ultraviolet Irradiation | Details: Direct photolysis of alachlor with experiments using UVA (320 to 400 nm) or UVB (290 to 320 nm) was not very effective. The effect of UV irradiation can be enhanced by using catalysts such as titanium dioxide. UV treament in the presence of titanium dioxide can be highly effective for alachlor degradation in water.

Contaminant Name: Alachlor | Function: Ultraviolet Irradiation and Hydrogen Peroxide | Details: One study suggests ultraviolet irradiation and hydrogen peroxide with iron as catalyst (i.e., a photo-Fenton process) can be effective for alachlor degradation following relatively long periods of exposure compared to typical water treatment technologies. Up to 50% degradation of alachlor was reached in approximately 10 hours. The presence of titanium dioxide increased the degradation to 65% with no increase in reaction time.
Nearly 100% degradation can be achieved in 30 minutes at very high doses (340 mg/L) of hydrogen peroxide in an acidic medium (pH=2.8).  These conditions are not typical of drinking water treatment.

Contaminant Name: Alachlor | Function: Ultraviolet Irradiation and Ozone | Details: Based on the results of one study, UV irradiation and ozone can be an effective process for alachlor degradation (85 percent in deionized water). Lower alachlor degradation was seen when experiment was conducted with surface water (60 percent) presumably as a result of oxidant demand and hydroxyl scavengers present in the natural surface water.  It is worth noting, that the use of ferrous ions as a catalyst in the UV/ozone process resulted no increase in alachlor degradation.

Contaminant Name: Alachlor Degradates | Description: Alachlor or 2-chloro-2'-6'-diethyl-N-methoxymethyl acetanilide is a widely used herbicide in the United States, Europe, and Japan. It is a pre-emergent herbicide used in corn and soybean agriculture and is also a known endocrine distruptor. USEPA has classified alachlor as a B2 carcinogen and has set a maximum contaminant level (MCL) of 2 μg/l for alachlor in drinking water [1449].
While historically, alachlor has been the primary concern, research has recently shown that many of its environmental degradates also occur in drinking water sources. Hydroxyalachlor (2-hydroxy-2',6'-diethyl-N-(methoxymethyl)acetanilide) has been shown to possess similar toxicity to alachlor. Two additional alachlor degradates, 2-choloro-2',6'-diethylacetanilide and 2-hydroxy-2',6'-diethyacetanilide have been shown to be weakly mutagenic, and 2-chloro-2',6'-diethylacetanilide has been shown to form DNA adducts (DNA bonded to a cancer causing chemical). The primary aniline degradate of alachlor, 2,6-diethylaniline, has been reported to be teratogenic and can be altered to demonstrate mutagenic behavior [1449].
Although environmental degradates of the chloroacetamide herbicides are not currently regulated in the United States, they are included on the US EPA's Contaminant Candidate List (CCL3) for which drinking water standards may be established in the future [1450]. Alachlor EthaneSulfonic Acid (ESA) and Alachlor Oxanilic Acid (OA) are the two degradates on the CCL3. 
Date of Literature Search: June 2009
