Contaminant Name: Alachlor Degradates | Function: Chlorine | Details: Chlorine was found to be ineffective in the removal of six of the seven alachlor degradates evaluated in surface water according to the results of one bench-scale study. Generally, removal ranged from 0 to 16 percent with a dose of 6 mg/L of chlorine and 6 hours of contact time. Greater than 99 percent removal, however, was achieved for 2,6-diethylaniline under the same conditions.

Contaminant Name: Alachlor Degradates | Function: Conventional Treatment | Details: Data from one full-scale study are suggestive that conventional treatment employing coagulation with either alum or ferric chloride was found to be ineffective in the removal of all of the evaluated neutral alachlor degradates. Between 2 and 7 percent removal was achieved with coagulant doses of 20 or 30 mg/L and a detention time of one hour.

Contaminant Name: Alachlor Degradates | Function: GAC Isotherm | Details: Alachlor and alachlor degradates vary considerably in their affinity for powdered activated carbon (PAC). Based on data from one study, alachlor was found to have a higher adsorption affinity to PAC (Freundlich K value of 266 mg/kg) than all of its environmental degradates (Freundlich K values of 46 to 131 mg/kg).

Contaminant Name: Alachlor Degradates | Function: Other Treatment | Details: Based on results from one study, river-bank filtration was found to be ineffective in removing very low concentrations of alachlor ethanesulfonic acid (ESA) and alachlor oxanilic acid (OA) from surface water (0 to 19 percent) in a full scale treatment plant.

Contaminant Name: Alachlor Degradates | Overall: Although data are limited, the processes found to be effective for the removal of certain alachlor degradates are ozone (60 to > 99 percent) and powdered activated carbon. Performance of these technologies can vary based on water quality and treatment characteristics.
Conventional treatment (2 to 7 percent removal), chlorine (0 to 16 percent), and river-bank filtration (0 to 19 percent) were not shown to be effective for the removal of alachlor degradates.
Research was identified evaluating the following treatment technologies for the removal of some alachlor degradates:

Contaminant Name: Alachlor Degradates | Function: Ozone | Details: Ozonation was found to be effective in the removal of certain alachlor degradates from the studies evaluated.
In one bench scale study, all the evaluated alachlor degradates reacted with ozone (3 mg/l applied dose for 30 min), exhibiting removal efficiencies in the range of 60 to greater than 99 percent.
Limited removal of alachlor ethanesulfonic acid (ESA) was found in a full scale  surface water study using ozone. However, alachlor oxanilic acid (OA) showed good degradation (> 83 percent) in the same full scale study.

Contaminant Name: Aldicarb | Description: Aldicarb is a systemic carbamate pesticide applied to the soil for control of insect, mite and nematode pests in plants [1490]. Its chemical name is 2-methyl-2-(methylthio) propionaldehyde O-methylcarbamoyl) oxime, with trade names of Temik or , UC21149 [1490].
 
Since its introduction in 1970s, aldicarb is used widely on cotton, soybeans, peanuts and many other crops all over the world. The United States consumes approximately 70% of world production [1490]. Due to aldiarb’s contamination of groundwater, Union Carbide Corporation excluded or limited the use of aldicarb products in some states (certain area or the whole state) in the United States, including New York, Maine, Wisconsin, Connecticut, Delaware, Massachusetts, Maryland, Atlantic Region, New Jersey, Rhode Island, and Virginia [1491].
 
On July 1, 1991, the USEPA established maximum contaminant levels (MCLs) and maximum contaminant level goals (MCLGs) for aldicarb and its degradation products (aldicarb sulfoxide and aldicarb sulfone) with an effective date of January 1, 1993. The MCLs are 0.003 mg/L, 0.004 mg/L and 0.002 mg/L for aldicarb, aldicarb sulfoxide and aldicarb sulfone, respectively.  The MCLGs are 0.001 mg/L for  each of these three chemicals. However, the effective date was postponed by EPA on May 27, 1992.  The MCLs and MCLGs are presently stayed [1492, 1493]. 
 
Aldicarb has been found to potentially cause nausea, diarrhea and relatively minor neurological symptoms as results of acute exposure at levels above the MCL of 0.003 mg/L [1491]. Chronic exposure to aldicarb above the MCL can potentially cause neurological effects, for example, sweating, papillary constriction and leg weakness [1491]. Aldicarb is categorized as “not classifiable as to human carcinogenicity” [1494].
 
Aldicarb may be released to the environment during its manufacturing and usage as a pesticide.  Since aldicarb is applied in soil, its primary release is to soil. Aldicarb degrades quickly in most soils. Half-lives of aldicarb in soil have been reported to be in a general range 1 to 15 days, depending on soil type, temperature, moisture and pH [1490, 1491]. Aldicarb is susceptible to alkaline hydrolysis so it is expected to be unstable in high soil pH  [1490]. On the other hand, its persistence in acidic soils with pH below 5.5 is expected to be longer [1485]. Leaching studies have indicated that aldicarb is mobile in coarse sandy soil; however, leaching is limited in clay loam and muck soils [1497].
 
Aldicarb is very persistent in groundwater, especially in water with low pH [1495]. Its half-life for degradation to non-toxic products ranges from a few weeks to years [1495]. The primary mode of degradation is chemical hydrolysis in cold areas and chemical hydrolysis and microbial decay in warm areas [1496].

Contaminant Name: Aldicarb | Function: GAC Isotherm | Details: An isotherm study reported Freundlich parameters for aldicarb in distilled-deionized water and filtered river water [1486]. Results and discussion were not presented in detail.

Contaminant Name: Aldicarb | Function: Granular Activated Carbon | Details: Removal of aldicarb by a granular activated carbon filter was reported as 97 percent in a lab water [1489].  The GAC filter, Pentek model RFC20-BB, was a component of a point-of-entry Hyd-RO-Secure drinking water treatment system. The system consisted of a RO module, an advanced simultaneous oxidation process (ASOP) module, and the GAC filter. The GAC filter was tested following the ASOP module. The removal percentage reported was for GAC only. The GAC influent concentration was 160 μg/L with the pH level of 7.6. GAC properties were not reported.

Contaminant Name: Aldicarb | Function: Membrane Separation | Details: Removal of aldicarb with reverse osmosis (RO) membrane was very effective in two studies (equal or greater than 98 percent removal) [1487, 1488]. The membrane units tested were components of two point-of-use drinking water treatment systems, which were Watts Premier WP-4V and Ecowater Systems ERO-R450E. The RO membrane of each system was tested alone for aldicarb removal. The RO influent concentrations were 1,000 μg/L and 1,100 μg/L in the two studies. The influent pH levels were in the range of 7.0 to 8.0 in the two studies.

Contaminant Name: Aldicarb | Overall: The following processes are effective for the removal of aldicarb: reverse osmosis membrane (equal or greater than 98 percent removal), granular activated carbon (97 percent removal) and ultraviolet irradiation with ozone (83 percent removal).  Performance of these technologies can vary based on dose, contact time and water quality characteristics.
 
Research has identified GAC isotherm data and evaluation of three treatment technologies for the removal of aldicarb:

Contaminant Name: Aldicarb | Function: Ozone | Details: One study found ozone to be effective in removing aldicarb from water over wide range of pH [1583].  Kinetics of aldicarb removal at  basic pH (>7) was found to be very effective [1583].

Contaminant Name: Aldicarb | Function: Ultraviolet Irradiation and Ozone | Details: Ultraviolet irradiation (UV) and ozone treatment was reported to remove aldicarb effectively in a lab water by 83 percent [1489]. The UV and ozone unit was a component of a point-of-entry drinking water treatment system. The system consisted of a reverse osmosis (RO) module, the UV and ozone module (named as advanced simultaneous oxidation process, or ASOP), followed by a granular activated carbon (GAC) filter. The UV contact time was 33 seconds; however, the UV intensity and ozone dose were not reported. The influent to the UV and ozone module contained 930 μg/L aldicarb with the pH level in the range of 7.0 to 8.0. The effluent aldicarb concentration from the UV and ozone unit was 160 μg/L.

Contaminant Name: Anatoxin-a | Description: Anatoxins are toxic constituents of blue-green algae produced by Anabaena flos-aquae and a number of other freshwater species of cyanobacteria [2809]. Cyanobacteria naturally occur in all freshwater ecosystems, but with excess phosphorus and nitrogen can reproduce rapidly, or bloom, decreasing dissolved oxygen and degrading overall water quality. This can create unsafe conditions in recreational waters, especially for animals that consume the water. Although there are relatively few documented cases of severe human health effects, exposure to cyanobacteria or their toxins may produce allergic reactions such as skin rashes, eye irritations, respiratory symptoms and in some cases gastroenteritis, liver and kidney failure or death [2810]. Anatoxin-a is a very soluble neurotoxin produced by cyanobacteria that can impair nerves and cause muscle paralysis [2809]. 
Date of literature search: April 2022

Contaminant Name: Anatoxin-a | Function: Adsorptive Media | Details: One bench-scale study [2745] evaluated the effectiveness of four types of naturally occurring adsorptive clay minerals for treatment of anatoxin-a at neutral pHs and ambient temperatures. The absorptive clays were moderately effective, with removal varying as a function of the clays adsorbent capacity and surface area (from 34 to 92 percent removal). The most effective clay mineral appeared to be kerolite which had the highest surface area and adsorptive capacity.

Contaminant Name: Anatoxin-a | Function: Chlorine | Details: Chlorine was investigated for anatoxin-a treatment in bench-scale studies [2775, 2776] that tested a variety treatment options for this cyanotoxin. Chlorine was not effective in reducing anatoxin-a concentrations with removal values ranging from 8 to 10 percent.

Contaminant Name: Anatoxin-a | Function: Chlorine Dioxide | Details: Chlorine dioxide was investigated for anatoxin-a treatment in a bench-scale study [2775] that tested a variety of treatment options. Like chlorine, chlorine dioxide was not effective in reducing anatoxin-a concentrations (approximately 10 percent reduction).

Contaminant Name: Anatoxin-a | Function: GAC Isotherm | Details: Two studies present isotherm constants for adsorption of anatoxin-a by GAC [2785, 2786]. The results suggest that GAC is potentially effective, depending on the GAC material type.

Contaminant Name: Anatoxin-a | Function: Hydrogen Peroxide | Details: One bench-scale study [2781] found hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) alone to be ineffective for control of anatoxin-a [2781]. Several other bench studies [2764, 2768, 2769] employed oxidation through Fenton-type reactions by combining H2O2 with the addition of iron. Maximum removal observed in these studies was greater than 99 percent.

Contaminant Name: Anatoxin-a | Overall: Most of the published papers reviewed on anatoxin-a water treatment were studies conducted at the bench scale. Based on the literature, the following treatment processes appear to be effective for the treatment of anatoxin-a in water: ozone (greater than 99 percent removal), ozone plus hydrogen peroxide (greater than 99 percent removal), and permanganate (greater than 95 percent removal). Granular activated carbon (GAC) is potentially effective based on isotherm data. 
 Adsorptive media was moderately to very effective depending on the treatment media used (34 to 92 percent removal). Hydrogen peroxide alone was ineffective, but when combined with Fenton-type oxidation reactions and the addition of iron, the removal rate increased (up to greater than 99 percent removal). PAC showed a range of effectiveness (22 to 98 percent removal) depending on the specific and contact time. UV irradiation alone was marginally to moderately effective (3 to 88 percent removal), but removal rates increased when combined with the additional of titanium dioxide or peroxymonosulfate. In bench studies, UV plus hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) was shown to be an effective treatment (up to 99 percent removal), but a wide range of removal rates were reported depending on UV radiation type, H2O2 dose, and contract times. 
Research was identified evaluating the following treatment technologies for anatoxin-a:

Contaminant Name: Arsenic | Description: Arsenic occurs naturally in rock, soil and biota, all of which release it to water. In water, inorganic forms are more common in than organic forms. [595]
Most (90 percent) arsenic use in the US is for the wood preservative chromated copper arsenate (CCA). Other uses include paint, pharmaceuticals, dyes and semi-conductors. [592] Improper discharges can release arsenic into water. Other releases may come from industrial process including burning of fuels, mining, smelting and paper production. [595]
As(3) is the predominant ground water form. In surface waters, As(5) is the predominant form as surface aeration oxidizes As(3) to As(5). [591] Factors affecting the fate and transport of arsenic in water include its oxidation state, oxidation-reduction potential, pH, the levels of iron and sulfide, temperature, salinity and biota. [595] Arsenic is also impacted by the presence of suspended solids. In surface water, arsenic is more likely sorbed to particulates than dissolved. [595]
Arsenic is linked to cancer of the bladder, lungs, skin, kidneys, liver and prostate and has non-carcinogenic cardiovascular, pulmonary, neurological and endocrine effects. [592] The maximum contaminant level (MCL) is to protect against effects of bladder and lung cancers, and to reduce the non-carcinogenic effects. [594]
Total arsenic is regulated in drinking water in the US with a MCL of 0.010 mg/L or 10 ug/L. USEPA has identified several treatment processes as Best Available Technologies (BATs)for control of arsenic in drinking water. They are: ion exchange, activated alumina, reverse osmosis, coagulation/filtration, lime softening (pH exceeding 10.5), electrodialysis reversal, and oxidation/filtration or iron removal (Fe:As greater than 20:1). [597] The BATS are based on the removal of As(5).
For purposes of research and design of treatment processes, the speciation of As(5) and As(3) needs to be known as do the soluble and particulate fractions. Measuring total arsenic, and passing the sample through a 0.45 micron filter to collect the soluble fraction, and passing the soluble fraction through an ion exchange column which retains As(5) allows for speciation and fractionation.
Arsenic in both surface water and in ground water is typically less than 10 ug/L. [591] A 1995 survey of arsenic in California waters found a median of 2 ug/L, and found that arsenic exceeded 5 ug/L or greater in 12 percent of wells. [591] Estimates of requirements to meet the 10 ug/L MCL are 7.6 percent of US ground water supplies [596] and 5.5 percent of community water systems (3,000 of the nation's 54,000). [592]
Because of the relatively large number of water systems that will have to meet the 10 ug/L MCL, the USEPA has established an Arsenic Demonstration Program involving 50 locations across the US and numerous treatment technologies including ion exchange, adsorptive media, coagulation/filtration, activated alumina, and oxidation/filtration. [593]

Ref#: 470

Contaminant Name: Arsenic | Function: Adsorptive Media | Details: Adsorption can be very effective (up to greater than 99 percent removal) and is a commonly used method for arsenic treatment. Arsenic removal by adsorptive media is largely dependent on the initial concentration, oxidation state, adsorbent type, and water chemistry (pH and competing anions). Adsorptive removal is more efficient for As(V) than for As(III), so As(III) is usually pre-oxidized to As(V) prior to adsorptive treatment. Aluminum and iron oxide/hydroxides (amorphous or granular) performed the most consistently, achieving greater than 99 percent of As(V). Adsorption on activated alumina is listed as one of the Best Available Technologies (BATs) for arsenic removal. Activated alumina may be used in either throw-away or regenerated modes.
Iron based resins, iron oxide coated sand, iron oxide impregnated activated carbon, iron oxide impregnated activated alumina, titanium based media, zirconium based media, and minerals such as goethite, magnetite also successfully removed up to 95 percent As(V) under appropriate treatment conditions.

Contaminant Name: Arsenic | Function: Aeration and Air Stripping | Details: Effect of aeration on the removal of arsenic was studied in full-scale on a well water sample. Aeration was used as a pre-treatment for the oxidation of arsenic prior to removal. Aeration was largely ineffective in oxidizing As(III) to As(V). Forced draft aeration performance was slightly better than staged bubble aeration. Up to 25 percent of As(III) oxidation was observed with forced draft aeration [606].

Contaminant Name: Arsenic | Function: Biological Filtration | Details: In one pilot study, biological filtration using biologically activated carbon (BAC) was found to be highly effective in the removal of As(V) (97 percent on average). The high removal of arsenic through the BAC filter was due to the presence of iron oxidizing bacteria (e.g. Gallionella) in the BAC filter. Iron present in the raw water was oxidized by the bacteria, and arsenic was removed by co-precipitation with biologically-oxidized iron [236]. At full-scale, however, the removal of total arsenic and As(III) using biological filtration was found to be less effective, with removals of 0 and 22 percent, respectively [2714, 2715]. A bench-scale study observed moderate removal of total arsenic (up to 69 percent) using a pyrolusite and quartzite media. The same study saw up to 77 percent removal of As(III) with the same media [2715].

Contaminant Name: Arsenic | Function: Chemical Treatment | Details: Removal of arsenic by chemical treatment can be effective under appropriate conditions. In one EPA study, Filox, a manganese dioxide-based media, was effective for arsenic (III) oxidation. When dissolved oxygen (DO) was not limiting, complete oxidation was observed under all conditions studied. However, when DO was reduced, incomplete oxidation was obtained in the presence of interfering reductants. The adverse effect of interfering reductants was completely eliminated by supplying enough DO or increasing the contact time. In addition to oxidizing arsenic (III), the Filox media also removed some arsenic by adsorption, which diminished greatly as the media came into equilibrium with the arsenic (III)-spiked synthetic water.

Contaminant Name: Arsenic | Function: Chloramine | Details: Effect of chloramines on the oxidation/ removal of arsenic was studied in two bench-scale experiments. One study used formulated challenge water similar in composition to surface and ground waters. In that case, a monochloramine dose of 0.10 milligrams per liter was added to the oxidation reactor. A 40 percent oxidation of As(III) to As(V) was observed. In the other study, a real surface water sample was used, and a high chloramine residual was obtained. Both studies indicated that chloramines are not very effective in the conversion from As(III) to As(V) [172, 607].

Contaminant Name: Arsenic | Function: Chlorine | Details: Chlorine is very commonly used as a pre-treatment for arsenic removal. If As(III) is present, the water is chlorinated in order to oxidize As(III) to As(V). Removal efficiencies of most arsenic treatment processes are higher with As(V) than with As(III). Greater than 95 percent of As(III) was oxidized to As(V) with the application of chlorine for both ground waters and surface waters.

Contaminant Name: Arsenic | Function: Chlorine Dioxide | Details: One full-scale study with groundwater used chlorine dioxide to oxidize As(III) and found 40 percent oxidation observed at a chlorine dioxide dose of 1.2 mg/L [2715]. Oxidation of As(III) to As(V) using chlorine dioxide was also studied in one bench-scale experiment [607]. The water used in the test was formulated challenge water similar in composition to surface and ground waters. A low dose (0.27 mg/L) of chlorine dioxide was not very effective in the oxidation of As(III) (20 to 30 percent oxidation was observed). However, when the chlorine dioxide dose was increased to 0.90 mg/L, greater than 95 percent oxidation of As(III) was achieved.

Contaminant Name: Arsenic | Function: Conventional Treatment | Details: Removal of arsenic by conventional treatment processes can be very effective under appropriate treatment conditions. Removal efficiencies are higher for As(V) (up to greater than 99 percent removal) than for As(III). Conventional treatment for arsenic removal can constitute of oxidation/filtration, or coagulation/filtration, both of which are Best Available Technologies (BATs) identified by the EPA.

Oxidation/filtration is also known an iron removal and refers to a precipitative process that is designed to remove naturally occurring iron and manganese from water. Arsenic can also be removed by this process using two mechanisms: adsorption and coprecipitation. First an oxidant is applied to the water which oxidizes soluble iron and As(III). Then As(V) adsorbs on to the iron hydroxide precipitates that is ultimately filtered out of the solution. Oxidation/filtration works well when the Fe:As ratio exceeds 20:1. 

Coagulation/filtration processes are more commonly used than oxidation/filtration processes for arsenic removal. Coagulation/filtration is a precipitative process that can be optimized to remove dissolved inorganic As(V) from water. The mechanism involves adsorption and co-precipitation of As(V) to a coagulant precipitate. Both alum and ferric coagulants have been shown to successfully remove As(V) below the maximum contaminant level (MCL) during the coagulation/ filtration process, even though removal efficiencies achieved with ferric were higher than alum. Performance of a coagulation/filtration process is improved when the addition of Fe elevates the Fe:As ratio to above 20:1. Both alum and ferric coagulants may require pH adjustment to achieve optimal arsenic removal for most water sources.

Contaminant Name: Arsenic | Function: Direct Filtration | Details: Removal of arsenic by direct filtration can be very effective (75 percent to greater than 99 percent removal of As(V)), especially with the addition of a coagulant. Direct filtration for arsenic removal can constitute of oxidation/ filtration, or coagulation/ filtration, both of which are Best Available Technologies (BATs) identified by the EPA.
Oxidation/filtration refers to precipitative processes that are designed to remove naturally occurring iron and manganese from water. Arsenic can also be removed by this process using two mechanisms: adsorption and coprecipitation. First an oxidant is applied to the water which oxidizes soluble iron and As(III). Then As(V) adsorbs on to the iron hydroxide precipitates that is ultimately filtered out of the solution.
Coagulation/ filtration processes are more commonly used than oxidation/ filtration processes for arsenic removal. Coagulation/ filtration is a precipitative process that can be optimized to remove dissolved inorganic As(V) from water. The mechanism involves adsorption and co-precipitation of As(V) to a coagulant precipitate. Both alum and ferric coagulants have been shown to successfully remove As(V) below the maximum contaminant level (MCL) during the coagulation/ filtration process, even though removal efficiencies achieved with ferric were higher than alum. Both coagulants may require pH adjustment to achieve optimal arsenic removal for most water sources.
In field applications, arsenic removal via direct filtration is primarily applied to groundwater sources with low turbidity. Sand is primarily used as filter media in direct filtration for the removal of arsenic.  The use of manganese greensand is presented in Ion Exchange.

Contaminant Name: Arsenic | Function: GAC Isotherm | Details: Various studies assessing the effectiveness of modified and unmodified granular activated carbon (GAC) [2699], as well as other adsorptive media, have reported isotherm data for the removal of arsenic. Modified GAC studies included iron-modified [2697, 2699, 2704, 2707, 2711, 2713], manganese-modified [2707, 2711], copper-modified [2705], calcium-modified [2706], and zirconium-modified [2712] GAC. Other adsorptive media with isotherm data included a macroporous polymer coated with coprecipitated iron–aluminum hydroxides [2700] and multiwall carbon nanotubes with zirconia [2709].

Contaminant Name: Arsenic | Function: Granular Activated Carbon | Details: The effectiveness of GAC for the treatment of arsenic has been assessed in various bench-, pilot-, and full-scale studies. In many of the studies, the GAC was modified by the incorporation of constituents such as organic chlorides [2694], organic bromides [2694], iron [2697, 2699, 2707, 2713], or manganese [2707]. Effectiveness was found to be highly variable, with bench-scale studies ranging from 72 to 98 percent removal [2694, 2699, 2707, 2710, 2713], pilot-scale studies ranging from 11 to 98 percent removal [604, 2717], and a full-scale study ranging from 0 to 95 percent [2689]. The treatment conditions and specific GAC chosen will ultimately determine the feasibility for GAC as a treatment for arsenic.

Contaminant Name: Arsenic | Function: Ion Exchange | Details: Ion exchange is among the Best Available Technologies listed by EPA for arsenic treatment. Ion exchange processes can remove arsenic from water very effectively (greater than 99 percent removal), when arsenic is in the As(V) oxidation state. If As(III) is present in the raw water, a concurrent oxidation is usually applied to convert As(III) to As(V). The removal efficiency is dependent upon the resin type, regeneration frequency, initial arsenic concentration, and competing ions in the water (especially sulfate). Strong base anion exchange resins in either the chloride form or the hydroxide form are most consistent in the removal of arsenic. The chloride form is preferred because regeneration is accomplished with a salt rather than caustic solution.

Contaminant Name: Arsenic | Function: Membrane Filtration | Details: Membrane filtration is generally very effective for the removal of arsenic depending on the treatment conditions (up to greater than 99 percent removal) [151, 166, 179, 188, 230, 248, 2689, 2718]. The process typically consists of the addition of an iron-based coagulant to water after oxidation of As(III). Without coagulation, removal of As(V) by membrane filtration is minimal (20% on UF membranes) and less so for As(III)[2718]. With coagulation, the precipitated As-Fe complexes are filtered out by the membrane via cake formation. Lowering of pH prior to the addition of coagulant lowers the coagulant dose necessary (thereby lowering solids loading on the membrane and increasing membrane flux) and also increases arsenic removal efficiency.

Contaminant Name: Arsenic | Function: Membrane Separation | Details: Removal of As(III), As(V), total arsenic, and arsenite from water by membrane separation processes can be very effective (25 to greater than 99 percent removal; 15 to greater than 99 percent removal; 58 to greater than 99 percent removal; and 26 to 93 percent removal, respectively). Reverse osmosis (RO) has been identified as a Best Available Technology for arsenic removal, while nanofiltration has also been used in some cases. RO is a pressure-driven membrane separation process capable of removing arsenic from water by means of particle size, dielectric characteristics, and hydrophilicity/ hydrophobicity. Some laboratory studies demonstrated effective As(V) removal by Micellar Enhanced Ultra-Filtration (MEUF) processes (55 to 96 percent removal) [2718]. Factors such as applied pressure on the membranes, feedwater temperature, influent arsenic concentration, and source water chemical composition, have strong influence on the efficiency of membrane processes.

Contaminant Name: Arsenic | Function: Other Treatment | Details: Three studies were found that included alternative processes for the treatment of arsenic, including electroadsorption [2692], distillation [2702], and electrochemical deposition [2708]. The electroadsorption study utilized GAC connected to either an anode or a cathode in a filter-press cell. Both scenarios proved to poorly remove arsenic, citing up to 66 and up to 50 percent removal, respectively. The point of use distillation study was found to be successful even without preoxidation, reporting greater than 99 percent removal of arsenic across three trials. Electrochemical deposition onto GAC was also found to be effective, removing up to greater than 99 percent arsenic. All three alternative treatments were studied at the bench-scale. Further research is needed to assess the feasibility of application of each process at full-scale.

Contaminant Name: Arsenic | Overall: Inorganic arsenic occurs in surface waters and groundwaters primarily as a combination of As(III) and As(V). As(III) is hydrophilic, neutral species below pH 9, and is not effectively removed by treatment processes. Oxidative treatment with processes such as chlorine, permanganate and ozone are normally utilized to convert As(III) to insoluble As(V), which increases removal efficiency in arsenic treatment processes. Chlorine dioxide can also be effective for oxidizing As (III). Aeration and chloramines are not effective methods for oxidizing As(III). 

The following processes are effective for the removal of As(V) and are among those designated as Best Available Technologies (BATs): activated alumina, ion exchange, reverse osmosis, precipitative softening, coagulation/filtration, and oxidation/filtration. 

In precipitative softening, pHs above 10.5 form magnesium hydroxide to which As(V) is removed by co-precipitation. In coagulation/filtration, As(V) is removed by co-precipitation and adsorption onto aluminum or iron hydroxide precipitates. In oxidation/filtration, soluble iron and As(III) are oxidized, and As(V) is adsorbed onto iron hydroxide precipitates and filtered. These latter two processes are more effective when the Fe:As mass ratio, either naturally or by iron addition, exceeds 20:1. 

All the above processes achieve As removals exceeding 90 percent. The performance of all of these technologies varies based on water quality and treatment characteristics. 
 Other means of filtration (biological filtration, membrane filtration, slow sand filtration, direct filtration, granular activated carbon filtration) may be effective if As(V) is present. Some literature reports biological control of arsenic. Various novel treatment techniques for the removal of arsenic have been studied at the bench scale, including electroadsorption onto GAC, point of use distillation, and electrochemical deposition with GAC, with varying levels of effectiveness.

Research has identified evaluation of the following treatment technologies for the removal of arsenic:

Contaminant Name: Arsenic | Function: Ozone | Details: Oxidation of As(III) to As(V) can be very effective with ozone. Ozone pre-treatment can be used prior to removal of As(V) by other treatment technologies. In two bench-scale studies, greater than 95 percent conversion of As(III) to As(V) was observed for both surface water and formulated challenge water (with composition similar to ground water and surface water) samples. The ozone doses in these experiments varied from 0.1 to 0.8 milligrams per liter and the contact times were between 0.0033 and 2.2 minutes [172, 607].

Contaminant Name: Arsenic | Function: Permanganate | Details: Oxidation of As(III) to As(V) can be very effective with potassium permanganate, as observed in one bench-scale study. The water used in the test was formulated challenge water similar in composition to surface and ground waters. The permanganate dose tested ranged from 0.16 to 3.20 milligrams per liter. The permanganate contact times ranged from 0.25 to 0.85 minutes. Greater than 95 percent oxidation of As(III) to As(V) was observed even at the lowest permanganate dose and the shortest contact time.

Contaminant Name: Arsenic | Function: Precipitative Softening | Details: Precipitative softening is a Best Available Technology (BAT) for arsenic removal. Removal of arsenic by precipitative softening can be effective under certain conditions. In several bench-scale studies, it was shown that arsenic removal by softening varied greatly (between less than 5 and greater than 95 percent) in synthetic groundwater. Lime, in-situ formed calcium carbonate, pre-formed or in-situ formed magnesium hydroxide, and in-situ formed manganese hydroxide were used in experiments to remove arsenic by precipitative softening. For optimal performance in removing both As(III) and As(V), the pH range used in softening experiments varied between 9 and 12. When softening is performed with lime at pHs higher than 10.5, magnesium hydroxide is formed and As(V) is removed by co-precipitation with magnesium hydroxide.

Contaminant Name: Arsenic | Function: Slow Sand Filtration | Details: Removal of arsenic from water by slow sand filtration was shown to be very effective (95 percent removal) in one pilot study. The source water was groundwater with a total arsenic concentration of 17.4 micrograms per liter. Iron was oxidized and precipitated from the water, and arsenic was removed by co-precipitation with iron. The sand filter media had a depth of 550 mm and was supported by quartz. The empty bed contact time of the slow sand filter was 36.9 minutes. One full-scale study found up to 85 percent removal, but performance declined over time and it was not clear whether the final observations would reflect long-term steady state performance [2695]. Another full-scale study found no removal of total arsenic [2689].

Contaminant Name: Arsenic | Function: Ultraviolet Irradiation | Details: Oxidation of As(III) to As(V) using ultraviolet irradiation was tested in one bench-scale study. The water used in the test was formulated challenge water similar in composition to surface and ground waters. For the majority of the experiments, UV was largely ineffective in oxidizing As(III) to As(V), with conversions lower than 50 percent. The UV intensity tested varied between 32 and 41.2 milliWatts per square centimeter. The UV contact times varied between 60 and 1440 minutes.

Contaminant Name: B. anthracis | Description: Bacillus anthracis is a Gram-negative, aerobic, spore-forming bacterium. [589] When environmental conditions become unfavorable, the vegetative cells form spores [590] of size 1 micron x 2 micron. [589] The spores are mammalian pathogens. [590] The spores resist heat, drying, freezing and can survive in soil for years. [590] Livestock can become infected by eating or inhaling the spores which germinate in the gut causing the disease in animals.
Anthrax is a disease in humans caused by B. anthracis spores. There are three clinical forms of anthrax: inhalation, dermal and intestinal. [590] The intestinal form results from eating infected animals [589] or by consumption of B. anthracis contaminated water. Human symptoms are nausea, fever, severe abdominal pain and bloody diarrhea. [588] It can lead to death. [589]
The concern for B. anthracis in drinking water is of a possible threat from intentional contamination of spores into water supplies.
The US Centers for Disease Control and Prevention classifies B. anthracis as a Category A agent meaning it poses a threat to the public, may spread across wide public areas, and requires significant planning to protect public health. [588]
B. anthracis is not regulated by USEPA in drinking water.
There are no occurrence data for B. anthracis in source waters to drinking water treatment plants.

Contaminant Name: B. anthracis | Function: Chlorine | Details: Inactivation of B. anthracis spores in water by chlorine can be very effective (2- to 4-log inactivation) where the effectiveness is dependent on pH, chlorine residual, CT or contact time, and temperature. pH has the most significant impact on efficacy of chlorine for B. anthracis spore inactivation. When the pH is 10 or higher and the temperature is less than ambient, the contact time or the chlorine concentration must be increased substantially to achieve a 2-log inactivation.

Contaminant Name: B. anthracis | Overall: The following processes are considered effective for the inactivation of B. anthracis: chlorine (2-log to 4-log inactivation) and UV irradiation (1-log inactivation). Performance of these technologies can vary based on dose, contact time, and water quality characteristics.
Research was identified evaluating the following treatment technologies for the removal of B. anthracis:

Contaminant Name: B. anthracis | Function: Ultraviolet Irradiation | Details: One study showed that the inactivation of B. anthracis spores in water by ultraviolet irradiation (UV) can be effective. The lethal dose to inactivate 90% (1 log) of spores is 275 J/m2. Higher log inactivation occurred at higher doses than 275 J/m2. The typical 254-nm UV-disinfection device can achieve 90% removal.

Contaminant Name: Benzene | Description: Benzene is a colorless, highly flammable, volatile organic chemical. It is used extensively as a solvent in the chemical and drug industries, as a starting material and intermediate in the production of numerous chemicals, including plastics, rubber, elastomers, phenol, acetone, and nylon, and as a gasoline additive. [687] Benzene’s trade names and synonyms are Polystream, benzol, pyrobenzol, coal naphtha, and phene. [686] More than 98% of the over 3 billion gallons of benzene produced in the United States annually is derived from the petrochemical and petroleum refining industries. [687]
Benzene is introduced to the environment primarily through industrial processes. Air emissions from burning coal and oil, motor vehicle exhaust, evaporation from gasoline service stations, and benzene storage and waste operations lead to increased levels of benzene in the atmosphere. Benzene is released into water and soil from sources such as industrial discharge, spills, gasoline leaks from underground storage tanks, and disposal of benzene and products containing benzene. [687]
Benzene's high volatility is the controlling physical property in the environmental transport of the chemical. Degradation reactions with other chemicals in the atmosphere (primarily hydroxyl radicals) limit the atmospheric residence time of benzene to only a few days. [687] Benzene is moderately soluble in water, and partitions readily to the atmosphere from surface water. The soil organic carbon sorption coefficient (Koc) for benzene has been measured with a range of 60-83, indicating that benzene is highly mobile in soil and readily leaches into groundwater. [687] In water and soil, benzene is subject to volatilization, photooxidation, and biodegradation. There is no evidence of the biomagnification of benzene in the aquatic food chain. [687]
Benzene is designated as a hazardous substance by USEPA in accordance with the Clean Water Act. In drinking water, the maximum contaminant level (MCL) set by USEPA is 5 ppb, while a maximum contaminant level goal (MCLG) is set at 0 ppb. [686, 687] USEPA has identified packed tower aeration and granular activated carbon as best available technologies capable of removing volatile organic compounds from water to their respective MCLs. [704] USEPA has determined that benzene is a known human carcinogen, classifying it as a Group A chemical. [687] Long-term exposure to benzene has been found to cause cancer, specifically leukemia, which is cancer of the blood-forming organs. Exposure is also likely to be harmful to the reproductive organs, immune system, formation of bone marrow, and can lead to anemia. [687]
Date of Literature Search: July 2009

Contaminant Name: Benzene | Function: Adsorptive Media | Details: Removal of benzene by adsorptive media can be quite effective and is dependent on the contaminant and media properties (chemical structure, porosity, surface area, etc.). One bench-scale equilibrium study showed a removal range of 86 to 96 percent for three types of commercially available macroreticular resins with varying properties. Another study investigating bench and field-scale columns containing surfactant-modified zeolite clays found benzene removal in the range of 94 to 99 percent, although it should be noted that breakthrough was quite rapid.
April 2009

Contaminant Name: Benzene | Function: Aeration and Air Stripping | Details: Aeration/air stripping is considered a best available technology by USEPA for the removal of volatile organic compounds from water. A study employing a mechanical turbine-type aeration system found removal rates of 91 to 99 percent.
A pilot study using the more traditional packed tower aeration removed benzene in the range of 65 to 77 percent, although it should be mentioned that the author purposely reduced the packed bed height in an effort to keep contaminant effluent concentrations at measurable levels for comparison to modeled data. Data from a full-scale treatment plant employing packed tower aeration achieved greater than 99 percent removal of benzene to below the contaminant’s MCL.
July 2009

Contaminant Name: Benzene | Function: Biological Treatment | Details: Removal of benzene by biological treatment was found to be quite effective. One bench-scale study employing a biomass reactor reported removal of approximately 99 percent by mixed microbial cultures obtained from combined sludge from domestic sewage, the paper industry, and poultry slaughterhouse wastewater. A pilot study performed with a fluidized bed reactor treated contaminated groundwater water with bacteria colonized as a biofilm on GAC also achieved 99 percent removal. Another bench-scale study performed with a strain of Rhodococcus isolated from a chemical spill in Sydney, Australia removed 89 to 95 percent of the initial benzene concentration.
Slightly less effective, a bench-scale study performed with cultures isolated from contaminated gas station soil in Macau, China achieved 83 percent removal of influent benzene. Finally, a benzene removal range varying between 5 and 80 percent was obtained using a mixed microbial culture from chemical wastewater in South Korea.
July 2009
Contaminant Name: Benzene | Function: GAC Isotherm | Details: GAC is Best Available Technology for benzene control.  One laboratory-water isotherm study found it moderately adsorbed.

Contaminant Name: Benzene | Function: Granular Activated Carbon | Details: GAC adsorption is considered a best available technology for the removal of benzene from drinking water. Based on data from a full scale treatment plant, greater than 99 percent of influent benzene was removed at the facility.  In two EPA Environmental Technology Verification studies, control of benzene in POU units was >99 percent. 
July 2009
Contaminant Name: Benzene | Function: Membrane Separation | Details: In four EPA Environmental Technology Verification studies, control of benzene ranged from 85 to >99 percent in POU and POE units.

Contaminant Name: Benzene | Function: Other Treatment | Details: There were several other treatment processes studied with varying effectiveness in the removal of benzene. Two studies employing novel biological reactors, one a pilot-scale membrane-based biomass concentrator reactor and the other a bench-scale porous pot reactor, each achieved greater than 99 percent removal.
Several treatment processes not typically employed in municipal water treatment using electrochemical or radiation methods varied in their effectiveness in the removal of benzene. Elimination values between 12 and greater than 99 percent of the contaminant were found.
Sonochemical oxidation, another process not typically seen in water treatment applications, generates ultrasonic waves in water creating hydrogen peroxide and cavitation bubbles from acoustic power. This method was found to be effective in removing 98 percent of the benzene present.
July 2009

Contaminant Name: Benzene | Overall: The following processes used in drinking water treatment were found to be effective for the removal of benzene: aeration and air stripping (65 to greater than 99 percent), granular activated carbon (greater than 99 percent), ozone (99 percent), ultraviolet irradiation (35 to 99 percent with medium pressure lamp without TiO2), ultraviolet irradiation hydrogen peroxide (94 to 99 percent removal) and ultraviolet irradiation ozone (99 percent). It should be noted that USEPA has identified packed tower aeration and granular activated carbon as best available technologies capable of removing volatile organic compounds from water to their respective MCLs.
Processes found to be ineffective for the removal of benzene were ultraviolet irradiation (7 percent with low pressure lamp), and ultraviolet irradiation with TiO2 (10 to 63 percent).
Other processes not typical to drinking water treatment were suggestive of benzene removal. They were: macroreticular resins and surfactant-modified zeolite clays, biological treatment, sonochemical oxidation, and electrochemical and radiation treatment.
Research was identified evaluating the following treatment technologies for the removal of benzene:

Contaminant Name: Benzene | Function: Ozone | Details: Ozone is very effective for the removal of benzene in drinking water applications. One bench-scale study demonstrated a 99 percent removal of benzene, although the ozone dose was much greater than doses typically applied during water treatment.
April 2009

Contaminant Name: Benzene | Function: Ultraviolet Irradiation | Details: Data are suggestive that ultraviolet (UV) irradiation can be effective for the removal of benzene. Based on data from one batch study, 99 percent photodegradation was observed for benzene using medium pressure lamps on a synthetic aqueous solution. For the same synthetic solution, 7 percent removal was obtained with a low pressure lamp, and 99 percent removal was obtained with the addition of 20 mg/L TiO2.
This study also found that treating actual contaminated groundwater in batch experiments, however, resulted in less efficient removal of benzene. Using a medium pressure lamp, 35 percent removal was achieved. Adding 20 mg/L and 200 mg/L of TiO2 to the solution resulted in 24 and 10 percent removal, respectively. Investigating the ability to remediate contaminated groundwater in a flow-through reactor, 73 percent removal was achieved for medium pressure UV alone, while adding 20 mg/L TiO2 decreased the removal to 63 percent. The author postulated that the composition of the groundwater, including the salt, particulate, and organic matter concentrations were likely responsible for the reduced removal rates in comparison to the synthetic water.
April 2009

Contaminant Name: Benzene | Function: Ultraviolet Irradiation and Hydrogen Peroxide | Details: Results of one study suggested that the presence of hydrogen peroxide increased the removal of benzene when irradiated with ultraviolet light in comparison to UV treatment alone as well as UV treatment with the addition of TiO2 (TiO2 data is presented under the Benzene/UV Irradiation pairing). This was the case for a synthetic aqueous solution, as well as contaminated groundwater in both batch and flow-through experiments. The addition of 130 or 2000 mg/L of hydrogen peroxide increased benzene removal to between 94 to greater than 99 percent. This is likely due to the increased formation of hydroxyl radicals which are a strong oxidizer.
April 2009

Contaminant Name: Benzene | Function: Ultraviolet Irradiation and Ozone | Details: A study examining the removal of benzene in multiple sources of groundwater contaminated with gasoline components found the application of ultraviolet irradiation in combination with ozone to be very effective. Greater than 99 percent removal was obtained, although the ozone doses were much greater than doses typically applied during water treatment.
April 2009

Contaminant Name: Carbofuran | Description: Carbofuran, a broad spectrum systemic insecticide included in the general group of the carbamate derivative pesticides, is widely used in agricultural cultivations and presents a high persistence in water due to its chemical stabiltity. It's IUPAC name is 2,3-dihydro-2,2-dimethylbenzofuran-7-yl methylcarbamate.
More than 5 million pounds of carbofuran were applied in the United States in 1995 [743]. Carbofuran is sprayed directly onto soil and plants just after emergence to control beetles, nematodes and rootworm. The greatest use of carbofuran is on alfalfa and rice, with turf and grapes making up most of the remainder. Earlier uses were primarily on corn crops [759].
Carbofuran is highly toxic by inhalation and ingestion and moderately toxic by dermal absorption. Carbofuran is poorly absorbed through the skin [758].
The maximum contaminant level (MCL) for carbofuran is 0.04 mg/L.  EPA has identified granular activated carbon as the best available technology for the removal of carbofuran from drinking water.
Carbofuran is increasingly detected in surface water and ground water [742].  The detected  carbofuran concentration in groundwater in different U.S. states is at levels between 0.2 mg/L and 0.1 g/L [743].  The half-life of carbofuran in water has been reported from 690 days at pH 5 to 1 week at pH 9 [743] [751]. 
Date of Literature Search: June 2009

Contaminant Name: Carbofuran | Function: Adsorptive Media | Details: Adsorption by modified, locally-available chestnut shells was shown to be effective for the removal of carbofuran in one study.  Removal from one ground water and one surface water was evaluated and greater than 97 percent removal was achieved.
Contaminant Name: Carbofuran | Function: Chlorine | Details: Bench-scale evaluation of the oxidation of carbofuran by chlorine showed no reduction in carbofuran concentrations in one surface water.

Contaminant Name: Carbofuran | Function: Chlorine Dioxide | Details: Oxidation of carbofuran by chlorine dioxide was shown to be ineffective in one bench-scale study evaluating the process for one surface water.

Contaminant Name: Carbofuran | Function: Conventional Treatment | Details: No carbofuran removal was observed in one bench-scale evaluation of conventional treatment (coagulation, flocculation, and sedimentation) of surface water.

Contaminant Name: Carbofuran | Function: GAC Isotherm | Details: A study evaluating the adsorption capacity of GAC in distilled water and river water found that the Freundlich K values varied significantly depending on the source water.
The greatest adsorption capacity was achieved with spiked lab water . The lowest adsorption capacity, on the other hand, was found with surface water.
GAC is Best Available Technology for carbofuran control.

Contaminant Name: Carbofuran | Function: Granular Activated Carbon | Details: Physical adsorption by granular activated carbon and by microorganisms on the GAC (biological activated carbon) is very effective for the removal of carbofuran.  Based on the results of one study, complete removal (100 percent) was achieved using new GAC, and 86 percent removal was achieved after 6-1/2 years in service.
GAC is Best Available Technology for carbofuran control.

Contaminant Name: Carbofuran | Function: Hydrogen Peroxide | Details: In presence of copper ions and L- ascorbic acid, hydrogen peroxide effectively (45 to 90 percent) oxidizes carbofuran after 10 minutes of reaction time.  However, low doses of hydrogen peroxide alone (0.0034 mg/L) are not effective for the removal of carbofuran. Based on the results of one study, only 5 to 10 percent removal was achieved after 125 to 245 hours using hydrogen peroxide in the absence of sunlight.

Contaminant Name: Carbofuran | Function: Membrane Separation | Details: Reverse osmosis (RO) was able to remove 85 to 93 percent of carbofuran in water based on the results of one bench-scale study using multiple membranes (cellulose acetate and polyamide) at pressures from 160 to 200 psi membranes in a single-pass arrangement.  In pilot-scale studies using polyamide membranes, 76 to 90 percent removal of carbofuran was achieved.
In EPA Environmental Technology Verification assessments, RO membranes controlled greater than 98 percent of carbofuran.

Contaminant Name: Carbofuran | Overall: The following processes have been demonstrated to be effective for the removal of carbofuran from drinking water: adsorption, granular activated carbon(> 99 percent removal), membrane separation using reverse osmosis (85 to 99 percent removal), ozone (71 to 95 percent), hydrogen peroxide (45 to 90 percent), ultraviolet irradiation (50 to > 99 percent), ultraviolet irradiation with hydrogen peroxide (> 99 percent removal), and ultraviolet irradiation with ozone (up to 98 percent removal). EPA has designated granular activated carbon as the Best Available Technology for carbofuran removal. Performance of these technologies can vary based on water quality and treatment characteristics.
Conventional treatment, chlorine, and chlorine dioxide were demonstrated to provide no significant removal of carbofuran.
Research was identified evaluating the following treatment technologies for the removal of carbofuran:

Contaminant Name: Carbofuran | Function: Ozone | Details: Ozonation can effectively oxidize carbofuran based on the results of one bench-scale study.  Carbofuran removals of 71 to 95 percent were achieved and it was observed that the removal effectiveness can be increased by increasing the reaction temperature over the range of 10 to 40 degrees celsius. It should be noted that the pH for this experiment was 2, which is atypical for drinking water treatment.

Contaminant Name: Carbofuran | Function: Ultraviolet Irradiation | Details: Ultaviolet irradiation at a wavelength of 2O8-2 ions, and photosynthesizers such as metheylene blue and rose bengal. Solar irradiation also results in carbofuran degradation in the presence of catalysts such as titanium dioxide.
The degradation of carbofuran by UV irradiation is influenced by pH, temperature, and the carbofuran concentration. With no catalyst present, UV irradiation is not influenced by pH.  In the presence of a catalyst, however, increases in pH can result in increased degradation of carbofuran.  Similarly, higher water temperatures generally resulted in increases carbofuran degradation. 
One study showed that the degradation of carbofuran using UV irradiation was faster in natural waters (seawater and pond water) than in distilled water, demonstrating that increased presence of dissolved salts and other compounds increase degradation rates of carbofuran.

Contaminant Name: Carbofuran | Function: Ultraviolet Irradiation and Hydrogen Peroxide | Details: Ultraviolet irradition with hydrogen peroxide can achieve complete degradation of carbofuran. Temperature and the initial carbofuran concentration were demonstrated to influence degradation by UV irradiation with hydrogen peroxide.  Increases in temperature and/or the carbofuran concentration were both demonstrated to result in increased degradation.  Ferric ion can also be used as a catalyst to accelerate the oxidation reaction (Fenton's reaction).

Contaminant Name: Carbofuran | Function: Ultraviolet Irradiation and Ozone | Details: Ultraviolet irradition with ozone can achieve 98 percent removal of carbofuran in 30 minutes based on the results of one bench-scale study.
It should be noted that Ozone dose of > 40mg/l is atypical for drinking water treatment.

Contaminant Name: Chlortetracycline | Description: Chlortetracycline, a member of tetracycline family, is a broad-spectrum antibiotic that is commonly given to poultry, swine, and livestock. Chlortetracycline may lead to the development of antibiotic resistance [1327]; other potential adverse effects are unknown.
Chlortetracycline can be used to prevent, control, and treat animal health problems as well as enhance growth rates in chickens, turkeys, ducks, swine, calves, beef cattle, and other animals [1326]. It has been in clinical use in 1948. [1348]
Chlortetracycline enters the environment primarily through the application of animal manure to fields. [1529] Chlortetracycline appears to be retained in the top soil layers after land application. [1350] The compound may also dissolve or associate with particles in surface runoff. The solubility of chlortetracycline is approximately 500 mg/L; values of soil/water partitioning depend on the type of soil and on the type of antibiotic formulation. [1326]
Chlortetracycline has been shown to be relatively persistent in soil. [1529] Primary attenuation mechanisms include hydrolysis, biodegradation and photolysis in sunlight-exposed soils. [1529, 1325] One study of chlortetracycline degradation from chicken feces over a 30-day period showed temperature dependence, with no significant degradation at 4 °C, 12% of the original concentration (5.6 mg/kg) degradation at 20 °C and 56% degradation at 30 °C. [1349]
The stability of chlortetracycline in water depends on pH, temperature, light, and other parameters. [1530] In a study of soil interstitial water and milliQ water, light appeared to generally accelerate chlortetracycline degradation, as did increasing the pH (from 3.0 to 9.0) and the temperature (from 6 °C to 20 °C). [1530] Half-lives of chlortetracycline at 20 °C and pH 8.5 were less than 10 days. [1530]
Chlortetracycline is not currently regulated in drinking water by U.S. Environmental Protection Agency. [1351] Toxicity information for chlortetracycline is limited, and whether it is carcinogenic or endocrine disruptive is unknown. [980]
Chlortetracycline is often analyzed by liquid chromatography (LC) combined with mass spectrometry (MS), such as LC/MS, LC-MS/MS, LC/ESI (electospray ionisation)-MS. [1527,1327, 1530, 1352]
Chlortetracycline is not often detected in water sources, perhaps due to its absorption to soil or bounding with other chemicals. [1354] Several studies focusing on the occurrence of antibiotics did not detect chlortetracycline in treated wastewater, surface water, and groundwater in the U.S., Canada, and Germany. [1352, 1353 and 1354]  However, one study detected chlortetracycline in 2.4% of 84 samples from streams in the U.S. using a LC/MS-ESI method developed by the U.S. Geological Survey (the method report limit was 0.10 µg/L). [1355] However, chlortetracycline was not detected using another LC/MS-ESI method in the same study (the method report limit was 0.05 µg/L). [1355] One study showed chlortetracycline detections in top soil (0 to 30 cm below ground surface) ranging from 4.6 – 7.3 µg/kg with no detections in soil layers below 30 cm. [1350]
Date of Literature Search: August 2009

Contaminant Name: Chlortetracycline | Function: Adsorptive Media | Details: One study showed that chlortetracycline strongly adsorbed to soils, with a preference for clays, followed by humic substances and clay-humic complexes [1523]. Humic substances in clay-humic complexes appear to reduce sorption by masking sorption sites or by inhibiting interlayer diffusion [1523]. Sorption was greater to calcium-saturated soil than potassium-saturated soil [1523]. Sorption decreased with increasing pH, suggesting that cation bridging and cation exchange contribute to sorption [1523].

Contaminant Name: Chlortetracycline | Function: Conventional Treatment | Details: One bench-scale study investigated removal efficiency of coagulation process for chlortetracycline [1526]. Two waters were tested, i.e. distilled water added with kaolin to achieve a turbidity level of 10 NTU and raw river water. Poly-aluminum chloride (PACl) was added as coagulant at dosages of 5 to 60 mg/L. The removal efficiency ranged from 21% to 43% for the distilled water and 38% to 55% for the raw river water.

Contaminant Name: Chlortetracycline | Function: Granular Activated Carbon | Details: One study reported effective removal of chlortetracycline by granular activated carbon (GAC) [1526]. Two GACs were tested, a coal-based carbon (Calgon F-400, U.S.A.) and a coconut-based carbon (Samchully, Korea). Chlortetracycline removal was 85 percent for the coal-based carbon and 80 percent for the coconut-based carbon with a contact time of 10 minutes.

Contaminant Name: Chlortetracycline | Function: Ion Exchange | Details: One study reported that chlortetracycline was effectively removed by an ion-exchange resin, MIEX, in a batch test [1527]. More than 90 percent of chlortetracycline was removed from de-ionized water using a resin dose ranging from 0.5 to 5.0 mL/L. A range of 65 to 95 percent removal was achieved from treated river water containing 0.8 mg/L DOC at the same resin dose range. Resin dose did not affect removal efficiency.

Contaminant Name: Chlortetracycline | Function: Other Treatment | Details: One study reported that chlortetracycline was removed using electrochemical oxidation [1528]. Three electrolytes were tested: sodium sulfate, sodium nitrate and sodium chloride. Chloride was the best electrolyte for chlortetracycline removal, and the study suggested this was due to indirect oxidation effect of chlorine/hypochlorite. Chlortetracycline removal efficiency increased with increasing electrolysis time. The most removal was observed after 180 minutes of electrolysis using sodium chloride.

Contaminant Name: Chlortetracycline | Overall: The following processes were found to be effective for the removal of chlortetracycline from drinking water: adsorptive media (equal to or greater than 94 percent removal), granular activated carbon (equal to or greater than 80 percent removal), ion exchange (equal to or greater than 65 percent depending on water quality) and electrochemical oxidation (92 percent for the study reviewed).  On the other hand, coagulation using poly-aluminum chloride was reported to be not effective for chlortetracycline removal (below 55 percent). 1526
Research has identified evaluation of the following treatment technologies for the removal of chlortetracycline:

Contaminant Name: Chromium | Description: Chromium and its compounds are used in metal alloys such as stainless steel, protective coatings on metal, magnetic tapes, and pigments for paints, cement paper, rubber, composition floor covering and other materials. Other users includes: chemical intermediate for wood preservatives, organic chemical synthesis, photochemical processing and industrial water treatment.  In medicine, chromium compounds are used in astringents and antiseptics.  They also are used in cooling waters, and in the leather tanning industry, in catalytic manufacture, and fungicides, as an algaecide against slime forming bacteria and yeasts in brewery processing water and brewery warmer water. [1381,1398,1414]
Chromium is not likely to migrate to groundwater.  A field trial on the application of wastewater treatment slude to soil found movement of heavy metals, including chromium, from the soil surface to a depth of 10 cm, but most of the metals (mean 87%) remained in the upper 5 cm of soil.  Uptake by plants is generally low, it was found to be greater from ultrabasic soils by a factor of 5-40 than on calcareous or silica-based soils.[1419]
Chromium compounds are very persistant in water.  Most of the chromium in surface waters may be present in particulate form as sediments.  Some of the particulate chromium would remain as suspended matter and ultimately be deposited in sediments. The exact chemical form of chromium in surface waters are not well defined.  Although most of the soluble chromium in surface waters may be present as Cr (VI), a small amount may be present as Cr (III) organic complexes.[1419]
EPA has found chromium to potentially cause the skin irritation or ulceration related health effects from acute exposures at levels above the MCL. Chromium has the potential to damage liver, kidney circulatory and nerve tissues, and dermatitis  from long-term exposures at levels above the MCL.  In late 2010 EPA noted recent risk assessment of chromium-6 as a likely human carcinogen by ingestion and that it was considering tightening the chromium drinking water standard in 2011. 
EPA has set an MCL of 100 ug/L for total chromium.  Background levels in water average 1 ug/L while municipal drinking water contain 0.1 - 35 ug/L.  The higher values of chromium can be related to source of anthropogenic pollution.  A survey of 3834 tap waters reported the concentrations of chromium to range from 0.4 - 8 ug/L.  The reported chromium concentrations in this study may be a little higher than the actual values due to inadequate flushing of tap water before collection of samples.  This indicates that the concentrations of chromium in household tap water have potential to increase due to house plumbing materials.[1419]
Total chromium is regulated in drinking water in the US with a MCL of 0.10 mg/L or 100 ug/L based on the total concentration of the trivalent and hexavalent forms of dissolved chromium.  USEPA has identified several teatment processes as Best Available Technologies (BATs) for control of chromium in drinkign water.  They are: Coagulation/filtration, ion exchange, reverse osmosis, and lime softening.[1419]
Date of Literature Search: March 2010

Contaminant Name: Chromium | Function: Adsorptive Media | Details: Chromium (VI) and chromium (III) removal by adsorption can be effective (up to 100 percent removal) and is strongly dependent on the adsorbent dose, influent pH, and initial concentration. Chromium (VI) adsorption is favorable in the acidic pH range for carbon and iron based sorbents. Iron based resins, carbon nanotubes, limestone, river bed sand are known to remove chromium (VI) under appropriate treatment conditions in lab scale experiments. Presence of organic compounds such as TCE can impede chromium (VI) removal by up to 40 percent.  One study found zeolite adsoprtive media effective in removig chromium (VI) from water at 30 minute empty bed contact time.

Contaminant Name: Chromium | Function: Conventional Treatment | Details: USEPA has identified coagulation/filtration as Best Avaialble Technology (BAT) for chromium control in drinking water. Removal of chromium (VI) in water by conventional processes can be very effective under appropriate treatment conditions.   One paper studied the chromium removal by reduction using ferrous sulfate, coagulation assisted by aeration, followed by medial filtration.  Removal efficiencies up to 100 percent can be achieved under optimized treatment conditions.  Reduction pH and aeration/filtration pH was found to be influential factor for chromium removal.  Mass ratio of Fe:Cr and loading rate had minimal impact on chromium (VI) removal.

Contaminant Name: Chromium | Function: GAC Isotherm | Details: GAC can remove chromium (III) and chromium (VI). Numerous studies have reported GAC isotherms for various carbon materials. Most of the isotherm experiments were performed with lab water. Chromium (VI) adsorption capacity was higher in the acidic pH range and chromium (III) adsorption capacity was higher in close to neutral pH range.

Contaminant Name: Chromium | Function: Granular Activated Carbon | Details: Chromium (VI) removal by granular activated carbon can be very effective (up to 100 percent) under acidic pH range (2 to 6). However, average GAC service life ranged between 0.14 to 0.39 days. One study reported the optimum pH for chromium (VI) removal as 2 for the wood-based activated carbons and between 3 to 4 for coconut based carbons. In addition to solution pH, chromium (VI) removal is also influenced by contact time, initial chromium (VI) concentration, and carbon dosages.

Contaminant Name: Chromium | Function: Ion Exchange | Details: Ion exchange is a one of the Best Available Technologies for chromium (III) and chromium (VI) removal.  Ion exchange can effectively remove chromium (III) (68 to 100 percent) and chromium (VI) (95 to 100 percent) under favorable water quality conditions. The cationic resins have shown promising results for chromium (III) removal whereas anionic resins work best for chromium (VI) removal (based on the literature cited here).  Removal efficiency can vary based upon the choice of resin, regeneration frequency, competing ions, and their concentrations, initial chromium (III) and or chromium (VI) concentrations, influent pHs and contact time. The popular resins in literature used for chromium (III) and chromium (VI) removal includes zeolite, zeolite modified with iron, cellulose based ion exchange resins, strong base anion exchange rasins and weak base anion exchange resins.

Contaminant Name: Chromium | Function: Membrane Filtration | Details: Chromium (III) ions are too small to be retained by the filter.  Membrane filtration requires chromium (III) to be adsorbed or precipitated with a filterable particulate. Two examples may include a water-soluble macroligand (polyethlene-imine) and r-Al2O3 nanoparticles. Research has also shown that the iron hydroxide (RCF) process can also effectively remove chromium (III) from water. Studies indicated that membrane filtration can be used for chromium (III) removal (up to 100 percent). The high percent removals were due to the filtration of chromium (III) adsorbed on nano-particles or macroligands. Removal of chromium (III) ions using membrane filtration is achieved by employing a combined adsorption-ultrafiltration parallel process at pH range of 2 to 7.

Contaminant Name: Chromium | Function: Membrane Separation | Details: USEPA has identified reverse osmosis as Best Available Technology (BAT) for chromium control in drinking water, however only data on electrodialysis (ED) was found.  Only one study reported chromium (VI) removal by membrane separation (15 to 99.6 percent) using ED. Chromium (VI) removal is a function of applied potential difference, contact time, and initial solution pH.

Contaminant Name: Chromium | Function: Other Treatment | Details: One paper demonstrated that chromium (VI) can be co-precipitated (up to 75 percent) in presence of other heavy metals such as copper (II), nickel (II), and zinc (II). However, chromium (VI) removal in the presence of only nickel or zinc is negligible (5 to 8 percent). Another study found the use of a reducing agent, such as stannous chloride, sulfide, and sulfite can reduce chromium (VI) by up to 80% at pH 5.

Contaminant Name: Chromium | Overall: Chromium is commonly found in form of chromium (III) and chromium (VI) in most municipal and industrial manufacturing waste waters and can effectively be removed under the acidic pH range from 2 to 6.  The following processes are effective for the removal of chromium (III) and chromium (VI) are: adsorption, granular activated carbon, ion exchange, and adsorption followed by filtration.  Coagulation/filtration, ion exchange, reverse osmosis, and lime softening are Best Avaialble Technologies (BATs) for chromium control in drinking water.
In ion-exchange and adsorption by granular activated carbon can effectively removal (up to 100 percent) chromium (III) and chromium (VI) from waters below pH 7.  The removal efficiencies vary based on pH, contact time, type of resin/adsorbent, and dosages.  In case of membrane filtration, chromium (VI) and chromium (III) adsorption onto filterable particulates such as nano particles or micro ligands is required in order to achieve higher removal efficiencies. Chromium (III) and chromium (VI) can be co-precipitated (78 to 80 percent) with other heavy metals such as zinc and nickel present in the electroplating or mining waste waters.
All the above processes can achieve chromium (III) and chromium (VI) removal efficiencies in excess of 75 percent under favorable conditions.  The performance of all the technologies discussed here greatly influenced by the water quality and treatment characteristics. 
Research has identified following treatment technologies for chromium (III) and chromium (VI) removal:

Contaminant Name: Chromium | Function: Precipitative Softening | Details: USEPA has identified precipitative softening as Best Available Technology (BAT) for chromium control in drinking water.  Based on one desktop study (that looked at 273 groundwater and surface water samples), precipitative softening is up to 98.8 percent effective for the removal of chromium (VI) for the conditions evaluated.

Contaminant Name: Cis-1,2-dichloroethylene | Description: Cis-1,2-dichloroethylene is one of two forms of 1,2-dichloroethylene (also called 1,2-dichloroethene). The other form is trans-1,2-dichloroethylene. Cis-1,2-dichloroethylene is a highly flammable, colorless liquid with a sharp, harsh odor. The most frequent uses of 1,2-Dichloroethene are solvent production and chemical mixtures [2300]. U.S. production estimates are available for 1994 (between 10,000 pounds and 0.5 million pounds) [2312].
1,2-Dichloroethene enters the environment through industrial activity, including releases from manufacturers who make or use 1,2-dichloroethylene, landfills, hazardous waste sites, chemical spills, burning of objects made of vinyl, and breakdown of other chlorinated chemicals [2300]. This chemical has been found in air, water, and soil.
Regardless of where a surface release of cis-1,2-dichloroethene occurs, rapid evaporation is likely to occur. In the air, it has a half-life of 5 to 12 days because reactions with radicals degrade cis-1,2-dichloroethylene. Subsurface releases of 1,2-dichloroethene (e.g., in landfills or hazardous waste sites) can result in groundwater contamination if water dissolves 1,2-dichloroethylene and transports it to an aquifer. In groundwater, the half-life is 13 to 48 weeks. For underground releases, it is possible for the 1,2-dichloroethene to eventually break down into vinyl chloride.
For cis-1,2-dichloroethylene, the maximum contaminant level (MCL) set by USEPA is 0.07 ppm, which equals the maximum contaminant level goal (MCLG). USEPA has identified packed tower aeration and granular activated carbon as best available technologies capable of removing volatile organic compounds from water to their respective MCLs. Additional small system compliance technologies are diffused aeration, multi-stage bubble aeration, tray aeration, and shallow tray aeration [2302]. Long-term exposure to cis-1,2-dichlrorethylene has been linked to increased kidney weight in animals [2314].

Contaminant Name: Cis-1,2-dichloroethylene | Function: Aeration and Air Stripping | Details: According to the World Health Organization (WHO), the achievable air stripping removal efficiency for 1,2-Dichloroethylene (both cis- and trans- isomers) is more than 80 percent, to concentrations lower than 10 µg/L (WHO, 2011). A full-scale study of packed tower aeration (PTA) by Hand, et al. (1986) reported 96.9 percent removal of cis-1,2-Dichloroethylene, from an influent concentration of 82.3 µg/L (greater than the current MCL) to a treated concentration of 2.6 µg/L (96.8 percent removal) [2303]. A bench-scale study of PTA starting from a much higher concentration (9,270 µg/L) found greater than 99 percent removal [2687]. An EPA pilot study of diffuse aeration found greater than 80 percent removal and calculated cost of treatment for various scenarios [2688]. Henry’s Law constants reported in several sources are high, ranging from 0.0934 to 0.307 atm m3/m3, indicating good treatment feasibility (Hand et al., 1986; Staudinger and Roberts, 2001; U.S. EPA, 1995). EPA has categorized the strippability of cis-1,2-Dichloroethylene as good (56 FR 3526, January 30, 1991).

Contaminant Name: Cis-1,2-dichloroethylene | Function: Granular Activated Carbon | Details: According to WHO, the achievable activated carbon removal efficiency for 1,2-Dichloroethylene (both cis- and trans- isomers) is more than 80 percent, to concentrations lower than 10 µg/L [2301]. Removal efficiencies demonstrated in the literature range from greater than 78 to greater than 99 percent [2311, 2305, 2307, 2309]. All of the studies demonstrating removal efficiencies greater than 99 percent started from concentrations greater than the current MCL, and achieved concentrations of 1 µg/L or less. These studies include a pilot and full-scale drinking water treatment [2307], a full-scale remediation site [2305], and accelerated column tests [2309].
Starting from concentrations just above the current MCL, Hand et al. [2307] reported carbon bed life from 4,500 to 7,800 bed volumes before detection of cis-1,2-Dichloroethylene using a detection limit of 0.3 µg/L. Expected bed life would be even longer to the threshold considered here. Carbon usage rates for cis-1,2-Dichloroethylene (0.174 to 0.5 lbs/1,000 gal) are higher than for other organic contaminants [2311, 56 FR 3526, January 30, 1991, 2309], suggesting higher treatment costs. Although EPA found that GAC may be more than twice as expensive as PTA for large systems on a dollar per household per year basis, if off-gas control of PTA is necessary, then costs between PTA and GAC are more competitive (56 FR 3526, January 30, 1991).

Contaminant Name: Cis-1,2-dichloroethylene | Overall: Drinking water treatment technologies that are effective for removing cis-1,2-dichloroethylene include: aeration or air stripping and granular activated carbon (both processes up to greater than 99 percent removal). Current best available technologies for cis-1,2-Dichloroethylene treatment are packed tower aeration and granular activated carbon. Additional small system compliance technologies are diffused aeration, multi-stage bubble aeration, tray aeration, and shallow tray aeration 2302.
