Contaminant Name: Cobalt | Description: Some drinking water sources have very low levels of radionuclides (radioactive elements).  While stable cobalt isotopes exist in nature, cobalt has two radionuclides that can be detected in drinking water. Cobalt-58 and cobalt-60 are both anthropogenic nuclides that mey be present from the cooling water used in nuclear power facilities. The primary anthropogenic source of cobalt-60 originates from nuclear power plants when neutrons from the nuclear reaction process bombard natural cobalt (cobalt-59) present in the metal making up the reactor itself.  Both nuclides are beta particle emitters and are regulated under the USEPA's Radionuclides Rule due to its radiotoxicity.  The half lives of cobalt-58 and cobalt-60 are 71 days and 5.27 years, respectively.  [1762]  Because of its prevalence and activity, cobalt-60 is a larger concern for water utilities.  Cobalt-57 is also radioactive and used in medical and scientific research.  Its half life is 272 days. 
Emitted particles ionize or destabilize atoms as they pass through the body's cells damaging chromosomes, which can lead to cancer. Radioactive cobalt exposure has been shown to cause a reduction in white cell counts and cause reproductive harm and even temporary sterility. [1762]
Radioactive cobalt and other beta particle emitters are regulated by USEPA in drinking water with a combined maximum contaminant level of 4 mrem per year [584] or 8 picoCuries/L. 
USEPA has identified ion exchange and reverse osmosis as Best Available Technologies (BATs) for the control of beta particles in drinking water. [566] 
In the National Inorganics and Radionuclides Survey (NIRS) of ground waters, gross beta-particle activity detects exceeded the survey screening level of 50 pCi/L in 9 of 990 public water supplies. [586]  Because radioactive cobalt is able to travel with percolating waters to underlying layers of soil and into groundwater, it is found in both surface and ground waters. [1762]
Naturally-occurring cobalt (cobalt-59) is present in most rocks, soil and plants.  It can be found in water in dissolved ionic form.  Cobalt typically combines with other elements such as oxygen, sulfur, and arsenic in nature and is critical in the formation of vitamin B-12.  Cobalt chloride has been used in pharmaceuticals.  
Non-radioactive cobalt has some health effects (possible fetal development, possible human carcinogen).  It will be monitored with unregulated contaminants as a part of the UCMR3 and is on the CCL3 for consideration for regulation in drinking water.      
Date of Literature Search: August 2010

Contaminant Name: Cobalt | Function: Adsorptive Media | Details: As indicated from batch isotherm tests, adsorption can be very effective (up to greater than 98 percent removal) with bentonite clay or almond materials for cobalt(II) removal. Data for full-scale water treatment or for radioactive cobalt removal were not available; full-scale implementation of this technology may not be feasible. Cobalt removal by adsorptive media is largely dependent on the initial concentration, oxidation state, adsorbent type, and water chemistry (pH and competing anions). Best removals were achieved at pH 6. As pH increased, precipitation played a larger role in cobalt removal.

Contaminant Name: Cobalt | Function: Granular Activated Carbon | Details: Based on limited bench-scale and isotherm tests, granular activated carbon was found to be effective for cobalt removal (up to 99 percent, but at pHs below typical drinking water treatment and at 2 hour EBCTs). The studies did not provide sufficient data to indicate whether granular activated carbon would be feasible on a full-scale level.  Based on study findings, cobalt removal by granular activated carbon is dependent on contaminant concentration, empty bed contact time, and media type.

Contaminant Name: Cobalt | Function: Ion Exchange | Details: Based on one article involving bench-scale isotherm tests, removal of cobalt(II) from water by ion exchange can be very effective (greater than 99 percent removal). Sufficient data were not available to make any generalizations on the factors that affect cobalt removal via ion exchange.

Contaminant Name: Cobalt | Function: Membrane Filtration | Details: Based on one study, removal of cobalt(II) from water by ultrafiltation can be very effective when combined with a sulfonated polymer (up to 98 percent removal). The study suggests that the use of no polymers or polyacrylic polymers are not effective at cobalt removal. As pH increased from 3 to 6, cobalt removal also increased.

Contaminant Name: Cobalt | Overall: The following processes are considered effective for the removal of cobalt: The level of control for ion exchange is consistent with its designation as a Best Available Technology for beta particle removal.  Data for cobalt removal via reverse osmosis (another Best Available Technology) was not available.  However, significant manufacturer's information claim effectiveness of RO for cobalt control. 
Adsorptive media (up to 98 percent removal) and ion exchange (greater than 99 percent removal) were also effective.  Performance of these technologies can vary based on water quality and treatment characteristics. Granular activated carbon (up to 99 percent removal) was able to remove cobalt in limited bench-scale experiments however, sufficient data were not available to assess whether this technology is feasible at a full-scale level. When used with a sulfonated polymer, bench-scale ultrafiltration tests (up to 99 percent removal) were also effective for the removal of cobalt.

Contaminant Name: Cryptosporidium | Description: Cryptosporidium occurs in many forms. Some species of the contaminant include C.bailey, C.hominis, C.meleagridis, C. muris, C.parvum and C.serpentis. Cryptosporidium oocysts contaminate most drinking water sources with the potential to cause disease outbreaks. It is a concern because the oocysts are resistant to the common chlorine and chloramine water disinfectants. [600]
Cryptosporidium oocysts are ubiquitous in source waters in the US. Principally related to livestock, C. parvum oocysts from deposited fecal matter can be picked up by run off over soil surfaces and transported to surface waters. The oocysts can also be transported though soil to ground water. They can persist in waters for extended periods of time. They can survive freezing better than survive 25 C. They have low specific gravity (about 1.05 g/cc) and are buoyant so that oocysts not bound to particles tend to float to the top of a water column. However, they are likely bound to organics and particulate surfaces and suspended in surface waters. [599]
Although other species may infrequently infect humans, most all Cryptosporidium infections in humans are caused by C. parvum. Cryptosporidiosis required only low infective dose in humans. C. parvum oocysts are pathogens completing their life cycle in the digestive tract of the host. Four sporozoites are released from each oocyst and parasitize cells of digestive surfaces. Resulting symptoms are self limiting in healthy adults with diarrhea, dehydration, cramps and fever lasting less than two weeks. It is more severe in people with weakened immune systems and sometimes fatal in people with compromised immune systems. [599]
Cryptosporidium oocysts were regulated under the Long Term 1 Enhanced Surface Water Treatment Rule (LT1ESWTR or LT1) to require microbial inactivation to ensure that microbial protection was not jeopardized when systems modified treatment to comply with the Stage 1 Disinfectants and Disinfection Byproduct Rule. [598] It required a 2-log removal of Cryptosporidium [598] in filtered water systems.
The Long Term 2 Enhanced Surface Water Treatment Rule (LT2ESWTR or LT2) required additional treatment of oocysts for higher risk systems. [600] Higher risk systems include unfiltered systems and filtered systems with higher levels of Cryptosporidium in their source waters. Systems must monitor their source waters to determine their treatment requirements. Filtered systems are classified into one of four treatment categories or bins. With relatively low occurrence, no additional treatment is required. With relatively higher Cryptosporidium levels from monitoring, systems must further reduce Cryptosporidium levels by 1 to 3 logs depending on the bin and the type of filtration. Impacted systems can select treatment options from a microbial tool box depending on their bin. Unfiltered systems must achieve 2 or 3 log inactivation depending on the results of their occurrence monitoring. Uncovered finished water reservoirs must be covered or have the effluent treated to inactivate 2 logs.
Measurement of Cryptosporidium oocyst infectivity is complicated by methodology. Methods include in vitro excystation, infection of cell lines, morphological examination by microscopy, uptake of dyes, and animal infectivity; only the latter assures the oocyst is alive and infective in the host. [599]
In the Information Collection Rule (ICR) the Cryptosporidium detection method had relatively low recovery and used relatively small sample volumes. Results from the ICR survey indicated: (1) 43 percent Cryptosporidium detections (with poorer methodology) in sources of filtered plants, (2) Cryptosporidium levels higher in flowing streams than in lakes and reservoirs, and (3) as expected because unfiltered systems must meet higher quality source water standards to avoid filtration, filtered systems had higher source levels (mean 0.068 oocysts/L) than unfiltered (mean 0.002 oocysts/L). [599]
In the ICR Supplemental Survey (ICRSS), which surveyed only filtered plants, the method had relatively high recovery and used relatively larger sample volumes. Results from the ICRSS indicated: 85 percent Cryptosporidium detections (with better methodology) in sources of filtered plants, with mean 0.006 oocysts/L level. [599]
Date of literature search: April 2007

Contaminant Name: Cryptosporidium | Function: Chemical Treatment | Details: Inactivation of Cryptosporidium in water by iodine, ammonia, and bromine can be marginally effective (nearly 1-log inactivation) where the effectiveness is dependent on dose. However, the doses required for inactivation are significantly higher than what is typically used in water treatment.
Inactivation of Cryptosporidium in water by mixed oxidants (MIOX-electrodialysis of a brine solution) was found to be very effective for one study (from 1.3 to >4.6-log inactivation at a dosage of 5 mg/L and contact times of 60 and 240 minutes, respectively). However, another study showed no inactivation for a dosage of 5 mg/L and a contact time of 90 minutes.
Inactivation of Cryptosporidium in water by iodine bromide, sulfur dioxide, chlorate, sodium thiosulfate, and sulfur dioxide is not effective. Simultaneous disinfection with chlorite and sodium thiosulfate and chlorate and sodium thiosulfate was also found to be ineffective for Cryptosporidium inactivation.
Contaminant Name: Cryptosporidium | Function: Chloramine | Details: Inactivation of Cryptosporidium in water by monochloramine is not effective (

Contaminant Name: Cryptosporidium | Function: Chlorine | Details: Inactivation of Cryptosporidium in water by chlorine is generally not effective (Cryptosporidium may be enhanced when it is applied as a secondary disinfectant following ozone or chlorine dioxide primary disinfection. Cryptosporidium inactivation with chlorine is highly dependent on temperature, pH, chlorine dose, and contact time.

Contaminant Name: Cryptosporidium | Function: Chlorine Dioxide | Details: Inactivation of Cryptosporidium in water by chlorine dioxide can be very effective (1- to over 3-log inactivation) where the effectiveness is dependent on temperature, chlorine dioxide dose, contact time, and water quality. Chlorine dioxide is in the LT2 microbial tool box for control of Cryptosporidium. Temperature has the most significant impact on efficacy of chlorine dioxide for Cryptosporidium inactivation. In addition, chlorine dioxide is less effective is highly turbid natural waters with high (>4 mg/L) TOC (Total Organic Carbon) levels. Chlorine dioxide has been shown to display synergism if applied with ozone, chlorine, or monochloramine in disinfection schemes.

Contaminant Name: Cryptosporidium | Function: Conventional Treatment | Details: Removal of Cryptosporidum in water by conventional treatment (coagulation, clarification and filtration) is effective, with removals as high as 6 logs reported. LT1 cites effective filtration, with sufficiently low turbidities, as means of Cryptosporidium removal. It is highly dependent on source water quality, chemical pretreatment as well as filtration rate, bed depth and media types. The coagulant dose is an important parameter in removal. Turbidity and natural organic matter removal have been shown to improve pathogen removal.

Contaminant Name: Cryptosporidium | Function: Diatomaceous Earth Filtration | Details: Removal of Cryptosporidium in water by diatomaceous earth (DE) filtration can be very effective (> 6-log reduction) where the effectiveness is dependent on DE grade permeability and filtration rate. Higher removal rates were observed at higher filtration rates. The removal of Cryptosporidium has been proven to be the most effective due to consistent and proper chemical coagulation and flocculation. A 1997 study found that DE filtration was much more effective at Cryptosporidium removal than conventional and direct granular media filtration. DE filtration is cited in LT1 for its control of Cryptosporidium, assuming proper concurrent turbidity control.

Contaminant Name: Cryptosporidium | Function: Direct Filtration | Details: Removal of Cryptosporidium in water by direct filtration can be very effective (up to 3-log reduction) where the effectiveness is dependent on turbidity removal. Removal of Cryptosporidium through direct filtration has been shown to be comparable to conventional treatment and has been proven to be the most effective due to consistent and proper chemical coagulation and flocculation. Direct filtration is cited in LT1 as an effective means, based on effective turbidity control, of Cryptosporidium removal.

Contaminant Name: Cryptosporidium | Function: Granular Activated Carbon | Details: Removal of Cryptosporidium in water by granular activated carbon was found to be very effective for one pilot study (100% removal) where  GAC filtration followed conventional treatment.  However, removal of Cryptosporidium in water by GAC following post ozonation and conventional treatment in this same pilot study was not effective (0% removal).

Contaminant Name: Cryptosporidium | Function: Hydrogen Peroxide | Details: Inactivation of Cryptosporidum in water by hydrogen peroxide can be effective at high hydrogen peroxide doses. A 1-log inactivation (or reduction in excystment) was seen with a dose of approximately 30 mg/L and almost a 2-log inactivation was seen with doses near 330 mg/L. These doses are most likely not realistic for water treatment plants. Hydrogen peroxide is not in the LT2 microbial tool box for control of Cryptosporidum. No data were available for the effect of temperature, pH or other water quality on the effectiveness of hydrogen peroxide.

Contaminant Name: Cryptosporidium | Function: Membrane Filtration | Details: Removal of Cryptosporidium in water by membrane filtration (microfiltration and ultrafiltration) can be effective (exceeding 6-log removal by both) provided adequate maintenance of the membrane system. The extent of removal is related to the extent of solids on the membrane surface as well as size exclusion. Size exclusion has been found to be the predominant removal mechanism for Cryptosporidium in both microfiltration and ultrafiltration. Membrane filtration is in the LT2 microbial tool box for control of Cryptosporidium, provided the membrane's integrity is maintained.

Contaminant Name: Cryptosporidium | Function: Membrane Separation | Details: Removal of Cryptosporidium in water by nanofiltration with a polyamide composite thin film (CTF) was found to be very effective for one study (>6-log removal or about three orders of magnitude greater removal than achieved with conventional treatment). In the same study, nanofiltration with a membrane that had a cellulose acetate (CA) thin film was significantly less effective in removing Cryptosporidium (2 to 3-log removal).

Contaminant Name: Cryptosporidium | Function: Other Treatment | Details: Inactivation of Cryptosporidium oocysts by shaking with sand was found to be very effective for one study (>99% of oocysts were killed after shaking with sand for 90 minutes). The study also found increased inactivation of Cryptosporidium with chlorine concentrations typically used in water treatment after shaking oocysts with sand for five minutes.
Removal of Cryptosporidium in water by a Fribrotex AX50 pilot plant was found to be effective for one study (98 to 99.1% oocyst removal) when treating a groundwater source under the influence of surface water.

Contaminant Name: Cryptosporidium | Overall: The following processes are effective for the removal or inactivation of Cryptosporidium: Filtration is a part of conventional treatment achieved removals in the 2-log to 6-log range. LT1 cites conventional filtration as effective. LT1 also cites direct filtration, diatomaceous earth filtration and slow sand filtration (SSF) as effective. Reported removals for these three processes were up to 3 logs, greater than 6 logs and greater than 2 logs, respectively. SSF is also in the LT2 microbial tool box for Cryptosporidium control. 
 In the LT2 microbial tool box, two-stage precipitative softening and membrane filtration were cited as effective. Reported removals for two-stage precipitative softening were in the 3.5- to 4-log range. Reported removals for micro-, ultra- and nanofiltration exceeded 6-log removal. 
 In the LT2 microbial tool box, chlorine dioxide, ozone and UV irradiation are cited as effective for inactivation of Cryptosporidium. Reported log inactivations were greater than 3, greater than 3 and greater than 4 logs, respectively. Chlorine and chloramines do not provide sufficient inactivation of Cryptosporidium oocysts when used alone at dosages and contact times typically used in drinking water treatment. These disinfection processes must be coupled with either chlorine dioxide, UV, or ozone. A degree of synergism is common when these sequential disinfectants are employed, with log inactivations exceeding 4 logs. The combination of ozone and hydrogen peroxide was not significantly better than ozone alone. Hydrogen peroxide alone provided less than 2-log inactivation. 
 Permanganate, iodine, ammonia, bromine, sulfur dioxide, chlorate, sodium thiosulfate, and sulfur dioxide were all reported to be largely ineffective for Cryptosporidium control. 
Research was identified evaluating the following treatment technologies for the removal or inactivation of Cryptosporidium:

Contaminant Name: Cryptosporidium | Function: Ozone | Details: Inactivation of Cryptosporidium in water by ozone can be very effective (1- to over 3-log inactivation) where the effectiveness is highly dependent on temperature, ozone dose, contact time, and water quality. Ozonation is in the LT2 tool box for control of Cryptosporidium. CT (concentration X time) requirements for 3-log inactivation of Cryptosporidium with ozone at 22°C are 8.0-8.3 mg-min/L whereas at 1°C the values are ~76 mg-min/L. Reactor modeling can be used to calculate the applied ozone dose and CT. Synergism between chlorine dioxide, free chlorine and monochloramine have all been documented where sufficiently long contact times are provided for the secondary disinfectant when ozone is used as primary disinfectant.

Contaminant Name: Cryptosporidium | Function: Ozone and Hydrogen Peroxide | Details: One study showed that inactivation of Cryptosporidum in water by ozone with hydrogen peroxide dosed to increase the production of the hydroxyl radical is less effective than water dosed with ozone alone (96.5% with ozone alone vs. 86.9% with a 0.6:1 peroxide to ozone ratio). This technology is not in the LT2 microbial tool box for control of Cryptosporidum.

Contaminant Name: Cryptosporidium | Function: Permanganate | Details: Based on one study, inactivation of Cryptosporidium in water by permanganate addition is not effective. Log inactivation levels of 0.21 to 0.27 were seen with a CT of 2,400 mg x min/L which corresponds either to a very high permanganate dose or an extensive contact time. Permanganate is not in the LT2 micobial tool box for Cryptosporidium control.

Contaminant Name: Cryptosporidium | Function: Precipitative Softening | Details: Removal of Cryptosporidium in water by precipitative softening can be effective (3.5- to 4-log removal) with two clarification stages and one filtration stage. Two-stage lime softening is in the LT2 microbial tool box for control of Cryptosporidium. Presedimentation prior to softening produced an additional 0.5- to 1-log removal.

Contaminant Name: Cryptosporidium | Function: Slow Sand Filtration | Details: Removal of Cryptosporidium in water by slow sand Ffltration can be very effective (>99%), where the effectiveness is dependent primarily on the source water quality and temperature. Cryptosporidium removal is highly dependent on water quality due to the chemical pretreatment in the process. Under cold water operating conditions, Cryptosporidium removal has not been found to be as effective, with removals averaging less than 50%. The slow sand filter media also impacts removal. LT1 cites slow sand filtration as an effective means of Cryptosporidium removal, provided that turbidity control is maintained.

Contaminant Name: Cryptosporidium | Function: Ultraviolet Irradiation | Details: Inactivation of Cryptosporidium in water by ultraviolet irradiation (UV) can be very effective (2- to over 4-log inactivation) where the effectiveness is dependent primarily on UV dose and contact time. Cryptosporidium inactivation is also highly dependent on water quality because of blinding effects of particles within the water (caused from turbidity, etc). Relatively low doses of UV (1-9mJ/cm2) have been shown to inactivate 2-4 log (99-99.9%) of Cryptosporidium oocysts. UV irradiation is in the LT2 microbial tool box for control of Cryptosporidium. Temperature and UV intensity have been shown to have little impact on inactivation by UV. Modeling efforts show that each full scale reactor should be characterized in order to properly understand flow patterns and calculate an applied UV dose.

Contaminant Name: Cyanide | Description: Cyanide refers to the –CΞN anion radical (CN-). Cyanide compounds can be inorganic or organic. Inorganic compounds may be simple (e.g., HCN, KCN) or complex (e.g., Cu[CN]2-). [1368] Free cyanide is defined as the sum of the cyanide present as either hydrogen cyanide (HCN) or CN-. [1368] Cyanide in this database focuses on free cyanide, as it is the most toxic form.
Cyanide naturally occurs in many foods. For example, hydrogen cyanide is released from glycoside by hydrolysis catalyzed by various enzymes in at least 2000 plants. [1356] Cyanide is widely used in industry and five processes account for most of cyanide wastes causing stream pollution, including metal plating, case hardening of steel, neutralizing of acid “pickle scum”, refining of gold and silver ores, and scrubbing of stack gases from blast and producer gas furnaces. [1364] Roughly 5 billion pounds of common cyanides were produced each year in the late 1980s and early 1990s. [1320]
USEPA established a maximum contaminant level (MCL) of 0.2 mg/L and a maximum contaminant level goal (MCLG) of 0.2 mg/L as free cyanide. [1320] Best Available Technologies for control of cyanide are: oxidation by chlorine, ion exchange and reverse osmosis. [1320] 
Cyanide is toxic and the lethal oral doses of cyanide compounds generally range from 50 to 200 mg CN (0.7 to 2.9 mg/Kg). [1322] Short-term exposure to cyanide above the MCL can cause rapid breathing, tremors and other neurological effects. [1320] Long-term exposure (e.g., lifetime) at levels above the MCL can cause weight loss, thyroid effects and nerve damage. [1320] At present, no adequate evidence that cyanide compounds have the potential to cause cancer from lifetime exposure in drinking water. [1321]
Cyanide can enter surface water through releases from industries using cyanides (e.g., metal finishing industries, iron and steel mills), runoff from disposal of cyanide wastes in landfills, pesticides and the use of cyanide-containing road salts. [1357] Cyanide is usually found at very low concentrations in drinking water sources. [1356] According to USEPA STORNET database, the mean cyanide concentration in most surface waters in the U.S. is less than 3.5 µg/L. [1356] In 1978, a USEPA survey showed that approximately 7% of drinking water sources had cyanide at levels greater than 10 µg/L. [1356]
Soluble cyanide compounds (e.g. HCN, KCN) are mobile in soils and have low adsorption to soils with high pH, high carbonate and low clay content. [1321] Cyanide may leach to groundwater. [1320] Under typical conditions in natural waters (pH less than 9.2), most free cyanide is expected to convert to hydrogen cyanide that is highly volatile. [1321] Thus, volatilization from water surfaces is expected to be an important fate process. Cyanide is not likely to accumulate in aquatic life. [1320]
Date of Literature Search: August 2009

Contaminant Name: Cyanide | Function: Adsorptive Media | Details: N/A

Contaminant Name: Cyanide | Function: Chemical Treatment | Details: One study reported efficient cyanide removal from deionized water as well as a diluted industrial rinse water using ferrate(VI) [1360]. Ferrate(VI) oxidizes cyanide and generate cyanate as a byproduct. Cyanate will further react with ferrate(VI) and generate nitrite and carbon dioxide as final products. The reactions were reported to be accomplished in minutes. Almost 100 percent cyanide removal was reported by 27.6 mg/L ferrate(VI) with an initial cyanide concentration of 6 mg/L in deionized water.

Contaminant Name: Cyanide | Function: Chlorine | Details: Chlorine can be used for free cyanide removal; however, the end products are dependent of pH levels. At pH below 8.5, cyanogen chloride (CNCl), a highly toxic and volatile compound, is formed [1364]. With pH increased to above 8.5, cyanogen chloride can be converted to cyanate (CNO-), which is several hundred times less toxic than cyanides [1364]. In addition, the conversion time to cyanate decreases significantly with increased pH [1364]. Therefore, pH needs to be adjusted to above 8.5 to avoid formation of cyanogen chloride, or typically above 11.5 to obtain rapid oxidation to cyanate. To oxidize cyanate, pH needs to be reduced to below 9.5 [1365]. Chlorine is commonly used for cyanide removal in wastewaters; however, limited data are found for drinking water treatment. A bench-scale lab study showed that cyanide was reduced from 100, 250 and 500 μg/L to below 5 μg/L with a free chlorine residual of 1 mg/L [1362]. However, the end products were not identified. A bench-scale study of shipboard wastewater found 58 to 97 percent cyanide oxidation from a starting concentration of 3.9 mg/L, depending on the chlorine dose applied [2461].

Contaminant Name: Cyanide | Function: GAC Isotherm | Details: One study investigated GAC isotherm for cyanide in drinking water, with an initial cyanide concentration of 1.0 mg/L [1367]. The sorption process proceeds slowly. After 33.5 hours of agitation, the system reached a pseudoequilibrium. The cyanide absorbed to GAC was 85 µmol/g of GAC. The study also suggest that other ions, including calcium(II), magnesium(II), iron(III), aluminum(III) and humic acid can inhibit the sorption of cyanide to GAC.

Contaminant Name: Cyanide | Function: Hydrogen Peroxide | Details: Cyanide removal by hydrogen peroxide alone is slow; however, hydrogen peroxide with copper as catalyst is shown to be effective. Cyanide removal efficiency is affected by hydrogen peroxide concentration, contact time, pH, copper concentration and temperature. One study showed enhanced cyanide removal with increasing pH in the range of 3.0 to 10.0 [1359]. However, another study showed lower cyanide removal rates at pH 11 compared to pH 8 with copper-impregnated pumice as catalyst [1358]. Cyanide removal rates were reported generally higher at a temperature of 20 °C compared with at 10 °C [1358].

Contaminant Name: Cyanide | Function: Ion Exchange | Details: Ion exchange is a best available technology for removal of cyanide [1320]. In bench-scale tests using packed bed anion exchange columns, initial removal of cyanide was 99.9 percent from a starting concentration of 200 mg/L. This removal was maintained for 80 to 90 bed volumes. The columns were completely exhausted at 250 to 420 bed volumes, depending on pH and the presence or absence of zinc [2459].

Contaminant Name: Cyanide | Function: Membrane Separation | Details: Reverse osmosis is a best available technology for removal of cyanide [1320]. A pilot-scale test on coking wastewater found 87.8 percent removal of total cyanide by nanofiltration, then an additional 99.6 percent removal by reverse osmosis [2457].

Contaminant Name: Cyanide | Overall: Limited data were found evaluating cyanide treatment techniques for drinking water. USEPA approved three best available technologies (BAT) for cyanide removal from drinking water: ion exchange (IX), reverse osmosis (RO) and chlorine oxidation. 1522 Limited data were found evaluating IX and RO for cyanide removal from drinking water. However, based on bench- and pilot-scale data, along with the chemical properties of cyanide, IX and RO are expected to be effective for cyanide removal. Chlorine oxidation under alkaline conditions is commonly used in waste water cyanide removal, and is expected to be effective for drinking water treatment.
Research identified several other treatment technologies that can be effective for cyanide removal from drinking water: chemical treatment (oxidation using ferrate (VI)), hydrogen peroxide, ozone, ozone combined with hydrogen peroxide, ultraviolet irradiation combined with hydrogen peroxide. Removal efficiency varies largely and generally depends on chemical doses, contact time, presence of catalysts, pH and temperature. In addition, granular activated carbon (GAC) isotherm indicates cyanide can be removed however, the process occurs slowly.

Contaminant Name: Cyanide | Function: Ozone | Details: Ozone reacts with cyanide ion extremely rapidly in alkaline media (i.e. pH 10.5 – 11.0) and produces cyanate ion (CNO-) [1363]. However, the oxidation of cyanate ion by ozone is much slower [1363]. In addition, the complex cyanides of copper, iron and other metals are less amenable to ozone decomposition [1363]. In general, ozone consumption for complete cyanide ion removal is so high that ozone is more suitable for treatment of cyanide ion at concentrations above 15 to 20 mg/L CN- [1363]. Bench-scale studies with influent concentrations of 240 to 500 mg/L confirm the effectiveness of ozone at these high concentrations [2458, 2460].
Although a bench-scale lab study suggests ozone can remove cyanide at low concentrations efficiently, a pilot study showed that ozone removed approximately 22 percent of initial cyanide of 0.2 mg/L in a surface water in France [1362]. Another pilot study showed removal of 99.5 percent, but with an initial cyanide concentration of 28.7 mg/L [2460]. The end products of ozonation were not identified in the lab and pilot studies.

Contaminant Name: Cyanide | Function: Ozone and Hydrogen Peroxide | Details: Ozone combined with hydrogen peroxide is shown to be effective for cyanide removal at low concentrations in water. One bench-scale study reported 90 percent removal for an initial cyanide concentration of 0.3 mg/L with an ozone dose of 3.0 mg/L and a hydrogen peroxide dose of 15 mg/L [1361]. Approximately 72 percent removal was reached for an initial cyanide dose of 0.5 mg/L by adding 15 mg/L hydrogen peroxide and 7.0 mg/L ozone [1361].The study suggested increased cyanide removal with increasing hydrogen peroxide doses (from 2 mg/L to 15 mg/L) with a fixed ozone dose.

Contaminant Name: Cyanide | Function: Ultraviolet Irradiation and Hydrogen Peroxide | Details: One study reported effective cyanide removal using ultraviolet (UV) irradiation combined with hydrogen peroxide with copper as catalyst [1359]. Initial cyanide concentration was 100 mg/L. The study showed cyanide removal was greater than 99 percent with a low-pressure UV lamp, 1200 mg/L hydrogen peroxide and 10 to 25 mg/L copper as catalyst within 8 to 14 minutes. UV and hydrogen peroxide without copper can also remove cyanide, but at a much lower rate. A 99 percent removal was reached in 45 minutes without copper compared to 8 to 14 minutes with copper.

Contaminant Name: Cyclonite (RDX) | Description: Cyclonite, also known as RDX (for Royal Demolition eXplosive), a highly explosive (HE) compound which has been in use since the late 19th century. It is manufactured at a limited number of military facilities by itself or in combination with other explosives.  RDX's IUPAC name is 1,3,5-trinitro-1,3,5-triazinane. 
It can be found in contaminated groundwater and soils near military sites throughout the country. Contamination has mostly occurred through improper disposal of manufacturing and process waste called pink water. Pink water is generated from loading, assembling, packing, and demilitarization activities in military ammunitions plants [1715]. There are several HEs found in contaminated groundwater, however RDX is one of the most common compounds found. Concentrations of RDX in pink water are reported to be approximately 12 to 76 mg/L [1715, 1729].
USEPA has set lifetime human health advisories of 0.002 mg/L for RDX assuming residential exposure with a duration of 70 years [1717].  It is a possible human carcinogen based on anuimal liver tumor studies.
RDX is not very stable in water.  It is mobile in groundwater due to the weak sorption to aquifer solids. Granular activated carbon (GAC) is the most common treatment technology employed at this time, primarily in pump and treat configurations [1712]. However, spent GAC must be treated, which can dramatically increases treatment costs [1719]. Research is ongoing to determine treatment methods for RDX in wastewater treatment (pink water), water treatment, and in-situ.
RDX is not regulated in drinking water in the US, but is on the Contaminant Candidate List 3. 
Date of Literature Search: August 2010

Contaminant Name: Cyclonite (RDX) | Function: Biological Treatment | Details: Biological treatment was found to be effective for the removal of RDX in anaerobic and anoxic conditions. One bench-scale anaerobic study found removals of 54 to 100%, with removal efficiencies increasing with H2 gas and/or Fe(0) augmentation.
Another bench-scale study investigating the removal of RDX in a contaminated aquifer found 39 to 100% removal after 51 days of incubation under anoxic conditions. The study found that amending the aquifer soils with Mn(IV), NO3, or C significantly enhanced microbial degradation. Under oxic conditions, however, no removal was observed.

Contaminant Name: Cyclonite (RDX) | Function: Chemical Treatment | Details: Homogeneous oxidation (no catalyst) of RDX occurred at temperatures as low as 85° C and complete oxidation occurred at temperatures above 110° C in less than 30 minutes at a constant pressure of 500 psig [1714]. The addition of the catalyst, Pt/TiO2 had little effect at 85° C, however the catalyst nearly doubled the reaction rate at 95° C resulting in total oxidation in 30 minutes. The results show that the oxidation of RDX can occur very rapidly and is strongly temperature dependent. The impact of catalyst addition is also dependent upon temperature, with increased reaction rates due to catalyst addition occurring at higher temperatures.
These conditions are extreme compared to typical drinking water treatment and are only suggestive of catalytic TiO2 treatment at typical temperatures and pressures.

Contaminant Name: Cyclonite (RDX) | Function: GAC Isotherm | Details: Isotherms play a major role in the designing of carbon adsorption systems. To accurately design granular activated carbon (GAC) treatement systems and predict their performance, accurate adsorption equilibrium parameters are required. Isotherm data from one study indicated that RDX and its metabolites are effectively adsorbed by GAC.

Contaminant Name: Cyclonite (RDX) | Function: Granular Activated Carbon | Details: Granular activated carbon (GAC) is commonly used for the treatment of RDX-contaminated groundwater [1710].  Based on the results of one bench-scale study, RDX was very effectively removed by GAC at empty bed contact times (EBCT) of 20 minutes.  In RDX-spiked laboratory water, removal of RDX was still greater than 90 percent at more than 300,000 bed volumes and greater than 70 percent at more than 400,000 bed volumes.  However, breakthrough can occur much more quickly when other contaminants, such as perchlorate or HMX, are present [1712]. Another bench-scale study found that RDX was removed to levels below detection limits for groundwater contaminated with very low levels of RDX [1735].

Contaminant Name: Cyclonite (RDX) | Function: Other Treatment | Details: Four technologies which have demonstrated the ability to remove/destroy RDX are alkaline hydrolysis, electrochemical reduction, Fe(0), and Fluidized Bed Reactor. 
Alkaline hydrolysis transforms RDX into organic and inorganic salts, soluble organic compounds, benign nitrogen gases and ammonia [1719]. The batch reactor experiments show that 100% removal of RDX is possible at pH 11 (4,500 minutes to 20,000 minutes depending on influent concentration), but RDX hydrolysis occurs more quickly at higher pH (complete hydrolysis within 150 to 375 minutes at pH 13).  These pHs and reactions are atypical of drinking water treatment conditions.
RDX is electrochemically active and easily reducible. Using a divided flow-through cell, an RVC cathode and a DSA, it was found that the rate of degradation of RDX increased with current density, and that total degradation was achieved with a small scale cascade of reactors [1729].
The use of Fe(0) was found to be effective in degrading RDX when added as a powder (85%) as well as in a continuous flow column (98%) in a bench-scale study [1731]. The same study also found that biological RDX removal was enhanced when augmented with Fe(0).
The data from the Fluidized Bed Reactor (FBR) study demonstrate that a GAC-based FBR fed acetic acid as an elctron donor is capable of successfully treating RDX in ground water. Moreover, the study demonstrates that ex situ biological treatment of very low leves of RDX (200 ug/L) is feasible. The choice of the electron donor for treatment of RDX is critical, as the performance of a molasses-fed reactor was poor in comparision to the acetic acid-fed system. (99.5 % vs 40 to 60%) [1732]

Contaminant Name: Cyclonite (RDX) | Overall: Granular activated carbon is most commonly used for the removal of RDX from contaminated groundwater.  Additiional technologies that have been demonstrated to be effective for RDX removal include: chemical oxidation, alkaline hydrolysis, electrochemical reduction, and UV irradiation. Ozonation and hydrogen peroxide oxidation are not generally effective for removing RDX.
Research was identified evaluating the following treatment technologies for the removal of RDX:

Contaminant Name: Cyclonite (RDX) | Function: Permanganate | Details: Potassium permanganate (KMnO4) is an oxidizing agent with a strong affinity for organic compounds containing carbon-carbon double bonds, aldehyde groups or hydroxyl groups. Although the chemical structure of RDX does not make it an ideal candidate for attack by permanganate, effective RDX loss by treatment  with KMnO4 has been demonstrated. [1730] Adams et al show 97% removal of RDX using KMnO4 within 11 days. Another bench-scale study found RDX removals of 72 to 100% using high concentrations of KMnO4 [1733]. However, the study showed that temperature strongly influenced the reaction kinetics, with a three-fold decrease in the reaction rate at 11.5 degrees C versus 23 degrees C.
Permanganate doses and contact times were extreme for drinking water treatment conditions.

Contaminant Name: Cyclonite (RDX) | Function: Ultraviolet Irradiation | Details: RDX is readily oxidized by UV radiation. Based on the results of one study with different initial RDX concentrations ranging from 23 to 55 ppm, UV irradiation oxidized 70 percent of the RDX initial concentration in 10 minutes and completely oxidized RDX in 25 minutes. The initial concentration had no significant effect on the reaction rates [1714].  The pH was low relative to typical drinking water treatment conditions.

Contaminant Name: Cyclonite (RDX) | Function: Ultraviolet Irradiation and Hydrogen Peroxide | Details: RDX is readily oxidized by UV radiation, and although hydrogen peroxide alone has no effect on RDX, in combination with UV irradiation it does perform slightly better than UV alone.  For example, UV alone was able to oxidize approximately 70 percent of the initial RDX concentration in 10 minutes and completely oxidize RDX in 25 minutes. When combined with hydrogen peroxide, 85% of the initial RDX concentration was oxidized in 10 minutes and complete oxidization of RDX occurred in 25 minutes [1714].  Conditions (H2O2 doses, pHs) were atypical of drinking water treatment conditions.

Contaminant Name: Cylindrospermopsin | Description: Cylindrospermopsin is a toxic constituents of blue-green algae produced by produced by a variety of cyanobacteria including: Cylindrospermopsis raciborskii (C. raciborskii), Aphanizomenon flos-aquae, Aphanizomenon gracile, Aphanizomenon ovalisporum, Umezakia natans, Anabaena bergii, Anabaena lapponica, Anabaena planctonica, Lyngbya wollei, Rhaphidiopsis curvata, and Rhaphidiopsis mediterranea. Although cylindrospermopsin-producing cyanobacteria (such as C. raciborskii) occur mostly in tropical or subtropical regions, they have also been found in warmer temperate regions, both in fresh and marine water systems. These species do not tend to form visible surface scums and the highest concentrations of cells occurs below the water surface [2811]. 
 Cylindrospermopsin generally exists in a zwitterionic state (with both positive and negative ions) and is highly soluble in water [2811]. The occurrence of cyanotoxins in finished drinking water depends on their levels in the raw source water and the effectiveness of the treatment methods used for removing cyanobacteria and cyanotoxins during the production of drinking water. Currently there is no federal or state program in place that requires monitoring for cyanotoxins at U.S. drinking water treatment plants. Therefore, data on the presence or absence of cyanotoxins in finished drinking water are limited [2811]. 
 In 2015, EPA released Drinking Water Health Advisory values for cylindrospermopsin by applying the Ten-day HA value calculated for infants over the first year of life (0.7 μg/L) to all bottle-fed infants and young children of pre-school age because these age groups have higher intake per body weight relative to adults. The calculated HA value for infants from birth to 3 months old is 0.4 μg/L. EPA believes that infants from birth to 3 months old are not at a disproportionate risk at a 0.7 μg/L advisory value because a safety factor of 30 is built into this calculation to account for human variability and deficiencies in the database. The estimated Ten-Day HA values for infants from 3 months old through pre-school age groups (less than 6 years old), are at or above the advisory value of 0.7 μg/L. Therefore, children within these age groups are adequately protected by the advisory value for bottle-fed infants and young children of pre-school age. EPA decided to apply the adult Ten-Day HA value of 3 μg/L to school age children (children older than or equal to 6 years) through adulthood because children’s intake of drinking water relative to body weight in this age group is almost the same as those of an adult (≥ 21 years) [2811]. 
 Date of literature search: June 2021

Contaminant Name: Cylindrospermopsin | Function: Adsorptive Media | Details: One bench-scale study [2758] investigated the effectiveness of magnetophoretic nanoparticle adsorptive media for cylindrospermopsin removal, finding a maximum removal of 89 percent. The study did not address the viability of using this novel media in full-scale drinking water treatment.

Contaminant Name: Cylindrospermopsin | Function: Biological Treatment | Details: Based on bench-scale studies, biological treatment for cylindrospermopsin removal shows a wide range of effectiveness values from 0 to greater than 99 percent removal. Treatment with biological agents alone seems to be ineffective to marginally effective (less than 0 to 24 percent) [2746, 2766]. Removal rates of greater than 90 percent were observed when biological agents (manganese-oxidizing bacteria) were pared with chemical additives (manganese, iron, and others) to stimulate bacterial oxidation of cyanotoxins. The results suggest that oxidation of manganese is pivotal for effective removal [2766]. These studies did not address practical implementation of the biological process within a drinking water treatment plant (e.g., bioreactor design and operating parameters).

Contaminant Name: Cylindrospermopsin | Function: Chemical Treatment | Details: One study [2778] showed that at the bench scale the Fenton-like reaction of carbon dioxide and peroxymonosulfate (PMS) can be very effective for cylindrospermopsin removal with (greater than 95 percent).

Contaminant Name: Cylindrospermopsin | Function: Chloramine | Details: One bench-scale study [2749] showed chloramine treatment to be ineffective at removing cylindrospermopsin (approximately 0 percent).

Contaminant Name: Cylindrospermopsin | Function: Chlorine | Details: Based on bench-scale studies [2749, 2759, 2767, 2775, 2776, 2779, 2791], chlorine appears to be an effective treatment for cylindrospermopsin (up to 100 percent removal). Effectiveness is a function of chlorine dose (ranged from 1 to 5 mg/L), contact time, and organic content, with higher doses and contract times generally leading to higher reported removals.

Contaminant Name: Cylindrospermopsin | Function: Chlorine Dioxide | Details: One bench-scale study [2749] showed chlorine dioxide treatment to be ineffective at removing cylindrospermopsin (approximately 0 percent).

Contaminant Name: Cylindrospermopsin | Function: Conventional Treatment | Details: One study [2761] reviewed the effectiveness of conventional treatment for cylindrospermopsin removal at a full-scale facility. The treatment train included a chlorination step prior to coagulations and filtration. This process was reasonably effective at removing the cyanotoxin with and reported removal of greater than 83 percent in post-filtration effluent.

Contaminant Name: Cylindrospermopsin | Function: GAC Isotherm | Details: One study [2758] reported isotherm constants for adsorption of cylindrospermopsin on a novel magnetophoretic nanoparticle media.

Contaminant Name: Cylindrospermopsin | Function: Granular Activated Carbon | Details: One bench-scale study [2765] reported up to 94 percent removal of cylindrospermopsin using granular activated carbon (GAC). Effectiveness was reduced (to 30 percent) in pre-loaded GAC tests.

Contaminant Name: Cylindrospermopsin | Function: Hydrogen Peroxide | Details: One bench-scale study [2781] found 85 percent removal of cylindrospermopsin by hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) [2778]. Several other bench studies [2754, 2764, 2769, 2770] employed oxidation through Fenton-type reactions by combining H2O2 with the addition of iron. Maximum removal observed in these studies was greater than 95 percent.

Contaminant Name: Cylindrospermopsin | Function: Membrane Separation | Details: Based on two bench-scale studies [2751, 2752], membrane separation appears to be a very effective treatment for cylindrospermopsin with reported removal rates of up to greater than 99 percent. Effectiveness varied by membrane manufacturer and reported molecular weight cutoff (MWCO) values (lower MWCO values were typically more effective).

Contaminant Name: Cylindrospermopsin | Function: Other Treatment | Details: Other treatment processes studied for cylindrospermopsin removal effectiveness include visible light photocatalysis, Fenton-like reactions without hydrogen peroxide, non-thermal plasma, and electrochemical treatment. 
Several bench studies [2755, 2762, 2773, 2787, 2793] on the treatment of cylindrospermopsin with visible light with and without addition of oxidants such as titanium dioxide and iron showed marginal to high levels of effectiveness (0 to 30 percent on the low end and greater than 85 percent on the higher end). The effectiveness seemed to be dependent on the chemical addition dose, as well as the visible light wavelength and contact time. 

Non-thermal plasma treatment seemed to be effective at removing cylindrospermopsin from solution at the bench scale (removals up to greater than 97 percent) [2777].
At the bench scale [2744, 2777], electrochemical treatment including pulsed dielectric barrier discharge and corona-like plasma showed a range of reported removal efficiencies (3 to greater than 95 percent) with removal rate depending on pH, operating voltage ranges, and treatment times. Generally, with higher removal rate at more basic pHs and longer treatment times.

Contaminant Name: Cylindrospermopsin | Overall: Most of the published papers reviewed on cylindrospermopsin water treatment were studies conducted at the bench scale. Treatment processes frequently found to be effective were chlorination (up to 100 percent removal), ozonation (up to greater than 99 percent removal), and powdered activated carbon (up to greater than 99 percent removal). A full-scale conventional treatment plant that included a pre-filtration chlorination step achieved 83 percent removal. Ultraviolet irradiation can be effective, but may require longer contact times than typically used in drinking water and/or the addition of titanium dioxide as an oxidizing agent. 
 Other processes that may be effective based on a limited number of studies include: adsorption using novel magnetophoretic nanoparticle media (89 percent removal), biological treatment in combination with chemical oxidizers (greater than 90 percent removal), chemical treatment using carbon dioxide and peroxymonosulfate (greater than 95 percent removal), hydrogen peroxide (85 percent removal), hydrogen peroxide combined with iron (up to greater than 95 percent removal), membrane separation (up to greater than 99 percent removal), and ultraviolet irradiation plus hydrogen peroxide (up to 99 percent removal). 
Research was identified evaluating the following treatment technologies for cylindrospermopsin:

Contaminant Name: Cylindrospermopsin | Function: Ozone | Details: In bench-scale studies [2749, 2771, 2775, 2788, 2789], ozone was moderately to very effective at removing cylindrospermopsin form water (up to greater than 99 percent). Some reported removal rates varied widely and seem to be a function of ozone dose, contact time, TOC, turbidity, and potentially other water quality parameters.

Contaminant Name: Cylindrospermopsin | Function: Permanganate | Details: Based on two available bench-scale studies [2749, 2775], permanganate addition appears to be ineffective at removing cylindrospermopsin (0 to 15 percent removal).

Contaminant Name: Cylindrospermopsin | Function: Powdered Activated Carbon | Details: Powdered activated carbon is an effective treatment for cylindrospermopsin based on a review of bench-scale studies (up to greater than 99 percent removal) [2759, 2760, 2765]. Effectiveness varied for different PAC manufacturers and PAC types but was generally high. TOC may also affect removal based on reported results.

Contaminant Name: Cylindrospermopsin | Function: Ultraviolet Irradiation | Details: Based on bench-scale studies [2746, 2748, 2749, 2753, 2755, 2756, 2773, 2774, 2780, 2792], UV irradiation seems to be an effective treatment for cylindrospermopsin when combined with an oxidating agent. Various treatment protocols showed a range of removal rates with higher removal rate in tests of titanium dioxide with UV exposures (up to 100 percent) and lower rates in tests with UV alone (8 percent to 90 percent). Effectiveness varied with UV contact time and dose. The contact times in some of these studies were significantly longer than those typically used in full-scale UV treatment of drinking water.

Contaminant Name: Cylindrospermopsin | Function: Ultraviolet Irradiation and Hydrogen Peroxide | Details: Based on two-bench scale studies [2756, 2757], UV irradiation plus hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) can be effective at treating cylindrospermopsin in water. Removal rates of up to 99 percent were reported. Performance varied as function of UV dose.

Contaminant Name: Dacthal and Dacthal Degradates | Description: Dacthal also known as DCPA, is a pre-emergence herbicide used to prevent the sprouting of seeds of annual grasses in turf and lawns, as well as in some vegetable crops including strawberries, onions and beans. [1473, 1474, 1475] Dacthal is a man-made organic chemical and does not occur naturally. It has been used since the 1960's. As of 1990, more than 80 percent of its use was for turf, including golf courses and home lawns. [7473] Available data indicate that Dacthal use declined significantly over the course of the 1990s. On July 27, 2005, in response to concerns about groundwater contamination (especially for one of the Dacthal degradates), the registrant voluntarily terminated most uses for products containing Dacthal. Dacthal is currently registered only for use on sweet potatoes, eggplant, kale, and turnips. [1475]
 
Dacthal is chemically known as dimethyl tetrachloroterephthalate (DCPA), a synthetic organic compound (SOC) marketed under the trade name "Dacthal". [1475]
 
Dacthal has been found at generally low levels in both surface and groundwater in various parts of the country. [1473] Dacthal is not particularly mobile or persistent in the environment. Biodegradation and volatilization are the primary dissipation routes. [1475]
 
Degradation of Dacthal forms two breakdown products, the mono-acid degradate (monomethyl tetrachloroterephthalate or MTP) and the di-acid degradate (tetrachloroterephthalic acid or TPA). The di-acid, which is the major degradate, is mobile and persistent in the field, with a potential to leach into water. [1475] Dacthal is not very mobile in soil, however, its degradates are suspected to move rapidly through the soil to the groundwater. [1474]
 
MTP and TPA are metabolites of Dacthal in humans and animals. Dacthal appears to be poorly adsorbed from the gastrointestinal tract in humans and animals. Low solubility in aqueous media would contribute to its poor intestinal uptake. Metabolically, Dacthal is converted first to MTP and them to TPA most likely by nonspecific esterases. The solubility of the degradates is likely to be greater than that for Dacthal since acids tend to be more soluble than esters. [1476]
 
Little is known about the distribution of Dacthal or its metabolites. The target organs for Dacthal suggest distributions to the liver, kidney, thyroid and lungs. Limited data from animal studies indicate that there is distribution for the metabolites to the liver and kidney; parent compound has been detected in adipose tissue. Most orally ingested Dacthal is excreted in the feces, unabsorbed; TPA and MTP have been identified in the urine of humans and animals treated with Dacthal; the levels of MTP far exceed this of TPA. There have been no toxicokinetic studies of MTP or TPA. TPA does not degrade in living systems based on projections of the META metabolism and biodegradation modeling program. [1476]
 
The limited studies referenced in [1476] indicate no observable effects in humans due to Dacthal. 
 

Date of Literature Search: June 2009

Contaminant Name: Dacthal and Dacthal Degradates | Overall: No data was found evaluating the removal of dacthal and its degradates from drinking water.  However, based on their chemical properties, the following processes are expected to be able to removal dacthal and its degradates with varying degrees of success: membrane separation, activated carbon, and advanced oxidation.
Membrane separation processes, including nanofiltration (NF) and reverse osmosis (RO), are capable of removing high molecular weight dissolved organic contaminants, based on their molecular weight, DCPA and its degradates are expected to be removed by both NF and RO.
Contaminants containing halogen groups and double bonds, as DCPA does, have a high affinity for granular activated carbon (GAC). In addition, compounds exhibiting low water solubility are expected to have high binding affinity for GAC. DCPA&rsquos degradates tend to be more soluble than the parent compound and therefore are expected to be less amenable to activated carbon treatment.
The susceptibility of a pesticide to oxidation may be inferred from aerobic soil metabolism data. Compounds with short aerobic metabolism half-lives are expected to be more amenable to treatment using AOPs (ozone, ozone/H2O2, UV/H2O2, and UV/O3). The half-life of DCPA is between 18 to 37 days, while the half-life of the mono-acid degradate is shorter (2.8 days) and that of the di-acid degradate is longer (virtually no degradation in 300 days). These findings suggest that AOPs may be effective for the mono-acid degradate, less effective for the parent compound, and not effective for the di-acid degradate 1476.

Contaminant Name: Diazinon | Description: Diazinon is an organophosphorus insecticide  classified by the World Health Organization (WHO) as "moderately hazardous" Class II.  It is relatively water soluble, non-polar, moderately mobile and persistent in soil [763]. It's IUPAC name is O,O-diethyl O-2-isopropyl-6-methylpyrimidin-4-yl phosphorothioate.
Over 13 million lbs of diazinon are applied annually in the United States. Diazinon and its metabolites have been detected in aquatic systems worldwide [763].  Additionally, high diazinon residues were found in urbane water-ways and effluents from sewage treatments plants [764].  Diazinon is manufactured in the United States and formulated as granules, a wettable powder, an emulsifiable solution, a dust, a seed dressing, or a mixed formulation with other insecticides[762].
Diazinon in drinking water is not regulated by the USEPA.
Fatal human doses were found to be in the range of 90 to 444 mg/kg.  Toxic effects of diazinon are attributed to its inhibition of the enzyme acetylcholinestrase [763].
Date of Literature Search: June 2009

Contaminant Name: Diazinon | Function: Chlorine | Details: Application of Chlorine, in the form of Chlorine or Sodium Hypochlorite,  was effective in oxidation of diazinon under the evaluated conditions. One study showed that 0.3 mg/l of diazinon in natural waters can be oxidized completely in 10 minutes by a 2.5 mg/l of chlorine dose. The same study also showed that with this chlorine dose, the formation of TTHMs was below the EU standard (0.1 mg/l) for drinking water [761].

Contaminant Name: Diazinon | Function: Hydrogen Peroxide | Details: Oxidation bench scale experiments using Fenton's reaction (hydrogen peroxide + ferrous ions) showed a positive influence on the degradation of diazinon [573]. Increase in ferrous ions concentration has more positive effect on the degradation than an increase in hydrogen peroxide dose. Addition of hydrogen peroxide alone does not produce any significant diazinon degradation.

Contaminant Name: Diazinon | Function: Other Treatment | Details: Other techniques like Irradiation with X-Rays or γ- rays and Ultrasonic treatment was effective in degradation of diazinon for the conditions presented. 
10 mg/l of diazinon in lab water was almost completely degraded (91 percent)  by 4.79 kGy/h of Cobalt 60 γ- rays in 1.3 hours.  Lower percent removal was observed in groundwater than in lab water by γ- ray irradiation.  80 percent of 40 mg/l diazinon is degraded by 50 nmGy/s white X-rays in 120 minutes.
70 percent removal was achieved using 1.7 Mhz wave length of ultrasound in 10 minutes. It must be noted that concentrations in the range of 1000 mg/l are not seen drinking water sources.

Contaminant Name: Diazinon | Overall: The following processes are considered effective for the removal of diazinon: chlorine (up to 100 percent), hydrogen peroxide (up to 90 percent), ozone (65 to 100 percent), ozone  hydrogen peroxide (up to 70 percent), ultraviolet irradiation (up to 100 percent),ultraviolet irradiation hydrogen peroxide (upto 100 percent) and other treatments like irradiation with X-rays or &gamma-rays and ultrasound (upto 100 percent). Performance of these technologies can vary based on water quality and treatment characteristics.
Research was identified evaluating the following treatment technologies for the removal of diazinon:

Contaminant Name: Diazinon | Function: Ozone | Details: Ozonation was an effective process to degrade diazinon from the conditions evaluated. Significant removal percentages  (65 to 100)were seen in both lab water and surface water. One study showed that increased ozone dosage (more than required for disinfection) significantly (by 91 percent) reduced  the diazinon concentration without significant bromate ion formation [1458].

Contaminant Name: Diazinon | Function: Ozone and Hydrogen Peroxide | Details: From one reference, addition of hydrogen peroxide and ozone increases the degradation of diazinon as compared to addition of ozone alone or hydrogen peroxide alone under similar conditions. The increase in hydrogen peroxide concentration does not significantly increase the degradation of diazinon.

Contaminant Name: Diazinon | Function: Ultraviolet Irradiation | Details: Significant removal of diazinon (upto 90 percent) from lab water was seen when irradiated using lower presssuire UV-C (254 nm) irradiation. Medium pressure lamp were less effective in degrading diazinon. The effectiveness of the medium pressure lamps were increased by using catalysts like Titanium di-oxide or Zinc Oxide.
Complete degradation of diazinon was achieved by using a 100 W  or 450 W medium pressure UV lamp and 1 mg/l of Titanium dioxide as catalyst. Increase in UV intensity increaed the rate of diazinon degradation [769].
Ultraviolet irradiation in the presence of 150 mg/l of Zinc oxide effectively degraded diazinon from ground water. The degradation was lesser than in distilled water. An optimal pH value for 5.2 was required for maximum removal from distilled water.

Contaminant Name: Diazinon | Function: Ultraviolet Irradiation and Hydrogen Peroxide | Details: Effective removal was seen by using ultraviolet irradiation and hydrogen peroxide under the conditions evaluated.
Increase in hydrogen peroxide dosage did not significantly increase the degradation percentage.  Higher pH (10) inhibited the degradation as compared to a neutral or acidic medium [763].
In one reference, complete degradation was achieved in less than 5 minutes using 5 mg/l ferrous ions as a catalyst (photo-Fenton reaction).

Contaminant Name: Dichlorvos | Description: Dichlorvos is an organophosphate insecticide. Its IUPAC name is 2, 2-dichlorovinyl dimethyl phosphate. It is commonly called DDVP and has a common trade name of Valpona. [495] Because it has a high vapor pressure, its principal use is as a fumigant and fogger. [495] It is commonly used as a fumigant in food storage areas, grain storage areas, barns, animal houses, and greenhouses. [527] It is also used on pest strips and pet flea collars. [602] 
Dichlorvos is not regulated by USEPA in drinking water.
It is toxic by ingestion. [527] As it is an acetylcholinesterase inhibitor, it affects the central nervous system. [602] USEPA classifies it as a possible human carcinogen. [602]
The following properties and phenomena suggest dichlorvos will not commonly be detected in water. [602] It has a low organic carbon partition coefficient and is not well sorbed to soil particles suggesting its movement through soil to groundwater. It has a low Henry's constant suggesting it does not volatilize from water. It does not strongly absorb UV light suggesting it will not photodegrade in water. While these properties suggest it will be stable in water, other properties suggest losses in surface waters and ground waters, and suggest it would not commonly be detected in source waters. In water, its hydrolysis half lives are in the order of days at acidic pHs and hours at basic pHs, and its hydrolysis half lives are in the order of weeks at 10 C and days at 30 C. In laboratory studies, its disappearance in soils was linked to Bacillus cereus. In sewage sludge studies, its transformation to dichloroethanol, dichloroacetic acid, ethyl dichloroacetate, and phosphate was related to Pseudomonas aeruginosa and other Pseudomonas species.
It enters water via manufacturing, landfill leachates, spills, and leaks. [528] It may be formulated with a variety of other pesticides. In water, dichlorvos may be detected as a result of the breakdown of trichlorfon, another organophosphate insecticide. [602] All of the 1430 National Priorities List hazardous waste sites were not sampled for dichlorvos, but it was detected at three of the sites. [528] Among those three sites, it was detected in only one of the groundwaters. [602] In USEPA's National Pesticide Survey of Drinking Water Wells, dichlorvos was not detected. [602]
In addition to those listed above, other transformation products of dichlorvos in water have been reported as dimethyl phosphate, dichloroacetaldehyde, 1, 1-dichloroethenol, and dimethyl phosphoric acid. [602]

Date of Literature Search: March 2007

Contaminant Name: Dichlorvos | Function: Granular Activated Carbon | Details: GAC adsorption was effective for the removal of dichlorvos based on data available from three Environmental Technology Verification studies. The validation studies tested proprietary GAC units. GAC removed 96 to greater than 99 percent dichlorvos. As adsorption is time dependent, these removals were observed after only a few hours run time.

Contaminant Name: Dichlorvos | Function: Membrane Separation | Details: Membrane separation using reverse osmosis was effective for the removal of dichlorvos. Four Environmental Technology Verification studies tested proprietary RO units. RO removed 95 to greater than 99 percent of dichlorvos.

Contaminant Name: Dichlorvos | Overall: The following processes are considered effective for the control of dichlorvos under drinking water conditions: membrane separation by reverse osmosis (95 to greater than 99 percent removal), granular activated carbon (96 to greater than 99 percent removal at near start up of the adsorber), ozone (in the 73 percent to greater than 80 percent oxidation range depending on the ozone dose, and ozone and hydrogen peroxide at a 0.5:1 H2O2:O3 ratio (up to 66 percent oxidation).
Other treatment processes employing conditions outside typical drinking water conditions were suggestive of dichlorvos control. They were: microporous silicates as a catalyst for ozonation, UV irradiation coupled with ferrous ion for photocatalytic oxidation, and ozone and UV coupled with titanium dioxide for photocatalytic oxidation.
Studies were identified evaluating the following treatment technologies for the removal of dichlorvos:

Contaminant Name: Dichlorvos | Function: Ozone | Details: Oxidation of dichlorvos by ozone can be effective depending on the ozone dose, contact time and water quality parameters. One study showed complete oxidation of dichlorvos in 20 minutes with ozone dose of 1 mg/L; the time required for complete removal decreased with increased dose. The pH, however, was 4. Another study at pH 7.3 showed that oxidation of dichlorvos increased with increased ozone dose from 1 mg/L to 5 mg/L; control ranged from 17 to 73 percent, respectively. A third study at pH 8 showed greater than 80 percent oxidation. No studies reported transformation products. One study looked at catalytic ozonation using microporous silicate column showed no dichlorvos removal after two hours of ozonation.

Contaminant Name: Dichlorvos | Function: Ozone and Hydrogen Peroxide | Details: Oxidation of dichlorvos by ozone can be effective depending on the ozone dose, contact time and water quality parameters. As shown in Figure 1, one study showed the removal of dichlorvos with ozone alone, and with ozone combined with hydrogen peroxide. The hydrogen peroxide to ozone ratio remained constant at 0.5 for this study. The pH was 7.3. Ozone with hydrogen peroxide showed more removal for ozone concentrations less than 4.33 mg/L. Transformation products were not reported.

Contaminant Name: Dichlorvos | Function: Ultraviolet Irradiation | Details: One study at pH 4 suggests that UV may be effective in drinking water applications. Depending on the concentration of ferrous iron (Fe2+), the photocatalytic oxidation of dichlorvos was either be enhanced or reduced.

Contaminant Name: Dichlorvos | Function: Ultraviolet Irradiation and Hydrogen Peroxide | Details: Results of one study suggest photocatalytic oxidation of dichlorvos may be effective in drinking water applications. A reactor at pH 4 with titanium dioxide suspensions at 254 nm was found to be more effective in the photocatalytic oxidation of dichlorvos in comparison to the reactor with titanium dioxide coated on the inner surface operating at 360 nm. Furthermore, this study concluded that the rate of dichlorvos degradation was reduced in the presence of hydrogen peroxide.

Contaminant Name: Dicrotophos | Description: Dicrotophos is an organophosphate insecticide used to control insects in cotton. Dicrotophos can also be applied by tree injection to control insects on ornamental and non food bearing trees[1813].
Annual domestic use of dicrotophos in the U.S. is approximately 550,000 pounds. The bulk of this use is on cotton in the southeast, Texas, and Arkanssas [1813].
Its IUPAC name is (E)-2-dimethylcarbamoyl-1-methylvinyl dimethyl phosphate.  It is amixture of (E) and (Z) isomers with the (E) isomer being pesticidally active. 
Hydrolysis of dicrotophos in soil and water is pH dependent with half lives near 117days at pH 5 and near 28 days at pH 9.  Hydrolysis degradates are N,N-dimethylacetoacetamide and O-desmethyldicrotophos.  It can be aerobically and anaerobically microbially degraded with N,N-dimethylacetoacetamide as the major degradate.  Dicrotophos is not volatile or photodegradable.    
Dicrotophos is highly toxic and causes cholinesterase inhibition in animals [1813].  Dicrotophos is an inhibitor of plasma and red blood cell cholinesterase activity in humans [1811]. It can overstimulate the nervous system causing nausea, dizziness, confusion, and at very high exposures (e.g., accidents or major spills), respiratory paralysis and death.  No carcinogenic effects have been noted.
Dicrotophos in drinking water is not regulated by USEPA.  It is on the Contaminant Candidate List 3.
Date of Literature Search: September 2010

Contaminant Name: Dicrotophos | Function: Granular Activated Carbon | Details: Based on the results of one study evaluating point-of-use treatment systems, dicrotophos can be effectively removed by granulated activated carbon (89 percent). When GAC was used following a point-of-use reverse osmosis device, almost complete removal (> 99 percent) of dicrotophos by both processes was observed [1812].

Contaminant Name: Dicrotophos | Function: Membrane Separation | Details: Based on the results of three studies evaluating point-of-use treatment systems, dicrotophos can be effectively removed by reverse osmosis (96 to >99 percent).

Contaminant Name: Dicrotophos | Overall: Based on the limited data available, the following processes are likely to be effective for the removal of dicrotophos: granulated activated carbon (89 percent) and membrane separation by reverse osmosis (>99 percent). Performance of these technologies can vary based on water quality and treatment characteristics.
Research was identified evaluating the following treatment technologies for the removal of dicrotophos:

Contaminant Name: Disulfoton | Description: Disulfoton is an organothiophosphate insecticide and acaricide used to control a variety of harmful pests that attack many field and vegetable crops [1809].  It is used to protect small grains, sorghum, corn, and other field crops; some vegetables, fruit, and nut crops; and ornamental and potted plants against certain insects. Although it is used mostly in agriculture, small quantities are used on home and garden plants, and for mosquito control in swamps [1809].
Its IUPAC name is O,O-diethyl S-2-ethylthioethyl phosphorodithioate.
It is found mainly in soil and water. It binds moderately to soil and typically does not travel deep into soil with rainwater. In water, it takes about 7 days for half of it to break down. In soil, it takes about 3.5-290 days for half of it to break down, depending on soil type, moisture, and temperature [1809].  It breaks down in water under alkaline conditions.  It does not volatilize or photodegrade. 
Degradates in water are disulfoton sulfone and disulfoton sulfoxide.  Both are more persistent than the parent pesticide.
Several monitoring studies found disulfoton and the two degradates infrequently (less than 1%) in surface and finished drinking waters at concentrations below 1 ug/L.  Disulfoton has been found in wells at concentrations below 7 ug/L. 
Disulfoton is a rodent cholinesterase inhibitor with neural effects.  It has not been found to be carcinogenic.
It is not regulated in drinking water but is on the Contaminant Candidate List 3.
Date of literature search: 2010

Contaminant Name: Disulfoton | Function: Chlorine | Details: Based on one study, Greater than 99 percent oxidation of disulfoton was achieved by chlorination. Chlorine almost completely oxidized 100 μg/L of disulfoton in 1 hour [1807].

Contaminant Name: Disulfoton | Overall: Chlorination was effective for the removal of disulfoton, but resulting products were not noted.
Ultraviolet irradiation and ultraviolet irradiation hydrogen peroxide were effective for the removal of disulfoton but not under typical water treatment conditions.  Resulting products were not noted.
Research was identified evaluating the following treatment technologies for the removal of disulfoton:

Contaminant Name: Disulfoton | Function: Ultraviolet Irradiation | Details: Degradation of disulfoton by direct photolysis using polychormatic irradiation is a very slow process. It takes 130 hours for 83 percent of disulfoton (100 μg/L) to be degraded by polychromatic irradiation ( λ > 285 nm) in lab water [1808]. This irradiation time increases when the experiment was carried out in surface water. These long irradiation times are not typical for a drinking water treatment.

Contaminant Name: Disulfoton | Function: Ultraviolet Irradiation and Hydrogen Peroxide | Details: One study suggests that ultraviolet irradiation in presense of hydrogen peroxide does not degrade disulfoton effectively. Only 14 percent of disulfoton is degraded by an ultraviolet irradiation dose 200 mWsec/cm2 and hydrogen peroxide dose of 25 mg/l.
100% degradation can be achieved in 30 minutes at very high doses (340 mg/L) of hydrogen peroxide in acidic medium (pH = 2.8). These conditions are not typical of drinking water treatment.

Contaminant Name: Diuron | Description: Diuron, a substituted phenylurea herbicide belonging to the family of phenylamides, is registered for pre- and early post emergent control of broad leaf and annual grassy weeds for agricultural and nonagricultural uses [791]. 
Concerns regarding diuron have been frequently reported worldwide. For example, in France, diuron was found to be one of the most used products in urbanized catchments and is also commonly found as a contaminant. In Germany, recurring detection of diuron residues in drinking water supplies has led to prohibtion of diuron usage in confined areas where excess water can flow into drainnage pipes and sewer. Around the Unites States, diuron is notably used as an algicide and herbicide. In California, diuron is one of the most heavily used hebicides with over 1.4 million pounds applied statewide in 2004; almost 50% of the total usage was applied to rights-of-way, e.g., along power lines and roadways. Nearly 95% of diuron in California is applied from November through March, with a peak in December. Nearly all detections of diuron especially those of high concentrations, ocurrred between December and March. The concentration observed in surface and ground water supplies in California were usually above 1 μg/l, which is the concentration of acute toxicity for algae, and some observations exceeded 10 μg/l [791]. 
Diuron is classified as a "known/likely" human carcinogen and is on the USEPA's "3rd Contaminant Candidate List" [620]. Diuron is considering as a highly toxic, persistent priority substance by the European Union (EU) and has a half-life of 300 days when applied to the soil [782].
Date of Literature Search: June 2009
