Contaminant Name: Diuron | Function: Chlorine | Details: From one reference, chlorine was very effective in degrading diuron for the conditions evaluated (0.6 chlorine/TOC, pH 8, contact time not given).

Contaminant Name: Diuron | Function: Conventional Treatment | Details: From one reference, conventional treatment of diuron using coagulation-flocculation was not very effective for the conditions evaluated.
The degradation of diuron is more effective using conventional treatment when combined with pre-oxidation using ozone or chlorine.
Complete degradation was achieved in one reference when coagulation-flocculation was combined with preoxidation with ozone/chlorine and activated carbon adsorption.
Contaminant Name: Diuron | Function: Hydrogen Peroxide | Details: From one reference, 91 percent of diuron was oxidized by hydrogen peroxide in the presence of ferrous ions using fenton's reaction.

Contaminant Name: Diuron | Function: Membrane Filtration | Details: Almost 86 percent of diuron (1 mg/L) removed  by ultrafiltration membranes. The percent removals was lower (11 to 30 percent removal) when these membranes encountered higher concentrations (12 mg/L) of diuron.
Increase in transmembrane pressure reduces the percentage removal. The percent removal also decreases with increasing molecular weight cut-off (MWCO).

Contaminant Name: Diuron | Function: Membrane Separation | Details: Membrane separation using nanofiltration is suitable process for the removal of diuron from different types of water. The choice of membrane is crucial. The membrane material influences the percentage of diuron removal [786].  Removals ranged 2 to 92 percent.
Increase in transmembrane pressure increases the percentage removal. The percent removal also decreases with increasing molecular weight cut-off (MWCO).
Nanofiltration was more effective in removing diuron from ground water than lab water. The presense of organics in the water reduced the diuron removal.

Contaminant Name: Diuron | Overall: The following processes are considered effective for the removal of diuron: chlorine (up to 100 percent), hydrogen peroxide (up to 90 percent), membrane filtration (up to 86 percent), membrane separation (up to 92 percent), ozone (70 to 100 percent), powdered activated carbon (75 to 100 percent), ultraviolet irradiation (up to 99 percent), and ultraviolet irradiation hydrogen peroxide (up to 100 percent). Performance of these technologies can vary based on water quality and treatment characteristics.
Conventional treatment was not effective by itself at 30 removal.   
Research was identified evaluating the following treatment technologies for the removal of diuron:

Contaminant Name: Diuron | Function: Ozone | Details: Ozonation was an effective process to degrade diuron from the conditions evaluated. Significant removal percentages  (73 to 100) were seen in both lab water and surface water. One study showed that doubled ozone dosage (more than required for disinfection) significantly reduced  the diuron concentration without significant bromate ion formation [1458]. 
One study showed that the effect of ozone on diuron removal can be enhanced when combined with activated carbon adsorption.

Contaminant Name: Diuron | Function: Powdered Activated Carbon | Details: From one study, powdered activated carbon (PAC) alone was 65 percent effective in diuron removal for the conditions evaluated (pH 8, 10 mg/L PAC, 5 min, surface water). The effectiveness of PAC was increased with it was combined with preoxidation using ozone/chlorine. Complete removal of diuron was seen when PAC adsorption was combined with preoxidation with chlorine/ozone and conventional treatment.

Contaminant Name: Diuron | Function: Ultraviolet Irradiation | Details: From one reference, 99 percent of diuron was degraded by monochromatic ultraviolet radiation (UV) in one hour. Increase in pH did not have any effect on degradation for the conditions evaluated.

Contaminant Name: Diuron | Function: Ultraviolet Irradiation and Hydrogen Peroxide | Details: Ultraviolet Irradiation  and Hydrogen Peroxide were  effective in removal of diuron from different types of water for the conditions examined.  Increase in hydrogen peroxide dosage increased the removal percentage.
Ferrous ions also act as a catalyst to the Ultraviolet Irradiation  and Hydrogen Peroxide reaction to increase diuron removal through the photo-Fenton reaction. At a constant diuron concentration, percent diuron removal increased with increasing hydrogen peroxide and ferrous ions concentrations up to a certain level above which diuron removal decreased due to scavenging effects of hydrogen peroxide on hydroxl radicals [782].

Contaminant Name: E. coli O157 | Description: Escherichia coli (E. coli) O157:H7, a serotype of the bacterium E. coli, can produce Shiga-like toxins which cause severe illnesses in humans [855]. E. coli is found in feces; illnesses may be associated with exposure to contaminated water supplies [857].
E. coli inhabits the intestines of animals and humans. When cultured aerobically, E. coli is the dominant species found in feces. In 1995, more than 60 E. coli serotypes had been identified that produced Shiga-like toxins; the serotype O157:H7 was the most predominant and most frequently associated with human infections worldwide [856]. E. coli O157:H7 has a low infectious dose; studies have estimated exposure between 20 and 700 organisms can cause illness.
The majority of outbreaks have been linked with the consumption of contaminated food. However, water-related exposures have been reported from recreational uses of surface waters and consumption of water from private drinking wells [857]. Drinking water sources may become contaminated with E coli O157:H7 when human or animal feces containing the bacteria are introduced into drinking water supplies via sewage overflows or agricultural runoff.
The survival of E. coli O157:H7 in surface waters depends on a number of biological, physical, and chemical factors. These include but are not limited to nutrient availability, ultraviolet (UV) radiation, temperature, and predation. E. coli O157:H7 were reported to survive for two months in samples collected from a lake, a fecal contaminated puddle, a river, and an animal trough [857]. In comparison, another study reported detections of E. coli O157:H7 after 17 days within a sterile distilled municipal water sample at ambient conditions. The same municipal source water maintained in the lab at 15°C had a positive detection for E. coli O157:H7 after 31 days [858].
Shiga toxins cause severe illness by affecting the lining of the intestine [855]. Once exposed, incubation periods range between one and eight days [855].
Public water systems are required to test their drinking water supplies for total coliforms (indicator bacteria for E. coli) under the USEPA Total Coliform Rule. If a water analysis yields positive results for total coliforms, the public water system must resample for either fecal coliforms or E. coli. Currently, there is not a maximum contaminant level (MCL) for E. coli or for the specific serotype E. coli O157:H7. However, E.coli O157 was included on the  USEPA Contaminant Contaminant List 3 (CCL3) published on September 21, 2009.  The CCL3 includes contaminants that are currently unregulated, are known or anticipated to occur in public water supplies, and which may require regulation under the Safe Drinking Water Act [859].
The largest waterborne outbreak of E. coli O157:H7 was documented in 1989 in Missouri, where more than 200 people became infected and four died. The pathway of exposure could not be identified, but potential explanations include contamination by backflow during a water main break [856].
Date of Literature Search: August 2009

Contaminant Name: E. coli O157 | Function: Chlorine | Details: E. coli O157:H7 inactivation with chlorine is dependent on temperature, pH, chlorine dose, and contact time. However, all experiments indicated that chlorine contact times commonly used in drinking water treatment inactivate E. coli O157:H7 to safe levels. For example, 1 to 4 log destruction was observed when chlorine residual levels ranged from 0.25 to 2 mg/L and contact times ranged from 0.5 to 5 minutes.

Contaminant Name: E. coli O157 | Function: Conventional Treatment | Details: Despite a comprehensive literature search, removal data for E. coli O157:H7 at the full-scale level for conventional treatment was not found.
Removal of E. coli O157:H7 by simulated conventional treatment (coagulation, clarification and filtration) was found to be minimally effective. An analysis of 46 pilot-scale effluent samples generated under different treatment conditions demonstrated that E. coli O157:H7 had the lowest mean log removal, 0.7, compared to four other waterborne microbes (Aeromonas hydrophila, MS2, Cryptosporidium parvum, Encephalitozoan intestinalis) [854]. In the same study, breakthrough for E. coli O157:H7 was observed within one hour of filter run time [853, 854].
Poor filterability of E. coli O157:H7 may be explained by hydrodynamic theory, which predict that a particle size of 1 μm will be the most difficult to remove in granular media filtration. However, pathogens associated with floc particles will be better removed based on altered size and surface charge. Achieving a turbidity target of 0.2 NTU or less has been shown to improve pathogen removal [854].

Contaminant Name: E. coli O157 | Overall: Effective processes for inactivating E. coli O157: H7 include chlorine and ultraviolet irradiation. Performance of these technologies varied based on dose, contact time, and water quality characteristics. Conventional treatment, on the other hand, has been shown to be relatively ineffective at controlling E. coli O157:H7.
Research was identified evaluating the following treatment technologies for the removal or inactivation of E. coli O157:H7:

Contaminant Name: E. coli O157 | Function: Ultraviolet Irradiation | Details: Ultraviolet (UV) irradiation can be very effective for inactivating E. coli O157:H7. Relatively low doses of UV (5 to 19 mWsec/cm2) have been shown to inactivate E. coli O157:H7, resulting in 2 to 4 log inactivation.

Contaminant Name: Fenamiphos | Description: Fenamiphos is an organophosphorous pesticide widely used for control of nematodes and thrips. [1509] Its chemical name is ethyl 3-methyl-4-(methylthio)phenyl-(1-methylethyl) phosphoramidate, with the trade name of Nemacur. Fenamiphos was formulated as Nemacur, a product that was intended to be further formulated into a variety of end-use products. [1509] Fenamiphos has been applied on agricultural crops, including citrus, grapes, peanuts, pineapples and tobacco, and non-agricultural sites, such as turf and ornamentals. [1509] Approximately 780,000 pounds of fenamiphos active ingredient was used annually in 2002. [1509]
 
Fenamiphos was first registered in the United States in 1972.  Due to its effects on extremely vulnerable soils and shallow ground water (less than 50 feet below ground surface), all registrations for the products containing the active ingredient fenamiphos were cancelled and the manufacture and use of fenamiphos was phased out by the end of 2008. [1502, 1510] Extremely vulnerable soils refer to USDA hydrologic soil group A, soils that are excessively drained and are predominately sand or loamy sand. [1509] Fenamiphos is not regulated under National Primary Drinking Water Regulations.
 
Fenamiphos can overstimulate the nervous system causing nausea, dizziness and confusion. [1509] Very high exposures of fenamiphos can cause respiratory paralysis and death. [1509] Fenamiphos residues in food do not pose risk concerns for people in the United States. [1509] Drinking water exposure is generally not a concern for surface and ground water sources associated with soils that are not extremely vulnerable. [1509] However, shallow ground water sources associated with extremely vulnerable soils would pose risk concerns if used as drinking water sources. [1509]
 
Fenamiphos has two primary degradates, fenamiphos sulfoxide and fenamiphos sulfone, which are also considered toxic and used in calculating risk to drinking water [1509]. In soil, fenamiphos is subject to photodegradation by natural light on the soil surface, with a half-life of 3.23 hours. [1509] The half-life of aerobic soil metabolism is short, approximately 15.7 days. [1509] While, the anaerobic soil metabolism half-life is long, approximately 87.9 days, which indicated fenamiphos will persist after reaching ground water. [1509]
 
Fenamiphos and its degradates are mobile and can leach to groundwater and migrate to surface water. [1509] Laboratory studies indicate sulfoxide and sulfone degradates are more mobile than the parent fenamiphos. [1509] Fenamiphos is relatively resistant to hydrolysis in sterile buffered water; half-lives were calculated as 245 days at pH 5.0, 301 days at pH 7.0 and 235 days at pH 9.0. [1506]
 
Fenamiphos and its degradates were detected in groundwater in Florida and California, the two major fenamiphos use states. [1509] Fenamiphos was detected in nine out of sixteen wells close to citrus use sites monitored in Florida during 1995-1996 at concentrations ranging from 0.10 to 0.58 µg/L. [1509] Another Florida golf course study detected fenamiphos at eight out of forty one courses at concentrations in the range of 0.03 to 0.71 µg/L. [1509] A California monitoring program detected fenamiphos at five out of sixteen wells during 1997- 2000. [1509] Surface water monitoring data for fenamiphos and its degradates are limited. [1509] One monitoring program sponsored by USEPA and US Geological Survey detected fenamiphos degradates at three out of twelve reservoirs at concentrations ranging from 0.005 to 0.033 µg/L, and in the treated drinking water at concentrations ranging from 0.007 to 0.022 µg/L. [1509]

Contaminant Name: Fenamiphos | Function: Diatomaceous Earth Filtration | Details: One study showed effective removal of fenamiphos by diatomaceous earth [1508]. Fenamiphos removal ranged from 64 percent to 85 percent, depending on pH values. Temperature did not affect the removal efficiency in the range of 4 to 40 °C, according to the study; however, no specific data were reported.

Contaminant Name: Fenamiphos | Function: Membrane Separation | Details: In three EPA Environmental Technology Verification studies, control of fenamiphos by reverse osmosis POU units excedded 99 percent.

Contaminant Name: Fluoride | Description: Fluoride is a naturally occurring element found in the earth’s crust.  Fluoride compounds are also utilized in industry applications for the production of semiconductors, fertilizers, high purity graphite, and nuclear applications [1892].  
Low levels of fluoride occur naturally in most sources of drinking water.  Fluoride can occur naturally in surface waters from the deposition of particles in the atmosphere and weathering of fluoride containing rocks and soils. Fluoride in groundwater occurs from leaching from rock formations.  Fluoride is also introduced in water by various human activities such as chemical manufacturing plants and production of fluoridated chemicals [1956][1960]. 
In drinking water treatment, fluoride may be applied at low levels to aid in dental and skeletal health.  The target range of fluoride in drinking water is 0.7 to 1.2 mg/L [1957][1961]. 
The US EPA has established a maximum contaminant level (MCL) of 4 ppm as long-term exposure to elevated levels of fluoride may lead to bone disease or dental diseae, and a secondary MCL of 2 ppm based on tooth discoloration and pitting. 
In January 2011 the USEPA stated its intent to review the MCL to determine whether revisions are appropriate.  
The US EPA has identified the following treatment processes as Best Available Technologies (BATs) for control of fluoride in drinking water: reverse osmosis and activated alumina [1957].  Because of the high effectiveness of the BATs, treatment would not be a limiting factor if a lower MCL was set [2009].   
Other treatment technologies available for fluoride removal include: ion exchange, lime softening, electrodialysis, and adsorption with various types of media including activated carbon, bone chars and clays [1895][1957][1958].  
In some regions of the world, fluoride concentrations up to 35 mg/L have been detected in groundwater; the fluoride concentration in surface waters is typically less than 0.3 mg/L [1857]. In general, groundwater with high fluoride content is soft, has high pH and has a high concentration of silica; however, the fluoride concentration can vary depending on the geological, chemical, and physical characteristics of the aquifer, the porosity and acidity of the soils and rock, temperature, and well depth [1857].

Contaminant Name: Fluoride | Function: Adsorptive Media | Details: Removal of fluoride in water by adsorptive media can be very effective (up tp 100% removal) depending on the type of media used and is the most common defluoridation method used.
Adsorptive media available for removal of fluoride in water includes activated alumina (AA), which is designated best available technology (BAT) for fluoride control.  17 studies with AA using laboratory and ground waters exhibited upper-range removals from 68 to 100% and averaging 92%.
There were a significant number of bench-scale studies for other adsorbents exhibiting high upper-range percent removals, including: modified aluminum media 93%, zirconium-based media 69%, iron-based media 99%, tricalcium phosphate 97%, bone char 100%, clay 97%, laterite 100% and magnesite 98%.
Bone char has been successfully used at several full scale defluoridation plants from the 1930s through the 1970s.  Tricalcium phosphate has also been used at several full scale defluoridation plants with limited success primarilly due to high attrition losses, and being less effective and requiring more frequent regeneration than bone char.  Activated alumina has also been successfully used in a full scale defluoridation plant in Bartlett, Texas from 1952 through 1977.  The activated alumina at this plant was characterized by a high fluoride adsorption capacity.   
The adsorption process is typically highly pH dependent and generally is most effective at a slightly acidic pH. The fluoride removal capacity generally increases directly with fluoride concentration and inversely with pH of the water. Removal of fluoride is also highly dependent on the amount of adsorptive media used and on the contact time between provided between fluoride and the adsorptive media.

Contaminant Name: Fluoride | Function: Biological Treatment | Details: Batch studies demonstrated that algal biosorbents were somewhat effective for the removal of fluoride from the aqueous phase, dependent on the species (up to 60 percent for Spirogyra IO2; up to 70 percent effective for Spirogyra IO1), and not effective for others (less than 10 percent effective for Anabaena fertilissima and Chlorococcum humicola, both pretreated with calcium). Removal was shown to be affected by the initial fluoride concentration, pH, adsorbent concentration, and temperature. Greater fluoride removal was observed with: lower initial fluoride concentration, lower pH, and greater adsorbent concentration. The maximum fluoride sorption was reported at 30 degrees C.

Contaminant Name: Fluoride | Function: Chemical Treatment | Details: Removal of fluoride in water by the Nalgonda technique can be effective (52-86% removal) where the effectiveness is primarily dependent on fluoride concentration, pH, and alum/lime dosages. The Nalgonda technique involves mixing of alum and lime solution with the raw water in a two-step process to remove fluoride. High alum dosages are typically required to reduce fluoride levels enough for drinking water applications and can therefore result in exceedances of safe levels of aluminum and sulfate in the treated water. This process also generates a significant quantity of sludge.
Removal of fluoride in water by various calcium phosphates was found to be effective for one study (>90% removal). The effectiveness of fluoride removal was primarily dependent on pH, amount of fluoride in the raw water and amount of calcium phosphates added. One study also reported the removal of fluoride (>86 percent) with magnesium oxide. The removal was independent of the initial fluoride concentration.
Removal of fluoride in water by alum alone was found to be marginally effective for several bench-scale studies (30% to >80% removal). However, the doses required for removal were higher than what is typically used in water treatment.

Contaminant Name: Fluoride | Function: Conventional Treatment | Details: Fluoride removal from groundwater by conventional treatment (i.e., alum coagulation, flocculation, sedimentation and filtration) was found to be moderately effective in one study (between 50% and 71% removal achieved) in both batch and pilot scale experiments.  Alum dosages used were higher than what is typically used in drinking water treatment.  Percent removals with and without granular activated carbon at the pilot scale were not significantly different.

Contaminant Name: Fluoride | Function: Ion Exchange | Details: Removal of fluoride from water can generally be effective (typically >85% removal) with ion exchange resins.  Upper-range removals of 75 to 100% represented the studies reported.  While most studies represented bench scale, one pilot-scale study reported 99% removal with an anion exchange resin.
Fluoride removal can occur with either cation and anion exchange resins.  In anion exchange resins, fluoride removal occurs when fluoride ions replace chloride ions of the resin. This process continues until all sites on the resin are occupied. The removal efficiency of ion exchange resins is dependent upon the resin type, regeneration frequency, initial fluoride concentration, and competing ions in the water. Strong base anion exchange resins in the chloride form are most consistent in the removal of fluoride. Regeneration of this type of resin is accomplished with a sodium chloride salt.

Contaminant Name: Fluoride | Function: Membrane Separation | Details: Membrane separation is effective for removing fluoride. Studies reported close to 95 percent removal with nanofiltration, reverse osmosis, and electrodialysis.  RO is designated best available technology (BAT) for control of fluoride and studied generally showed removals in the high 80s to 100 percent.
Removal is influenced by membrane porosity, initial fluoride concentration, feed water composition, flow rate, and the applied pressure. In membrane separation involving potential differentials (electrodialysis and Donnan dialysis), the fluoride separation is also affected by the volatage applied.  The electrodialysis studies reported greater removal with higher voltage and initial fluoride concentration.

Contaminant Name: Fluoride | Function: Other Treatment | Details: Removal of fluoride from water using the electrocoagulation process with aluminum electrodes was found to be very effective (>95% removal) without co-existing anions for one study. The types and concentrations of co-existing anions can have a significant impact on the defluoridation capacity of the electrocoagulation system. Another study found that the hydroxide and fluoride molar ratio had a significant impact on the defluoridation capacity of the electrocoagulation system. The study found that the efficiency of defluoridation was close to 100% when the sum of the hydroxide and fluoride molar ratios was close to 3.
Another batch study showed somewhat effective removal of fluoride with phytoremediation (up to 64 percent in 7 days) with Hydrilla verticillata. Removal was affected by the initial fluoride concentration and contact time.

Contaminant Name: Fluoride | Overall: The following processes were found to be effective for the removal of fluoride: Adsorptive media (up to 100 percent removal) including activated alumina (up to 100 percent removal) chemical treatment by the Nalgonda technique (> 86 percent removal), calcium phosphates (>90 percent removal), magnesium oxide (>86 percent removal), or alum (>90 percent) ion exchange with anion exchange resins (up to 85 percent removal) membrane separation with reverse osmosis, nanofiltration, and electrodialysis (> 95 percent removal) and an electrocoagulation process (> 95 percent removal).  
The US EPA has identified reverse osmosis and activated alumina as Best Available Technologies (BATs) for control of fluoride in drinking water 1957.  
Adsorptive media is the most common treatment for fluoride removal.  Removal is affected by water quality parameters and the type of media used, including: activated alumina, activated carbon, bone char, laterite, kaolonite, or calcite.  Parameters that affect removal include pH, fluoride concentration, and contact time, and competing ions. The removal is most effective at slightly acidic pH.  
Fluoride removal with precipitative softening, conventional treatment, biological treatment and phytoremediation was found to be marginally effective. 
Research was identified evaluating the following treatment technologies for the removal of fluoride:

Contaminant Name: Fluoride | Function: Powdered Activated Carbon | Details: Removal of fluoride with powdered activated carbon (PAC) was found to be very effective in one study (up to 100% removal achieved).  However, the process is pH dependent with effective removals obtainable only at a pH of 3.0 or less.  At a pH of 8.0, very little fluoride removal is achieved (

Contaminant Name: Fluoride | Function: Precipitative Softening | Details: Removal of fluoride through lime softening has been found to be marginally effective (0 - 80% removal) in both bench and full scale applications but is highly dependent on the amount of magnesium removed during the softening process.  If sufficient amounts of magnesium are not present in water, a magnesium salt would need to be added to provide the desired level of fluoride removal.  This method may be adaptable to low-fluoride-high-magnesium waters requiring softening.  The effectiveness of lime softening on fluoride removal is highly dependent on source water quality conditions.

Contaminant Name: Glyphosate | Description: Glyphosate is a broad-spectrum, systemic herbicide used to control a wide variety of annual and perennial plants including grasses, sedges, broad-leaved weeds, and woody plants [804]. This non-selective herbicide is also used on a number of crops [802, 804]. It is one of the most widely used pesticides in the world [805].  It is widely used on residential weeds as Roundup.
Although glyphosate is an acid, it is often produced in salt form, most commonly the isopropylamine salt. It is also available in a trimethylsulfonium salt form. Glyphosate is generally applied as water-soluble concentrate or powder [804].
Glyphosate is regulated by the Safe Drinking Water Act (SDWA) amendment of 1986. Although glyphosate may be controlled by oxidation, it's "best available technology" for treatment is GAC adsorption. The maximum contaminant limit is 0.7 mg/L [801].
In the past, research on the chemical had shown a low mammalian toxicity, leading to widespread usage of the compound. Recently, however, some researchers have identified secondary effects in animals such as reproductive dysfunction [805].
Studies have shown glyphosate to be present at low levels in some natural waters. A sampling survey conducted in the Netherlands found surface water containing 0.5 - 1.0 μg/L of glyphosate. Another study conducted in Canada found glyphosate residues as high as 5153 μg/L, measured after direct application over lakes, ponds, and streams [800].
Date of Literature Search: June 2009

Contaminant Name: Glyphosate | Function: Chemical Treatment | Details: Data from one bench-scale study indicate that 95 percent of glyphosate was removed by titanium dioxide packed in a spiral glass tube reactor.

Contaminant Name: Glyphosate | Function: Chlorine | Details: Oxidation of glyphosate with chlorine was found to be very effective. Pilot-scale and bench-scale studies reported removal of greater than 99 percent of the herbicide under conditions typically found during drinking water treatment.

Contaminant Name: Glyphosate | Function: Chlorine Dioxide | Details: A bench-scale study evaluating the ability of chlorine dioxide to remove glyphosate in deionized water found that this form of oxidation was generally not successful (20 percent) in removing the contaminant. Additionally, a pilot-scale study achieved 56 percent removal of glyphosate when treated with chlorine dioxide in combination with conventional treatment.

Contaminant Name: Glyphosate | Function: Conventional Treatment | Details: Data from one pilot study showed that alum coagulation, sedimentation, and sand filtration were not effective in the removal of glyphosate from surface water (7% removal). Another pilot-scale study achieved 56 percent removal of glyphosate when treated with chlorine dioxide in combination with conventional treatment.

Contaminant Name: Glyphosate | Function: GAC Isotherm | Details: A study evaluating the adsorption capacity of GAC in distilled water and river water found that the Freundlich K values varied significantly depending on the source water and pH value.  GAC is the Best Available Technology for glyphosate control. 
The greatest adsorption capacity was achieved with spiked lab water in slightly acidic conditions. The lowest adsorption capacity, on the other hand, was found with surface water in slightly acidic conditions. Interestingly, spiked lab water investigated at a basic pH of 10 achieved better adsorption than the surface water, but much less adsorption than the slightly acidic lab water solution. This suggests adsorption capacity is a function of pH, but is also dependent on source water characteristics.

Contaminant Name: Glyphosate | Function: Hydrogen Peroxide | Details: Data from one bench-scale study of surface water contaminated with glyphosate found that the addition of 1 mg/L hydrogen peroxide achieved no removal during a 15 minute time period.

Contaminant Name: Glyphosate | Function: Membrane Filtration | Details: Data from one study are suggestive that the ability to remove glyphosate using membrane filtration is dependent upon the membrane’s molecular weight cut-off (MWCO). The study found that 50% and > 99% removal of glyphosate from contaminated river water could be achieved with 1000 and 500 dalton MWCO membranes, respectively.
Furthermore, no removal was achieved from river water using 10,000 dalton MWCO ultrafiltration membranes or 0.45 μm microfiltration filters.

Contaminant Name: Glyphosate | Overall: The following processes were found to be effective for the removal of glyphosate: chemical treatment with TiO2 (95 percent removal), chlorine (> 99 percent), granular activated carbon, membrane filtration (up to 99 percent), ozone (60 to > 99 percent removal), ultraviolet irradiation with TiO2 (10 to > 99 percent), and ultraviolet irradiation hydrogen peroxide with TiO2 (42 to 60 percent removal).
Studies indicate that the removal achieved with oxidation using chlorine and ozone is important for glyphosate removal.  Permanganate at 1 mg/L for only 15 minutes resulted in 74 percent removal.  The Best Available Technology is GAC adsorption.
Processes ineffective for the removal of glyphosate or not typical to drinking water treatment were chlorine dioxide (20 percent removal), conventional treatment (6 percent), and hydrogen peroxide (no removal).
Research was identified evaluating the following treatment technologies for the removal of glyphosate:

Contaminant Name: Glyphosate | Function: Ozone | Details: Ozone was found to be quite effective in oxidizing the contaminant in pilot-scale work. A dose of 1 mg/L ozone achieved 60% removal, while increasing the dose to 2.9 mg/L achieved > 99% removal. Contact times for the study were just over 7 minutes. One study found complete removal of glyphosate at applied ozone dose of 1.9 mg/L with 7 minute contact time.

Contaminant Name: Glyphosate | Function: Permanganate | Details: Data from one study are suggestive that permanganate is effective in the removal of glyphosate. A bench-scale study found that 74% removal was achieved with 1 mg/L permanganate added to contaminated surface water.

Contaminant Name: Glyphosate | Function: Ultraviolet Irradiation | Details: Two bench-scale studies evaluating the ability of ultraviolet irradiation to degrade high concentrations of glyphosate in the presence of the catalyst TiO2 found this treatment to be effective. Depending on several factors, including pH, contact time, lamp type, and catalyst dose, 10 to greater than 99 percent removal was achieved.

Contaminant Name: Glyphosate | Function: Ultraviolet Irradiation and Hydrogen Peroxide | Details: Results from a bench-scale study evaluating the ability of ultraviolet irradiation to degrade high concentrations of glyphosate in the presence of hydrogen peroxide and TiO2 found this treatment to be effective. The effect of hydrogen peroxide dose on contaminant degradation was investigated, and lower H2O2 doses were found to achieve better removal. In this case, 42 and 60 percent removal was achieved with doses of 340 and 3.4 mg/L H2O2, respectively.

Contaminant Name: Ibuprofen | Description: Ibuprofen is a common nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drug widely used in the treatment of pain and fever. Its IUPAC name is 2-[4-(2-methylpropyl)phenyl]propanoic acid. It is marketed under the trade names of Advil and Motrin, among others. It is one of many chemicals included in a general group referred to as “pharmaceuticals and personal care products (PPCPs)”. Concern has stemmed from the low-level detection of PPCPs in surface waters and drinking water sources worldwide, though no adverse human health effects at these low levels are known. [1468]
It is not regulated in drinking water.
Ibuprofen is a chiral, propionic acid derivative which exhibits analgesic, fever-reducing, and anti-inflammatory action comparable to, and even surpassing that of aspirin and acetaminophen. [703] Ibuprofen is an important nonprescription drug, and is the third-most popular drug in the world. [700]
Although a significant portion of ibuprofen is degraded during wastewater treatment, the primary route with which ibuprofen enters surface waters is through wastewater treatment plant effluent. [699] It has a relatively high therapeutic dose of 600 to 1200 mg/d, 70-80% of which is excreted as the parent compound, or in the form of metabolites. [700] Incompletely absorbed medication is excreted into the sewage system along with unused drugs that may be disposed of via drains and toilets. [699] Other potential sources to the environment include leachate from landfill sites, run-off from agricultural land to which biosolids have been applied, and wastewater discharges from hospital wastes and industrial manufacturing. [697, 699]
Ibuprofen’s physiochemical properties (i.e. high water solubility, low volatility) suggest a high mobility in the aquatic environment, and consequently, it is a commonly detected PPCP in the environment [700, 1466, 1471] However, it is not very persistent and behaves differently in comparison to some other pharmaceutical compounds. [700]
Although the effects on humans from low-level drinking water contamination by PPCPs are not currently fully understood, pharmaceuticals are designed and created to have biological effects at low doses. [730, 1471, 1472] There is growing concern on the occurrence, fate, and possible effects of such substances in the environment. [700]
Date of Literature Search: July 2009

Contaminant Name: Ibuprofen | Function: Biological Filtration | Details: A pilot study evaluating the ability of a biologically active GAC filter unit found that this treatment was successful in removing low levels of ibuprofen in surface water to below the limits of quantification (

Contaminant Name: Ibuprofen | Function: Biological Treatment | Details: Removal of ibuprofen by biological treatment was found to be quite effective. One full-scale study reported removal of almost 99 percent by an activated sludge process. A bench-scale study performed with two different spiked surface waters and biofilms grown in rotating annular reactors each resulted in 97 percent removal.
July 2009

Contaminant Name: Ibuprofen | Function: Chemical Treatment | Details: A bench-scale study evaluating the effectiveness of coagulation followed by settling found a removal of less than 20 percent for ibuprofen, considered statistically insignificant by the author.
July 2009

Contaminant Name: Ibuprofen | Function: Chlorine | Details: A full-scale study found that chlorine is ineffective in removing ibuprofen (6 percent removal) because the reaction is too slow under typical pH and chlorination conditions. However, data from two bench-scale studies found mixed results, as ibuprofen was removed from 0 to 79 percent. It should be noted that the 79 percent removal was achieved for only one of the four source waters examined in this particular study.

Contaminant Name: Ibuprofen | Function: Chlorine Dioxide | Details: Chlorine dioxide is ineffective for the removal of ibuprofen. Based on data from one bench-scale study employing chlorine dioxide doses of 0.95 and 11.5 mg/L, removals of 6 and 0 percent were achieved, respectively.
July 2009

Contaminant Name: Ibuprofen | Function: Conventional Treatment | Details: Conventional treatment was found to be ineffective for removing ibuprofen in a pilot study, with removal rates ranging from 9 to 25 percent. A full scale plant achieved removals of 56 to greater than 94 percent, although the majority of this removal (33 and greater than 88 percent, respectively) was achieved in the GAC filtration step of the treatment process.
July 2009

Contaminant Name: Ibuprofen | Function: Direct Filtration | Details: Data from one full-scale study showed a range of effectiveness of direct sand filtration for the removal of ibuprofen. Up to 96 percent elimination was achieved with high variability in results. Ibuprofen is not very hydrophobic, suggesting that it would not be well-removed by direct filtration.
July 2009

Contaminant Name: Ibuprofen | Function: Hydrogen Peroxide | Details: Results from a bench scale study showed that the addition of hydrogen peroxide, in combination with the application of ultrasonic waves, removed 95 percent of the initial ibuprofen concentration in deionized water.
July 2009

Contaminant Name: Ibuprofen | Function: Membrane Separation | Details: Data suggest that membrane separation is very effective in eliminating ibuprofen from water sources. A bench-scale study evaluating five types of NF and RO membranes under various hydrodynamic ratios and organic matter concentrations found ibuprofen removal rates ranging from 92 to 99 percent for four of the five membranes.
The other membrane, achieving 22 to 34 percent removal, had the highest molecular weight cutoff (MWCO) of any of the membranes at 400 Dalton. Poor removal by this particular membrane could possibly be attributed to the relatively low molecular weight of ibuprofen at 206 Dalton.
July 2009

Contaminant Name: Ibuprofen | Function: Other Treatment | Details: A bench-scale study evaluating the ability of ultrasonic waves to remove ibuprofen in deionized water found that this technology was generally successful, removing 35 to 95 percent ibuprofen. Removal increased as ultrasonic power outputs increased and as pH decreased.
July 2009

Contaminant Name: Ibuprofen | Overall: The following processes were found to be effective for the removal of ibuprofen: biological filtration (>75 percent), direct filtration (to 96 percent), membrane separation (to 99 percent), ozone (to 92 percent), powdered activated carbon (to 80 percent), ultraviolet irradiation (62 to 67 percent), chlorine (to 79 percent), and ozone hydrogen peroxide (to 99 percent removal).
Processes ineffective for the removal of ibuprofen included chemical treatment (
Other treatment processes not typical to drinking water treatment are promising for ibuprofen removal, including biological treatment and the use of ultrasonic waves with and without hydrogen peroxide.
Research was identified evaluating the following treatment technologies for the removal of ibuprofen:

Contaminant Name: Ibuprofen | Function: Ozone | Details: Ozone varies in its effectiveness for the removal of ibuprofen in drinking water applications. One bench-scale study demonstrated a removal range from 40 to 77 percent conducted on various surface waters. Treatment efficiency was a function of organic carbon and alkalinity concentrations of the source waters.
Two other bench-scale studies varied greatly in their elimination results: one achieving 80 percent removal as an average of four spiked surface waters (2.5 to 4 mg/L ozone dose), and one achieving a 12 percent reduction in distilled water spiked with contaminant (1 mg/L ozone dose).
A full-scale study treating the effluent of a wastewater treatment plant also found variability in the effectiveness of ozone treatment (> 75 percent and 36 percent), although the concentrations of ibuprofen analyzed were in the sub-ng/L range, likely influencing the accuracy of the analysis. A pilot-scale study found results from greater than 89 to greater than 92 percent removal of ibuprofen in treating contaminated surface waters.
July 2009

Contaminant Name: Ibuprofen | Function: Ozone and Hydrogen Peroxide | Details: The use of ozone in combination with hydrogen peroxide resulted in mixed treatment efficiencies, depending on the dose of ozone and H2O2 employed. One bench-scale study conducted on surface and ground water achieved 78 to 90 percent removal with an ozone dose of 2 mg/L and an H2O2 dose of 0.7 mg/L.
Another bench-scale study found removals of 50 and 30 percent with spiked lab and surface water, respectively, with an ozone dose of 1 mg/L and an H2O2 dose of 0.4 mg/L. However, when the dose of ozone and H2O2 were increased to 3.7 and 1.4 mg/L, 93 percent removal was achieved. Even greater removal of the contaminant, over 99 percent, was obtained with an ozone dose of 5.0 mg/L and a hydrogen peroxide dose of 1.8 mg/L.
July 2009

Contaminant Name: Ibuprofen | Function: Powdered Activated Carbon | Details: Removal of ibuprofen with PAC varied in its effectiveness as a function of PAC dosage, according to the results of one bench-scale study. A range of 2 to 80 percent removal was achieved with PAC doses of 1 to 20 mg/L. As PAC doses increased, contaminant removal increased as well.
July 2009

Contaminant Name: Ibuprofen | Function: Ultraviolet Irradiation | Details: One study of ultraviolet (UV) irradiation observed a range of 62 to 67 percent photodegradation in various source waters spiked with high ibuprofen concentrations (10 to 40 mg/L). This study was designed to simulate solar irradiation. Increased removal could be possible with more intense irradiation.
July 2009

Contaminant Name: Lithium | Description: Lithium is a soft, silvery-white alkali metal element found in igneous rocks. 
Lithium occurs naturally in some groundwater, in varying concentrations throughout the world, as a result of interactions between water and minerals. Lithium concentrations in the surface and underground waters may be higher than general environment in places where lithium-rich brines and minerals occur, and in places where lithium batteries are disposed of [2814].

Lithium is used in a variety of industrial processes and products such as in rechargeable batteries for electronics and non-rechargeable batteries for things like heart pacemakers, toys and clocks. Lithium metal is made into alloys with aluminum and magnesium, improving their strength and making them lighter. Lithium oxide is used in special glasses and glass ceramics. Lithium chloride is one of the most hygroscopic materials known, and is used in air conditioning and industrial drying systems (as is lithium bromide). Lithium hydride is used as a means of storing hydrogen for use as a fuel. Lithium stearate is used as an all-purpose and high-temperature lubricant. Lithium carbonate is used in drugs to treat manic depression, although its action on the brain is still not fully understood [2813]. 
 A review published in 2008 indicated that lithium is not expected to bioaccumulate and its human and environmental toxicity are low [2814]. To date, there is insufficient exposure and treatment data for lithium in drinking water. EPA included lithium on the Contaminant Candidate List 5 (CCL5) as a contaminant of potential concern. Lithium is part of the current fifth Unregulated Contaminant Monitoring Rule (UCMR5) sampling. EPA also calculated a non-regulatory Health-Based Screening Level (HBSL) of 10 micrograms per liter (ug/L) or parts per billion (ppb) for drinking water [2812, 2813]. 
 Date of Literature Search: April 2022

Contaminant Name: Lithium | Function: Adsorptive Media | Details: Bench-scale studies suggest that removal of lithium from water by adsorptive media can be effective depending on the type of media. One study [2736] using an iron-based media achieved a maximum of greater than 99 percent removal of lithium. Two studies evaluated the effectiveness of manganese dioxide-based medias, showing up to 90 [2739] and greater than 90 [2740] percent removal. One study [2737] evaluated the feasibility of using fabricated titanate nanotubes to remove lithium and reported 63 to 80 percent removal. Another study [2741] testing the effectiveness of two zeolitic rocks for removal of lithium found clinoptilolite to be up to 67 percent effective and laumontite to be up to 33 percent effective.

A study [2742] using date pits as an adsorptive media displayed varying removal rates depending on the addition of an ionic liquid and cellulose. Results suggested that the vast majority of removal was accomplished by the addition of the ionic liquid and cellulose, as date pits without the addition achieved only 18 to 45 percent removal, whereas the same media with the addition of the ionic solution and cellulose achieved 55 to 90 percent removal.

Contaminant Name: Lithium | Function: GAC Isotherm | Details: Two studies assessing the effectiveness of adsorptive media also reported isotherm data for the removal of lithium. Freundlich K values ranged from 2.854 to 3.777 (mg/g) (L/mg) were reported in an isotherm study using manganese dioxide cages for the removal of lithium [2739]. Date pits with and without the addition of an ionic liquid and cellulose were also studied, with lower Freundlich K values being reported in pits containing the ionic liquid and cellulose (Freundlich K value ranged from 114.9 to 221.6 as opposed to 128.8 to 529.8) [2742].

Contaminant Name: Lithium | Function: Ion Exchange | Details: Bench- and pilot-scale studies suggest that removal of lithium from water using cation exchange media can be effective. Two bench-scale studies found maximum removals of 98 percent [2734] and 87.5 percent [2738]. Lithium removal in a pilot study [2735] was greater than 86 percent initially, declining with run time as the cation exchange media’s capacity was consumed. The authors concluded that the media’s exchange capacity could be completely restored by regeneration with sodium chloride.
Contaminant Name: Lithium | Function: Membrane Separation | Details: A pilot study [2735] reported 46 percent removal of lithium from groundwater using reverse osmosis.

Contaminant Name: Lithium | Overall: Bench- and pilot-scale data suggest ion exchange is effective for removal of lithium (86 to 98 percent) using regenerable cation exchange resin. Adsorption using certain novel media may also be effective, with iron-based media (greater than 99 percent removal at bench scale) and manganese dioxide-based media (90 percent removal at bench scale) showing the most promise.

Contaminant Name: Malathion | Description: Malathion is an organophosphorous insecticide [733] and has become one of the most widely used groups of pest control chemicals.  Its IUPAC name is dietyl (dimethoxyphospinothioylthio) succinate.
Malathion comes in two forms: a pure form of a colorless liquid and a technical grade solution (brownish-yellow liquid), which contains malathion (> 90%) and impurities in a solvent [738].  It is primarily applied by aerial or ground spraying, and fogging equipment, but is also used in bait-type formulations. The annual use of the malathion in 1994 was reported 3,377,681 pounds, of which 782,434 pounds  were used in the state of California [738].
Malathion is currently not regulated by the USEPA in drinking water.
Malathion interferes with the normal functions of the nervous system. Exposure to high amounts of malathion in the air, water, or food may cause difficulty breathing, chest tightness, vomiting, cramps, diarrhea, watery eyes, blurred vision, salivation, sweating, headaches, dizziness, loss of consciousness, and death. If persons who are exposed to these high amounts are rapidly given apporiatre treatment, there may be  no long-term harmful effects [738].
Malathion has been detected in three rivers, which are the three largest tributaries to the Chesapeake bay. Malathion was identified in 6-16 percent of the water samples collected from the Susquehanna, Potomac, and James rivers in 1992-1993; mean concentrations of malathion in the rivers were 6, 12, and 7 ng/L, respectively. Malathion was detected in the surface waters transecting the Central Valley to the Sierra Nevada mountains at concentrations of 65-83 ng/L [738].
Date of Literature Search: June 2009

Contaminant Name: Malathion | Function: Hydrogen Peroxide | Details: A combination of hydrogen peroxide and electrochemically-generated iron was found to be effecitve for the oxidation of malathion based on the results of one study. In that study, 60 to 80 percent removal was achieved in 2 hours.  Adding the hydrogen peroxide slowly over the course of the treatment rather than all at that beginning of each treatment greatly improved performance of the process (99 percent in 2 hours). [732]

Contaminant Name: Malathion | Function: Membrane Separation | Details: Reverse osmosis was found to be very effective for the removal of malathion based on the results of one study. More than 99 percent removal was achieved using conventional cellulose acetate and polyethylenimine membranes in bench-scale studies.

Contaminant Name: Malathion | Overall: The following processes are considered effective for the removal of malathion: membrane separation using reverse osmosis (> 99 percent), ozone (> 99 percent), ozone with hydrogen peroxide (> 99 percent), hydrogen peroxide (60 to 99 percent) and ultraviolet irradiation with hydrogen peroxide  (> 99 percent removal). Performance of these technologies can vary based on water quality and treatment characteristics.
Research was identified evaluating the following treatment technologies for the removal of malathion:

Contaminant Name: Malathion | Function: Ozone | Details: Ozone can be used effectively to degrade malathion based on the results of one study. Ozonation at a dose of 5 mg/L resulted in 99 percent removal of malathion after 10 minutes of contact time.

Contaminant Name: Malathion | Function: Ozone and Hydrogen Peroxide | Details: Ozone with hydrogen peroxide was highly effective in removing malathion. Greater than 99 percent removal of malathion was achieved in 10 minutes with 1 mg/L of ozone and 0.4 mg/L of hydrogen peroxide.

Contaminant Name: Malathion | Function: Ultraviolet Irradiation and Hydrogen Peroxide | Details: Ultraviolet Irradiation + Hydrogen Peroxide in the presence of Fe ions effectively degrades Malathion. Photo-fenton reaction is the mechanism of removal. Adding the hydrogen peroxide slowly over the course of the treatment rather than all at that beginning of each treatment greatly improves the system.

Contaminant Name: Mercury | Description: Mercury is a naturally occurring element found in air, water, and soil.  Mercury can exist in elemental, inorganic, or organic forms. The most common forms of mercury existing in the environment are metallic mercury, mercuric sulfide, mercuric chloride, and methylmercury.  Microorganisms (bacteria and fungi) and other natural processes can change mercury from one form to another [648].
In the past, mercury was used primarily as a fungicide, antibacterial agent, paint dye, and preservative.  It has since been replaced by safer and more effective agents.  Today, one-third to two-thirds of the mercury released is the result of human related activity.  Approximately 80 percent of mercury released by humans is from fossil fuel combustion, mining, and smelting. Released as elemental mercury, the compound combines with other inorganic compounds and settles on lands and in streams. Fifteen percent of human released mercury is released to the soil from fertilizers, fungicides, and municipal solids wastes, and the remaining 5 percent is released from industrial wastewater [648].
The consumption of contaminated fish is one of the bigger causes of mercury poisoning in addition to exposure in the workplace.  Permanent damage to the brain, kidneys, and nervous system often result from the ingestion of high mercury doses.  Organic mercury compounds are especially dangerous because they adsorb into the body much more readily than elemental or inorganic mercury.  Mercury poisoning in children or a developing fetus are more extreme; often affecting brain and nervous system development [648].
Inorganic mercury is regulated in drinking water in the US with a MCL of 0.002 mg/L. The United State Environmental Protection Agency has identified several treatment processes as Best Available Technologies (BATs) for control of mercury in drinking water. They are: conventional treatment, granular activated carbon, lime softening, and reverse osmosis [1267].
Because of human related activities, mercury can be detected in both groundwater and surface water supplies.  Treatment of mercury is often the result of human related releases (to air or soil) and not to naturally occurring mercury in the groundwater.
Date of Literature Search: June 2009

Contaminant Name: Mercury | Function: Adsorptive Media | Details: As indicated from batch isotherm tests, adsorption can be very effective (up to 99 percent removal) for mercury removal. Data for full-scale water treatment were not available.  Mercury removal by adsorptive media is largely dependent on the initial concentration, oxidation state, adsorbent type, and water chemistry (pH and competing anions).  Best removals were achieved in the pH 4 to 9 range depending on the media type. Iron-based medias containing sulfur were very effective at mercury removal (83 to greater than 99 percent). Bentonite clay and turbidity were also shown to remove mercury by adsorption.  In most of the tests, inorganic mercury was used, but sufficent data were not available to conclude anything about the differences between inorganic and organic removal by adsorption.

Contaminant Name: Mercury | Function: Aeration and Air Stripping | Details: In one study, the effect of aeration on mercury removal was studied.  The study indicated that aeration alone is not effective (0 percent removal) at removing Hg(2).  After the addition of a reducing agent (stannous chloride) converting Hg(2) to Hg(0), mercury removal was greater than 99 percent. Aeration, however, is not a BAT for mercury.  Air permitting may also be necessary when releasing mercury into the atmosphere.

Contaminant Name: Mercury | Function: Conventional Treatment | Details: Removal of mercury by conventional treatment processes can be very effective (up to 90 percent) under appropriate treatment conditions. All data collected were performed on the bench-scale level.  Conventional treatment has been identified by the EPA as a Best Available Technology (BAT) for mercury removal. Based on the findings of one study, alum and ferric coagulants performed similarly for mercury removal. The membrane filters used in the bench-scale experiments were also shown to remove mercury depending on the nature of the membrane.

Contaminant Name: Mercury | Function: Granular Activated Carbon | Details: Removal of mercury by granular activated carbon (50 to greater than 99 percent removal) was documented in sorption and bench-scale studies. In one study evaluating the effectiveness of different GAC media types, Westvaco's Nuchar SA appeared to perform the best over the widest range in pH (2.5 to 11). Nuchar SA also seemed to perform well with varying concentrations and competing ions, all of which affect mercury removal. In a few limited tests, organic mercury compounds were found to adsorb onto GAC better than inorganic mercury compounds.
In three EPA Environmental Technology Verification studies, removal of inorganic mercury by GAC in POU or POE units ranged 95 to 99 percent.  
GAC is a Best Available Technology for mercury control.

Contaminant Name: Mercury | Function: Ion Exchange | Details: In batch sorption studies, removal of mercury from water by ion exchange can be very effective (greater than 90 percent removal in most cases). Metal chelating ion exchange resins were used in most cases. Mercury removal is dependent upon the resin type, regeneration frequency, competing ions, mercury speciation, and initial concentration. No data were available on resin regeneration. Most studies were performed on inorganic mercury created in a laboratory.

Contaminant Name: Mercury | Function: Membrane Filtration | Details: Removal of mercury from water by membrane filtration (specifically ultrafiltration) can be very effective (15 percent to greater than 99 percent removal). Membrane filtration is not a a Best Available Technology for control of mercury; however, hollow fiber UF membranes appear to perform well in preliminary bench-scale testing. Microfiltration membranes used to filter laboratory samples containing mercury were also shown to remove mercury in small amounts (22 percent). In this test, the author indicated that the removal was likely due to the charges on the membrane.

Contaminant Name: Mercury | Function: Membrane Separation | Details: Although reverse osmosis is a Best Available Technology for inorganic mercury control, in four EPA Environmental Technology Verification studies, removal by reverse osmosis ranged only 9 to 44 percent in POU or POE units.

Contaminant Name: Mercury | Function: Other Treatment | Details: In one select study, off-gas produced by anaerobic organisms (hydrogen sulfide) was shown to reduce the concentration of mercury when it was bubbled through laboratory waters.  The removal mechanism was largely due to the precipitation of mercury sulfide.  This application may not be applicable for full-scale potable water treatment applications.

Contaminant Name: Mercury | Overall: Based on available studies, the following processes are considered effective for the removal of mercury: adsorptive media (up to greater than 99 percent), conventional treatment (up to 90 percent), granular activated carbon (50 to greater than 99 percent), ion exchange (greater than 90 percent in most cases), precipitative softening (30 to 70 percent), and membrane filtration (ultrafiltration 15 to greater than 99 percent). The level of control for conventional treatment, precipitative softening and granular activated carbon is consistent with their designations as Best Available Technologies for mercury removal. Performance of these technologies varies based on water quality, mercury speciation, and treatment characteristics.  Reverse osmosis is a Best Available Technology for mercury control, but four POU or POE studies demonstrated only 9 to 44 percent removal.    
Other treatment technologies, such as aeration with hydrogen sulfide gas were shown to reduce mercury levels but may not be suitable for drinking water applications. 
Aeration (except in the case of elemental mercury) and all forms of chemical oxidation (e.g. chlorine, permanganate, ozone) do not provide significant control.
Research was identified evaluating the following treatment technologies for the removal of mercury:

Contaminant Name: Mercury | Function: Precipitative Softening | Details: Based on one study, removal of inorganic mercury from water by precipitative softening can be effective (30 to 70 percent removal).  In the same study, precipitative softening did not effectively remove organic mercury from the water (0 percent removal).  Precipitative softening is considered a Best Available Technology by the USEPA for removing mercury.  Removal of mercury by lime softening depends on pH, mercury species, lime doses, and the presence of co-occuring solutes, such as manganese and iron.  A softening pH between 9 and 11 appears to be ideal for mercury removal.

Contaminant Name: Methiocarb | Description: Methiocarb is an n-methyl carbamate pesticide used since the 1960s to control snails, slugs, mites, thrips, aphids, leafhoppers, fruit flies, & biting insects; & some soil pests of field crops. It is also used as a bird repellent on fruit crops [2116].  There currently are no food or feed uses for methiocarb.  Its IUPAC name is 4-methylthio-3,5-xylymethylcarbamate [2120]. 
  
Methiocarb was first registered as a pesticide in the U.S. in 1972 and EPA issued a Reregistration Eligibility Decision document for methiocarb in 1994.  Wettable powder formulations of methiocarb are restricted use pesticides available only to professional applicators.  One formulation of methiocarb is a general use pesticide which can be used by homeowners [2121]. 
 
The mechanism of toxicity for methiocarb is to inhibit the production of cholinesterase, an enzyme necessary for transmission of nerve impulses [2121]. 

 
National usage figures are not available for methiocarb; however, usage in California from 2005 to 2007 was over 6,000 pounds of active ingredient [2120].  
Methiocarb is highly toxic by the oral route and moderately toxic by the inhalation route. It is not an eye or skin irritant and does not cause delayed neurotoxicity [2121]. It is unclassifiable as a carcinogen based on insufficient data.  
 
Methiocarb is moderately persistent and relatively immobile in soil, and generally unlikely to contaminate groundwater [2121].  However, it has been detected in groundwaters in America, Australia, Mexico and Spain at concentrations ranging between 0.03 and 5.4 ug/L [2119].  The half life of methiocarb in water has been reported from 35 days at pH 7 to 6 hours at pH 9 [2116].

Methiocarb degradates include methiocarb sulfoxide, methiocarb sulfone, methiocarb phenol, methiocarb sulfoxide phenol, and methiocarb sulfone phenol [2119].  Based on oral LD50 values in rats, methiocarb sulfoxide has been found to be more toxic than methiocarb [2119].  
 
Methiocarb is not regulated in drinking water in the US. 

Date of Literature Search: March 2011

Contaminant Name: Methiocarb | Function: Chlorine Dioxide | Details: Oxidation of methiocarb by chlorine dioxide was found to be very effective in one study (up to 100% oxidation achieved);  however, the dosage used (67 mg/L) is  too high for drinking water treatment.  About 80 to 95% oxidation was achieved at chlorine dioxide dosages more typical for drinking water treatment.  Although methiocarb was effectively oxidized, the formation of the more toxic byproduct methiocarb sulfoxide was observed, raising questions on the potential risk of increased toxicity from chlorine dioxide to reduce methiocarb.  The solution pH significantly affected the type and quantity of degradation by-products. Higher methiocarb and by-product reductions were observed at higher pH values (i.e. pH of 8.6 compared to a pH of 6.5).

Contaminant Name: Methiocarb | Function: Other Treatment | Details: Removal of methiocarb by radiation processing was found to be moderately effective in one study (26 to 58 percent degradation observed). The amount of degradation achieved was highly dependent upon the radiation dose and solution pH, with higher removals observed at higher radiation dosages and solution pH values.    The highest removals were observed at a radiation dose of 10 kGy and pH of 8.

Contaminant Name: Methiocarb | Overall: The following processes have been demonstrated to be effective for the reduction of methiocarb from drinking water: chlorine dioxide (>99 oxidation) and radiation processing (26 to 58 oxidation).  Although chlorine dioxide was found to effectively oxidize methiocarb, a concern for increased toxicity is raised with this treatment method due to the formation of the more toxic by-product methiocarb sulfoxide.  Performance of these technologies can vary based on water quality and applied dosages.
Research was identified evaluating the following treatment technologies for the removal of methiocarb:

Contaminant Name: Methomyl | Description: Methomyl is an oxime carbamate insecticide. Its IUPAC name is S-methyl (EZ)-N-(methylcarbamoyloxy) thioacetimidate.  It is a broad spectrum insecticide used in a wide variety of treatment applications, including field, vegetable, and orchard crops, sod farms, livestock quarters, and commercial ornamentals. It is also used as an acaricide.  Methomyl was first registered in the United States in 1968, and is not registered for residential use [2000].  It is registered for use as an aerial dispersant.
Methomyl is highly soluble in water and is moderately persistent. It does not readily adsorb to soil and is considered highly mobile [495, 2000]. In water, it is stable under neutral to acidic pH ranges, but degrades in alkaline conditions. Methomyl can contaminate surface waters from spray drift during application or by runoff from treated sites. It has been detected in frequently in ground water and in surface water in monitoring studies at concentrations typically below 1 ug/L [2000]. Methomyl is not likely to persist in soil, as it has been found to degrade relatively rapidly by microbes [495].
Its principal hydrolysis and microbial degradates are S-methyl-N-hydroxythioacetimidate and methomyl oxime.  These may be further degraded to acetonitrile and carbon dioxide [2000] [2005].   It photodegrades in water with th eprincipal degadant as acetonitrile [2000].  It can slowly volatilize with acetonitrile and carbon dioxide as products [2000].
Methomyl is not regulated by USEPA in drinking water. Based on the results of several studies, it has been classified as a Group E carcinogen, meaning it is not likely to be carcinogenic. However, in acute toxicity testing for ingestion methomyl is classified as a Toxic Category I chemical (the highest toxicity category) [2000]. This is because methomyl inhibits cholinesterase, an enzyme that is essential to the nervous system.  It has no demonstrated reproductive, teratogenic, mutagenic or carcinogenic effects.
Methomyl is a degradate of thiodicarb, another registered insecticide, as thiodicarb is two methomyl molecules connected at nitrogen sites by a sulfur atom.  Thus, methomyl may be present in field and drinking waters resulting from the application of either thiodicarb or methomyl.
Date of Literature Search: October 2010

Contaminant Name: Methomyl | Function: Adsorptive Media | Details: A bench-scale study using two types of supported iron catalysts found mixed results for methomyl removal. Fe-ZSM-5 zeolite removed 28 to 89 percent of the contaminant, while AlFe-pillared montmorillonite removed 2 to 15 percent. Contaminant removal was dependent on the amount and type of supported iron catalysts used.

Contaminant Name: Methomyl | Function: Biological Treatment | Details: Elimination of methomyl by biological treatment was found to be effective. A bench-scale study performed with a relatively high concentration of contaminant (106 mg/L) mixed with activated sludge,mineral nutrients, and the bacteria vibrio fischeri resulted in 72 percent removal of methomyl.

Contaminant Name: Methomyl | Function: Direct Filtration | Details: Data from one pilot-scale study showed greater than 99 percent removal of methomyl two-stage direct filtration. Study water was first circulated 10-20 times through a sand filter that included multiple layers of media, including, fine and medium sand, fine and medium graded gravel, and coarse gravel.  Average removal efficiency through the sand filters was 61 percent.  After being filtered 10-20 times through the sand filter, the water was then filtered once through a carbon filter, which was identical in configuration with the exception that the fine sand layer was replaced by anthracite filter media.  Total removal by the two-stage system averaged greater than 99 percent.  As methomyl is strongly bound to particles, removal may have been due to microorganisms in the sea water matrix.

Contaminant Name: Methomyl | Function: GAC Isotherm | Details: A powdered activated carbon isotherm study showed a relatively low adsorption capacity for methomyl by PAC. The study examined the correlation between octanol-water partition coefficients and adsorption and found that pesticides with higher polarity (or lower Kow values) are more difficult to remove.
Studies investigating the adsorption capacity of non-ionic, hypercrosslinked polymeric resins to adsorb methomyl from aqueous solution found that the effectiveness of the polymer ranged substantially depending on the characteristics of the polymer’s functional group.
Contaminant Name: Methomyl | Function: Granular Activated Carbon | Details: In one EPA Environmental Technology Verification assessment, methomyl control by GAC was 98 percent in a point-of-use unit.

Contaminant Name: Methomyl | Function: Hydrogen Peroxide | Details: Results from a bench-scale study showed that the addition of hydrogen peroxide resulted in no methomyl removal in deionized water. However, a bench-scale study evaluating the effectiveness of Fenton’s reagent, or hydrogen peroxide in combination with Fe2+, achieved 86 percent removal of the contaminant. This increased removal is due to the formation of hydroxyl radicals during Fenton’s process.
pH of 3 was atypical of drinking water treatment.
Contaminant Name: Methomyl | Function: Membrane Separation | Details: In one EPA Environmental Technology Verification assessment, control of methomyl by a point-of-use unit was 96 percent.

Contaminant Name: Methomyl | Overall: The following processes were found to be effective in POU studies for the removal of methomyl: reverse osmosis (96 percent) and GAC (98 percent).  Also effective were hydrogen peroxide with ferrous iron addition(86 percent), ozone (100 percent), ultraviolet irradiation with titanium dioxide addition (90 to 100 percent), ultraviolet irradiation with hydrogen peroxide (60 percent removal), and ultraviolet irradiation with hydrogen peroxide and iron addition (48 to 100 percent removal).  Many of these studies employed methomyl concentrations (high), pHs (low) and temperatures (high) compared to typical drinking water treatment.  
Activated carbon was found to have relatively low adsorption capacity for methomyl in isotherm studies, whereas non-ionic polymeric resins were observed to have varying adsorption capacity dependent on the functional group in isotherm studies.  Data for the resin isotherm studies are presented with the "GAC Isotherm" data for this contaminant.
Hydrogen peroxide alone was found to be ineffective for the removal of methomyl (0 percent).
Other treatments not typically employed in drinking water treatment were suggestive of methomyl removal. They were: biological treatment and adsorption using certain varieties of iron supported catalysts.
Research was identified evaluating the following treatment technologies for the removal of methomyl:

Contaminant Name: Methomyl | Function: Ozone | Details: Ozone is quite effective for the removal of methomyl in drinking water applications. One bench-scale study demonstrated the complete removal of a relatively large concentration of methomyl. It should be noted, however, that the investigated ozone dose in this study was higher, and the pH lower, than typically employed for ozonation of drinking water.

Contaminant Name: Methomyl | Function: Ultraviolet Irradiation | Details: The use of ultraviolet (UV) irradiation alone provided mixed results for the removal of methomyl. Based on data from two bench-scale studies, a range of 4 to 92 percent photodegradation of the contaminant was observed depending on time of exposure and UV intensity. The addition of Fe2+ was found to increase removal efficiency, resulting in 48 percent removal  compared to 4 percent removal by UV irradiation alone under similar conditions.
Data are suggestive that UV irradiation in combination with titanium dioxide was effective, resulting in 90 to 100 percent removal in studies utilizing UV lamps or compound parabolic solar collectors. The removal efficiency decreased to 57 and 77 percent with the addition of 5 and 1 percent NaCl solutions, respectively, however. Solar UV + TiO2 treated in open quartz glass flasks achieved 50 percent removal of the contaminant. Also, zinc oxide was slightly less effective than titanium dioxide, removing 69 percent of methomyl in the presence of UV irradiation (versus 91 percent for TiO2).
Many of these studies were conducted at methomyl concentrations, pHs and temperatures atypical of drinking water treatment.  Some of these studies were conducted in sea water.

Contaminant Name: Methomyl | Function: Ultraviolet Irradiation and Hydrogen Peroxide | Details: One bench-scale study achieved 60 percent removal of methomyl utilizing UV irradiation coupled with hydrogen peroxide. The addition of Fe ions resulted in 48 to 90 percent removal of the contaminant in two bench-scale studies. Photo-Fenton removal efficiency was dependent on concentrations of the contaminant, Fe ions, and H2O2.
Pilot-scale studies utilizing compound parabolic solar collectors were able to eliminate 90 to 100 percent of methomyl with the addition of high concentrations of hydrogen peroxide.
Results of three bench-scale studies investigating the use of Fe-ZSM-5 zeolite and AlFe-pillared montmorillonite in a photo-Fenton process found Fe-ZSM-5 zeolite to be more effective at removing methomyl, with 97 to 99 percent removal, versus 50 to 65 percent removal for AlFe-pillared montmorillonite.
Methomyl concentrations were high, pHs were low and waters were atypical of drinking water treatment conditions in many of these studies.

Contaminant Name: Methyl tert-butyl ether | Description: Methyl tertiary-butyl ether (MTBE) is a chemical compound that is manufactured by the chemical reaction of methanol and isobutylene [1578]. MTBE has been added to gasoline as an octane enhancing replacement for lead and as an oxygenate to lower the ozone and carbon monoxide emission levels [1557]. MTBE is highly volatile in room temperature because of its high vapor pressure (245-276 mm Hg @ 25 C) but it is difficult to volatilize once it dissolves in water because of its low Henry's law constant (0.023 to 0.12).
The main sources of MTBE contamination of groundwater supplies are leaking underground storage tanks and pipelines, spills and contaminated sites and releases from manufacturing and storage sites. The primary sources of MTBE contamination in urban surface water supplies are releases from gasoline-powered recreation watercrafts and atmospheric deposition through precipitation of industrial or vehicular emissions [1557][1577].
A growing number of studies have detected MTBE in ground water throughout the country; in some instances, these contaminated waters are sources of drinking water [1578].  Because of its small molecular size and because of its high solubility, it moves rapidly in ground water.  MTBE by itself is not likely to cause environmental harm at levels normally found in the environment but can contribute to the formation of photochemical smog when it reacts with other volatile organic carbon substances in air [1577].
The majority of the human health-related research conducted to date on MTBE has focused on effects associated with the inhalation of the chemical. EPA's Office of Water has concluded that available data are not adequate to estimate potential health risks of MTBE at low exposure levels in drinking water but that the data support the conclusion that MTBE is a potential human carcinogen at high doses [1578].
MTBE is not regulated by USEPA in drinking water, but it has been assessed as a part of the Unregulated Contaminants Monitoring Rule.  It is on USEPA's Contaminant Candidate List 3. The drinking water advisory recommended keeping the concentrations in the range of 20 and 40 ppb or below to likely avert unpleasant taste and odor effects.  The Agency advises that below the 20 to 40 ppb range, the public will be protected against potential health effects [1577]. 
The Agency has noted air strippping, granular activated carbon adsorption, and advanced oxidation processes as technologies that can control MTBE in drinking water. 
MTBE can be biodegraded under aerobic conditions.  A product is tertiary butyl alcohol (TBA).  In spill situations near a source, MTBE can also be found with benzene, toluene, ethyl benzene and xylenes (BTEX), thus, treatment may involve targets beyond MTBE itself [1555, 1557].
Date of Literature Search: July 2009
