Contaminant Name: Methyl tert-butyl ether | Function: Adsorptive Media | Details: Removal of MTBE from water using synthetic resin sorbents can be very effective (greater than 90 percent).   High-silica zeolites, perfluorooctylalumina, and carbonaceous Ambersorb resins have been used to effectively remove MTBE. Resin isotherm data for MTBE shows that carbonaceous resin are currently the most effective resins for MTBE removal from water.
Resins can have potential cost savings relative to air stripping, GAC, and AOPs because of their minimal interference by NOM, flexible regenerative properties, shorter EBCT requirements, high sorption affinity for TBA (a by-product or co-occurring contaminant sometimes observed with MTBE), minimal interference with other organics, and no by-products formation.
Performance of resin in MTBE reductions has been presented in terms of equilibrium isotherms from adsorption isotherm experiments and percent removal from dynamic column tests. One of the primary advantages of resin is their on-site regenerability using steam stripping or irradiations.

Contaminant Name: Methyl tert-butyl ether | Function: Aeration and Air Stripping | Details: Removal efficiencies for MTBE using aeration and air stripping ranged up to greater than 99 percent in pilot- and full-scale studies treating ground waters.  The full-scale study reported on the combined effectiveness of packed tower aeration and GAC adsorption.  EPA notes air stripping as an effective technology for MTBE control.
Due to high water solubility and low Henry's law constant, MTBE is difficult to remove using aeration or air stripping. Temperature plays an important role in MTBE reduction while using air strippers as the volatility of MTBE decreases with decrease in temperature. However, air strippers can be optimized by maximizing the contact time between air and water. In one study, the depth of packing required to achieve maximum treatment efficiency increased with decreasing air/water ratios.
The low profile and packed tower air stripper systems are commonly used for MTBE removal. Packed tower aeration was found to be superior to the other air stripping technologies from a cost perspective, regardless of hydraulic capacity, removal efficiency requirement, or initial MTBE concentrations. Off-gas from the air strippers were treated with treatment technologies such as vapor-phase GAC, thermal oxidation, catalytic oxidation, and adsorption/thermal desorption and destruction system.

Contaminant Name: Methyl tert-butyl ether | Function: Biological Treatment | Details: Removal of MTBE from water using biological treatment can be effective (from 60 to greater than 90 percent). The common method of biological treatment is by passing contaminated water through biologically activated carbon (BAC) columns. Batch studies have been done to explore the effect of microbial consortium composition and reactor configuration on MTBE in presence of benzene, toluene, ethylbenzene, and p-xylenes (BTEX). Results show that MTBE bioremediation in the presence of BTEX is feasible and the culture composition and reactor configuration are key factors.
Implementing biological GAC as a post treatment for an advanced oxidation process (AOP) can provide an additional treatment barrier for MTBE during the possible upset of the system. In a post treatment application, the BAC pilot-scale column treating surface or ground waters enhanced overall MTBE and byproducts (TBA) removal efficiency by providing some chemical adsorption and biological reduction.

Contaminant Name: Methyl tert-butyl ether | Function: GAC Isotherm | Details: Studies have reported data on GAC isotherms for various carbon materials. Isotherm experiments show that MTBE is effectively adsorbed to GAC. MTBE adsorption capacity decreased as it competed with NOM or other anions present in surface or groundwater, as expected. Isotherm experiments were also conducted for high-silica zeolites showing their effectiveness to adsorb MTBE.

Contaminant Name: Methyl tert-butyl ether | Function: Granular Activated Carbon | Details: Removal of MTBE from water by GAC can be very effective (greater than 95 percent). High percent removals in these studies were based on rapid small scale column tests (RSSCTs) of surface waters and ground waters.  One study monitoring a full-scale ground water did not differentiate between the effectiveness of packed tower aeration and GAC adsorption.  EPA notes GAC as effective for control of MTBE.
Based on cost analysis, GAC is often the most cost-effective technology for the removal of lower MTBE concentrations, which results in lower carbon usage rates. The effectiveness of GAC for the treatment of MTBE is limited by its poor physical and chemical adsorption characteristics compared with many organic compounds. Furthermore, GAC is more effective for waters that have relatively low levels of NOM and SOCs, which do not compete with MTBE for the adsorption sites of GAC.

Contaminant Name: Methyl tert-butyl ether | Function: Hydrogen Peroxide | Details: Iron-catalyzed hydrogen peroxide is effective in reducing MTBE in contaminated water. One study showed the effectiveness of using zero-valent iron (Fe) as the iron source in Fenton’s oxidation for treating MTBE contaminated water. Fenton’s oxidation with iron removed over 99% of MTBE at pH 4.0 and 7.0 with H2O2: MTBE molar ratio of 220:1.  Reaction times were atypically long.

Contaminant Name: Methyl tert-butyl ether | Function: Membrane Separation | Details: One pilot-scale study showed that nanofiltration and reverse-osmosis membranes consistently removed greater than 97 percent of MTBE. NF and RO membranes also effectively reduced turbidity, particle count, and TOC.

Contaminant Name: Methyl tert-butyl ether | Function: Other Treatment | Details: The electronic beam (E-beam)process was shown to be effective in destroying MTBE in groundwater. The E-beam unit is made of an electron accelerator, a scanner, a contact chamber, and lead shielding. MTBE contaminated water that passes through the scanner was reduced below MDL of 0.078 ug/L at the lowest dose (1.67 kGy) of E-beam. Similarly, irradiation of MTBE contaminated water by Cobalt-60 gamma rays completely degraded MTBE within 5 min with irradiation of 59.7 Gy/min.

Contaminant Name: Methyl tert-butyl ether | Overall: The following processes were found to be effective for the control of MTBE: air stripping (up to 99 percent), adsorption onto granular activated carbon (greater than 95 percent), ozone and hydrogen peroxide (up to 80 percent), and ultraviolet irradiation with hydrogen peroxide (up to 95 percent).  These technologies were noted by EPA as effective for MTBE control. 
Also found to be effectivewere: adsorptive media with carbonaceous resins (greater than 90 percent), ozone (up to 90 percent), ultraviolet irradiation (up to 75 percent), UV with TiO2 (up to 98 percent), membrane separation by RO or by NF (greater than 95 percent), and biological treatment by biologically active carbon (up to 90 percent).  Performance of these technologies can vary based on water quality and pre-treatment characteristics.
Other treatments that were effective in removal of MTBE were electronic beam process (up to 95 percent) and irradiation of MTBE by Cobalt-60 gamma rays (100 percent).  Fenton's oxidation with iron removal (up to 99 percent) was also effective in MTBE control, but with very long reaction times.
Filtration methods such as conventional, biological, and slow sand filtration along with ion exchange processes were not effective in MTBE control.
Research was identified evaluating the following treatment technologies for the removal of MTBE:

Contaminant Name: Methyl tert-butyl ether | Function: Ozone | Details: At higher concentrations of MTBE, ozone was able to reduce up to 50% of MTBE, while at lower concentrations of MTBE, ozone reduced from 70 to 90% of MTBE. MTBE degradation occurred relatively slowly in conventional ozonation experiments due to the low reactivity of MTBE with molecular ozone. MTBE removal increased with increased ozone dose, but ozone doses were oftentimes higher than typically employed in drinking water treatment. MTBE oxidation by ozone may have to be optimized to minimize bromate formation.

Contaminant Name: Methyl tert-butyl ether | Function: Ozone and Hydrogen Peroxide | Details: More than 80% of MTBE removal, and up to 99% removal, was observed in bench- and pilot-scale studies including ground waters.  Higher percent removals were observed with higher ozone and H2O2 doses.  EPA notes advanced oxidation processes like ozone/hydrogen peroxide as effective for MTBE control. 
The combination of ozone and hydrogen peroxide was more effective in MTBE removal than ozone alone.  The addition of hydrogen peroxide to ozone accelerated the ozone decomposition into OH radicals, which led to faster MTBE degradation.
MTBE oxidation by H2O2/Ozone (peroxone) may have to be optimized with regard to bromate formation. Results indicate that bromate formation could be effectively controlled with hydrogen peroxide to ozone ratios >1.0.

Contaminant Name: Methyl tert-butyl ether | Function: Ultraviolet Irradiation | Details: MTBE removal efficiency was dependent on availability of additional source of hydroxyl radicals. In one study, pulsed-UV irradiation, which emits UV light below 200nm, was able to degrade MTBE (40 to 75%). In another study involving indirect photolysis, caused by UV irradiation in presence of titanium dioxide, up to 80% MTBE was reduced. MTBE removal efficiency increased with decreasing initial concentrations of MTBE, and increasing doses of TiO2 (0.5 to 3 g/L).

Contaminant Name: Methyl tert-butyl ether | Function: Ultraviolet Irradiation and Hydrogen Peroxide | Details: Indirect photolysis caused by UV irradiation in the presence of hydrogen peroxide generates the hydroxyl radicals needed to degrade MTBE.   These studies included pilot- and full-scale treatment of ground waters.  Removals exceeded 90% when H2O2 doses were below 5 mg/L and when they exceeded 10 mg/L.  Generally, above 10 mg/L, increasing levels of hydrogen peroxide increased MTBE removal efficiency.  EPA has noted advanced oxidation processes like UV and H2O2 as effective for MTBE control.
MTBE removal (and by-products removal) were more sensitive to hydrogen peroxide level than UV dose. The efficiency of the UV/H2O2 process is dependent on factors such as absorbance efficiency of applied UV by hydrogen peroxide in the solution, the fraction of generated hydroxyl radicals reacting with MTBE versus scavenging species, and the effect of pH on these processes. UV/H2O2 application after pre-treatment of raw waters with high TOC and carbonate species increased MTBE removal efficiency.

Contaminant Name: Metolachlor | Description: Metolachlor is a synthetic organic compound of the chloroacetanilide class of herbicides. Its IUPAC name is 2-chloro-N-(2-ethyl-6-methylphenyl)-N-(2-methoxy-1-methylethyl) acetamide, with common trade names of Dual, Bicep, Codal and Milocep. [529] It is primarily used as a pre-plant, pre-emergence weed control for corn, cotton, peanuts, soybeans, and sorghum. Non-agricultural uses include ornamental plants and rights-of-way. An estimated 59 million pounds is applied annually in the United States. [529] 
Metolachlor is not regulated by USEPA in drinking water, but is an unregulated chemical drinking water contaminant listed on the USEPA's Contaminant Candidate Lists 1, 2 and 3. [529, 620]
USEPA classified Metolachlor as a possible human carcinogen based on liver effects in animals.[529]
The following properties and phenomena suggest metolachlor would be present in water treatment plant intakes. [529, 530] Based on relatively low Henry's Law constant and vapor pressure, is considered nonvolatile under ambient environmental conditions. Photolysis of metolachlor is not expected to be significant. It is relatively resistant to hydrolysis at moderate pH values of 5 to 9. Reported half lives in aerobic surface waters were weeks to months. It has relatively low soil/water partitioning coefficient indicating it adsorbs moderately to soil, thus it would be expected to have lower concentrations in ground water than in surface water.
Its primary method of degradation is microbial activity. [529, 530] Therefore metolachlor degrades much faster in soil than in water, again implying lower concentrations in ground water than in surface water. Four degradates have been identified, but the most commonly detected are metolachlor ethane sulfonic acid (ESA) and metolachlor oxanilic acid (OA). [529] For information on metolachlor degradates, go to Find a Contaminant. ESA and OA are more soluble than the parent herbicide and more resistant to microbial degradation than the parent. [529] These factors imply ESA and OA would be detected more frequently and at higher concentrations than their parent. Other reported degradates are 2-ethyl-6-methylaniline and 2-[(2-ethyl-6-methylphenyl)-amino]-1-propanol. [558]
Metolachlor enters the water through accidental spills, spraying, leaching from fields via run off, and leaching of groundwater. It is often used in combination with other herbicides. [530] USEPA reported on three studies of metolachlor occurrence. [529] In the USGS National Water Quality Assessment (NAWQA) (1992-2001) for surface waters, median reported concentrations were 0.029 ug/L in agricultural areas and less than the reporting level of 0.013 ug/l in urban areas. In the NAWQA (1992-2001) for ground waters, reported concentrations of metolachlor were nondetects. In USEPA's Unregulated Contaminant Monitoring (1993-1999) of public water supplies where reporting levels varied, metolachlor was detected in 0.86 percent of samples with a median concentration of 1 ug/L. In a Wisconsin study (1999-2000) metolachlor was found in 36 percent of private drinking water wells averaging 1.4 ug/L.

Date of Literature Search: March 2007

Contaminant Name: Metolachlor | Function: Chlorine | Details: Removal of metolachlor from water by chlorine was found to be ineffective. In full-scale studies, removals were less than 17 percent. In one bench-scale study, depression of the pH to 5.5 resulted in less than 40 percent removal.

Contaminant Name: Metolachlor | Function: Chlorine Dioxide | Details: Removal of metolachlor by chlorine dioxide was found to be ineffective. Based on data from one bench-scale study, there was no removal. In another study, metolachlor was measured before and after unit processes at two full-scale water treatment plants. Chlorine dioxide followed by conventional treatment, which is ineffective for metolachlor control, showed less than 30 percent removal of metolachlor.

Contaminant Name: Metolachlor | Function: Conventional Treatment | Details: Removal of metolachlor by conventional treatment was found to be ineffective. Four studies reported less than 30 percent removal. The results were confirmed at bench-scale and full-scale systems. No difference was shown based on the type of coagulant used - aluminum sulfate, ferric chloride or polyaluminum chloride.
Contaminant Name: Metolachlor | Function: Direct Filtration | Details: A full-scale study showed less than 25% removal of metolachlor with a combination of direct filtration and chlorine.

Contaminant Name: Metolachlor | Function: GAC Isotherm | Details: Isotherm studies showed a relatively high, and likely cost effective, adsorption capacity for metolachlor by GAC. Capacity was higher in non-competitive laboratory water than in a surface water with TOC of 3 mg/L. In one study, carbon showed more capacity for metolachlor than for its degradates.

Contaminant Name: Metolachlor | Function: Granular Activated Carbon | Details: Removal of metolachlor in drinking water at full-scale plants by GAC ranged 31 to 56 percent. The former may have been, in part, biological degradation as the contactor was downstream of ozonation. The latter was in place for approx three years when monitored.

Contaminant Name: Metolachlor | Overall: The following processes were found to be effective for the control of metolachlor: membrane separation using reverse osmosis (greater than 99 percent removal), powdered activated carbon (43 to greater than 99 percent removal), ozone (approx 60 percent removal)and ozone coupled with hydrogen peroxide (96 percent removal). Isotherm studies showed GAC capacities to be high for metolachlor, and in full-scale plants removals ranged 31 to 56 percent at the time of monitoring.
 Chlorine and chlorine dioxide were not significantly reactive with metolachlor as control was less than 30 percent. Conventional treatment provided less than 17 percent removal.
 Using conditions not typical of drinking water treatment, UV alone and UV H2O2 were suggestive of being efffective.

Studies were identified evaluating the following treatment technologies for the removal of metolachlor:

Contaminant Name: Metolachlor | Function: Ozone | Details: Ozone is effective for the removal of metolachlor in drinking water applications. In both bench-scale and full-scale studies, metolachlor removal was approximately 60 percent. Removal was affected by the water quality parameters, ozone dose and contact time. Ozonation byproducts were not identified in the studies.

Contaminant Name: Metolachlor | Function: Ozone and Hydrogen Peroxide | Details: Removal of metolachlor from water by ozone combined with hydrogen peroxide was found to be effective in one bench-scale study with 96 percent loss of metolachlor. The H2O2:O3 ratio was 0.5:1. Removal is affected by ozone dose, hydrogen peroxide dose and the concentration of natural organic matter of the water.

Contaminant Name: Metolachlor | Function: Powdered Activated Carbon | Details: Removal of metolachlor with PAC is effective (70 to 100 percent removal) under a range of water quality parameters and PAC sorption conditions in bench-scale studies with waters taken from four surface waters. Higher PAC dose and more contact time increased removal; depressing the pH or increasing the NOM concentration decreased removal. In full-scale applications, metolachlor removal ranged 43 to 94 percent and was dependent on the PAC dose.

Contaminant Name: Metolachlor | Function: Ultraviolet Irradiation | Details: Data are suggestive that ultraviolet (UV) irradiation is effective for the removal of metolachlor. Based on data from one study, and at pH below 3, and with Fenton's reagent, complete removal of metolachlor was observed in bench-scale experiments. Metolachlor degradates were also identified.

Contaminant Name: Metolachlor | Function: Ultraviolet Irradiation and Hydrogen Peroxide | Details: The use of ultraviolet (UV) irradiation and hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) is suggestive of being effective in drinking water. In two bench-scale studies with O3 and H2O2 at pH below 3, and with Fenton's reagent, loss of metolachlor exceeded 90 percent. Increasing the H2O2 to UV ratio increased the control of metolachlor. Metolachlor degradates were observed.

Contaminant Name: Metolachlor | Function: Ultraviolet Irradiation and Ozone | Details: One study reported rapid conversion of metolachlor by ozone/UV AOP.  Actual percent metolachlor removal is unknown.

Contaminant Name: Metolachlor Degradates | Description: Metolachlor is a synthetic organic compound of the chloroacetanelide class of herbicides. For information on metolachlor, go to Find a Contaminant. Metolachlor is not regulated by USEPA in drinking water, but is an unregulated chemical drinking water contaminant listed on the USEPA's Contaminant Candidate Lists (CCL) 1, 2 and 3. [529, 620] 
The primary method of metolachlor degradation is microbial activity. [529] Therefore metolachlor degrades much faster in soil than in water, implying lower concentrations of the parent herbicide in ground water than in surface water. Four degradates have been identified, but the most commonly detected are metolachlor ethane sulfonic acid (ESA) and metolachlor oxanilic acid (OA). ESA and OA are more soluble than the parent herbicide and more resistant to microbial degradation than the parent, implying ESA and OA would be detected more frequently and at higher concentrations than their parent. [561] Other reported degradates are 2-ethyl-6-methylaniline and 2-[(2-ethyl-6-methylphenyl)-amino]-1-propanol. [558]
USEPA reported that the ESA and OA degradates were less toxic than the parent herbicide. [561] The PAN Pesticides Database lists no known health effects for any of the four degradates. [558]
Metolachlor degradates are not regulated by USEPA in drinking water, but are unregulated chemical drinking water contaminants listed on the USEPA's Contaminant Candidate Lists 1, 2 and 3 [529, 620]
A Wisconsin study of municipal wells reported no detections of the parent herbicide metolachlor, but 39 percent detections of ESA averaging 1.3 ug/L and 35 percent detections of OA averaging 0.57 ug/L. [529] A Georgia study of surface waters reported no detections of the parent herbicide, but 74 percent detections of ESA with a maximum of 1.47 ug/L and 61 percent detections of OA with a 0.28 ug/L maximum. [562] An Ohio study of a treated drinking water using a reservoir in an agricultural area reported the parent in the 0.01 to 0.5 ug/L range, ESA in the 0.1 to 1 ug/L range, and OA in the 0.1 to 0.5 ug/L range. [563]
CAS numbers for ESA and OA are, respectively, 171118-09-5 and 152019-73-3.

Date of Literature Search: March 2007

Contaminant Name: Metolachlor Degradates | Function: Chlorine | Details: Control by chlorine can be effective for some degradates. Data from one study reported variable removal (2 to 100 percent) of metolachlor degradates by chlorine. The results suggest that removal is dependent on the compound's functional group. For the conditions tested, chlorine was effective for degradates lacking an acetanilide functional group. Hydroxymetolachlor (8%), deschlorometolachlor (2%), metolachlor morpholinone (13%) and metolachlor propanol (7%) were removed less by chlorine in comparison to deschloroacetyl metolachlor(100%) and deschloroacetyl metolachlor propanol (100%).

Contaminant Name: Metolachlor Degradates | Function: Conventional Treatment | Details: Removal of metolachlor degradates from water by coagulation/clarification was found to be ineffective. Based on data from one bench-scale study, 3 to 10 percent removal was reported for the following metolachlor degradates: hydroxy metolachlor, deschloro metolachlor, metolachlor morpholinone, metolachlor propanol, deschloroacetyl metolachlor, and deschloroacetyl metolachlor propanol. No difference was shown based on the type of coagulant used, i.e. aluminum sulfate or ferric chloride. In another study monitoring a full-scale plant, a total 67 to 74 percent removal of the degradates metolachlor sulphonic acid (ME-ESA) and metolachlor oxanilic acid (ME-OXA) was observed for conventional treatment with GAC filters and PAC.

Contaminant Name: Metolachlor Degradates | Function: GAC Isotherm | Details: PAC adsorption data was modeled as a Freundlich isotherm in one study for the following metolachlor degradates: hydroxymetolachlor, deschlorometolachlor, metolachlor morpholinone, metolachlor propanol, deschloroacetylmetolachlor, deschloroacetylmetolachlor propanol. Hydroxymetolachlor, deschlorometolachlor, metolachlor morpholinone, metolachlor propanol and deschloroacetylmetolachlor were better removed (higher K values); deschloroacetylmetolachlor propanol had poor removal (lower K value). The parent compound, metolachlor, had higher affinity for than all of the degradates except for metolachlor morpholinone.

Contaminant Name: Metolachlor Degradates | Function: Granular Activated Carbon | Details: One full-scale study reported totals of 67 to 74 percent removal of metolachlor degradates for PAC followed by conventional treatment with GAC filters.  The degradates were metolachlor sulphoic acid (ME-ESA) and metolachlor oxanilic acid (ME-OXA).

Contaminant Name: Metolachlor Degradates | Overall: The following processes were effective for the control of metolachlor degradates. Powdered activated carbon ranged 23 to 98 percent removal. Chlorine was reactive with four degradates (2 to 13 percent) and very reactive with two degradates (greater than 99 percent). Ozone was reactive with six degradates (63 to greater than 99 percent). Performance of these technologies can vary based on water quality and treatment characteristics. Additionally, removal is dependent on the functional groups of the individual metolachlor degradates.
Conventional treatment (3 to 10 percent removal) was not effective for the removal of metolachlor degradates.
The two degradates (metolachlor ESA and metolachlor OA) on the CCL3 were monitored in a full-scale plant and were adsorbable onto PAC and ozone reactive.
Studies were identified evaluating the following treatment technologies for the removal of metolachlor degradates:

Contaminant Name: Metolachlor Degradates | Function: Ozone | Details: Ozone is effective for the removal of metolachlor degradates in drinking water applications. The removal efficiencies were reported in the range of 63 to 100 percent in bench-scale studies and 50 (metolachlor OA) to greater than 57 percent (metolachlor ESA) in full-scale monitoring. Full-scale studies and were affected by the water quality parameters, ozone dose and contact time. Ozonation byproducts were not identified in the studies.

Contaminant Name: Metolachlor Degradates | Function: Powdered Activated Carbon | Details: Removal of the metolachlor degradates: metolachlor oxanilic acid (ME-OXA) and metolachlor sulphonic acid (ME-ESA) with PAC can be effective (23 to 98 percent removal) under a range of water quality parameters and PAC sorption conditions tested in bench-scale studies for two PACs and and four surface waters. From data presented in the bench-scale study, higher PAC dose and more contact time increased removal; depressing the pH or increasing the NOM concentration decreased removal. PAC showed greater removal of metolachlor in comparison to the removal of the metolachlor degradates. Monitoring at a full-scale plant showed 46 to 48 percent removal of the degradates by PAC alone, and 67 to 74 percent total removal of the degradates through a conventional treatment plant with GAC filters and PAC.

Contaminant Name: Mevinphos | Description: Mevinphos is an organophosphorus insecticide, which was used to control a broad spectrum of insects on forage, vegetables, and fruit crops [1637].  It ia also an acaracide.  Its CAS name is 3-[(Dimethoxyphosphinyl)oxy]-2-butenoate.
Mevinphos was initially registered as a pesticide in 1957 [1638]. However, mevinphos was determined ineligible for reregistration by the U.S. Environmental Protection Agency (USEPA) in 1994 [1638]. Its sale and distribution was prohibited after December 31, 1994, and its use was completely banned after February 28, 1995 [1638].
Mevinphos is highly toxic via oral and dermal routes. It is a cholinesterase inhibitor.  [1652, 1653]  When humans are exposed to mevinphos, poisoning symptoms may appear in fifteen minutes to two days [1652]. Symptoms may include numbness, tingling sensation, headache, dizziness, difficulty breathing, blurred vision, abdominal cramps, slow heartbeat, even unconsciousness or fatality [1652].  It is not known to be carcinogenic.
Mevinphos is miscible with water. Its hydrolysis half-lives were reported as 120 days, 35 days, 3 days and 1.4 hours at pH 6, 7, 9 and 11, respectively [1642]. Thus, the rate of hydroloysis is a function of pH. Mevinphos' degradation rate was reported similar in natural water and de-ionized water with and without sterilization, which suggest chemical degradation plays a primary role [1647]. Its half life in soils was reported in the range of 3 to 13 days [1640, 1648]. Due to its short life time in water, mevinphos is not expected to reach groundwater.  It does not sorb to soil particles.
Mevinphos is currently not regulated by USEPA for drinking water.
Mevinphos has rarely been detected in drinking water sources [1649, 1650, 1651]. For example, mevinphos was not found in any of 665 wells monitored throughout the U.S. over a 20-year period between 1971 and 1991 in a study compiled by USEPA Office of Prevention Pesticides and Toxic Substances [1649].  In addtion, mevinphos was not found in any of  29 wells in California study conducted in the early 1980s [1650].
Date of Literature Search: August 2010

Contaminant Name: Mevinphos | Function: Granular Activated Carbon | Details: Removal of mevinphos by an activated carbon filter was reported as 95 to 99 percent in three EPA environmental Technology Verification studies [16, 618, 1489]. The GAC filter tested was a component of a point-of-use drinking water treatment system.

Contaminant Name: Mevinphos | Function: Membrane Separation | Details: Removal of mevinphos by reverse osmosis (RO) was effective in four EPA Environmental Technology Verification studiestwo studies (equal to or greater than 96 percent removal) [617, 618, 1487, 1488]. The membrane units tested were components of two point-of-use drinking water treatment systems. The RO membrane of each system was tested alone for mevinphos removal. For these studies, pH ranged 7.0 to 8.0.

Contaminant Name: Mevinphos | Overall: The following processes were found to be effective for the removal of mevinphos: granular activated carbon (equal to 99 percent removal based on three studies), reverse osmosis membrane (equal to or greater than 96 percent removal based on four studies), ultraviolet irradiation with photocatalyst (removal efficiency depending on contact time), ultraviolet irradiation with ozone (removal efficiency depending on contact time).
Studies were identified evaluating the following treatment technologies for the removal of mevinphos:

Contaminant Name: Mevinphos | Function: Ultraviolet Irradiation | Details: Ultraviolet (UV) irradiation combined with titanium dioxide (TiO2) as a photocatalyst was shown to be effective for mevinphos removal. One study investigated degradation of mevinphos (based on COD reduction) at two pH levels (6.5 and 9.0) using a UV dose of 3 mW/cm2 and 1 g/L TiO2 [1654]. The results showed 77.2 and 85.4 percent of COD reduction at pH 6.5 and 9.0, respectively, with an approximate UV contact time of three hours [1654]. UV alone is not effective for mevinphos removal; only 30.4 percent COD reduction was achieved at pH 9.0 with an approximate UV contact time of three hours [1654].

Contaminant Name: Mevinphos | Function: Ultraviolet Irradiation and Ozone | Details: Ultraviolet (UV) irradiation with ozone is effective for mevinphos removal. One study investigated degradation of mevinphos (based on COD reduction) at two pH levels (6.5 and 9.0) using a UV dose of 3 mW/cm2 combined with an ozone dose of 400 mg/hour/liter solution [1654]. The results showed 76.4 and 92.8 percent of COD reduction at pH 6.5 and 9.0, respectively, with an approximate UV contact time of three hours [1654].

Contaminant Name: Microcystins | Description: Microcystins are toxins produced by cyanobacteria. Cyanobacteria are also known as blue-green algae and are ubiquitous in surface water when conditions are favorable for growth and formation of algal blooms. Cyanobacteria release toxins upon cell death or lysis. When released, toxins may persist for weeks to months. [564] Toxins of most concern are microcystins. Microcystins take their name from the genera Microcystis. Most microcystins are hepatotoxins (liver toxins). [564] Hepatotoxins are produced by species of the genera Microcystis, Anabena, Nodularia, Oscillatoria among others. [567] Most microcystins are associated with Microcystis aeruginosa. While the liver is the primary target of microcystins, it is also a skin, eye and throat irritant. [567] 
Microcystins are cyclic heptapeptides with seven amino acids. Microcystins are named for the various amino acids on the peptide structure. Microcystin-LR is named for the leucine (L) and argentine (R) amino acids. Microcystin-LR was the first identified and is the most commonly studied. Other common microcystins are RR, YR and LA. [565]
Cyanobacterial blooms can persist with adequate levels of phosphorous and nitrogen, temperatures in the 5 to 30 C range and pH in the 6 to 9 range, with most blooms occurring in late summer and early fall. [567] Microcystin toxins are nonvolatile, hydrophilic, and stable in sunlight, and stable over a wide temperature and pH range. [565] Factors affecting toxin production are light and temperature, with optimum temperatures from cyanobacteria ranging 20 to 25 C. [567] These conditions suggest microcystins present in surface water supplies in warm and sunny climates.
Microcystins are not regulated by USEPA in drinking water, but are unregulated microbial drinking water contaminants listed on the USEPA's Contaminant Candidate Lists (CCLs) 1 and 2 as cyanobacteria and their toxins. [564] Microcystins, specifically Anatoxin-A, Microcystin-LR and Cylindrospermopsin, are on the CCL3. [620] The WHO has established a provisional guideline provisional of 1 ug/L for microcystin-LR. [567]
Reference 564 cites a report of the AWWA Research Foundation wherein 80 percent of samples in Canada and US were positive for microcystins and 4.3 percent were above the WHO guideline of 1 ug/L. Microcystins were reported in raw waters for two Alberta supplies ranging 0.15 to 4.3 ug/L with wide fluctuation over time. [568] A study of 160 surface water supplies in Manitoba reported microcystin-LR detected in 70 percent of raw water supplies ranging up to 1.0 ug/L. [569] Microcystin-LR was detected over a June to December period in Manitoba water supplies. It was found to persist for up to two months following the decline of algal populations. [570]

Contaminant Name: Microcystins | Function: Adsorptive Media | Details: One study demonstrated PPy/Fe3O4 nanoparticle adsorption as an effective removal technique for MC-LA, MC-LR, and MC-YR microcystin species in laboratory water at the bench scale. Authors observed removals of >99 percent which varied with the initial adsorbent concentration added to solution.

Contaminant Name: Microcystins | Function: Chloramine | Details: One study showed that inactivation of total microcystins in water by monochloramine is not effective. A dose of 20 mg/L at a contact time of 5 days was only able to reduce the toxin concentration by 17% at room temperature.

Contaminant Name: Microcystins | Function: Chlorine | Details: Oxidation of microcystin M-LR in water by chlorine is effective (up to 100 percent removal) in bench-scale studies where the effectiveness is dependent on chlorine dose, contact time, pH, temperature and water quality characteristics. Data from full-scale water treatment plants indicate a much lower removal.

Contaminant Name: Microcystins | Function: Chlorine Dioxide | Details: Data show moderate to high reduction of microcystin-LR by chlorine dioxide, yet using doses or contact times much greater than practiced in water treatment plants.

Contaminant Name: Microcystins | Function: Conventional Treatment | Details: Removal of microcystins in water by coagulation and clarification can be little to moderately effective (96 percent maximum reported). This maximum value was reported in a study looking at algal toxin cell viability in conventional treatment with the addition of ferrate (VI) as a coagulant and oxidant. Other studies indicate much lower effectiveness (maximum removal of ~42%) for conventional treatment processes. The process effectiveness is dependent on initial toxin concentrations and the form of the toxin (intracellular versus extracellular). Conventional treatment is most effective at removing cell bound (intracellular) microcystins. Dissolved (extracellular) microcystins are not well removed through this process. If conventional treatment conditions promote cell lysis, conventional treatment may actually increase dissolved microcystins

Contaminant Name: Microcystins | Function: Direct Filtration | Details: One study showed that direct filtration is not effective at reducing dissolved microcystins and may contribute to an increase in concentration.

Contaminant Name: Microcystins | Function: GAC Isotherm | Details: Based on isotherm studies, adsorption of microcystins in water by granular activated carbon (GAC) can be very effective. Six microcystins were studied on eight GACs. The effectiveness of the adsorption depends on the GAC material type.

Contaminant Name: Microcystins | Function: Granular Activated Carbon | Details: Removal of total microcystins, M-LR, and M-LA, in water by granular activated carbon (GAC) can be very effective where the effectiveness is based on the empty bed contact time, the carbon's age, and possible biodegradation of the toxin. Time-dependent monitoring in a full-scale plant ranged 43 to 60 percent removal for M-LR. Time-dependent monitoring in pilot-scale studies ranged from greater than 99 percent at one month to 73 percent at 3.5 months for M-LR, and from greater than 99 percent at one month to 38 percent at 3.5 months for M-LA.

Contaminant Name: Microcystins | Function: Hydrogen Peroxide | Details: Two studies showed that oxidation of the microcystin M-LR in water by hydrogen peroxide is not effective. No significant control of M-LR was observed. Another study assessed removal of M-LR and M-RR by catalytic wet peroxide oxidation (CWPO) using modified natural magnetite (Fe3O4-R400/H2O2). This modified process yielded better removal results (64% for M-LR and >95% for M-RR).

Contaminant Name: Microcystins | Function: Membrane Filtration | Details: Removal of total microcystins in water by ulfiltration is moderately to very effective. One study indicated that microfiltration is not effective at removing microcystins. It should be noted that there were no increases of the total microcystins or M-LR in the permeates as anticipated from possible cell lysis at the membrane surface.

Contaminant Name: Microcystins | Function: Membrane Separation | Details: Removal of microcystins in water by membrane separation is very effective for various microcystin covariants, and for a wide range of microcystin concentrations. Some studies indicated variability in the effectiveness of different membrane types but the majority of products removed >95 percent of covariants.

Contaminant Name: Microcystins | Function: Other Treatment | Details: Removal of total microcystins and M-LR in water by combined treatment processes can be moderately to very effective, up to 100 percent removal. Due to the unique nature of each combined treatment process, performance must be evaluated on a case-by-case basis. Three references report on numerous full-scale monitoring efforts, however, typically only the raw waters and the finished waters were monitored. Processes included conventional treatment, chlorine, chloramine, chlorine dioxide, permanganate, softening, GAC, PAC and bank filtration. Most of these, reported for microcystins in the TDB, are fully or partially effective, therefore, effectiveness can not be attributed to specific treatment processes at these full-scale plants. However, in one study, pretreatment by bank filtration alone lowered M-LR concentrations by 61 percent.

Contaminant Name: Microcystins | Overall: The literature identified studies for total microcystins and for M-LR, M-LA, M-RR, M-YR and M-LY. See the individual treatment processes (below) for which microcystins were evaluated by those processes. This summary will address microcystins in general.
 The following processes are considered effective for the removal/oxidation of microcystins: ozone (up to 100), ozone with hydrogen peroxide (up to 100), membrane filtration with ultrafiltration (up to 98), membrane separation with reverse osmosis (up to 99), membrane separation with nanofiltration (up to 99), powdered activated carbon (up to 100 for some microcystins but less so for others), granular activated carbon (time-dependent from 100 near start up to 38 to 73 at 3.5 months), slow sand filtration (time-dependent ranging 65 to 100), bank filtration (see other treatment) (one assessment at 61), chlorine (up to 95), permanganate (up to 90), and conventional treatment (up to 42 percent, but more effective for intracellular microcystins than for extracellular (dissolved) microcystins).
Adsorptive media using novel nanoparticles was shown to be >99 percent effective, but the technology has been tested at the bench scale only.
Even though ozone coupled with hydrogen peroxide is effective, it is not significantly more effective than ozone alone.
UV irradiation and UV coupled with hydrogen peroxide were marginally effective but relatively long contact times were required.
 Chloramine, chlorine dioxide and hydrogen peroxide were not effective under typical operating conditions.
Research was identified evaluating the following treatment technologies for the removal or inactivation of microcystins:

Contaminant Name: Microcystins | Function: Ozone | Details: Oxidation of microcystins in water by ozone is moderately to very effective (up to 100 percent removal of M-LA and M-LR in several studies) where the effectiveness is highly dependent on temperature, ozone dose, contact time, and water quality. pHs were generally below 8. In one reference, the inlet and outlet concentrations for M-LA and M-LR were given and had similar control (ranging from 50 to 100 percent). It is necessary for residual ozone to be present to oxidize the natural organic matter in the water to maximize the oxidation of microcystins.

Contaminant Name: Microcystins | Function: Ozone and Hydrogen Peroxide | Details: Oxidation of microcystin M-LR in water by ozone (O3) with hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) at 0.5:1 H2O2:O3 is effective with 45 to 100 percent removal. However, H2O2:O3 was not significantly better than ozone alone.

Contaminant Name: Microcystins | Function: Permanganate | Details: Oxidation of microcystins in water by permanganate addition is little to very effective. Performance is highly dependent on potassium permanganate dose. Greater than 90 percent control was achieved at a dose of 1.25 mg/L, neutral pH and ambient temperature conditions.

Contaminant Name: Microcystins | Function: Powdered Activated Carbon | Details: Removal of microcystins in water by powdered activated carbon (PAC) is moderately to highly effective, up to 100% depending on the microcystin congener being removed and the PAC dose, PAC type and contact time. Based on reviewed references, M-RR is the most easily removed followed by M-YR, M-LR and M-LA.

Contaminant Name: Microcystins | Function: Slow Sand Filtration | Details: Elimination of microcystins in water by slow sand filtration can be very effective, where the effectiveness is based on contact time. Greater contact times increase the effectiveness of slow sand filtration.

Contaminant Name: Microcystins | Function: Ultraviolet Irradiation | Details: The studies reviewed indicate that the oxidation of microcystin-LR in water by ultraviolet irradiation (UV) can be moderately effective (up to 49% removal). However, testing conditions included exposure times that are far in excess of typical exposure times in drinking water plants' UV reactors.
Three references showed higher removal percentages (up to >99%) for UV coupled with the addition of titanium dioxide (TiO2) as opposed to traditional UV treatment. The UV + TiO2 studies found that effectiveness varied as a function of chemical addition concentration, pH, UV dose, and UV contact time. These studies were conducted at the bench scale and, like the studies discussed above of UV irradiation alone, involved UV contact times much longer than those typically employed at full-scale drinking water treatment plants.

Contaminant Name: Microcystins | Function: Ultraviolet Irradiation and Hydrogen Peroxide | Details: Oxidation of the microcystins MR-LR and M-RR in water by ultraviolet irradiation (UV) with hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) is moderately to very effective. Most tests utilized a very high dose of hydrogen peroxide. Studies indicate increased effectiveness with higher H2O2 doses.

Contaminant Name: Molinate | Description: Molinate is a selective thiocarbamate herbicide used to control broad-leaved and grassy plants in rice and other crops [495]. Its CAS name is 3S-ethyl hexahydro-1H-azepine-1-carbothioate.
Molinate is no longer used in the United States. A voluntary cancellation phased down the use from 2007 to 2009 and no use of products containing molinate was permitted after August 31, 2009 [1800]. Molinate is not currently regulated by USEPA for drinking water, but does have a maximum contaminant level in the state of California of 20 micrograms per liter [1801].  It is on the Contaminant Candidate List 3 for regulatory consideration.
Molinate has suspected endochrine, reproductive and neurotoxic end points.  It has not been demonstrated to be carcinogenic in humans.
Molinate is miscible with and persistent in water. The aqueous hydrolysis half-life was reported as 365 days and the aqueous photolysis in natural sunlight was reported as stable [1791]. Molinate is not persistent in soil with an aerobic soil degradation half life of 28 days and an anaerobic soil degradation half life of 129 days [1791, 1793]. With a Henry's Law constant of 4.1E-6, molinate is considered volatile and dissipation of molinate from flooded rice paddies occurs as a result of vaporization from the water [1790, 1793].  By hydrolysis or microbial action, molinte may degrade to 4-hydroxy molinate, molinate acid or molinate sulfoxide.
Molinate has not been detected in drinking water sources frequently. For example, molinate was only detected in 4 of 355 wells monitored throughout the U.S. over a 20-year period between 1971 and 1991 in a study compiled by USEPA Office of Prevention Pesticides and Toxic Substances [1649]. In more recent studies, molinate was not detected in California groundwater wells sampled between Ju

ly 1, 1997, and June 30, 1999 [1801, 1802, 1803], but was detected in surface waters in the Sacramento Valley in a 1995 survey [1801, 1804].

Contaminant Name: Molinate | Function: Chemical Treatment | Details: Chemical addition of nano-scale zero valent iron (ZVI) for the degradation of molinate can be effective (30 to 92 percent removal with three hours of contact time depending on dose). ZVI-mediated degradation of contaminants typically occurs under fully anoxic conditions; however, for molinate, two studies utilizing stirred batch systems with 100 micrograms per liter molinate found that oxic conditions are needed (no molinate removal was observed under anoxic conditions) [1796, 1797]. Additionally, the rate of molinate degradation increased with decreasing pH.
Chemical addition of aluminum sulphate (alone or in combination with ozone and powdered activated carbon) was considered in one study [723]. By itself, coagulation with aluminum sulfate at 10 to 40 milligrams per liter as Al removed only 15 percent of molinate (551 nanogram per liter initial concentration), compared to 75 and 95 percent removal when in combination with ozone or both ozone and powdered activated carbon.

Contaminant Name: Molinate | Function: Chlorine | Details: Pre-oxidation with chlorine is effective for molinate removal.  One study found complete (100 percent) removal with the addition of 18 mg/L chlorine [723]. The chlorine demand of the Ebro River water tested in this study was used to determine the chlorine dose.  No other chlorine doses were considered. Pre-oxidation with chlorine should be balanced with the formation of other contaminants of concern such as disinfection byproducts.

Contaminant Name: Molinate | Overall: The following processes were found to be effective for the removal of molinate: pre-oxidation with chlorine (100 percent removal in one study), pre-oxidation with ozone (75 percent removal or greater in combination with other treatment processes), powdered activated carbon (60 percent removal or greater in combination with other treatment processes), ultraviolet irradiation with photocatalyst (removal efficiency depending on contact time and dose), and ultraviolet irradiation with hydrogen peroxide (45 to 95 percent removal depending on dose), chemical addition of nanoscale zero-valent iron (30 to 92 percent removal with three hours contact time depending on dose), and chemical addition of aluminum sulphate (15 percent removal or greater in combination with other treatment processes), Studies were identified evaluating the following treatment technologies for the removal of molinate:

Contaminant Name: Molinate | Function: Ozone | Details: Pre-oxidation with ozone is effective for molinate degradation. One study found that 3 milligrams per liter ozone removed 75 percent of molinate in less than one minute [723]. The same study showed that ozone in combination with coagulation (40 milligrams per liter of aluminum sulphate as Al), did not improve molinate removal, but when used in combination with coagulation and powdered activated carbon (10 milligrams per liter), molinate removal increased to 95 percent.

Contaminant Name: Molinate | Function: Powdered Activated Carbon | Details: Adsorption on powdered activated carbon can be an effective treatment for molinate removal. In one study, a dose of 10 milligrams per liter powdered activated carbon removed 60 percent of molinate in five minutes [723]. When used in combination with ozone or ozone and aluminum sulfphate coagulation, molinate removal increased to 85 and 95 percent.

Contaminant Name: Molinate | Function: Ultraviolet Irradiation | Details: Photocatalytic degradation with ultraviolet irradiation and titanium dioxide can be an effective treatment for molinate. Studies found that although little molinate degradation is achieved by photolysis alone, in a batch system with 100 to 1000 milligrams per liter of titanium dioxide 50 to 99 percent removal is achieved in less than 30 minutes [1794, 1795]. Photocatalytic degradation of molinate can be effective; however molinate by-products could be formed. One study suggested that the great number of compounds detected during the degradation of molinate shows that the photocatalytic process is complex and that various degradation routes exist [1795]. Cost-effective treatments to complete compound mineralization are usually not practicable, and the presence of by-products during and at the end of the water treatment appears to be unavoidable [1795].

Contaminant Name: Molinate | Function: Ultraviolet Irradiation and Hydrogen Peroxide | Details: Advanced oxidation with ultraviolet irradiation and hydrogen peroxide can be an effective treatment option for the removal of molinate. One study found that with 25 to 50 milligrams per liter hydrogen peroxide and a UV dose of 300 milliwatt-second per cubic centimeters, 92 to 95 percent removal of molinate can be achieved [1799].

Contaminant Name: Natural Organic Matter | Description: Natural organic matter (NOM) occurs in most natural water sources as a result of the decomposition of plant and animal residues through biotic and abiotic reactions [2446, 2467, 2470]. NOM consists of a variety of substances and chemical groups including humic acids, fulvic acids, carboxylic acids, phenols, aromatics, proteins, alcohols, aldehydes, ketones, esters, amines, hydroxy acids, amino acids, sugars, polysaccharides, and hydrocarbons [2470, 2471]. Fractions of NOM are often categorized according to their hydrophobicity and/or biodegradability [2454, 2470, 2471]. The amount of NOM and in a given water, and its characterization according to these categories, can vary with climate and season [2471].
NOM is commonly measured as total organic carbon (TOC) or dissolved organic carbon (DOC). Other measures, either of fractions of NOM or as indicators of NOM, include biodegradable organic carbon (BDOC), assimilable organic carbon (AOC), specific ultraviolet absorbance (SUVA), and formation potential for various disinfection byproducts (DBPs). Some studies also measure certain of the individual substances or chemical groups that can make up NOM (e.g., humic acids).
NOM is of concern in drinking water because it provides precursor materials for the formation of DBPs including trihalomethanes and haloacetic acids [2446, 2454, 2467, 2469, 2470, 2471]. Other impacts of NOM in drinking water include taste and odor, color, and increased chlorine demand in disinfection processes. NOM can also act as a carrier for toxic contaminants and contribute to bacterial regrowth and biofilm formation in distribution systems [2446, 2454, 2467, 2469, 2470].
Under the Stage 1 and Stage 2 Disinfection Byproducts Rules, the U.S. EPA requires monitoring for TOC as a measure of DBP precursors. It also requires drinking water systems using conventional treatment or softening to remove specific percentages of organic materials, measured as TOC. The percent removal requirements vary by source water TOC and alkalinity, ranging from 15.0 to 50.0 percent [2472].
Date of Literature Search: 2014

Contaminant Name: Natural Organic Matter | Function: Biological Filtration | Details: A number of full-scale [2442, 2443, 2445, 2446, 2452, 2453] and bench-scale [2444, 2447, 2450, 2451] studies, both with and without ozone pre-treatment, observed a wide range of natural organic matter removal. The maximum removals reported for the biofiltration step alone were 77 percent for TOC, 65 percent for DOC, 84 percent for AOC and BDOC, and 54 percent for nonbiodegradable DOC. Factors that affected removal included EBCT and media age.

Contaminant Name: Natural Organic Matter | Function: Biological Treatment | Details: Several bench-scale studies found moderate removal of natural organic matter through biological treatment. The maximum removals reported in studies with contact times of up to 3 hours were 46 percent of DOC [2450, 2449] and 49 percent of BDOC [2445]. One bench-scale study reported 97 to 99 percent removal of TOC with a contact time of 24 hours [2448].
A pilot-scale study using a membrane biological reactor incorporating powdered activated carbon (PAC) found up to 33 percent removal of DOC and 51 percent removal of BDOC. Without PAC, the reactor achieved only 6 to 16 percent removal of DOC and 29 percent removal BDOC [2453].

Contaminant Name: Natural Organic Matter | Function: Conventional Treatment | Details: Jar tests show that conventional treatment can remove up 54 percent of TOC, 70 percent of DOC, and 74 percent of BDOC. Removal percentage depends on the influent water, coagulant type, and coagulant dose [2447, 2454, 2465, 2466, 2467].

Contaminant Name: Natural Organic Matter | Function: Granular Activated Carbon | Details: A full-scale study observed initial removal in GAC contactors of 60 percent of TOC and 70 to 77 percent of DOC. Removal declined over time. After 40,000 bed volumes treated, removal decreased to 40 percent of TOC and 26 percent of DOC for coal-based GAC and 20 percent of TOC and 8 percent of DOC for peat GAC [2469]. Full-scale and bench-scale studies of contactors designed to be biological activated carbon filters also showed high initial removals (87 to 90 percent), which the authors attributed to GAC adsorption prior to the development of biological activity [2446, 2447].

Contaminant Name: Natural Organic Matter | Function: Ion Exchange | Details: Jar tests of magnetic ion exchange resin (MIEX) showed removal of 32 to 86 percent of DOC and 41 to 68 percent of TOC, with increasing removals at higher resin doses and contact times [2462, 2463, 2464]. Removals were also higher in waters with higher starting specific UV absorbance [2464]. Comparable jar tests using conventional anion exchange resins removed only 7 to 13 percent of DOC, although removals did increase above these levels at high doses and long contact times [2463].

Contaminant Name: Natural Organic Matter | Function: Membrane Filtration | Details: A full-scale drinking water treatment plant in Canada reported removing 64 percent of DOC using ultrafiltration [2468]. In a study of the Fyne process [2473], ultrafiltration membranes demonstrated good TOC removal. An ES404 membrane achieved 86 to 91 percent TOC removal in recycle mode and 90 to 96 percent TOC removal in dead-end mode.  A CA2PF membrane achieved 62 to 71 percent TOC removal in recycle mode and 86 percent TOC removal in dead-end mode.

Contaminant Name: Natural Organic Matter | Function: Membrane Separation | Details: A full-scale drinking water treatment plant in Canada reported removing 64 percent of DOC using ultrafiltration [2468]. In a study of the Fyne process [2473], Nanofiltration membranes demonstrated good TOC removal. An AFC30 membrane achieved 91 and 95 percent TOC removal in recycle mode and 94 percent TOC removal in dead-end mode.

Contaminant Name: Natural Organic Matter | Function: Other Treatment | Details: A bench-scale study using a photocatalytic batch reactor with lamps emitting UV light at 253.7 nm and a 2,000 mg/L dose of titanium dioxide found DOC removal of 35 percent, increasing to 55 percent when 50 mg/L of powdered activated carbon was added to the reactor [2447].

Contaminant Name: Natural Organic Matter | Overall: Research was identified evaluating the following treatment technologies for the removal of natural organic matter, measured in various ways, including as TOC, DOC, BDOC, and AOC:

Contaminant Name: Natural Organic Matter | Function: Ozone | Details: Two full-scale studies measured natural organic matter removal by ozone as a pre-treatment prior to biological filtration. Removal of TOC and DOC by the ozonation process alone ranged from 2.6 to 31.2 percent and 2.1 to 30.8 percent, respectively [2442, 2452]. Ozonation actually increased BDOC and AOC, while removing the nonbiodegradable fraction of DOC, thus making the remaining fraction more amenable to removal by biological treatment [2442].
Contaminant Name: Natural Organic Matter | Function: Powdered Activated Carbon | Details: Jar tests show a maximum of 78 percent removal of DOC by PAC [2447].

Contaminant Name: Nitrobenzene | Description: Nitrobenzene is a yellow liquid with an almond-like odor.  Most of (97%) nitrobenzene is produced is retained in closed systems for use in synthesizing  aniline and aniline derivaties, such as methyl diphenyl diisocyanate (MDI).  Nitrobenzene is also used as solvent in manufacturing of paints, shoe polishes, floor polishes, metal polishes, explosives, rubber chemicals, pesticides, dyes, and pharmaceuticals (such as acetaminophen). [1905, 1906]   
In 1992, releases of nitrobenzene to environment was reported to the Toxic Chemical Release Inventory by certain types of U.S.industries, totaled about 917,000 lbs.  Of this amount, 52,000 (5.6%) was released to the atmosphere, 442 lbs (0.05%) released to surface water, and 865,000 lbs (94.32%) were released in underground injection sites.  Nitrobenzene in air can photochemically degrade within few hours, and easily biodegradable in soil and water.  [1905] 
Occurane of nitrobenzene in water is most likely due to effluent discharge during the manufacturing process.  Nitrobenzene rarely is carried through finsihed water; it is lkely to be lost to air or degrade by sewage organisms.  The survey of 4,000 publicly owned treatment works and industrial sites showed highest nitrobenzne concentration in effluent associated with wastewater from the organic and plastic industries.  [1908]
Nitrobenzene is reasonably anticipated to be a human carcinogen based on sufficinet evidence of carcinogenicity in experiemntal animal. [1907]  The general public is potentially exposed to nitrobenzene in the environment through inhalation of ambient air, ingestion of water, or dermal contact with products or water containing nitrobenzene. [1907]  The primary toxic effects resulting from acute exposure to nitrobenzene by inhalation, oral or dermal routes is methemoglobinemia and accompanying anoxia and erythrocyte damage.  Nervous system effects may also be experienced, but may be partially due to the anoxia from the methemoglobinemia. [1905, 1906]  Limited evidence suggests that the liver may be a target organ in humans following extended inhalation exposure to nitrobenzene. [1906]
The US EPA regulates nitrobenzene under the Clean Air Act (CAA) and Clean Water Act (CWA).  Nitrobenzene is also regulated under the Federal Insecticide, Fungicide, and Rodenticide Act (FIFRA) and Resource Conservation and Recovery Act (RCRA).  Nitrobenzene is not regulated in drinking water however it is included in US EPA's Contaminant Candidate List 3 (CCL3).  The CCL3 is a list of contaminants that are currently not subjected to any proposed or promulgated national primary drinking water regulations, that are known or anticipated to occur in public water system, and which may get regulated under the Safe Drinking Water Act (SDWA).
Date of Literature Search: October 2010

Contaminant Name: Nitrobenzene | Function: Adsorptive Media | Details: Based on the results of one study, 72.6 percent nitrobenzene can be removed using mesoporous molecular sieves (MCM-41) from aqueous solution in 1 minute contact time. Nitrobenzene removal using MCM-41 is largely dependent on the initial concentration and solution pH.

Contaminant Name: Nitrobenzene | Function: Biological Filtration | Details: Biologial filtration using a hybrid reactor comprising of trickling filter and activated sludge process was found to be highly effective in the removal of nitrobenzene (97.93%) based on the data available from one study.  Nitrobenzene removal is largely dependent on carbon : nitrogen (substrate : co-substrate) and hydraulic retention time (HRT) in the system.

Contaminant Name: Nitrobenzene | Function: Biological Treatment | Details: Biological degradation of nitrobenzene using a sequential anaerobic-aerobic process (54 to 100 percent), Shewanella putrefaciens CN32 (53 to 100 percent), bacteria containing toluene degradative pathways (50 to 100 percent), and Comamonas sp. Strain JS765 (28 to 100 percent) was found to be highly effective in bench-scale studies.  Biodegradation can be used to achieve higher nitrobenzene degradation.

Contaminant Name: Nitrobenzene | Function: Other Treatment | Details: Nitrobenzene degradation using zero-valent iron (up to 95 percent) and carbon nano tube electrode was found to be effective.  Nitrobenzene removal using zero-valent iron was found to be dependant on solution pH and reaction time.  Carbon nano tube packed bed reactor was able to remove over 95 percent of nitrobenzene in the 50 minute reaction time.

Contaminant Name: Nitrobenzene | Overall: The following treatment processes are considered effective for the removal of nitrobenzene: adsorptive media using MCM-41 (up to 72 percent removal) 1987, biological filtration (up to 98 percent removal)1913, biological treatment using Shewanella putrefaciens CN32 1900, Comamonas sp. Strain JS765 (up to 100 percent removal) 1909, ozone in combination of copper and Fe(III) (95 percent removal) 1923, ultraviolet irradiation (up to 90 percent) 1921, ultraviolet irradiation hydrogen peroxide (up to 100 percent) 1920,  and ultraviolet irradiation ozone (up to 100 percent) 1922, 1923.  Performance of these technologies can vary based on water quality and treatment characteristics.
Research identified evaluating the following treatment technologies for removal of nitrobenzene:

Contaminant Name: Nitrobenzene | Function: Ozone | Details: Ozone alone was moderately effective (up to 30 percent) in removing nitrobenzene from aqueous solution. However effective removal (up to 65 percent) of nitrobenzene was observed in bench-scale studies where ozone was used in combination with copper and FeOOH.  Higher removal (up to 95 percent) were reported in absence of humic acid present in the water.

Contaminant Name: Nitrobenzene | Function: Ultraviolet Irradiation | Details: Direct photolysis of nitrobenzene with experiments using UVC (100 to 290 nm) was found to be effetive (up to 90 percent).  The effect of UV irradiation can not be enhanced by Fe (III).

Contaminant Name: Nitrobenzene | Function: Ultraviolet Irradiation and Hydrogen Peroxide | Details: Ultraviolet irradiation of nitrobenzene in presence of hydrogen peroxide was found to be effective (up to 95 percent).  Addition of Fe(III) was found to enhance nitrobenzene degradation.  H2O2 doses were high, and pHs were low, compared to typical drinking water treatment conditions.

Contaminant Name: Nitrobenzene | Function: Ultraviolet Irradiation and Ozone | Details: Ultraviolet irradiation and ozone can be very effective process for nitrobenzene degradation (up to 100 percent).  Lower nitrobenzene degradation was observed with experiments conducted in presence of higher humic acid concentrations.

Contaminant Name: Nitrofen | Description: Nitrofen is the common name of the herbicide with the IUPAC name 2, 4-dichlorophenyl-p-nitrophenyl ether.  There are no current commercial uses for nitrofen.  Nitrofen was previously used as a contact herbicide for pre- and post-emergence control of annual grasses and broadleaf weeds on a variety of food and ornamental crops. [1941]  Nitrofen is no longer manufactured or sold in the United States [1941, 1942] 
It is not regulated in drinking water in the United States.  It was listed on the draft Contaminant Candidate List 3, but was removed before the list was finalized. 
Nitrofen is reasonably anticipated to be an endocrine disruptor [1944] and human carcinogen based on sufficient evidence of carcinogenicity experimental animals but no data are available in humans. [1941, 1942]  The primary routes of potential human exposure to nitrofen are inhalation, dermal contact, and ingestion. [1941, 1942]  
It is not known to degrade in water. 
Date of literature search: 2010

Contaminant Name: Nitrofen | Function: Adsorptive Media | Details: One study showed that muck soil, H-AL-Bentonite and Ca-Bentonite was able to remove 95% and 79% nitrofen at various initial concentrations, respectively.  Nitrofen adsorption using H-AL-Kaolinite and Ca-Kaolinite was considerably lower, 0 to 25% range. [1943]

Contaminant Name: Nitrofen | Function: Biological Treatment | Details: Removal of nitrofen by biological treatment was found to be quite effective.  One bench-scale study reported removal between 10 to 78 percent by white-rot basidiomycete Coriolus versicolor microbial cultures.  Rapid nitrofen degradation was observed at lower ammonium nitrate concentrations. [1943]

Contaminant Name: Nitrofen | Overall: Adsorption and biodegradation process were fond to be effective in removing nitrofen from solution. Performance of these technologies can vary based on water quality and treatment characteristics.
Research was identified evaluating the following treatment technologies for the removal of nitrofen:

Contaminant Name: Oxamyl | Description: Oxamyl is a carbamate pesticide for control of insecticide, mites and nematodes [1511]. Its chemical name is methyl N',N'-dimethyl-N-[(methylcarbamoyl)oxy]-1-thiooxamimidate, with trade names of Vydate and Vydate L [1511].
Oxamyl was first registered in 1974 [1511]. It is used on many crops, including apples, bananas, carrots, celery, citrus and cotton etc [1511]. Cotton accounts for most of the usage; approximately 600 thousand pounds out of 800 thousand pounds of oxamyl active ingredient is used on cotton on an annual basis [1511]. Oxamyl is not applied for residential uses [1511].  Oxamyl can over stimulate the nervous system and cause nausea, dizziness, confusion [1511]. Very high exposures, for example, from accidents or major spills, can cause respiratory paralysis and death [1511].
The U.S. Environmental Protection Agency established a maximum contaminant level (MCL) of 0.2 mg/L and a maximum contaminant level goal (MCLG) of 0.2 mg/L for oxamyl in drinking water.  Adsorption onto granular activated carbon is the designated Best Available Technology for control of oxamyl.
Oxamyl is highly soluble in water.  Its major degradates identified in fate studies were oxime and dimethyloxamic acid (DMOA) [1511]. Hydrolysis of oxamyl depends on pH; it degrades rapidly in neutral or alkaline conditions (30% degradation in six hours at pH 9.1), but more stable in acidic conditions (no degradation in 11 days at pH 4.7) [1518]. Degradation by photolysis appears significant in acidic surface water but not in soil [1511]. Oxamyl degrades in soil by chemical and biological processes [1511]. Under aerobic conditions, oxamyl has a half-life of two to four weeks in soil; under anaerobic conditions, its half-life is less than one week in soil [1511].
Oxamyl concentrations in groundwater were generally between 1 to 2 ppb based on a non-chemical specific study in Suffolk County, Long Island and a prospective groundwater study in cotton growing area of North Carolina [1511]. Oxamyl was not detected in 432 wells monitored in California that were not necessarily located close to an agriculture field, according to well data reported to the Department of Pesticide Regulation between 1996 and 1997 [1519]. In finished drinking water, less than 0.1% of approximately 13,000 systems detected oxamyl, while no system had oxamyl concentration above the MCL [1516].
Date of Literature Search: July 2009

Contaminant Name: Oxamyl | Function: GAC Isotherm | Details: An isotherm study reported Freundlich parameters for oxamyl in distilled-deionized water. The reported Freundlich constants are (1,740 μg/g) (L/μg)^1/n and 0.793 for K and 1/n, respectively [1486]. Results and discussion were not presented in detail.

Contaminant Name: Oxamyl | Function: Granular Activated Carbon | Details: Granular activated carbon is currently the best available technology (BAT) designated by US Environmental Protection Agency [1522]. Removal efficiency ranges from 85 to 95 percent depending upon design parameters [1520, 1521, 1522]. Removal of oxamyl by an activated carbon filter (Pentek model RFC20-BB) was reported as 99 percent in a lab water [1489].  The GAC filter tested was a component of a point-of-entry Hyd-RO-Secure drinking water treatment system. The GAC influent contained 210 μg/L oxamyl with the pH level of 7.2.

Contaminant Name: Oxamyl | Function: Membrane Separation | Details: Removal of oxamyl with reverse osmosis membrane was very effective in four POU or POE studies (equal or greater than 99 percent removal) under the EPA's Environmental Technology Verification program.

Contaminant Name: Oxamyl | Overall: The following processes were found to be effective for the removal of oxamyl: Granular Activated Carbon (greater than 85 percent removal), reverse osmosis membrane (greater than 99 percent removal), solar ultraviolet irradiation with catalyst (removal efficiency depending on contact time), solar ultraviolet irradiation and hydrogen peroxide with catalyst (removal efficiency depending on contact time), and ultraviolet irradiation with ozone (83 percent removal).  GAC is the designated best available technology for control of oxamyl.
Research has identified GAC isotherm data and evaluation of five treatment technologies for oxamyl removal:

Contaminant Name: Oxamyl | Function: Ultraviolet Irradiation | Details: Two studies suggest that ultraviolet irradiation from natural sunlight combined with a catalyst can effectively degradate oxamyl [1515, 1517]. The catalysts tested were titanium dioxide (TiO2) and sodium decatungstate (Na4W10O32). One study showed oxamyl degradation of 80% with 0.2 g/L TiO2 and 85% with 1.5E-3 mol/L Na4W10O32 [1515]. The other study showed approximately 84% removal in 65 minutes with 0.2 g/L TiO2 [1517].

Contaminant Name: Oxamyl | Function: Ultraviolet Irradiation and Hydrogen Peroxide | Details: One study suggests solar ultraviolet (UV) irradiation and hydrogen peroxide with iron as catalyst (i.e. photo-Fenton process) can be effective for oxamyl degradation. Oxamyl removal efficiency increases with increasing UV contact time while hydrogen peroxide and iron concentrations are fixed. 82% degradation was reached in approximately 23 minutes with 200 - 500 mg/L hydrogen peroxide and 20 mg/L iron [1517]. The pH level was in the range of 2.7 to 2.9 to avoid iron hydroxide precipitation [1517].

Contaminant Name: Oxamyl | Function: Ultraviolet Irradiation and Ozone | Details: Ultraviolet irradiation (UV) and ozone treatment was reported to remove oxamyl effectively in a lab water by 83 percent [1489]. The UV and ozone unit was a component of a point-of-entry drinking water treatment system. The system consisted of a reverse osmosis (RO) module, the UV and ozone module (named as advanced simultaneous oxidation process, or ASOP), followed by a granular activated carbon (GAC) filter. The UV contact time was 33 seconds; however, the UV intensity and ozone dose were not reported. The influent to the UV and ozone module contained 1200 μg/L oxamyl with the pH level in the range of 7.0 to 8.0. The effluent oxamyl concentration from the UV and ozone unit was 210 μg/L.

Contaminant Name: Per- and Polyfluoroalkyl Substances | Description: Per- and polyfluoroalkyl substances (PFASs) are fluorinated aliphatic substances with unique properties, such as being both hydrophobic, lipophobic, and extremely stable due to the strength of the C-F bond [2539]. Their properties have led to their extensive use as surface active agents in products like stain repellants and fire-fighting foams [2527, 2539]. The two most frequently studied PFASs are perfluorooctane sulphonate (PFOS) and perfluorooctanoic acid (PFOA). Both PFOS and PFOA are compounds with eight carbon atoms. This group entry covers treatability data for PFOS, PFOA, and a number of other PFASs, including those with more or fewer carbon atoms. It currently includes data for:

Carboxylic acids:
 Perfluorotetradecanioc acid (PFTetA): Chemical Abstract Service number (CAS No.) 376-06-7, a 14-carbon compound

PFOA is a synthetic chemical used to make fluoropolymers, which are utilized in non-stick cookware, waterproof clothing, and many other industrial applications (aerospace, automotive, building/construction, chemical processing, electronics, semiconductors, and textile industries) due to their valuable properties of fire resistance and repellency of oil, stain, grease, and water [1758]. PFOS has a number of industrial and commercial uses, including as a surfactant for fire-fighting foam, a mist suppressant for metal-plating baths, and providing grease and water resistance to materials such as textiles, carpets, and paper [2166, 2167]. The U.S. manufacturer of PFOS phased out production of PFOS and PFHxS in 2002 and major companies worldwide are working to reduce product content, use, and emissions of PFOA and longer chain perfluorocarboxylic acids [2166, 2441, 2512]. These PFASs, however, are persistent and bioaccumulative [2441, 2512, 2520, 2541]. Therefore, they are expected to remain of environmental relevance for the foreseeable future [2441, 2512]. In addition, the phase out of longer chain PFASs could lead to shifts in production towards shorter chain PFASs [2521].
PFASs can be emitted into the air and water by industrial processes that manufacture or use fluorochemicals. PFASs can also enter groundwater and surface water through disposal of waste and sewage sludge and as a result of fire-fighting activities and training [2540]. PFASs have been detected in waters worldwide, including in surface water, groundwater, tap and bottled water, and in industrial and municipal wastewater [1705, 1756, 2164. 2166. 2441, 2512, 2520].
EPA finalized a National Primary Drinking Water Regulation (NPDWR) establishing legally enforceable levels, called Maximum Contaminant Levels (MCLs), for six PFAS in drinking water. PFOA, PFOS, PFHxS, PFNA, and HFPO-DA as contaminants with individual MCLs, and PFAS mixtures containing at least two or more of PFHxS, PFNA, HFPO-DA, and PFBS using a Hazard Index MCL to account for the combined and co-occurring levels of these PFAS in drinking water. For more information regarding the final PFAS NPDWR, please use the Safe Drinking Water Act link at the top of this page.

Contaminant Name: Per- and Polyfluoroalkyl Substances | Function: Adsorptive Media | Details: Bench-scale studies tested adsorption of per- and polyfluoroalkyl substances (PFASs) using novel media including: magnetic nanoparticles with different polymer coatings [2535], gravity-driven nanofibrous membrane [2549], functionalized and hybrid hydrogel sorbents [2617, 2606], adsorptive photocatalysts [2623], non-ion exchange polymers [2631], swellable modified silica with an entrapped cationic polymer [2633], hemp protein powder [2636], modified bentonite [2641, 2837], amine-functionalized covalent organic frameworks [2618], cationic covalent organic frameworks [2670] mixed mineral and graphene/carbon-based materials [2622, 2656, 2831], zinc-aluminum and magnesium-aluminum layered double hydroxides [2654], zirconium-based metal organic frameworks [2661], amidoxime surface-functionalized electrospun polyacrylonitrile nanofibrous adsorbent [2664], biochar materials [2663, 2669, 2843], clay-based adsorbents [2665], cyclodextrin polymers [2638, 2820, 2837, 2844], nonionic resin adsorbents [2677, 2822], two-dimensional titanium metal carbides [2677], amphiphilic-coated aluminum oxide hydroxide membranes [2828], wood bark [2843], and aluminium and silicon reduced argon-oxygen decarburization steel slags [2843]. One bench-scale study found that amyloid fibril membranes had the capacity to remove certain PFASs, although they generally required low pH levels to achieve high removal efficiency. The study concluded that PFAS removal was due to adsorption (as opposed to size exclusion, which would otherwise categorize the technology as membrane filtration or separation) [2657]. A pilot study achieved greater than 89 percent removal of PFOS from groundwater using a batch reactor with an aluminum- based sorbent that was not replaced between batches over a period of 14 days [2570].
 Some of these media achieved moderate to high removals in naturally occurring surface water or groundwater [2535, 2636, 2641, 2606, 2622, 2638, 2656, 2657, 2661, 2663, 2665, 2669, 2670, 2675]; others were tested only in spiked ultrapure water. 

For specific PFASs, results for the best performing of these media include:
• Up to greater than 99 percent removal of perfluorooctanoic acid (PFOA) 
• Up to greater than 99 percent removal of perfluorooctanesulfonic acid (PFOS) 
• Up to greater than 99 percent removal of perfluorononanoic acid (PFNA) 
• Up to greater than 99 percent removal of perfluorohexyl sulfonate (PFHxS)
• Up to greater than 99 percent removal of perfluorobutyl sulfonate (PFBS)
