Although results varied depending on the specific media tested, many of these studies show that perfluorinated sulfonates are more readily adsorbed than perfluoroalkyl acids and longer chain PFASs are more readily adsorbed than shorter chain compounds [2535, 2617, 2636, 2641, 2622, 2638, 2654, 2657, 2663, 2670].
Contaminant Name: Per- and Polyfluoroalkyl Substances | Function: Aeration and Air Stripping | Details: At a full-scale site, packed tower aeration was not effective for removing PFASs [2441].

Contaminant Name: Per- and Polyfluoroalkyl Substances | Function: Biological Filtration | Details: A full-scale study [2175] of a plant treating reclaimed domestic wastewater found biological filtration to be ineffective for removing most per- and polyfluoroalkyl substances (PFASs) with some success in removing perfluorooctane sulfonate (PFOS) (48 percent). In one sampling event, PFOS was removed to below the limits of reporting (0.3 ng/L). A second full-scale study [2851] found 89 to 93 percent removal of perfluorooctanoic acid (PFOA), PFOS, perfluorohexanoic acid (PFHxA), and perfluoroheptanoic acid (PFHpA) and 52 percent removal of PFBA. The same study found increases in concentrations of PFHxS. Moderate removal of perfluorodecanoic acid (PFDA) (57 percent) and PFOS (63 percent) was observed in the biological filtration step at a pilot-scale potable reuse facility, but removal of shorter chain PFASs was much lower (-59 to 25 percent) [2659]. Removal of perfluorononanoic acid (PFNA) and PFOS in a full-scale, biologically active carbon filter treating surface water was inconsistent, with some samples showing reductions of PFOS, but others showing increases [2668].

Contaminant Name: Per- and Polyfluoroalkyl Substances | Function: Biological Treatment | Details: One bench-scale study [2161] examined removal of various PFAS using supernatant from a domestic wastewater activated sludge process as a microbial source in both aerobic and anaerobic test conditions. Although decreases in some of the PFAS tested were observed, concentrations of these PFAS also decreased in the controls. Therefore, the authors concluded that there was no evidence supporting biodegradation for any of the PFAS tested.

Contaminant Name: Per- and Polyfluoroalkyl Substances | Function: Chemical Treatment | Details: One lab study observed >90% removal of perfluorooctanoic acid (PFOA) via oxidation by persulfate in hot (85 degrees C) water after 30 hours reaction time. Removal in cooler water (25 degrees C) was negligible. The presence of iron reduced PFOA oxidation somewhat. Degradation intermediates included perfluoroheptanoic acid (PFHpA), perfluorohexanoic acid (PFHxA), perfluoropentanoic acid (PFPeA), and perfluorobutanoic acid (PFBA) [2550].

Contaminant Name: Per- and Polyfluoroalkyl Substances | Function: Chloramine | Details: A full-scale study [2175] found that chloramines are ineffective in removing perfluorooctane sulfonate (PFOS) (-96 percent removal) in combination with a primary settling process at a treatment plant treating reclaimed water. The author stated that the difficulty in assessing contaminant removal more accurately was likely due to not accounting for hydraulic residence times during the study's grab sampling procedures.

Contaminant Name: Per- and Polyfluoroalkyl Substances | Function: Chlorine | Details: Chlorination was ineffective for removing per- and polyfluoroalkyl substances (PFASs) at seven full-scale sites [2441, 2509, 2619, 2685].

Contaminant Name: Per- and Polyfluoroalkyl Substances | Function: Chlorine Dioxide | Details: Chlorine dioxide was ineffective for removing per- and polyfluoroalkyl substances at a full-scale site [2441].

Contaminant Name: Per- and Polyfluoroalkyl Substances | Function: Conventional Treatment | Details: Bench-scale studies [2536, 2548, 2553, 2642] were able to achieve moderate removals (50 to 95 percent) of some per- and polyfluoroalkyl substances (PFASs) by coagulation, but only by using high coagulant dosage and/or low pH. Removals at lower doses and neutral pH were more limited (40 percent or less). Sampling at full scale [1705, 1706, 1756, 2164, 2174, 2175, 2441, 2508, 2509, 2518, 2619, 2645, 2668, 2683, 2685] and pilot [2518] drinking water treatment facilities observed either no removal or inconsistent removal of PFASs by conventional treatment.

Contaminant Name: Per- and Polyfluoroalkyl Substances | Function: GAC Isotherm | Details: Isotherm data are available for adsorption onto various types of granular activated carbon and onto other media, powdered activated carbon [2637], including biochar [2830], anion exchange resin [2426, 2611, 2621, 2637, 2822] and novel adsorbents [2522, 2822, 2835, 2840, 2842].

Contaminant Name: Per- and Polyfluoroalkyl Substances | Function: Granular Activated Carbon | Details: Removal of per- and polyfluoroalkyl substances (PFASs) by granular activated carbon (GAC) has been examined in a number of bench and pilot studies and in full scale application. For some of these studies, the primary focus was optimizing the removal of perfluorooctane sulfonate (PFOS) and/or perfluorooctanoic acid (PFOA). Other studies reported on full-scale GAC installations whose objective was treating conventional, as opposed to trace, contaminants. As a result, GAC's performance for the removal of PFASs was highly variable. In the best cases, however, GAC can be quite effective for many PFASs, with removals of up to greater than 99 percent at bench [1700, 2157, 2158, 2423, 2426, 2506, 2510, 2511, 2515, 2516, 2517, 2519, 2528, 2534, 2536, 2558, 2561, 2566, 2567, 2568, 2574, 2575, 2620, 2621, 2623, 2626, 2631, 2638, 2641, 2643, 2646, 2647, 2663, 2665, 2667, 2675, 2682, 2818, 2822, 2824, 2827, 2828, 2831, 2832, 2839, 2840, 2844, 2848, 2849], pilot [2430, 2518, 2532, 2538, 2559, 2560, 2562, 2566, 2571, 2574, 2616, 2628, 2651, 2652, 2658, 2659, 2668, 2672, 2676, 2682, 2821, 2834, 2837, 2841], and full-scale [1696, 1756, 1757, 2166, 2174, 2424, 2441, 2504, 2505, 2508, 2509, 2513, 2518, 2558, 2564, 2565, 2568, 2570, 2572, 2609, 2616, 2619, 2645, 2667, 2682]. Point-of-use GAC devices and pitcher filters are capable of high removals (up to greater than 99 percent) [2430, 2655].
For specific PFASs, results include:

• Up to greater than 99 percent removal of perfluorooctanoic acid (PFOA)
• Up to greater than 99 percent of perfluorooctane sulfonate (PFOS)
• Up to greater than 99 percent removal of perfluorononanoic acid (PFNA)
• Up to greater than 99 percent removal of perfluorohexyl sulfonate (PFHxS)
• Up to greater than 99 percent removal of perfluorobutyl sulfonate (PFBS)
• Up to 95 percent removal of HFPO-DA (commonly known as GenX)
• Up to 90 percent removal of perfluoroethane sulfonate (PFEtS)
• Up to 90 percent removal of perfluoropropane sulfonate (PFPrS)
• Up to greater than 99 percent removal of perfluorobutanoic acid (PFBA)
• Up to 90 percent removal of perfluoropentanoic acid (PFPeA)
• Up to greater than 99 percent removal of perfluoropentyl sulfonate (PFPeS)
• Up to greater than 99 percent removal of perfluorohexanoic acid (PFHxA)
• Up to greater than 99 percent removal of perfluoroheptanoic acid (PFHpA)
• Up to greater than 99 percent removal of perfluoroheptyl sulfonate (PFHpS)
• 96 percent removal of perfluorononane sulfonate (PFNS)
• Up to greater than 99 percent removal of perfluorodecanoic acid (PFDA)
• Up to greater than 99 percent removal of perfluoroundecanoic acid (PFUnA)
• Up to greater than 99 percent removal of perfluorododecanoic acid (PFDoA)
• Up to greater than 99 percent removal of perfluorotridecanoic acid (PFTriA)
• Up to greater than 99 percent removal of perfluorotetradecanoic acid (PFTetA)
• Up to 90 percent removal of perfluorobutylsulfonamide (PFBSA)
• Up to 59 percent removal of perfluorohexanesulfonamide (PFHxSA)
• Up to greater than 99 percent removal of perfluorooctanesulfonamide (PFOSA)
• Up to 70 percent removal of perfluoro-2-methoxyacetic acid (PFMOAA)
• 90 percent removal of perﬂuoro-3,5-dioxahexanoic acid (PFO2HxA)
• 90 percent removal of perfluoro-3,5,7-trioxaoctanoic acid (PFO3OA)
• 90 percent removal of perfluoro-3,5,7,9-butaoxadecanoic acid (PFO4DA)
• Up to 98 percent removal of fluorotelomer sulfonate 4:2 (FtS 4:2)
• Up to greater than 99 percent removal of fluorotelomer sulfonate 6:2 (FtS 6:2)
• Up to greater than 99 percent removal of fluorotelomer sulfonate 8:2 (FtS 8:2)
• Up to 65 percent removal of perfluoro-4-(perfluoroethyl)cyclohexylsulfonate (PFECHS)
• Up to greater than 99 percent removal of Nafion BP2
• 90 percent removal of perfluoropropanoic acid (PFPrA)
• Up to greater than 99 percent removal of 9-chlorohexadecafluoro-3-oxanone-1-sulfonic acid (9Cl-PF3ONS)
• Up to greater than 99 percent removal of 11-chloroeicosafluoro-3-oxaundecane-1-sulfonic acid (11Cl-PF3OUdS)
• Up to 96 percent removal of ammonium 4,8-dioxa-3H-perfluorononanoate (ADONA)
• Up to greater than 99 percent removal of 2-(N-Ethyl-perfluorooctanesulfonamido)acetate (N-EtFOSAA)
• Up to greater than 99 percent removal of 2-(N-Methylperfluorooctanesulfonamido)acetate (N-MeFOSAA)

The literature generally shows that perfluorinated sulfonates are more readily adsorbed than perfluoroalkyl acids and longer chain PFASs are more readily adsorbed than shorter chain compounds [2423, 2424, 2515, 2532, 2540, 2577, 2609, 2620, 2626, 2643, 2638, 2663]. The presence of organic matter can have a negative effect on performance, particularly for shorter chain PFASs [2577].
Some full-scale studies found PFAS concentration of the treated water to equal or exceed that of the raw water. In most of these cases, however, it appears that the GAC had been in service for a long period (e.g., at least 12 months, more than 6 years) without recent regeneration or replacement [2166, 2174, 2424, 2441, 2518, 2667]. In another case, GAC time in service was not reported. However, the negative removal efficiency was attributed to desorption of previously adsorbed PFOA, suggesting the media had been in use for some significant length of time [2645]. Full-scale studies with more frequent replacement showed successful contaminant removal [1756, 1757, 2174, 2424, 2441, 2504, 2508, 2509, 2513, 2518, 2558, 2564, 2565, 2568, 2570, 2572, 2609, 2682].

Contaminant Name: Per- and Polyfluoroalkyl Substances | Function: Hydrogen Peroxide | Details: Removal of perfluorooctane sulfonate (PFOS) from water using hydrogen peroxide was found to be ineffective in one study [2160]. The bench-scale study achieved 2 percent PFOS removal with an H2O2 dose of 3.0 mg/L. The same study showed a Fenton's reagent test achieved no removal utilizing 500 mg/L FeSO4 in combination with 5 mg/L H2O2. The lack of removal was thought to be due to the chemical structure of PFOS. Perfluorinated surfactants are designed to resist heat and reducing/oxidizing agents.

Contaminant Name: Per- and Polyfluoroalkyl Substances | Function: Ion Exchange | Details: Removal of per- and polyfluoroalkyl substances (PFASs) using anion exchange resins can be effective. Bench- [2426, 2427, 2503, 2515, 2535, 2563, 2576, 2612, 2613, 2620, 2621, 2625, 2627, 2638, 2641, 2643, 2671, 2675, 2677, 2686, 2820, 2822, 2827, 2829, 2835, 2840, 2844] and pilot- [2504, 2523, 2534, 2538, 2559, 2560, 2562, 2564, 2571, 2616, 2821, 2823, 2834, 2837, 2841] scale studies found removals up to greater than 99 percent. Full-scale applications varied in their results, often depending on whether the treatment process was designed with the objective of removing the specific PFASs measured. The full-scale results showed removals from less than zero to greater than 99 percent [2424, 2441, 2504, 2568]. Among the resins that showed effectiveness were those designed for arsenic removal and perchlorate removal, as well as purpose-designed PFAS-selective resins [2424, 2441, 2504, 2523, 2534, 2538, 2559, 2560, 2562, 2564, 2638, 2641, 2643, 2820, 2821, 2823, 2827, 2834, 2835, 2837, 2840, 2844, 2892]. Magnetic resins designed for TOC removal achieved 55 to 90 percent removal in bench tests [2627], but similar resins showed little to no PFOA removal at full-scale [2441].
For specific PFASs, results include:

Up to greater than 99 percent removal of perfluorooctanoic acid (PFOA)
Up to greater than 99 percent removal of perfluorooctane sulfonate (PFOS)
Up to greater than 99 percent removal of perfluorononanoic acid (PFNA)
Up to greater than 99 percent removal of perfluorohexyl sulfonate (PFHxS)
Up to greater than 99 percent removal of perfluorobutyl sulfonate (PFBS)
Up to greater than 99 percent removal of HFPO-DA (commonly known as GenX)
90 percent removal of perfluoroethane sulfonate (PFEtS)
Up to greater than 95 percent removal of perfluoropentanoic acid (PFPeA) 
 Up to 74 percent removal of perfluoropentyl sulfonate (PFPeS)
Up to greater than 97 percent removal of perfluorohexanoic acid (PFHxA)
Up to 99 percent removal of perfluoroheptanoic acid (PFHpA)
Up to 93 percent removal of perfluoroheptyl sulfonate (PFHpS)
55 percent removal of perfluorononane sulfonate (PFNS)
Up to greater than 99 percent removal of perfluorodecanoic acid (PFDA)
90 percent removal of perfluoroundecanoic acid(PFUnA)

90 percent removal of perfluorotridecanoic acid (PFTriA)
98 percent removal of perfluorobutylsulfonamide (PFBSA)
99 percent removal of of perfluorohexanesulfonamide (PFHxSA)

Up to greater than 99 percent removal of fluorotelomer sulfonate 6:2 (FtS 6:2)

Ineffective (less than 0 percent removal) for 2-(N-Ethyl-perfluorooctanesulfonamido)acetate and 2-(N-Methylperfluorooctanesulfonamido)acetate (N-EtFOSAA and N-MeFOSAA)
Ineffective (less than 1 percent removal) for 4:2 fluorotelomer alcohol (4:2 FTOH)
Ineffective (5 percent removal) for 8:2 fluorotelomer alcohol (8:2 FTOH)
Up to 20 percent removal of 6:2 fluorotelomer sulfonamidopropyl betaine (6:2 FTAB)
Up to 65 percent removal of n-dimethylammoniopropyl perfluorohexane sulfonamide (AmPr-FHxSA)
Up to 75 percent removal of n-trimethylammoniopropyl perfluorohexane sulfonamide (TAmPr-FHxSA)
Up to 45 percent removal of perfluorooctanesulfonamide (PFOSA)

The literature also generally shows that ion exchange removes perfluoronated sulfonates more easily than perfluoroalkyl acids and longer chain PFASs more easily than shorter chain compounds [2424, 2515, 2523, 2538, 2540, 2577, 2621, 2627, 2827, 2829, 2834, 2841, 2844]. The presence of organic matter can have a negative effect on performance, particularly for shorter chain PFASs [2577].

Contaminant Name: Per- and Polyfluoroalkyl Substances | Function: Membrane Filtration | Details: Membrane filtration, including ultrafiltration and microfiltration, varied in effectiveness for removal of per- and polyfluoroalkyl substances (PFASs) in full-scale studies [1756, 2175, 2441]. These studies observed 2 to 56 percent removal of perfluorooctanoic acid (PFOA), -30 to 43 percent removal of perfluorooctane sulfonate (PFOS), and similarly inconsistent removal of other PFASs including: perfluorononanoic acid (PFNA), perfluorohexyl sulfonate (PFHxS), perfluorobutyl sulfonate (PFBS), perfluorobutanoic acid (PFBA), perfluoropentanoic acid (PFPeA), perfluorohexanoic acid (PFHxA), perfluoroheptanoic acid (PFHpA), perfluorodecanoic acid (PFDA), perfluoroundecanoic acid (PFUnA), perfluorododecanoic acid (PFDoA), perfluorooctanesulfonamide (PFOSA), fluorotelomer sulfonate 6:2 (FtS 6:2), 2-(N-Ethyl-perfluorooctanesulfonamido)acetate (N-EtFOSAA), and 2-(N-Methylperfluorooctanesulfonamido)acetate (N-MeFOSAA).
Removal of PFOS by ultrafiltration in a pilot-scale potable reuse facility was greater than 80 percent, starting from very low influent concentration (1.25 ng/L) [2659]. The same study showed only 5 percent removal of PFOA, 14 percent removal of PFHxS, 4 percent removal of PFDA, and concentrations of PFNA, PFHxA, PFHpA, PFPeA, and PFBS that increased between the feed and the filtrate (i.e., negative removal).
One bench-scale study [2524] observed moderate to high (69 to 84 percent) removal of perfluorohexanoic acid (PFHxA) from spiked lab water by ultrafiltration membranes. Another bench-scale study of ultrafiltration found an average 21 percent removal of PFOA and 22 percent removal of PFOS using a conventional membrane, increasing to an average of 50 percent and 52 percent respectively using a chemically modified membrane [2666]. A third bench-scale study [2832] found 32 percent removal of PFOS and 23 percent removal of PFOA by ultrafiltration.

Contaminant Name: Per- and Polyfluoroalkyl Substances | Function: Membrane Separation | Details: Removal of per- and polyfluoroalkyl substances (PFASs) from water using membrane separation was found to be quite effective. Bench [2163, 2173, 2423, 2441, 2514, 2524, 2530, 2547, 2610, 2642, 2647, 2650, 2653, 2836, 2850], pilot [2567, 2568, 2569, 2571, 2573, 2624, 2642, 2647, 2649, 2651, 2653, 2658], and full-scale [2175, 2424, 2428, 2441, 2509, 2651, 2658] studies evaluating several types of nanofiltration (NF) and reverse osmosis (RO) membranes achieved PFAS removals of up to greater than 99 percent. Point-of-use RO devices also obtained high removals [2430, 2567].
For specific PFASs, results include:

Up to greater than 99.9 percent removal of perfluorooctanoic acid (PFOA)
Up to greater than 99.3 percent removal of perfluorooctane sulfonate (PFOS)
Up to 99 percent removal of perfluorononanoic acid (PFNA)
Up to 99.8 percent removal of perfluorobutyl sulfonate (PFBS)
Up to greater than 99 percent removal of perfluorohexyl sulfonate (PFHxS)
Up to greater than 99 percent removal for HFPO-DA (commonly known as GenX)
Up to 99.9 percent removal of perfluorobutanoic acid (PFBA)
Up to greater than 99 percent removal of perfluoropentanoic acid (PFPeA)
Greater than 97.5 percent removal of perfluoropentanesulfonic acid (PFPeS)
Up to greater than 99 percent removal of perfluorohexanoic acid (PFHxA)
Up to greater than 99 percent removal of perfluorohexyl sulfonate (PFHxS)
Up to 99 percent removal of perfluoroheptanoic acid (PFHpA)
Up to 99 percent removal of perfluorononanoic acid (PFNA)
Up to greater than 99 percent removal of perfluorodecanoic acid (PFDA)
Up to 99 percent removal of perfluorodecyl sulfonate (PFDS)
Up to 99 percent removal of perfluoroundecanoic acid (PFUnA)
Up to greater than 87 percent removal of perfluorododecanoic acid (PFDoA)
Up to greater than 80 percent removal of perfluoro-3,5-dioxahexanoic acid (PFO2HxA)
From low influent levels to below limits of quantitation for perfluoro-3,5,7-trioxaoctanoic acid (PFO3OA)
Up to 98.5 percent removal of perfluorooctanesulfonamide (PFOSA)
Up to 98.5 percent removal of difluoro(perfluoromethoxy)acetic acid, also known as perfluoro-2-methoxyacetic acid (PFMOAA)
Up to greater than 99 percent removal of fluorotelomer sulfonate 6:2 (FtS 6:2)
From very low influent levels to below limits of quantitation for 2-(N-Ethyl-perfluorooctanesulfonamido)acetate (N-EtFOSAA)
Up to greater than 84 percent removal for 2-(N-Methylperfluorooctanesulfonamido)acetate (N-MeFOSAA)

Contaminant Name: Per- and Polyfluoroalkyl Substances | Function: Other Treatment | Details: Other processes that have been evaluated for the treatment of per- and polyfluoroalkyl substances (PFASs) in groundwater or at environmentally relevant concentrations (e.g., 1 milligram per liter or less) include electrochemical treatment [2629, 2630], electrocoagulation [2608], plasma treatment [2634], and ultrasonic irradiation [2815, 2816, 2817, 2819, 2825, 2833, 2838, 2845, 2846]. These studies were conducted at the bench scale and did not evaluate the treatment processes for practical use in larger-scale drinking water applications. 
The electrochemical processes [2629, 2630] achieved from less than 0 to greater than 99 percent removal or destruction of the PFASs studied, including perfluorooctanoic acid (PFOA), perfluorooctane sulfonate (PFOS), perfluorohexyl sulfonate (PFHxS), perfluorobutyl sulfonate (PFBS), perfluorobutanoic acid (PFBA), perfluoropentanoic acid (PFPeA), perfluorohexanoic acid (PFHxA), perfluoroheptanoic acid (PFHpA), and perfluoroheptyl sulfonate (PFHpS). These processes were generally more effective with increasing current density and increasing PFAS concentration. 
Electrocoagulation [2608] achieved from 59 to greater than 99 percent removal of or destruction of PFASs including PFOS, PFHxS, and PFBS.
Plasma treatment [2634] achieved 70 percent removal or destruction of PFHxS and greater than 99 percent removal or destruction of PFOS.
Ultrasound treatment [2815, 2816, 2817, 2819, 2825, 2833, 2838, 2845, 2846] varied in effectiveness from 9.5 to 99 percent removal or destruction of the PFASs studied, including PFOA, PFOS, PFHxS, PFBS, PFBA, and PFHxA. These processes were generally more effective with increasing irradiation time and frequency and power inputs.

Contaminant Name: Per- and Polyfluoroalkyl Substances | Overall: The following processes were found to be effective for the removal of perfluorooctanoic acid (PFOA), perfluorooctane sulfonate (PFOS), and various other per- and polyfluoroalkyl substances (PFASs): granular activated carbon (GAC) (up to > 99 percent), membrane separation (high pressure membranes such as nanofiltration and reverse osmosis) (up to > 99 percent), and ion exchange (up to > 99 percent). Various types of novel adsorptive media have also been found to effectively remove PFASs (up to > 99 percent removal) but results for these media published to date have been limited to bench scale. These results cover the removal of specific PFASs including, but not limited to, PFOA, PFOS, perfluorononanoic acid (PFNA), perfluorohexyl sulfonate (PFHxS), perfluorobutyl sulfonate (PFBS), and HFPO-DA (commonly known as GenX).
Additionally, powdered activated carbon has been found to remove PFOA (up to 95 percent removal). The exact percentage removal a water system may achieve with a given technology will be dependent upon a variety of factors, including source water quality and water system characteristics. Adsorptive media, GAC, and anion exchange are non-steady state technologies where the media will need to be periodically replaced or reactivated/regenerated to prevent contaminant breakthrough.
Processes found to be ineffective for the removal of PFAS were biological treatment (0 to 10 percent removal), chloramines (-96 percent removal), conventional treatment (comprised of the unit processes coagulation, flocculation, clarification, and filtration) (-222 to 59 percent removal), hydrogen peroxide (0 to 2 percent removal), ozone (-11 to 7 percent removal), ozone plus hydrogen peroxide (2-13 percent removal under typical drinking water pH conditions), slow sand filtration (-21 to 15 percent removal), UV photocatalysis using titanium dioxide (15 percent removal), and UV irradiation plus ozone (-2 percent removal).
Other treatment processes with varying results include low-pressure membrane filtration (specifically microfiltration or ultrafiltration) (-46 to 84 percent), biological filtration (-97 to 93 percent) and permanganate (1 to 53 percent). Negative values indicate that a study found a higher concentration in effluent water than in influent water possibly due to the analytical uncertainties or the oxidation of PFOS precursors into PFOS within the process. Some of the percent removal values reported (e.g., conventional treatment, permanganate) are higher than reasonably anticipated due to a variety of factors such as study conditions differing from the conditions of typical drinking water facilities, outlier data points, or multiple treatment processes being run simultaneously.
Studies were identified evaluating the following treatment technologies for the removal of PFASs:

Contaminant Name: Per- and Polyfluoroalkyl Substances | Function: Ozone | Details: Bench- [2160, 2647], pilot [2659, 2668], and full-scale studies [2174, 2175, 2441, 2508, 2509, 2518, 2619, 2645] found conventional ozonation ineffective for removal of per- and polyfluoroalkyl substances (PFASs). A demonstration study of a patented ozofractionation process, which used ozone gas to separate PFASs into a foam residual, found greater than 98 percent removal of total PFAS, including greater than 97 percent removal of perfluorooctanoic acid (PFOA), greater than 82 percent removal of perfluoropentanoic acid (PFPeA), greater than 96 percent removal of perfluorohexanoic acid (PFHxA), and greater than 99 percent removal of fluorotelomer sulfonate 6:2 (FtS 6:2) [2573].

Contaminant Name: Per- and Polyfluoroalkyl Substances | Function: Ozone and Hydrogen Peroxide | Details: A bench-scale study [2635] using ozone, followed by increasing pH to 11, followed by hydrogen peroxide addition consistently achieved reductions (14 percent to greater than 97 percent) of per- and polyfluoroalkyl substances (PFASs), including perfluorooctane sulfonate (PFOS), perfluorooctanoic acid (PFOA), perfluorohexyl sulfonate (PFHxS), perfluorobutanoic acid (PFBA), perfluorononanoic acid (PFNA), perfluoropentane sulfonate (PFPeS), and fluorotelomer sulfonate 6:2 (FtS 6:2). However, under some treatment conditions, concentrations of perfluoropentanoic acid (PFPeA), perfluorohexanoic acid (PFHxA), and perfluorobutyl sulfonate (PFBS) increased. The study did not test the treatment process at natural pH or evaluate whether treatment at very high pH would be practical for use in larger-scale drinking water applications. 
Other studies [2160, 2647, 2847] found removal of PFASs from water using ozone and hydrogen peroxide to be ineffective. One bench-scale study [2647], using an ozone dose of 1 mg/L and an unspecified hydrogen peroxide dose, found 4 to 13 percent removal of PFASs, including PFOA, PFOS, PFHxS, and perfluorohexanoic acid (PFHxA). Another bench-scale study [2160] achieved only 2 percent removal of PFOS with an applied ozone dose of 2.6 grams per hour and hydrogen peroxide dose of 3.0 mg/L. A pilot-scale study [2847] using an ozone dose of 15 mg/L and a hydrogen peroxide dose of 21.2 mg/L observed a 2 percent increase in the concentration of PFOS and a 5 percent increase in the concentration of PFOA.

Contaminant Name: Per- and Polyfluoroalkyl Substances | Function: Permanganate | Details: Removal of perfluorooctane sulfonate (PFOS) from water using permanganate was found to be somewhat effective in a bench-scale study [2162]. The study found contaminant removal of between 1 and 53 percent. Generally, removal was enhanced when dose, temperature, and reaction time increased, and when pH decreased.

Contaminant Name: Per- and Polyfluoroalkyl Substances | Function: Powdered Activated Carbon | Details: Bench- [1700, 2158, 2521, 2536, 2542, 2544, 2546, 2548, 2621, 2824] and pilot-scale [2518] tests have shown that PAC can be effective for removal of some per- and polyfluoroalkyl substances (PFASs). Removal depends on factors including PAC dosage, PAC particle size, contact time, and influent water organic carbon. The literature consistently shows that perfluorinated sulfonates are more readily adsorbed than perfluoroalkyl acids and longer chain PFASs are more readily adsorbed than shorter chain compounds. Specific results include:

Up to greater than 99 percent removal of perfluorooctanoic acid (PFOA)
Up to greater than 99 percent removal of perfluorooctane sulfonate (PFOS)

Up to 98 percent removal of perfluorononanoic acid (PFNA)
Up to greater than 90 percent removal of perfluorobutyl sulfonate (PFBS)
Up to 99 percent removal of perfluorohexyl sulfonate (PFHxS)
Less than 10 percent removal of perfluorobutanoic acid (PFBA)
Up to 40 percent removal of perfluoropentanoic acid (PFPeA)
Up to greater than 90 percent removal of perfluorohexanoic acid (PFHxA)
Up to greater than 90 percent removal of perfluoroheptanoic acid (PFHpA)
Up to greater than 90 percent removal of perfluorodecanoic acid (PFDA)
Contaminant Name: Per- and Polyfluoroalkyl Substances | Function: Precipitative Softening | Details: N/A

Contaminant Name: Per- and Polyfluoroalkyl Substances | Function: Slow Sand Filtration | Details: Removal of perfluorooctane sulfonate (PFOS) from water by slow sand filtration was not effective in two full-scale studies. Both studies found PFOS concentration of the treated water to exceed that of the raw water. One study found that slow sand filtration together with chlorination was able to remove between -6% to 15% of PFOS from lake water with influent concentration of 1.8 and 2.7 ng/L, respectively [1756]. Another study reported the combined removal of PFOS from groundwater (unconfined aquifer) by slow sand filtration/ozone/GAC to be between -21% to 5.3% before chlorination and 16% after chlorine contact tanks (influent = 20 ng/L) [2166].

Contaminant Name: Per- and Polyfluoroalkyl Substances | Function: Ultraviolet Irradiation | Details: A full-scale study showed ultraviolet irradiation to be ineffective for removing most of the PFASs sampled, including perfluorooctanoic acid (PFOA), perfluorononanoic acid (PFNA), perfluorobutyl sulfonate (PFBS), perfluoroheptanoic acid (PFHpA), perfluorohexanoic acid (PFHxA), perfluoropentanoic acid (PFPeA), and perfluorobutanoic acid (PFBA). However, the study observed partial removal (an average of 38 percent) of perfluorooctane sulfonate (PFOS). Partial removals (32 to 56 percent) also were observed in a few samples for perfluorohexylsulfonate (PFHxS) and fluorotelomer sulfonate 6:2 (FtS 6:2) [2441].

Bench-scale studies [1697, 1698, 1699, 1701, 1702, 1708, 2607, 2614, 2640, 2644] have shown that degradation of per- and polyfluoroalkyl substances (PFASs) using ultraviolet (UV) irradiation varies greatly, depending on UV wavelength, UV contact time, and the addition of chemical catalysts. Tests that evaluated perfluorooctanoic acid (PFOA) degradation with UV that included 185 nm UV light achieved removal percentages that increased from 57 to 99 percent removal as contact time increased from one hour to four hours [1697, 1698, 1699, 2614]. Tests using 254 nm UV light alone achieved 4 to 34 percent removal of PFOA [1697, 1698, 1699, 1702, 1708, 2614]. One additional study achieved 44 to 89.5 percent PFOA removal using wavelengths in the 220 to 460 nm range in combination with very long contact times of 24 to 72 hours [1701].

Bench-scale studies [2607, 2644] found that UV degradation of various PFAS compounds can be enhanced (achieving up to 99 percent removal, depending on the specific PFAS) with the addition of sulfite using a contact time of 30 minutes or greater. One of these studies [2644] suggested this approach would be promising at scale in groundwater remediation but did not evaluate whether it would be practical for use in drinking water applications.

Additional bench-scale studies found that UV degradation of PFOA can be enhanced (achieving up to greater than 99 percent removal) with the addition of other chemicals/catalysts including sodium periodate [1697], persulfate [1698], iron [1708], and the combination of gallium oxide and peroxymonosulfate [2640]. All of these tests involved contact times of 30 minutes or greater. 
One study using a photocatalytic reactor membrane pilot system, employing UV/TiO2 photocatalysis, found 15 percent photodegradation of PFOS for surface water spiked with low levels of 32 pharmaceuticals and EDCs (ng/L to ug/L range) [2172]. Another pilot-scale study utilizing UV/TiO2 and UV with chlorine photocatalysis saw an increase in concentrations of all PFAS compounds studied in all scenarios [2847].

Contaminant Name: Per- and Polyfluoroalkyl Substances | Function: Ultraviolet Irradiation and Hydrogen Peroxide | Details: In a bench-scale study assessing PFOA, removal with UV (220-460nm) in combination with H2O2 was not significant. The study reported 35 percent removal after 24 hours of irradiation; removal was less effective in comparison to UV alone (45 percent) [1701]. In pilot [2659, 2847] and full-scale [2441] studies, advanced oxidation with ultraviolet irradiation and hydrogen peroxide was ineffective for removing any of the PFASs sampled.

Contaminant Name: Per- and Polyfluoroalkyl Substances | Function: Ultraviolet Irradiation and Ozone | Details: Removal of PFOS from water using a combination of ozone and UV irradiation was found to be ineffective based on data from one bench-scale study [2160]. In this study, a removal of -2 percent was achieved. The lack of removal was thought to be due to the chemical structure of PFOS, as perfluorinated surfactants are designed to resist heat and reducing/oxidizing agents.

Contaminant Name: Perchlorate | Description: Perchlorate (ClO4-) is a naturally occurring and man made chemical and a strong oxidizing anion. It is most commonly associated with the salts of ammonium, potassium and sodium, with ammonium the most common. [571] The most common use in the US is as a primary ingredient in solid rocket propellants. [572] Other related uses are explosives, military munitions, mortars, grenades and flairs. [571] 90 percent of production is for military and aerospace use. [573] It is also used in fireworks, matches, batteries and automobile air bags. [571, 573, 574] It is manufactured or used in 44 of 50 states. [571, 574] Wastes from the manufacture and improper disposal are increasingly being discovered in soil and water. [572] It occurs naturally in highly arid environments, [573] and has been found in nitrate fertilizer deposits in Chile and fertilizers derived from those deposits. [571, 574]
The Food and Drug Administration has detected in perchlorate in 27 foods and beverages with milk, tomatoes, spinach and fruit juices having the highest relevance. [575]
Perchlorate has properties that make it likely to be found in waters near its points of manufacture, use and disposal. It is highly soluble in water, is relatively inert, and adheres poorly to particle surfaces and to organics. Thus, it is mobile in both surface and ground waters and can persist for very long periods of time. [571, 573, 574]
Perchlorate competes with iodide in the thyroid, reduces iodine uptake into the thyroid and reduces hormone production. [574] In adults it interferes with metabolism, and in children it interferes with proper development. [573]
Perchlorate is not regulated by USEPA in drinking water, but is an unregulated chemical drinking water contaminant listed on the USEPA's Contaminant Candidate Lists 1, 2 and 3. [564,620] The National Academy of Sciences recommended a reference dose of 0.7ug/kg/day. [572] Based on this, USEPA established a drinking water equivalent level (DWEL) of 24.5 ug/L which accounts for both food and water intake. [573] A DWEL is an exposure level not expected to cause adverse health effects in humans. The California Department of Health Services's action level is 18 ug/L. [571, 574]
Logan [571] and Gullick et al [574] cite a survey of California ground waters finding perchlorate in 27 percent of 428 wells with concentrations at 33 sites exceeding the California 18 ug/L action level. Gullick et al [574] cite a survey of Colorado River water and the Colorado River aqueduct system with detects in the 5 to 9 ug/L range. The American Water System [571] surveyed 40 surface waters and 367 ground water wells across the US. They found no detects in the surface waters and detects in nine wells ranging less than the 4 ug/L detection level to 7 ug/L. USEPA monitored 3066 public water systems. [577] They found 152 detects above the 4 ug/L detection level with a mean of 10 ug/L and a maximum of 420 ug/L.
Date of Literature Search: August 2017

Contaminant Name: Perchlorate | Function: Biological Filtration | Details: Reduction of perchlorate by biological filtration can be very effective (exceeding 99 perecent) under anerobic conditions (removal below detection limit of 4 ppb) and is regulated by presence of oxygen, molybdate, and to some extent nitrate. Biological perchlorate reduction technologies such as packed bed reactor uses different filter media such as GAC, sand, diatomaeous earth, with perchlorate-reducing bacteria to reduce perchlorate from the drinking water.
Microbial reduction is attractive because the hazardous perchlorate is converted to two innocuous compounds, chloride and oxygen. Acetate, ethanol, lactate, methanol, sulfur, and hydrogen gas are suitable electron donor for perchlorate-reducing bacteria and they play important role in effective biochemical reduction of perchlorate. Microbial reduction technology may require high monitoring and maintenance and are difficult to implement in existing facility with high output. The specific issues with this treatment option include sustainability; stability; the multiple barrier concept; potential pathogen release; distribution system impacts; and utility, public, and primacy agency acceptance.
 
Anaerobic biological treatment unit processes present challenges because they are not currently used to treat drinking water in the United States. Additionally, these processes require the control of any potential microbial, nutrient, and electron donor releases into the distribution system.

Contaminant Name: Perchlorate | Function: Biological Treatment | Details: Reduction of perchlorate by different biological treatment methods can be very effective (exceeding 99 percent and to below detection limits of 4 ppb or lower) under anaerobic and is regulated by presence of oxygen, molybdate, and to some extent nitrate. Biological perchlorate reduction technologies such as fluidized bed reactor, biofilm reactor, fixed film reactor and suspended growth reactor uses different filter media with perchlorate-reducing bacteria to reduce perchlorate from the drinking water. Other biological treatment methods that do not include biological filtration such as microbial reduction of perchlorate with zero-valent iron, bioreactors filled with iron fillings or glass beads also falls in this treatment category.
Microbial reduction is attractive because the hazardous perchlorate is converted to two innocuous compounds, chloride and oxygen. Acetate, ethanol, lactate, methanol, sulfur, and hydrogen gas are suitable electron donor for perchlorate-reducing bacteria and they play important role in effective biochemical reduction of perchlorate. Microbial reduction technology may require high monitoring and maintenance and are difficult to implement in existing facility with high output. The specific issues with this treatment option include sustainability; stability; the multiple barrier concept; potential pathogen release; distribution system impacts; and utility, public, and primacy agency acceptance. These processes require the control of any potential microbial, nutrient, and electron donor releases into the distribution system.

Contaminant Name: Perchlorate | Function: Chemical Treatment | Details: Removal of perchlorate by electrochemical reduction can be effective (up to 99 percent removal) and is dependent on a number of factors, including: chemical treatment technology, temperature, pressure, contact time, iron type and dose, initial concentration, and pH. Removals above 90 percent were associated with temperatures higher than those encountered in drinking water treatment. Otherwise, removals were generally below 50 percent.
For the electrochemical reduction of perchlorate, removal was limited by competition of anions for the active sites on the electrode. Perchlorate was adsorbed less strongly in comparison to sulfate and chloride. Chemical treatment of perchlorate is currently being tested in bench-scale or batch studies. It is unknown if the percent removal achieved in the laboratory setting can be scaled to full-scale applications.

Contaminant Name: Perchlorate | Function: GAC Isotherm | Details: GAC can remove perchlorate due to the ion exchange properties of GAC. These capabilities can be enhanced by modifying or tailoring the carbon with materials like ammonia. One publication presents GAC isotherm data. The isotherm experiments were performed with deionized-distilled water. Perchlorate adsorption capacity decreased as it competed with NOM or other anions present in surface or groundwater, as expected.

Contaminant Name: Perchlorate | Function: Granular Activated Carbon | Details: Removal of perchlorate from water by tailored or modified granular activated carbon (GAC) can be very effective (removal below detection limit of 4 ppb). Tailored or modified GAC preloaded with cationic surfactants like ammonium ion provided effective adsorbent for perchlorate. However, activated carbon has only a very limited capacity for perchlorate removal and can treat approximately 1,500 to 2,000 bed volumes of water.
GAC is only cost effective to treat low concentrations of perchlorate, as the spent carbon has to be reactivated to destroy the captured perchlorate allowing the carbon to be reused. GAC treatment process combined with biological filtration provides additional barrier for perchlorate in drinking water due to concurrent ion exchange and anaerobic reduction mechanisms. GAC treatment shares similar distribution system concerns with ion exchange treatment.

Contaminant Name: Perchlorate | Function: Ion Exchange | Details: Removal of perchlorate from water by ion exchange (IX) can be very effective (greater than 90 percent) to below detection limit of 4 ppb and is dependent upon the media type, regeneration frequency, competing ions, and initial concentration. The most common resin used was strong-base anionic resins, which showed high affinity for perchlorate. Polystyrene, polyvinylpyridine, and polyacrylic are promising ion exchange resins for perchlorate. Regardless of regenerating or directly disposing of the spent resins, IX is an incomplete technology. The necessity for regeneration or periodic replacement of ion-exchange resins makes the operation and maintenance of IX treatment systems expensive compared to anaerobic biofiltration (which has other concerns). High concentration of sodium chloride or a caustic solution or low concentration of ammonium hydroxide can be used for regeneration depending upon the resin. Perchlorate has as high affinity for virtually all anion resins, all IX columns removing perchlorate should be operated in a countercurrent fashion i.e., exhaustion in one direction and regeneration in another direction.   Distribution system impacts from ion exchange treatment processes will largely depend on the type and specificity of the resins and the mode of use. The more specific the resin is to perchlorate, the less inadvertent co-removal of beneficial anionic species will occur over the bed-life of the resin. The balance among pH and concentrations of ions such as bicarbonate, carbonate, sulfate, and chloride is critical in achieving regulatory compliance with the Lead and Copper Rule, and in maintaining a stable and non-corrosive drinking water throughout the distribution system. This situation is considerably different from the common application of anion exchange for removal of nitrate or fluoride, where only a relatively small fraction of the contaminated source water usually needs to be sent through full treatment.

Contaminant Name: Perchlorate | Function: Membrane Filtration | Details: Removal of perchlorate by membrane filtration is not effective in natural waters. Studies with ultrafiltration membrane coated with polyamide showed less than 3 to 80 percent of perchlorate removal. The high percent removals were in distilled-deionized water due to membrane charge rejection. This mechanism disappeared in natural waters resulting in poor removals in all such cases. Membrane filtration is not effective for perchlorate rejection mostly because the membrane's pore size is orders of magnitude larger than the perchlorate molecule.

Contaminant Name: Perchlorate | Function: Membrane Separation | Details: Removal of perchlorate by membrane separation is very effective (up to greater than 95 percent removal). Membrane separation technologies include reverse osmosis (RO), nanofiltration (NF), electrodialysis (ED), and electrodialysis reversal (EDR). The EDR units are usually made of ion exchange resins and the NF and RO membranes were mostly made out of polyamide materials. The cost, residual stream issues, and possible distribution system impacts of high-pressure membrane systems makes this technology difficult to justify unless other difficult-to-remove contaminants such as arsenic, pesticides, and DBP precursors are present. The presence of silica may make ED/EDR cost effective as compared to the pressure driven processes.

Contaminant Name: Perchlorate | Function: Other Treatment | Details: Other treatment processes that are applicable for perchlorate reduction are hollow-fiber membrane bioreactor with hydrogen gas supply or permeable barrier with vegetable oil. The anaerobic autotrophic membrane bioreactor and the permeable barrier approach were effective in reducing perchlorate below detection limit of 4 ppb, but concerns remain about the ability of these systems to be scaled up to practical sizes. Electrochemical reduction was also effective, but at temperatures higher than encountered in drinking water treatment.

Contaminant Name: Perchlorate | Overall: The following processes are considered effective for the removal of perchlorate at drinking water treatment scale: ion exchange, biological treatment, tailored granular activated carbon, and membrane separation. Of the technologies that have near-term applicability for perchlorate removal, ion exchange is the furthest along with regard to demonstrating its ability to remove perchlorate in a stable and sustainable fashion. Performance of these technologies can vary based on water quality, materials of construction, and treatment characteristics. Distribution system impacts from ion exchange treatment processes will largely depend on the type and specificity of the resins and the mode of use. The more specific the resin is to perchlorate, the less inadvertent co-removal of beneficial anionic species will occur over the bed-life of the resin.
Anaerobic biological treatment has promise for perchlorate removal. Issues to address in considering this treatment option include sustainability stability the multiple barrier concept potential pathogen release distribution system impacts and utility, public, and primacy agency acceptance. These processes require the control of any potential microbial, nutrient, and electron donor releases into the distribution system.
Reverse osmosis or tight nanofiltration membranes are relatively effective for controlling perchlorate however, these technologies are known to incur high treatment costs, often requiring extensive pre-treatment, post-treatment, and residual treatment processes. Reverse osmosis or tight nanofiltration membranes are perhaps best suited for point-of-use applications with regard to perchlorate. Membranes used for particulate removal such as microfiltration or ultrafiltration would not be practical for perchlorate removal, unless the perchlorate could be sequestered or complexed to a larger molecule, particle, or adsorbent. Some data with laboratory-clean water has shown that ultrafiltration membranes could be effective, but their performance diminished significantly in the presence of natural ions.
Activated carbon has a certain amount of ion exchange capacity present in the functional groups on its surface. Therefore, many GACs will remove perchlorate however, the capacity is very small when compared with ion exchange resins. To extend this capability, researchers have recently modified, or tailored, activated carbon with various materials. To date, the best results came from the use of quaternary ammonia monomers. An advantage of this technology is that it can adsorb organic contaminants concurrently with perchlorate. Other issues need to be evaluated, such as determining the regeneration efficiency, the degree of quaternary ammonia monomers released from the carbon during operation and regeneration, the impact of this potential release, the potential of NDMA formation upon subsequent disinfection, and whether the modifying agent will be approved as a drinking water additive. Tailored GAC treatment would be expected to produce finished water that is similar to that produced by an ion exchange resin. Therefore, all of the same residual and distribution system issues that are a concern for ion exchange treatment also pertain to modified GAC treatment.
Research identified following treatment technologies for the removal of perchlorate:

Contaminant Name: Perchlorate | Function: Ozone | Details: Experiments investigating the removal of perchlorate in ozone indicated no perchlorate reduction. Based on the data available from one study the perchlorate reduction efficiency was less than 2 percent. This is expected due to perchlorate's fully oxidized form.

Contaminant Name: Perchlorate | Function: Ozone and Hydrogen Peroxide | Details: Experiments investigating the removal of perchlorate in ozone indicated no perchlorate reduction. Based on the data available from one study with the addition of hydrogen peroxide the perchlorate reduction efficiency was less than 5 percent. This is expected due to perchlorate's fully oxidized form.

Contaminant Name: Perchlorate | Function: Ultraviolet Irradiation | Details: One study showed that ultraviolet irradiation could be effective in removing perchlorate but only in the presence of metallic iron concentration. When UV light was used as a catalyst to accelerate the removal of perchlorate by metallic iron, 40 to 77 percent of the perchlorate was removed. No perchlorate removal is expected without the presence of metallic iron as shown by another study.

Contaminant Name: Permethrin | Description: Permethrin is a contact insecticide used for a broad range of insects in agriculture, forestry and public health. [1434]
Surface waters may become contaminated by permethrin applied directly to water for mosquito control purposes, in discharges from production plant and from agriculture. [1434]
Permethrin is rapidly degraded in soil by hydrolysis and microbial action under aerobic conditions. Similar reactions occur under anaerobic conditions also. The half-life in soils is approximately 28 day, and 10 days in plants. [1434]
Human exposure is low, and the World Health Organization has classified permethrin as "moderately hazardous." [1434]
Studies in which rats, mice, rabbits, guinea-pigs and dogs received repeated administration by the inhalation, oral and dermal routes showed that the main effects of technical-grade permethrin are on clinical signs, especially tremor and hyperexcitability, body weight and liver weight. [1434]
Permethrin is used in the UK at doses between 10 and 20 ug/L to control aquatic invertebrates in water mains. [1433]
Very limited data are available regarding treatment for the control of permethrin in drinking water. It has been noted that permethrin readily adsorbs to many materials in the natural environment and is generally not present in raw water. It is generally considered that permethrin would be removed by conventional treatment methods. [1434]
Date of Literature search: 2009

Contaminant Name: Permethrin | Overall: Permethrin removal from raw water is not normally an issue. Permethrin adsorbs to a wide range of materials, and it is generally considered that permethrin is readily removed by conventional treatment methods.

Contaminant Name: Propoxur | Description: Propoxur is an N-methyl carbamate pesticide used to control ants, fleas, flies, ticks, mosquitos, apids, roaches and hornets in and around residences and commercial food handling establishments.  It is available in many formulations including aerosols, baits, emulsifiable concentrates, wettable powders, dusts and powders, etc [2122, 2116].  Its IUPAC name is 2-isopropoxyphenyl methylcarbamate. 
 
Propoxur was first registered as a pesticide in the U.S. in 1963. EPA issued a Reregistration Eligibility Decision document for propoxur in 1997 and it is currently registered as a general use pesticide [2122], but not permitted on food crops [2116]. 
 
The mechanism of toxicity for propoxur is to inhibit the production of cholinesterase, an enzyme necessary for transmission of nerve impulses.  Symptoms of propoxur poisoning include nausea, vomiting, abdominal cramps, sweating, diarrhea, amongst others [2123].  It is not considered mutagenic, teratogenic or embryotoxic [2126].   It is a B2 probable human carcinogen [2122].  Propoxur has been shown to have moderate acute toxicity and has been classified in the second of four toxicity categories for oral route exposure and in the third of four toxicity categories for dermal and inhalation routes.  
 
Propoxur is not sorbed strongly to soils and sediments [2124, 2126].  With a high solubilty, it has potential for being mobile in ground waters [2126].  It has a low Henry's constant so is not subject to volatilization [2124].  It is hydrolytically stable at acid to neutral pHs (pH between 3 and 7) and degrades rapidly at increasing alkaline pH [2122].  It is photodegradable, especially in upper strata of surface waters [2124, 2126].  It is rarely detected in surface or ground waters [2126]. 
  
Two transformation products are reported: N-methylformamide and 2-isopropoxyphenol [2125].  The latter is also know as propoxur phenol.  pH-dependant basic hydrolysis and exposure to light promote transformation [2125]. 
 
It is not regulated in drinking water in the US. 
 
Date of Literature Search: March 2011

Contaminant Name: Propoxur | Function: Chlorine | Details: Chlorine was found to be ineffective in degrading propoxur in one study.  Propoxur did not react with chlorine at a pH of 7.0 within 24 hours at a chlorine to carbaryl ratio of 28 to 1, which was selected to simulate realistic chlorine concentrations in drinking water.

Contaminant Name: Propoxur | Function: Chlorine Dioxide | Details: Chlorine dioxide was found to be ineffective in degrading propoxur in one study.  Propoxur did not react with chlorine dioxide at a pH of 7.0 within 24 hours at a chlorine dioxide dosage of 6 mg/L, which is well above typical dosages used in drinking water applications.

Contaminant Name: Propoxur | Overall: The following processes have been demonstrated to be effective for the removal of propoxur from drinking water: ozone (>99 percent removal) and UV in the presence of a photocatalyst such as titanium dioxide, 2,4,6-triphenylpyrylium-Zeolite, zeolite supported TiO2 and GAC supported TiO2 (up to 100 degradation can be achieved). Performance of these technologies can vary based on water quality and treatment characteristics.
Chlorine and chlorine dioxide were demonstrated to provide no significant removal of propoxur.
Research was identified evaluating the following treatment technologies for the removal of propoxur:

Contaminant Name: Propoxur | Function: Ozone | Details: Removal of propoxur was found to be very effective with ozone (100% removal achieved) in one study . Complete propoxur removal was achieved by applying 6 mg/L of ozone to 1 mg/L of propoxur.  Ozone oxidation was found to result in complete destruction of propoxur by hydrolysis and ring hydroxylation.

Contaminant Name: Propoxur | Function: Ultraviolet Irradiation | Details: Ultraviolet irradiation was found to be effective in degrading propoxur (up to 90% degradation) in one study; however, it produced a relatively minor degree of mineralization (about 16%) due to the formation of photo-Fries products.  Degradation of propoxur has been found to be effective in serveral studies with UV in the presence of a photocatalyst such as titanium dioxide, 2,4,6-triphenylpyrylium-Zeolite, zeolite supported TiO2 and GAC supported TiO2 (up to 100% degradation can be achieved).
The degradation of propoxur by UV radiation with a photocatalyst is generally influenced by pH, initial propoxur concentration, photocatalyst loading and UV light intensity. UV reaction times used were generally much longer than those used in typical drinking water applications of UV.

Contaminant Name: Radium | Description: Some drinking water sources have very low levels of radionuclides (radioactive elements). A radionuclide is an unstable (decaying) form of a nuclide. Radium has two radionuclides that are detected in and regulated in drinking water. Ra-226 is an alpha emitter and the fifth-member decay product of the uranium-238 series. Its half life approximately 1622 yrs. Ra-228 is a beta emitter and the second-member decay product of the thorium-232 series. Its half life is approximately 5.7 yrs. Long half lives lead to their stability in water. Ra-224 is an alpha emitter and the fifth-member decay product of the thorium-232 series. Its half life approximately 3.6 days. [586] Ra-224 is not regulated in drinking water.
Radium is naturally occurring and depends, in part, on the solubility of the parents and decay products ahead of it in its decay series. [586] Radium can adsorb to particulate surfaces. In ground waters, high total dissolved solids offer other ions, particularly divalent cations like calcium and barium, competing for sorption sites that keep radium in solution. [586] Deep wells have limited sorption sites, therefore are more likely to have radium in solution than shallow wells. [586] Radionuclides are detected more frequently in ground water than in surface water. [585] Radium detections in surface water are related to discharges from ground waters or from man-made activities. [586]
Emitted particles ionize or destabilize atoms as they pass thru the body's cells damaging chromosomes that can lead to cancer. [585] Radium is regulated in drinking water because of its increased risk of cancer. [584]
Combined Ra-226 and Ra-228 are regulated by USEPA in drinking water with a maximum contaminant level of 5 pCi/L. [584] USEPA has identified ion exchange, lime softening and reverse osmosis as Best Available Technologies for the control of Ra-226 and Ra-228 in drinking water. [566]
In the National Inorganics and Radionuclides Survey (NIRS) of ground waters [587], Ra-226 detects exceeded the detection level of 0.18 pCi/L in 40.2 percent of 989 sites. The median, mean and maximum levels were, respectively, 0.40, 0.87 and 15.1 pCi/L. Also in the NIRS, Ra-228 detects exceeded the detection level of 1.0 pCi/L in 11.9 percent of 987 sites, and the median, mean and maximum levels were, respectively, 1.47, 2.05 and 12.1 pCi/L. Ref 586 cites a USGS survey of 104 ground water sites in the US known or suspected of having high radium levels. The Ra-226 median, mean and maximum levels were, respectively, 0.4, 1.6 and 16.9 pCi/L, and the Ra-228 levels were, respectively, 0.5, 2.1 and 72.3 pCi/L.
Date of Literature Search: March 2007

Contaminant Name: Radium | Function: Adsorptive Media | Details: One study showed that, by utilizing the natural sorption properties of conventional filter sand, removal of radium from water by adsorption can be very effective (up to 90 percent removal).  This is dependent upon the occurrence of competing ions, initial concentration, media pretreatment (in this case a dilute acid rince), and regeneration frequency.

Contaminant Name: Radium | Function: Conventional Treatment | Details: Removal of radium from water by conventional treatment alone is not very effective (less than 44 percent). Conventional treatment alone may be suitable for applications where influent radium concentrations are slightly above the maximum contaminant limit and minimial removal is required. Radium removal is dependent on the initial concentration, filtration/media type, coagulent used, filter treatments, and the presence of co-occuring solutes, particularly manganese.

Contaminant Name: Radium | Function: Granular Activated Carbon | Details: Based on one article, removal of radium from water by granular activated carbon (GAC) alone is not very effective (approximately 1 to 23 percent). The article suggests that radium was not adsorbed onto the GAC. As a filter media, like for conventional filtration, it would not be expected to be effective.

Contaminant Name: Radium | Function: Ion Exchange | Details: Removal of radium from water by ion exchange can be very effective (62 percent to greater than 99 percent removal). Ion exchange is a Best Available Technology for the control of radium. It is dependent upon the resin type, regeneration frequency, competing ions, and initial concentration. Radium is preferred over calcium, magnesium and other cations by most ion exchange resins. The most common resin used was a cationic, polystyrene-based zeolite resin which performed equally well in the calcium, sodium, and hydrogen form.  Another IEX media commonly used was manganese greensand which was equally effective (84 percent to 98 percent removel).  Although the literature focused on Ra-226, Ra-228 is removed equally as well as both are divalent. Although virgin resin performed well, the best removal was reported by regenerating the resin using sodium chloride or dilute acid.

Contaminant Name: Radium | Function: Membrane Separation | Details: Removal of radium from water by membrane separation (specifically reverse osmosis) can be very effective (87 percent to greater than 99 percent removal). Reverse osmosis is a Best Available Technology for control of radium. Contaminant removal by reverse osmosis is dependent upon membrane type, pH, recovery, and initial contaminant concentration.

Contaminant Name: Radium | Overall: The following processes are considered effective for the removal of radium-226 and/or radium-228: ion exchange (62 to greater than 99 percent removal), membrane separation using reverse osmosis (87 to greater than 99 percent removal), precipitative softening (75 to 96 percent), and adsorptive media (up to 90 percent removal). The level of control for precipitative softening, ion exchange, and membrane separation (often greater than 90 percent removal), is consistent with their designations as Best Available Technologies for radium removal. Control by adsorptive media was provided by conventional filter sand rince with dilute concentrations of acid. Performance of these technologies can vary based on water quality and treatment characteristics.
Conventional filtration does not provide significant control, nor do slow sand filtration or filtration by granular activated carbon. Removal can be provided by oxidation with chlorine or permanganate followed by filtration.
Research was identified evaluating the following treatment technologies for the removal of radium:

Contaminant Name: Radium | Function: Precipitative Softening | Details: Removal of radium from water by precipitative softening can be very effective. In all referenced articles, filtration was used to some degree in the process, although the filter specifics were not always mentioned. Coupled with filtration, precipitative softening is able to achieve notable removal (75 to 96 percent). Lime softening is a Best Available Technology for the control of radium. Lime and lime/soda ash addition appeared to produce similar results. Radium removal is dependent on the initial concentration, lime and soda ash doses, and the presence of co-occuring solutes, such as manganese and iron.

Contaminant Name: Radium | Function: Slow Sand Filtration | Details: Removal of radium from water by slow sand filtration was found, like for conventional filtration, to be ineffective (0 percent removal) based on data available from one study.

Contaminant Name: Ricin | Description: Ricin is a potent protein that acts as a toxin if inhaled, injected, or ingested. Ricin can be used as a chemical warfare agent and has been weaponized in the past; however, it is not considered practical nor likely to be a significant threat in drinking water. [1275]
Ricin has a molecular weight of about 63,000 Da and consists of two polypeptide chains (termed A and B) linked through a single disulfide bond. [1319]  Ricin inhibits protein synthesis. The B-chain binds to cell surface receptors and the toxin-receptor complex is taken into the cell. The A-chain then inhibits DNA replication and protein synthesis.
Ricin is easily extracted from castor-oil manufacturing waste material left from processing castor beans (Ricinus communis). Waste materials are approximately 3% to 5% ricin by weight [1306]. Ricin is easily purifed from this waste using simple chromatographic techniques. Approximately 1 million tons of castor beans are processed annually; precursor materials for ricin are readily available in large quantities throughout the world. Purified ricin can be in the form of powder, a mist, or a pellet, which can be dissolved in water. Ricin can be used as a chemical warfare agent in the past.
When ingested, ricin can cause severe gastrointestinal symptoms including nausea, vomiting, and diarrhea, followed by vascular collapse and possible death [1312].
Ricin is inactivated much more readily than other biochemical agents, such as botulism and anthrax. (The protein changes structure and becomes less toxic). Because ricin is significantly less toxic by oral ingestion than by other routes, it is a less credible threat in drinking water [1275].
Although ricin is easy to produce, it is not considered practical nor likely to be used in drinking water as a weapon to cause significant military or civilian casualties. Ricin is not regulated by USEPA in drinking water. Little is known about ricin occurrence in drinking water sources.
Date of Literature Search: June 2009

Contaminant Name: Ricin | Function: Chemical Treatment | Details: Iodine and bromine were found to be very effective for inactivating ricin in animal injection studies. One study used high dosages and extended contact times (e.g., 300 mg/L for a contact time of 1,200 minutes). However, another study showed that iodine was not effective in oxidizing ricin at a lower dosage of 16 mg/L.  These data suggest bomine and iodine may be effective in drinking water treatment.
Ricin inactivation in water by 4-diazophenyl-B-d-galactopyranoside was found to be relatively effective (50% to 90% inactivation at a ratio of 25 and 125 mol of 4-diazophenyl-B-d-galactopyranoside to ricin, respectively, with a contact time of 90 minutes).  Acetic acid and urea were found to effectively inactivate ricin in high dosages for one study; up to 90% inactivation with acetic acid and >97% inactivation with urea were observed.  pH was not given.  These data are only suggestive as they are not representative of drinking water treatment conditions.

Contaminant Name: Ricin | Function: Chlorine | Details: Ricin inactivation by chlorine in water can be very effective at high doses and long contact times not typical of drinking water treatment.  Inactivation values in excess of 99% were observed for chlorine dosages that ranged from 100 to 223 mg/L and contact times ranging from 20 to 2,880 minutes. A chlorine dose of 5 to 10 mg/L and 20 to 30 minute contact times were ineffective in controlling ricin.

Contaminant Name: Ricin | Function: Hydrogen Peroxide | Details: Hydrogen peroxide was found to be effective in inactivating ricin over long contact times (24 hours). Treated ricin was injected subcutaneously into mice and showed no toxic effect on the mice even through the quantity of ricin injected was equivalent to two lethal doses of untreated ricin.  This suggests H2O2 might be effective under drinking water conditions.

Contaminant Name: Ricin | Function: Membrane Separation | Details: Removal of ricin from water can be very effective using reverse osmosis. Greater than 99.8% removal was achieved by an Army field reverse osmosis water purification unit. Similar removal might be expected in conventional reverse osmosis systems that are used in municipal drinking water treatment.

Contaminant Name: Ricin | Overall: The following processes might be effective for inactivating ricin under drinking water conditions: use of chlorine, hydrogen peroxide, ozone, permanganate, and ultraviolet irradiation.  These studies were based on responses in animals to determine effectiveness. 
Membrane separation by reverse osmosis was effective in field-unit demonstrations.  In bench-scale studies, chlorine's effectiveness varied as high doses and long contact times were effective but lower doses and contact times were not.
Use of atypical treatment processes may also remove or destroy ricin, including use of iodine, bromine, 4-diazophenyl-B-d-galactopyranoside, acetic acid, and urea, but conditions were genrally not those of drinking water treqtment.
Studies were identified evaluating the following treatment technologies for the inactivation of ricin:

Contaminant Name: Ricin | Function: Ozone | Details: Ozone was found to be very effective at inactivating ricin over a six hour contact time. Treated ricin was injected subcutaneously into mice and showed no toxic effect on the mice even though the quantity of ricin injected was equivalent to 40 lethal doses of untreated ricin.  This suggests ozone may be effective under drinking water conditions.

Contaminant Name: Ricin | Function: Permanganate | Details: Inactivation of ricin in water by permanganate was found to be very effective at a high doses and short contact time (e.g., permanganate dose of 70,000 mg/L over a 2.5 minute contact time). Treated ricin was injected subcutaneously into a rabbit and showed no toxic effect on the rabbit even though the quantity of ricin injected was equivalent to 5,000 lethal doses of untreated ricin. This suggested that permanganate completely oxidized ricin.

Contaminant Name: Ricin | Function: Ultraviolet Irradiation | Details: Inactivation of ricin in water by ultraviolet (UV) irradiation was found to be effective by one study. Treated ricin was injected subcutaneously to mice. One to five minutes of contact time were sufficient to destroy toxicity so that two to three times the lethal doses of treated ricin could be injected into mice without causing the same symptoms observed with untreated ricin.  This suggests UV could be effective under drinking water conditions.

Contaminant Name: S. typhi | Description: Salmonella typhi (S. typhi) is a bacterium that causes typhoid fever in humans [965]; illnesses are typically caused by ingesting contaminated food or water [953].
S. typhi is a facultative anaerobic, gram negative bacterium that belongs to the family Enterobacteriaceae and the genus Salmonella. The nomenclature and classification of salmonella genus have changed over the years [965]. The current classification of S. typhi is Salmonella enterica serovar typhi [965].
Contaminated water and food are the common sources of typhoid fever in endemic areas [965, 953]. Patients with typhoid fever (either acute or chronic) excrete large numbers of organisms and serve as the principal transmission sources. [965]
Although S. typhi is strictly adapted to humans, it can survive in the environment. It may survive in water or ice for many weeks [963]; some studies suggest that S. typhi can survive for days to a few weeks in groundwater, pond water, or seawater [967, 968]. Survival in sewage is usually less than a week [963]. The optimal growth temperature for S. typhi is 37 °C and it can be killed by a temperature of 60 °C for 15 minutes, and rapidly by boiling [965]. S. typhi is acid sensitive and will generally be killed at a pH level of less than 2.5 [969].
S. typhi is strictly adapted to humans and only causes illness in humans [965]. Typhoid fever is an acute systemic infection that can be fatal [953]. A similar disease, paratyphoid fever, is often discussed together with typhoid fever. Paratyphoid is caused by S. Paratyphi A, B and sometimes C, and is usually less severe than typhoid fever [953]. Typhoid and paratyphoid fevers are collectively referred to as enteric fevers [953]. Typical symptoms of typhoid fever include prolonged fever, headache, enlargement of the spleen, rose-colored spots on the abdominal surface, constipation, or diarrhea [953, 965].
Typhoid fever has been a severe epidemic human disease for centuries [963]. In the United States, improvements in sanitation, quality of water supplies, and food hygiene since the 1920s have dramatically decreased the prevalence of typhoid fever. For example, between 1990 and 1999, 420 cases and less than one death per year have been attributed to typhoid fever [966]. A large portion of infections is related to foreign travels [970, 971].
S. typhi is currently not regulated by USEPA in drinking water.
No occurrence data for S. typhi were found for source waters currently used for drinking water in the United States.
Date of Literature Search: August 2009
