Contaminant Name: S. typhi | Function: Chloramine | Details: Chloramines can effectively inactivate S. typhi; however, chloramines are much less efficient than free chlorine, as shown in a summary paper [959] that compares the data of related E. typhosa  inactivation by chlorine [960] and chloramines [961]. A lab study showed that disinfection efficacy is affected by chloramines dose, contact time, pH and temperature [961]. Disinfection activity of chloramine is reduced with increased pH. The study tested two temperature ranges, from 2 to 6 °C and from 20 to 26 °C. In the lower temperature range, the disinfection efficacy of chloramines was lower. The study also showed that contact time and chloramine residual are the primary factors governing disinfection efficacy.

Contaminant Name: S. typhi | Function: Chlorine | Details: Free chlorine can effectively inactivate S. typhi. The disinfection efficacy is dependent on contact time, chlorine dose, pH and temperature. A lab study in 1940s investigated the disinfection efficiency of free chlorine at various pH levels and two temperature ranges, from 2 to 5 °C and from 20 to 25 °C [960]. The study showed 100 percent inactivation of related E. typhosa using a free chlorine dose of 0.06 mg/L and a contact time of 3 minutes at pH 7.0 from 20 to 25 °C. Study results suggest disinfection efficiency decreases as pH increases and temperature decreases. A more recent study showed that 0.5 mg/L chlorine in tap water can achieve 100 percent S. typhi inactivation within 24 hours in the temperature range of -20 to 37 °C [958].

Contaminant Name: S. typhi | Function: Hydrogen Peroxide | Details: Hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) can effectively inactivate S. typhi; however, a relatively large dose and a long contact time are needed to achieve effective inactivation. For example, to inactivate tap water containing 10E+5 S. typhi bacteria per ml, a dose of 90 mg/L H2O2 over a contact time of 60 minutes is needed [954].

Contaminant Name: S. typhi | Overall: The following processes were effective for inactivating S. typhi: use of chloramine, chlorine, hydrogen peroxide and ultraviolet (UV) irradiation. The performance of these technologies varies with dose, contact time, and water quality characteristics.
Research was identified evaluating the following treatment technologies for the removal or inactivation of S. typhi:

Contaminant Name: S. typhi | Function: Ultraviolet Irradiation | Details: Ultraviolet (UV) irradiation is effective for S. typhi inactivation. Two studies evaluating low-pressure UV lamps showed 1 to 5.5 log inactivation of S. typhi were achieved using UV doses ranging from 2.0 to 9.0 mWsec/cm2 [951, 952]. Another study tested S. typhi inactivation by a point-of-use water treatment system, which consisted of an activated carbon filter followed by a UV reactor [955]. The test system achieved >99.9999 percent (six log) S. typhi inactivation.

Contaminant Name: Simazine and Simazine Degradates | Description: Simazine is an s-triazine herbicide and is used extensively as a pre-emergent on a variety of crops, and on broad-leaf and grassy weeds.  It is used to control weeds in turf grasses, and to control algae in ponds and fish hatcheries.  It is principally used for corn, but also for vegeatbles, fruits and berries.  For corn, it is often used in combination with atrazine, the most commonly used s-triazine herbicide.   Simazine acts by inhibiting photosynthesis.
Simazine is 6-chloro-2,4-diethyl-1,3,5-triazine-2,4-diamine. 
Simazine and its degradates reach source waters largely through runoff, typically one to three months following agricultural application. [502]
The transformation of simazine to its degradates is complex, invloving not only simazine but possibly other triazine herbicides.  They are: 
An ethyl group may be cleaved resulting in deethylsimazine (6-chloro-4-ethyl-1,3,5-triazine-2,4-diamine) (CAS Number 1007-28-9).  Deethylsimazine is an identical molecule to deisopropylatrazine (DIA) which results from the degradation of atrazine.  DIA may be detected with simazine as its degradate or as a degradate of atrazine commonly used in combination with simazine. [2010]  Literature for control of deethylsimazine were not found, but were found for DIA.  DIA results will be reported in the Treatment tab.  
Cleaving both ethyl groups results in diaminochlorotriazine (6-chloro-1,3,5-triazine-2,4-diamine) (CAS Number 3397-62-4).  Diaminochlorotriazine can also be named didealkylsimazine, or didealkylatrazine (DDA) or didealkyltriazine.  This molecule may result from degradation of any of the four most commonly used triazines: atrazine, simazine, propazine or cyanazine.  Literature for control of diaminochlorotriazine were not found, but were found for DDA.  DDA results will be reported in the Treatment tab.
Biotic action is the most common means of simazine degradation. [2010]  By microbial action, hydroxysimazine may result (6-hydroxy-2,4-diethyl-1,3,5-triazine-2,4-diamine) (CAS Number 2599-11-3). This degradate may only result from simazine.  One of the ethyl groups may be cleaved and deethylhydroxysimazine may result (6-hydroxy-4-ethyl-1,3,5-triazine-2,4-diamine). [2010]  This degradate may also result from other triazines with a single ethyl group, i.e., atrazine and cyanazine.  Literature for control of these latter two degradates were not found.          
Simazine is commonly detected in ground and surface source waters in numerous states. [1424]  In a literture review  reporting on California monitoring in 2001, it was detected in 166 of 460 surface waters with a median of 0.024 ug/L and a maximum of 3.7 ug/L, and in 87 of 1173 wells with a median of 0.104 ug/L and a maximum of 0.244 ug/L.  In another study, of 131 wells monitored in California, simazine was detected in 85, diaminochlorotriazine in 105 and deethylsimazine (as DIA) in 110.  Hydroxysimazine and deethylhydroxysimazine were not monitored. [2010]  Concentrations of simazine reaching 1 to 2 ug/L have been reported in ground water in the USA. [1435]
The US EPA states that simazine's acute effects are changes in blood and weight loss, and that its chronic effects involve damage to testicles, liver, thyroid and kidneys. [2011] It is implicated in gene mutation. [2011, 2013]  There is some evidence of carcinogenic effects of simazine [2011], but its carcinogenic effects are unclassifiable. [2012, 2013]  There are no reported reproductive or teratogenic effects. [2012]  It is suspected to have endocrine disruptive mechanisms, as are its deethylsimazine degradate. [1428, 2013]  Simazine and its degradates deethylsimazine, hydroxysimazine and deethylhydroxysimazine are reported not to be cholinesterase inhibitors. [2013]  
Simazine is regulated in drinking water in the US at 4 ug/L because of its blood-associated problems.  Simazine degradates were on Contaminant Candidate Lists 1 and 2 under the umbrella of triazine degradates.  Simazine's and other triazine degradates did not appear on Contaminant Candidate List 3.
The best available technology for control of simazine is granular activated carbon.  There is signifcant literature on the control of simazine in drinking water, but only scant literature on control of its degradates.  
Photodegradative effects of simazine are small [2011-2013], but possibly result in the formation of deethylsimazine (deisopropylatrazine) and diaminochlorotriazine. [2010]  Simazine hydrolyzes only at low pH [2010, 2011],  so would not be expected to be controlled during precipitative softening.  It is has a low Henry's coefficient [2010, 2011] so would not be expected to be controlled by air stripping.  It is poorly bound to soils, clays and organic matter [2010-2012], so moves readily to ground waters [2010], and would not be expected to be well controlled by conventional treatment.  In surface waters it may persist for months following agricultural application. [2010]  
Date of Literature Search: June 2009

Contaminant Name: Simazine and Simazine Degradates | Function: Chemical Treatment | Details: Zero valent iron powder (ZVIP) appears to be effective for rapid treatment of water spiked with simazine. The half-lives of the reductive reactions were close to several minutes. The major final by-product was diaminochlorotriazine. [1426] Removal was 100%, however, under very high ZVIP dosage.

Contaminant Name: Simazine and Simazine Degradates | Function: Chlorine | Details: In bench-scale testing of spiked waters, no control of simazine, deethylsimazine or diaminochlorotriazine was noted.  In monitoring full-scale plants, no significant control of simazine was observed.

Contaminant Name: Simazine and Simazine Degradates | Function: Chlorine Dioxide | Details: In a bench-scale study using surface water, a chlorine dioxide dosage of 1.5 mg/L with a 6 hr contact time provided little significant removal of simazine.

Contaminant Name: Simazine and Simazine Degradates | Function: Conventional Treatment | Details: In evaluating alum and iron coagulation of surface waters in jar tests, no significant removal of simazine, deethylsimazine or diaminochlorotriazine was achieved.  No control of simazine was observed in monitoring coagulation, clarification and filtration in full-scale plants.

Contaminant Name: Simazine and Simazine Degradates | Function: GAC Isotherm | Details: In studies with Filtrasorb 400, simazine was found to have adsorption capacities that would be cost-effective in full-scale treatment.  Adsorption capacities were lower in field waters with background organics competing with simazine for adsorption. [502, 1486]
In studies with two commercially available powdered activated carbons (PACs), adsorption capacities for simazine were generally higher than for deethylsimazine. [1428]

Contaminant Name: Simazine and Simazine Degradates | Function: Granular Activated Carbon | Details: Monitoring over 11 sample days at a full-scale plant found mean 62 percent removal with only an 18-inch cap of 18-month-old GAC over a  sand filter. [502]  GAC is the best available technology for simazine control.
Overall, GAC offers a very attractive process for controlling chloro-s-triazines and the their metabolites. While PAC is also effective for herbicide control, GAC offers advantages especially for systems where herbicides and metabolite concentrations are significant for an appreciable portion of the year in addition to the growing season. [1425]

Contaminant Name: Simazine and Simazine Degradates | Function: Membrane Separation | Details: In one study, the average rejection rate of simazine in a bench-scale, hollow-fiber NF membrane was 42.2%. It was shown that phenylic pesticides (Alachlor, metolachlor, methoxychlor, and thiobencarb) were rejected more effectively than non-phenylic pesticides (aldicarb, simazine, atrazine, and pirimicarb). [1430]  In another study, removal percentages for simazine by NF membranes ranged from 65 to 95% in bench-scale, flat sheet configuration.

Contaminant Name: Simazine and Simazine Degradates | Overall: Technologies that are at least partially effective for control of simazine include powdered activated carbon (PAC), granular activated carbon (GAC), ozonation, and membrane separation. GAC is the designated Best Available Technology for simazine control.  Chlorination, chlorine dioxide, conventional treatment and precipitative softening were not generally effective for removing simazine from drinking water. 
Control of deethylsimazine was observed in bench-scale studies with PAC.  Dethylsimazine was not contolled by ozonation, chlorination, conventional treatment or precipitative softening.  
Control of diaminochlorotriazine  was not observed in studies examining chlorination, conventional treatment or precipitative softening. 
Research was found evaluating the following treatment technologies for the removal of simazine and its degradates:

Contaminant Name: Simazine and Simazine Degradates | Function: Ozone | Details: In one study, removal using ozone was near 65% with only a few seconds of contact time, suggesting simazine may be sufficiently ozone reactive to be cost effective [1431].  In another study, atrazine was well controlled in a river water at pHs 6 and 9 in 30 minutes at ozone doses up to 5 mg/L, suggesting structurally similar simazine may be likely reactive with ozone [1428].
Deethylsimazine was not reactive with ozone in at pHs 6 and 9 in 30 minutes at ozone doses up to 5 mg/L [1428].

Contaminant Name: Simazine and Simazine Degradates | Function: Powdered Activated Carbon | Details: Removal percentages ranged from 55% to 88% for simazine for PAC dosage ranging from 5 to 13 mg/L, depending on PAC type and contact time.  [1424, 1428, 1431] 
Removal percentages ranged from 80% to 90% for deethylsimazine for PAC dosage of 10 mg/L, depending on PAC type. [1428] 
The use of PAC technology to treat simazine contaminated water supplies has several advantages over the other technologies, including the following: (1) No oxidation byproducts are formed with PAC, (2) the ease of PAC application and dosage control; and (3) the current use of PAC in many plants for taste and odor control during the same months that herbicide contamination tends to be most severe (i.e., late spring and early summer). [1424]

Contaminant Name: Simazine and Simazine Degradates | Function: Precipitative Softening | Details: In jar testing of well water from Rolla, Missouri with excess lime/soda ash softening, no removal of simazine, deethylsimazine or diaminochlorotriazine was found.  Monitoring a full-scale plant treating Maumee River water found no removal of simazine.

Contaminant Name: Simazine and Simazine Degradates | Function: Ultraviolet Irradiation and Hydrogen Peroxide | Details: The photo-Fenton study demonstrates the rapid decomposition of simazine by the photo-Fenton reaction under mild conditions. The limited tests on commercial formulations suggest that the photo-Fenton reaction may be effective in removing simazine. [1427]

Contaminant Name: Strontium | Description: Some drinking water sources have low levels of radionuclides.  While stable strontium isotopes exist in nature, strontium has two primary radionuclides that are detected in drinking water. Strontium-89 and strontium-90 are both anthropogenic nuclides present in water from the use of cooling water for nuclear reactors and from industrial sources.  Both radionuclides are beta particle emitters and are regulated in drinking water.  Radioactive strontium is regulated in drinking water due to its radiotoxicity [584].  The half-lives of strontium-89 and strontium-90 are 51 days and 29 years, respectively [1760].  Because of its prevalence and activity, strontium-90 is a larger concern for water utilities.
Emitted particles ionize or destabilize atoms as they pass through the body's cells damaging chromosomes, which can lead to cancer. Radioactive strontium can be taken up by bone, damaging bone marrow and reducing blood cell counts. It is not known whether radioactive strontium has harmful effects on human reproduction [1760].
Radioactive strontium and other beta particle emitters are regulated in drinking water with a combined maximum contaminant level of 4 mrem per year [584], or 8 picoCuries/L. 
USEPA has identified ion exchange and reverse osmosis as Best Available Technologies (BATs) for the control of radioactive strontium in drinking water [566].
Because radioactive strontium is able to travel with percolating waters to underlying layers of soil and into groundwater, it is found in both surface and ground waters [1764].  In the National Inorganics and Radionuclides Survey (NIRS) of ground waters, gross beta-particle activity detects exceeded the study screening level of 50 pCi/L in 9 of 990 public water supplies [586].
Naturally-occurring strontium is present in the earth's crust at an average concentration of 0.04%. In seawater, it is the tenth most abundant element [1764].  Strontium is used industrially as a lead scavenger and in steel production.  Strontium carbonate is used in pyrotechnics.  Strontium chloride is used in toothpaste.  Natural strontium is not regulated in drinking water, but is of concern because of its bone and skeletal effects.  This concern has led to its inclusion in the third round of the Unregulated Contaminants Monitoring Rule, and in the Contaminants Candidate List 3. 
Date of Literature Search: August 2010, July 2014

Contaminant Name: Strontium | Function: Adsorptive Media | Details: As indicated from batch isotherm tests, adsorption can be very effective (up to 99 percent removal) for strontium removal [1766]. Data for full-scale water treatment were not available; full-scale implementation of this technology may not be feasible. Strontium removal by adsorptive media is largely dependent on the initial concentration, adsorbent type, and water chemistry (pH and competing anions).  As pH increased, precipitation played a larger role in strontium removal.
For studies with strontium-90, a number of adsorptive media were effective, with a synthetic zeolite being most effective (75 to 80% removal) [1765]. Bentonite clay at higher pH (7.4 to 8) was also effective (85 to 99% removal) [1766].
For studies with natural strontium, hydrated manganese oxide at higher pH (8 to 10) was most effective (90 to 92% removal) [1767].
A study of adsorption onto expanded perlite (EP) showed 50% removal [2181]. Sorption experiments indicated that the process was strongly pH-dependent. The EP was directly used as adsorbent without any chemical treatment as this would be the most economical commercial product. The optimum value for pH of solution is 6.0. Adsorption efficiency improved with an increase in the amount of EP (up to 60% removal) and an increase in contact time. Equilibrium isotherm data were analysed using Langmuir, Freundlich, and Dubinin–Radushkevich isotherms. The present study showed that EP can be used as a low-cost adsorbent for removal of strontium ions from aqueous solutions.

Contaminant Name: Strontium | Function: Conventional Treatment | Details: Based on one full-scale study [373] examining both alum and ferric chloride, removal of strontium-90 from water by conventional treatment was not effective (0 percent removal).

Contaminant Name: Strontium | Function: Ion Exchange | Details: Based on one study involving bench-scale isotherm tests using a ground water, removal of strontium-90 by two cation exchange resins was found to be very effective (greater than 99 percent removal in one case) [1765]. Removal improved with contact time. Ion exchange is a Best Available Technology for control of beta particle emitters like strontium-90.
Another study found that for corresponding adsorbent concentrations, removal with synthetic zeolite A4 (100% with 0.1% adsorbent concentration) was far more effective than removal with natural zeolite, mordenite (90% with 1% adsorbent concentration) [2177]. The removal efficiency increased with increased concentration of adsorbent. Application of synthetic or natural zeolite at water purifications plants may be also effective to remove radioactive strontium.
A fixed bed column sorption study showed that ion removal was a strong function of initial flow rate, bed height and initial ion concentration. An increase in bed height and initial ion concentration resulted in a decrease in effluent volume whereas an increase in bed height led to higher contact times thus higher effluent volume. Reported results showed that synthetic zeolite A is an efficient ion exchange material with complete removal of strontium ions from aqueous solutions [2178].

Contaminant Name: Strontium | Function: Membrane Filtration | Details: A study [2179] using co-precipitation followed by microfiltration showed high removal (99.4%). Although the mean decontamination factor (DF) value was lower for a sodium carbonate dosage of 1000 mg/L than for a dosage of 2000 mg/L, the rate of decline in the specific flux (SF) of the membrane was retarded, and the amount of water treated increased. The concentrations of calcium and magnesium in the water significantly affected the amount of solids rejected by the membranes.

Contaminant Name: Strontium | Function: Membrane Separation | Details: There are a number of commercially available products employing reverse osmosis for control of strontium in drinking water.  Four were tested in USEPA's Environmental Technology Verification project.  Natural strontium was effectively removed (97 to greater than 99%) [15] [16] [617] [1015]. Reverse osmosis is a Best Available Technology for the control of beta particle emitters like radioactive strontium. 
Another study using selective complexation combined with a nanofiltration membrane achieved 100% removal [2185]. Effects of transmembrane pressure, pH, ligand concentration and ionic strength on salts retention are described, and the complexation constants are estimated. Strontium retentions are nearly constant with increasing transmembrane pressure. Above pH 6, strontium retention increases sharply (90% to 100% retention) [2185]. Strontium retention increases with poly(acrylic acid) concentration (76% to 98% retention) [2185]. Experiments showed high sodium salt retention involves a decline in filtrations fluxes, large water addition to stabilize retentate salinity during concentrations, and a decrease in strontium retention (100% to 65% retention) [2185].

Contaminant Name: Strontium | Function: Other Treatment | Details: A study [2179] using co-precipitation followed by microfiltration showed high removal (99.4%). Although the mean decontamination factor (DF) value was lower for a sodium carbonate dosage of 1000 mg/L than for a dosage of 2000 mg/L, the rate of decline in the specific flux (SF) of the membrane was retarded, and the amount of water treated increased. The concentrations of calcium and magnesium in the water significantly affected the amount of solids rejected by the membranes.

Contaminant Name: Strontium | Overall: The following processes were found effective for the removal of radioactive or natural strontium: adsorptive media (up to 99 percent removal), reverse osmosis (greater than 99 percent removal), ion exchange (greater than 99 percent removal), co-precipitation followed by microfiltration (greater than 99 percent removal), and powdered activated carbon coupled with ion exchange (greater than 90 percent removal). Conventional treatment (0 percent removal) was not effective at removing strontium from water. The level of control for ion exchange and for reverse osmosis is consistent with their designation as a Best Available Technology for beta particle removal. Performance of these technologies can vary based on water quality and treatment characteristics.
Adsorptive media provided limited removal based on specific water chemistry. Removal was largely dependent on pH and the presence of competing ions. As pH increased, precipitation played a larger role in strontium removal.
Research was identified evaluating the following treatment technologies for the removal of strontium:

Contaminant Name: Strontium | Function: Powdered Activated Carbon | Details: Removal with PAC [2177] was somewhat effective with a 0.01% PAC concentration (62% removal). It was even less effective with lower PAC concentration (35% removal at 0.001% concentration).
Removal with four pot-type water purifiers purchased from four major manufacturers (models A, B, C, and D) were effective [2177]. Initial removal efficiencies from Models A and B are about 70% and from Models C and D are about 90%. Efficiencies were slightly decreased by use, but efficiency for Model D decreased the least by use. Models A and B are activated carbon, ceramic and hollow fiber membrane filters. Models C and D are activated carbon and ion exchanger filters. In the case of radioactive contamination of tap water, water purifiers may be available for convenient decontamination of drinking water in the home.

Contaminant Name: Strontium | Function: Precipitative Softening | Details: Removal of strontium from water by precipitative softening can be moderately effective in several cities (up to 75% removal during routine water softening procedures). A reduction of strontium concentration accompanies the removal of calcium. Coagulation and filtration, with or without softening, reduce the burden of dissolved strontium. Alum, however is apparently much less effective alone than in combination with softening agents such as lime-soda ash, phosphates, or ion exchangers. The strontium concentrations of raw surface waters were related to the predominant soil type in their drainage basins. Interferences concerning the removal of radiostrontium from contaminated drinking water were drawn [2176].

Contaminant Name: Strychnine | Description: Strychnine (strychnine alkaloid) is a synthetic organic pesticide used to destroy rodents and predatory animals [2153, 2155]. Strychnine is currently registered for use only below-ground as a bait application to control pocket gophers. Above-ground uses of strychnine have been prohibited by a U.S. Court injunction since 1988. Most strychnine products are classified as restricted use pesticides by USEPA. The USEPA published its Reregistration Eligibility Decision (RED) for strychnine in 1996 [2155]. Its IUPAC name is Strychnidin-10-one [2153].
Strychnine is not regulated by USEPA in drinking water.
USEPA placed strychnine in Toxicity Category I, indicating the greatest degree of acute toxicity for oral and ocular effects. Inhalation toxicity is also presumed to be high. Strychnine is a powerful convulsant which acts as a selective competitive antagonist to block the inhibitory effects of glycine [2155].
Neither groundwater nor surface water seem to be at risk from contamination of strychnine due to the following properties and phenomena. Strychnine is essentially immobile; the strychnine molecule (parent) is adsorbed to organic matter and clay. It is very insoluble in water (0.0115 g/100 mL). The parent does not hydrolyze at pH 5, 7, or 9. Parent is stable to soil photolysis. Current use pattern of strychnine involves application into specific below-ground burrows [2154].
Date of Literature Search: April 2012

Contaminant Name: Strychnine | Function: Granular Activated Carbon | Details: Removal of strychnine from water by GAC is generally effective (77 percent [1489] to greater than 86 percent removal - this percent removal was limited by the detection limit for the contaminant[618]). One GAC unit tested was a component of a point-of-use drinking water treatment system consisting of a RO module, a UV+ozone module, and the GAC filter [1489]. The other GAC unit tested was a post-membrane carbon filter part of a point-of-entry drinking water treatment system [618]. The percent removal range is for GAC alone. Both studies showed that the removal of strychnine at greater than 99 percent can be achieved by the RO+GAC and UV/ozone advanced simultaneous oxidation+GAC treatment combinations. GAC properties were not reported.

Contaminant Name: Strychnine | Function: Membrane Separation | Details: Removal of strychnine from water by membrane separation (specifically reverse osmosis) was very effective in four studies (98 percent removal [618] to greater than 99 percent removal [15, 16, 617]). The membrane units tested were components of four different point-of-use or point-of-entry drinking water treatment systems. The RO membrane of each system was tested alone and only for strychnine removal. Initial strychnine concentrations ranged between 900 and 1,100 micrograms per liter. One study showed that when permeate strychnine concentration exceeded 10 micrograms per liter, the use of a post-membrane carbon filter helped achieve greater than 99 percent removal of strychnine [618]. Contaminant removal by reverse osmosis is dependent upon membrane type, pH, recovery, and initial contaminant concentration.

Contaminant Name: Strychnine | Overall: The following processes are considered effective for the removal of strychnine: membrane separation using reverse osmosis (RO) (98 to greater than 99 percent removal), ultraviolet (UV) irradiation ozone (98 percent removal), granular activated carbon (GAC) (86 percent removal, this percent removal was limited by the detection limit of the contaminant). These treatment technologies are components of point-of-use and point-of-entry drinking water treatment systems which were tested independently. Greater than 99 percent removal of strychnine can be achieved by the ROGAC and UV/ozoneGAC treatment combinations.
Research was identified evaluating the following treatment technologies for the removal of strychnine:
Granular Activated Carbon, Membrane Separation and Ultraviolet Irradiation Ozone.

Contaminant Name: Strychnine | Function: Ultraviolet Irradiation and Ozone | Details: Ultraviolet (UV) irradiation and ozone was reported to remove strychnine in water by 98 percent in one study [1489].  The UV and ozone unit was a component of a point-of-entry drinking water system. The system consisted of a reverse osmosis (RO) module, the UV and ozone module (named as advanced simultaneous oxidation process, or ASOP), followed by a granular activiated carbon (GAC) filter. The UV light output was 30,000 microwatt-seconds/cm2 with a contact time of 33 seconds. The ozone dose was not reported. The influent to the UV+ozone module contained 910 μg/L strychnine with the pH level between 7.7 to 7.8. The effluent strychnine concentration from the UV+ozone unit was 20 μg/L.

Contaminant Name: Tebuconazole | Description: Tebuconazole is a synthetic triazole fungicide effective against various smut and bunt diseases in cereals and other field crops [2152], and commonly used on peppers [2145]. It is widely used as a biocide in various products for material protection e.g. treatment of wood, concrete, paints and roofs [2143]. Its IUPAC name is (RS)-1-p-chlorophenyl)-4,4-dimethyl-3-(1H-1,2,4-triazole-1-ylmethyl)pentan-3-ol [2148].
Tebuconazole is not regulated by USEPA in drinking water. However, it is regulated by the European Union (EU) under the EU Drinking Water Directive (98/83/EC) which requires pesticide concentrations in drinking water not exceed 0.1 μg/L for a single pesticide and 0.5 μg/L for total pesticides [2146].
Tebuconazole is often supplied as an oil in water emulsion or concentrate that is mixed with water and used as a spray [2152]. Similar to a great number of pesticides, tebuconazole is bio-recalcitrant and non-biodegradable [2146]. It is resistant to chemical and/or photochemical degradation under typical environmental conditions [2111]. It is non-volatile with a very low Henry's Law constant. Lab and field soil degradation studies found tebuconazole to be moderately to very persistent in soil (lab studies DT50>365 days, field studies DT50 range 19.9 to 91.6 days). Its key metabolite is 1,2,4-triazole which is formed in the soil. Its solubility in water is low, 36 mg/L at 20°C [2152]. The primary pathway for tebuconazole to enter water courses is through surface runoff and spray drift [2144].
Azole fungicides including tebuconazole may have the potential to affect the endocrine system of aquatic organisms. Their biological activity is based on the inhibition of certain pathways of steroidogenesis [2144]. In humans, it is known to impact the liver/blood system [2152]. 
Date of Literature Search: April 2012.

Contaminant Name: Tebuconazole | Function: Hydrogen Peroxide | Details: Removal of tebuconazole from water by hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) was found to be very effective in one study (100 percent removal) [2145] when using photo-Fenton treatment.  A Fenton process is an advanced oxidation process which employs Fe2+ and H2O2.  Fenton reagents act as a photocatalyst under natural light irradiation process. Molecular oxygen, pH, and temperature are important parameters of the photo-Fenton process.

Contaminant Name: Tebuconazole | Function: Other Treatment | Details: Other treatment processes that have been tested for the removal of tebuconazole in water include photolysis under natural sunlight with no catalyst [2145, 2146, 2147]; advanced oxidation processes (specifcally photocatalysis under natural sunlight) [2145, 2146, 2147]; and adsorption using natural vegetation substrate [2150]. One group of researchers found advanced oxidation processes to be very effective achieving greater than 99 percent removal of tebuconazole from leachate from an experimental greenhouse [2145, 2146]. The treatment using ZnO (photosensitizer) in the presence of Na2S2O8 under natural sunlight irradiation reduced a 500 μg/L tebuconazole influent to below detection limit [2146]. Another study confirmed the effectiveness of photocatalysis in degrading tebuconazole. Faster degradation and higher percent removal of tebuconazole were achieved under solar irridiation in the presence of a catalyst (Mo doped TiO2) and an oxidizing agent [(NH4)2S2O8] than under solar irridiation alone (93 versus 28 percent after 150-min reaction time) [2147]. Photolysis under natural sunlight alone (with no catalyst) was not effective in removing tebuconazole, 1 to 38 percent removal reported [2145, 2146]. The removal of tebuconazole from water through adsorption using hemp substrate was also not very effective. A 48 percent removal was achieved (influent = 20 μg/L) when the substrate was initially dry and only 20 percent was removed when the substrate was initially saturated with water [2150].

Contaminant Name: Tebuconazole | Overall: The following processes are considered effective for the removal of tebuconazole: advanced oxidation using Fenton treatment, Fe2 H2O2 (greater than 99 percent removal) and advanced oxidation using a photosensitizer (ZnO) and an oxidant (Na2S2O8) under natural sunlight irradiation (greater than 99 percent removal). Ultraviolet irridiation (UV) can be effective in the presence of both a photocatalyst (TiO2) and an oxidizing agent (NH4)2S2O8 achieving 93 removal.
Lesser extent of removal was achieved by adsoprtion using a natural hemp substrate (48 percent removal). This treatment is not suitable for drinking water treatment but is applicable to the mitigation of tebuconazole in agricultural ditches. 
Research was identified evaluating the following treatment technologies for the removal of tebuconazole:  
Hydrogen Peroxide, Other Treatment and Ultraviolet Irradiation.

Contaminant Name: Tebuconazole | Function: Ultraviolet Irradiation | Details: Removal of tebuconazole from water by ultraviolet irradiation (UV) can be effective in the presence of both photocatalyst (TiO2) and oxidizing agent [(NH4)2S2O8] at 93% removal versus 10% (UV irradiation alone) and 23% (UV + catalyst only) after 150-minute reaction time in one study [2147]. Doping of the photocatalyst with the transition metal Mo, in turn decreased photocatalytic efficiency under UV light. Less than 80 percent removal was achieved under different dopant concentrations.

Contaminant Name: Tebufenozide | Description: Tebufenozide is an insecticide, targeting lepidopteran species, specifically the gypsy moth.  Tebufenozide is used on forestry, ornamentals, and a variety of crops including grapes, berries, and tuberous and corm vegetables (except potatoes). There are no residential uses.  Approximately 150,000 pounds of tebufenozide is applied annually in the U.S. [1814].  It mimics the action molting hormones resulting in a lethal unsuccessful molt. [2003]
Its IUPAC name is N-tert-butyl-N'-(4-ethylbenzoyl)-3,5-dimethylbenzohydrazide.  In soil its major metabolite is 4-(N'-(3,5-dimethylbenzoyl-N-(1,1-dimethylethyl)hydrazinocarbonyl) benzoic acid, and minor metabolites are 4-(N'-(3,5-dimethylbenzoyl-N-(1,1-dimethylethyl)hydrazinocarbonyl) phenylacetic acid and N-(1,1-dimetylethyl)-N-(4-acetylbenzoyl)-3,5-dimethylbenzoylhydrazine. [2001] 
Tebufenozide is of low acute toxicity, categorized as Toxicity Category IV for acute oral, dermal, and inhalation exposures, and for ocular and dermal irritation. There is no evidence of carcinogenicity for tebufenozide from available mammalian
studies and tebufenozide is classified as “not likely to be carcinogenic to humans.” [1814]  It is hemotoxic with red blood cell damage. [2003]
There are scant occurrence data.  It is not regulated in drinking water, but is on the Contaminant Candidate List 3.
Date of Literature Search: September 2010

Contaminant Name: Tebufenozide | Overall: No data were found regarding the efficacy of any drinking water treatment process for the control of tebufenozide.  Physical/chemical property data are relatively scant. Its Henry's law constant suggests it is not likely air strippable.  Its Koc suggests it may be bound to soil particles and be controlled during coagulation or precipitative softening.  Using Polanyi models, its molecular weight, solubilty and density suggest it would be strongly adsorbed to granular activated carbon or controlled by powdered activated carbon.  Its structure suggests it may be reactive to stronger oxidants like chlorine, chlorine dioxide, ozone or advanced oxidation processes utilizing the hydroxyl readical, resulting in transformation products.  Its size suggests it would be controlled by reverse osmosis.

Contaminant Name: Terbufos | Description: Terbufos [S-tert-butylthiomethyl O,O-diethyl phosphorodithioate] is an organothiophosphate insecticide-nematicide applied to corn, sugar beets and grain sorghum to control wireworms, seedcorn maggots, white grubs, corn rootworm larvae, and other pests. It is classified as toxicity class I (highly toxic).
Products containing 15 percent or more terbufos are classified as Restricted Use Pesticides (RUPs), which may be purchased and used only by certified applicators [1938].  Terbufos can be introduced in humans through oral, inhalation or dermal routes but has not been found to accumulate in body tissues [1940].  It has acute effects a cholinestrase inhibitor with nervous system effects.  It is not likely a human carcinogen.
Terbufos was first registered in 1974; the average domestic use was reported to be approximately 7.5 million pounds of active ingredient per year for 1987 to 1996 [1947]. It is not registered for use in residential settings. An estimated 87% is used on corn [1948].
Terbufos is considered to be low to moderately persistent in soil; it converts rapidly to its metabolites, which then persist in the soil. Reported half-lives are 5 to 30 days, while its metabolites persist 2 to 3 times longer [1938]. Its dissipation is faster in soils with low organic carbon content and higher temperatures. Due to its immobility and low water solubility, terbufos is not expected to impact groundwater supplies. Terbufos does hydrolyze quickly, with half lives of 4.5 to 8.5 days at pH in range of 5-9 [1938]. Vapor pressure and Henry's constant suggest terbufos may volatilize.  Principal degradates are its sulfone, sulfoxide and oxon sulfone metabolites. 
Although not currently regulated by the USEPA in drinking water, it is included on the USEPA's Contaminant Candidate List 3 [620].  USGS NAWQA monitoring found terbufos infrequently (

Contaminant Name: Terbufos | Function: Chlorine | Details: One bench-scale study showed effective oxidation of terbufos with chlorination at doses typically applied at drinking water plants. In chlorinated water, terbufos was not detected within one hour of contact time. The degradation of terbufos in non-chlorinated water was attributed to hydrolysis. Two analysis methods were employed in the study. Significantly lower concentrations with the LC-MS/MS method were attributed to the instability of terbufos in water, since these samples remained in water before injection.

Contaminant Name: Terbufos | Overall: Very limited data is available on the effectiveness of current treatment technologies for the removal of terbufos from drinking water.  Terbufos and its degradates are reported to be unstable in water and degrade naturally in source waters via hydrolysis.  One study showed complete oxidation of terbufos with chlorine within one hour of contact time.  Another study showed 70 to >99 degradation with UV irradiation in the presence of TiO2 particles.

Contaminant Name: Terbufos | Function: Ultraviolet Irradiation | Details: One bench-scale study showed effective degradation of terbufos by UV irradiation in the presence of TiO2 particles. Removals ranged from 70 percent to up to >99 percent. The degradation was influenced by the pH and TiO2 dose. Alkaline pH and initial TiO2 concentrations of 0.5 to 1 g/L were favorable for the degradation of terbufos.

Contaminant Name: Tetrachloroethylene | Description: Tetrachloroethylene, also known as perchloroethylene (PCE), tetrachloroethene, or perc, is a nonflammable colorless organic liquid with a mild, chloroform-like odor. It is used in the textile industry, as a component of aerosol dry-cleaning products, as a metal degreasing solvent, and as a chemical intermediate [2439, 2438].
PCE is a volatile organic compound (VOC) that transfers quickly from water to air. Therefore, it is found more often in groundwater than in surface water, from which it tends to volatilize rapidly. In a U.S. Geological Survey (USGS) study of groundwater, PCE was among the 15 most frequently detected VOCs, found in approximately 4 percent of 3,498 samples [2438]. In groundwater, PCE can biodegrade to trichloroethylene, dichloroethylene, vinyl chloride, and ethene [2438, 2301]. U.S. EPA has found PCE in at least 945 of the 1,699 current or former NPL sites [2438].
PCE is regulated in drinking water in the US at a maximum contaminant level (MCL) of 0.005 mg/L. USEPA has identified granular activated carbon and packed tower aeration as the two Best Available Technologies (BATs) for removal of PCE from drinking water [1250]. PCE can harm the nervous system, liver, kidneys, and reproductive system, and may be harmful to unborn children. EPA has classified it as a likely human carcinogen [2439, 2440, 2438, 2301].
Date of Literature Search: 2016

Contaminant Name: Tetrachloroethylene | Function: Aeration and Air Stripping | Details: Packed tower aeration can achieve very high PCE removal (greater than 90 to greater than 99.9 percent), including in full-scale treatment of drinking water [1627, 2303, 2434]. USEPA has identified packed tower aeration as a Best Available Technology for removal of TCE from drinking water [1250]. Studies also have shown high removals using tray aeration (greater than 99.99 percent) and membrane air stripping (up to 79 percent) [1627, 2437].

Contaminant Name: Tetrachloroethylene | Function: GAC Isotherm | Details: PCE has been shown to be readily adsorbed by granular activated carbon, based on data from isotherm studies with Freundlich K values ranging from 50.8 to 273 [2308, 2432].

Contaminant Name: Tetrachloroethylene | Function: Granular Activated Carbon | Details: Granular activated carbon can achieve very high removals of PCE (up to greater than 99.9 percent removal). These results include data from pilot and  full-scale sites, including remediation sites with very high PCE concentrations (up to 170,000 mg/L) and from drinking water facilities with more moderate concentrations (3.2 to approximately 16 mg/L) [2308, 2411, 2431, 2432, 2433, 2434, 2435]. One full-scale study of drinking water treatment found GAC maintained very high removal (greater than 90 percent) of PCE for more than 8 months, declining to less than 50 percent only after 20 months [2435]. USEPA has identified granular activated carbon as a Best Available Technology for removal of PCE from drinking water [1250].

Contaminant Name: Tetrachloroethylene | Overall: USEPA has identified granular activated carbon and packed tower aeration as the two Best Available Technologies (BATs) for removal of PCE from drinking water. Studies also have shown other aeration processes to be effective for PCE removal, including tray aeration and membrane air stripping.

Contaminant Name: Thiodicarb | Description: Thiodicarb [CAS name dimethyl-N,N'-thiobis(methylimino)carbonyloxy-bis-ethanimidothioate] is an N-methyl carbamate insecticide of the carbamate class of pesticides. It is primarily used for control of a number of insect pests on cotton, sweet corn, and soybeans, although it is also used to a lesser extent on leafy vegetables, cole crops, and ornamentals. It is also a mulluscicide.  Thiodicarb was first registered in the United States in 1984, and approximately 113,000 pounds are used on agricultural use sites per year [1949]. Thiodicarb is not registered for residential use.  It is registered for application via aerosal dispersal [1950].
Thiodicarb is two methomyl molecules connected by a sulfur atom.  Methomyl results from cleavage of the -N-S-N- bond.
In the environment, thiodicarb rapidly degrades to methomyl, which is also registered for use as an insecticide. Studies show that thiodicarb degrades within several hours to a few days with the primary degradation mechanisms thought to be biodegradation, hydrolysis, and photolysis [1950, 1951]. While thiodicarb does not appear to be very persistent or highly mobile, its metabolite, methomyl, is more persistent, more mobile, and more toxic [1950]. Thiodicarb is not expected to have a high potential to contaminate groundwater, however, methomyl has been detected infrequently in groundwater due to its moderate persistence and high mobility [1950]. 
Thiodicarb is not regulated by USEPA in drinking water, but is an unregulated contaminant listed on the USEPA's Contaminant Candidate List 3 [620]. Thiodicarb is classified as a Group B2 agent, meaning it is a probable human carcinogen [1950].  It is also a blood toxicant, neurotoxicant and an acetylcholinesterase inhibitor.
Because of its instabilty (principally by hydrolysis) thiodicarb is not expected to be present in ground water, or in surface water unless in close time and proximity to aerial application.  Thus it is not expected to be present in drinking water.  Methomyl, however, mey be present in field waters and in drinking waters as a degradate.  For treatment of methomyl, go to List of Contaminants.  
Date of Literature Search: September 2010

Contaminant Name: Thiodicarb | Overall: No data was found evaluating the removal of thiodicarb from drinking water. This is likely due to the fact that in the environment thiodicarb degrades very rapidly to methomyl. One study found the half life of thiodicarb to be less than four hours when applied to soil. Because of its lack of persistence, it is unlikely that thiodicarb would be required to be removed from source water at a drinking water treatment facility.
Its major degradant, however, is methomyl, another registered pesticide.  As a degradate, methomyl may be present in source and drinking waters.  For its treatment, go to List of Contaminants.

Contaminant Name: Trichloroethylene | Description: Trichloroethylene (TCE) is used primarily in metal degreasing operations. It is also used as a solvent for greases, oils, fats and tars, in paint removers, coatings and vinyl resins, and by the textile processing industry to scour cotton, wool and other fabrics. TCE may be used as a chemical intermediate in the production of polyvinyl chloride, pharmaceuticals, flame retardant chemicals and insecticides [1629]. TCE can also be found in household chemicals such as typewriter correction fluids and spot removers. [1251]
It has been found in underground water sources and many surface waters as a result of the manufacture, use, and disposal of the chemical [1251]. TCE may be introduced into surface water and groundwater in industrial effluents. Poor handling as well as improper disposal of TCE in landfills have been the main causes of groundwater contamination. The biodegradation of another volatile organic pollutant, tetrachloroethene (or perchloroethylene, PCE), in groundwater may also lead to the formation of TCE. [1629]
Because of their volatility, TCE and related solvents are seldom detected in concentrations greater than a few micrograms per liter in surface waters [1622], where volatilization is the principal route of degradation, while photodegradation and hydrolysis play minor roles [1629]. However, bodies of fresh water receiving wastewater discharges may contain elevated concentrations of organic solvents during periods of ice cover when volatilization is restricted [1622]. In groundwater, TCE is degraded slowly by microorganisms. Bioconcentration of trichloroethene in aquatic species is low. [1629]
Trichloroethylene has been detected in many samples taken from drinking water supplied by contaminated sources from which trichloroethylene and other volatile organic compounds are not always completely removed by conventional water treatment. The EPA Groundwater Supply Survey of finished water from 945 drinking water systems nationwide using groundwater sources found trichloroethylene in 91 water systems (detection limit 0.2 ppb); the median level of the positive samples was approximately 1 μg /L, with a single maximum level of 130 μg /L, as reported in 1984. A summary of U.S. groundwater analyses from both federal and state studies reported that trichloroethylene was the most frequently detected organic solvent and the one present in the highest concentration, as reported in 1982.  An analysis of the EPA STORET Data Base (1980-1982) found that trichloroethylene had been positively detected in 28% of 9,295 surface water reporting stations nationwide. [1252] As of 2003, TCE has been found in at least 852 of the 1,430 National Priorities List sites identified by the US Environmental Protection Agency (USEPA). [1251]
TCE is regulated in drinking water in the US at a maximum contaminant level (MCL) of 0.005 mg/L. USEPA has identified granular activated carbon and packed tower aeration as the two Best Available Technologies (BATs) for removal of TCE from drinking water. [1250]
It is regulated based on its potential for adverse effects on the liver and for risk of inducing cancer.
Date of Literature Search: June 2009

Contaminant Name: Trichloroethylene | Function: Adsorptive Media | Details: One study at the pilot scale treating a ground water reported high to very high removals of TCE (90 to 99.9%) by XE-340 resin adsorption. Capacity of this resin was typically two to three times that of GAC on a weight basis for control to breakthrough.

Contaminant Name: Trichloroethylene | Function: Aeration and Air Stripping | Details: Packed tower aeration can achieve moderate to complete TCE removal (60 to 100%). These studies included pilot- and full-scale monitoring of ground waters.  One study at very high air-to-water ratios shows complete removal. Another study of twin towers operated in series shows near complete removal of TCE. One study showed overall better performance of hydrophilized packing as compared to non-hydrophilized packing. USEPA has identified packed tower aeration as a Best Available Technology for removal of TCE from drinking water.
One study demonstrated that diffused aeration can achieve low to complete removal of TCE, depending on air-to-water ratio.
One study evaluated membrane air stripping. Results indicate a possible higher efficiency as compared to packed tower aeration.

Contaminant Name: Trichloroethylene | Function: Conventional Treatment | Details: Conventional treatment was found to be marginally effective for the removal of TCE in one study. Based on data available from a bench-scale study, 40 percent of the contaminant was removed by coagulation with the use of alum, although it should be noted that volitilization also contributed to the removal of TCE in this study.
The study also found that 85.6 percent of the contaminant was removed with coagulation using pottasium ferrate (a combined coagulant/oxidizer).
Further, complete removal was obtained using nitrogen gas flocculation and potasium ferrate. Control is attributed to volatilization and to oxidation.

Contaminant Name: Trichloroethylene | Function: GAC Isotherm | Details: One study showed that the effectiveness of GAC to remove TCE is negatively impacted by the concentration of natural organic matter. More specifically, another study showed the reduction of TCE adsorption by GAC due to competition from lower molecular weight humic substances. GAC adsorption capacity for TCE was also reported to decrease with increasing temperature and presence of ethyl acetate, however, not significantly impacted by pH or salinity (specific Freundlich parameters not reported) [2136].

Contaminant Name: Trichloroethylene | Function: Granular Activated Carbon | Details: Granular activated carbon can achieve high to very high removals of TCE (from 75% up to greater than 99% removal). One study monitored full-scale treatment of a ground water for four months without detectable breakthrough.  Another study reported short times to breakthrough but utilized a very high influent TCE concentration (1,000 ug/L). USEPA has identified granular activated carbon as a Best Available Technology for removal of TCE from drinking water.

Contaminant Name: Trichloroethylene | Function: Hydrogen Peroxide | Details: The use of hydrogen peroxide in combination with synthesized reactive nanostructured stacked membranes was found to be effective in removing TCE (70 to 80 percent for groundwater and lab water, respectively), according to results of one bench-scale study.  It should be noted that the influent contaminant concentration and reaction time were higher than conditions typically experienced in drinking water treatment applications.

Contaminant Name: Trichloroethylene | Function: Membrane Separation | Details: Two studies presented mixed results on the ability of membranes to remove TCE from drinking water.  One study documented the ability of nanofiltration to achieve high to very high TCE reductions (81 to 98%), while the other study showed rejections of 0 to 3 percent for most membranes tested.

Contaminant Name: Trichloroethylene | Function: Other Treatment | Details: The use of synthesized reactive nanostructured stacked membranes in combination with hydrogen peroxide was found to be effective in removing TCE (70 to 80 percent for groundwater and lab water, respectively), according to results of one bench-scale study.  It should be noted that the influent contaminant concentration and reaction time were higher than conditions typically experienced in drinking water treatment applications.

Contaminant Name: Trichloroethylene | Overall: Several treatment processes can be highly effective at reducing TCE concentrations in contaminated source waters. These processes include packed tower aeration, granular activated carbon adsorption, membrane separation, ozone hydrogen peroxide, and UV irradiation TiO2.  Of these, packed tower aeration and granular activated carbon adsorption are Best Available Technologies. 
There are other processes that can also achieve high TCE removals, but which only possess marginal applicability due to operational requirements outside of typical ranges (e.g., require high doses or long contact times). These processes include ozone, permanganate, UV irradiation hydrogen peroxide, and UV irradiation ozone.
Membrane air stripping and resin adsorption are two less commonly used processes that were shown by single studies to be effective at removing TCE.
Following is a summary of the effectiveness of each treatment process to remove TCE from water, based on identified and referenced literature:

Contaminant Name: Trichloroethylene | Function: Ozone | Details: Ozone can achieve fair to very high reductions of TCE. However, high reductions require a high ozone dose and a long contact time. One study showed that pH (4 to 10) had no effect on TCE reduction.

Contaminant Name: Trichloroethylene | Function: Ozone and Hydrogen Peroxide | Details: Several studies document that ozone coupled with hydrogen peroxide can be extremely effective at reducing TCE. One study showed the high effectiveness (>90%) of the technology across a wide range of test conditions, including a wide range of TCE influent concentrations (0.4 to 477 ug/L).
Another study showed the influence of inorganic carbon concentration (as bicarbonate) on process performance. Inorganic carbon is a scavenger of hydroxyl radicals, and increasing concentrations will decrease the overall AOP performance.
One study showed an optimum peroxide-to-ozone ratio of 0.5. The results also indicated that reduction effectiveness was not dependent of contact time, and that TCE removal increased with ozone dosage for a given peroxide-to-ozone ratio.
Another study showed that increasing peroxide beyond a certain dose does not yield significant benefits. However, increasing the ozone dose accelerates TCE removal. Bicarbonate is a hydroxyl radical scavenger that competes with TCE and can lessen the overal ozone-peroxide efficiency.

Contaminant Name: Trichloroethylene | Function: Permanganate | Details: A wide range of low to very high reductions of TCE can be achieved by permanganate. However, very high permanganate doses and long contact times are required to achieve significant reductions. One study shows the beneficial effect of warmer temperatures on TCE reduction.

Contaminant Name: Trichloroethylene | Function: Ultraviolet Irradiation | Details: Two studies presenting TCE concentrations typical of those seen in drinking water applications found that UV irradiation alone was somewhat ineffective at reducing TCE. With UV exposure times of 18 to 300 seconds, removals of 18 to 21 percent were achieved. It was found that increasing UV exposure time did not significantly improve TCE reduction. However, the addition of TiO2 significantly increased the effectiveness of UV treatment to 90% removal.
For TCE concentrations higher than those typically seen in drinking water applications, the use of UV treatment resulted in removal efficiencies of 43 to 84 percent, depending on the water type and UV intensity. The use of TiO2 in combination with UV achieved 35 to 98 percent removal in three bench scale studies. Increased removal was achieved with increased UV intensity.

Contaminant Name: Trichloroethylene | Function: Ultraviolet Irradiation and Hydrogen Peroxide | Details: UV irradiation coupled with hydrogen peroxide can achieve high to very high reductions of TCE in contaminated water (up to greater than 99 percent removal). In most cases, however, this technology may not be feasible for full-scale water treatment due to the high hydrogen peroxide doses and long UV exposure times required.

Contaminant Name: Trichloroethylene | Function: Ultraviolet Irradiation and Ozone | Details: One study showed that the corona discharge advanced oxidation process can achieve high to very high (up to 95 percent) reductions of TCE in contaminated water.  This technology is innovative, however, may not be practical for full-scale water treatment applications.

Contaminant Name: Uranium | Description: Some drinking water sources have very low levels of radionuclides (radioactive elements). A radionuclide is an unstable (decaying) form of a nuclide. Uranium has three radionuclides that are detected in and regulated in drinking water. U-238 is an alpha emitter and the parent compound in the uranium-238 series. Its half life is approximately 4.5 billion years. U-235 is also an alpha emitter and the parent compound in the actinium series. Its half life is approximately 710 million years. U-234 is a beta emitter and the third-member decay product of the uranium-238 series. Its half life is approximately 250,000 years. Long half lives lead to their stability in water. [586] Uranium is regulated in drinking water as the sum of its isotopes.
Uranium is naturally occurring and predominantly found in groundwater in the Colorado Plateau, the Western Central Platform, the Rocky Mountain System, Basin and Range, and the Pacific Mountain System. Granite, metamorphic rocks, lignites, monazite sand, and phosphate deposits typically contain uranium. Before it is transported into water, however, it must be oxidized. [586] The abundance of uranium isotopes found in the Earth's crust is typically not duplicated in ground water. U-234 is enriched in water when standardized to U-238. While more prevalent in ground water, uranium can be found in both ground and surface water. [586]
Emitted particles ionize or destabilize atoms as they pass thru the body's cells damaging chromosomes, which can lead to cancer. In addition to radiotoxicity, exposure to elevated uranium levels in drinking water may lead to kidney failure (chemical toxicity). [585] Uranium is regulated in drinking water due to its chemical and radio toxicity. [584]
Uranium is regulated by USEPA in drinking water with a maximum contaminant level of 30 µg/L. [584] USEPA has identified ion exchange, lime softening, reverse osmosis, and enhanced coagulation/filtration as Best Available Technologies (BATs) for the control of uranium in drinking water. [566]
In the National Inorganics and Radionuclides Survey (NIRS) of ground waters, uranium detects exceeded the detection level of 0.08 µg/L in 72.2 percent of 978 sites. The median, mean and maximum levels were, respectively, 0.53, 2.6 and 88.2 µg/L. [587]
Date of Literature Search: June 2009

Contaminant Name: Uranium | Function: Adsorptive Media | Details: As indicated from batch isotherm tests, adsorption can be very effective (up to greater than 99 percent removal) for uranium removal.  Data for full-scale water treatment were not available.  Uranium removal by adsorptive media is largely dependent on the initial concentration, oxidation state, adsorbent type, and water chemistry (pH and competing anions).  Best removals were achieved in the pH 4 to 7 range.  Iron-based medias, particularly zero valent iron, were most effective at uranium removal.  Most of the studies acknowledged that adsorption was not the only mechanism that played a role in uranium removal.  Uranium reduction from U(VI) to U(IV) and co-precipitation also played a role in removing uranium.  Other polymer-based medias were also effective at removing uranium in batch isotherm tests.

Contaminant Name: Uranium | Function: Biological Treatment | Details: One study found biological treatment to be effective at removing uranium (25 to 88 percent) in situ bioremediation and in a bench-scale soil column test.   The study indicated that both adsorption and reduction likely played a role in uranium removal.  In both cases, acetate was used to develop the microorganism population.  Uranium removal continued after acetate addition.  Sterlized soil columns were not effective at uranium removal.

Contaminant Name: Uranium | Function: Conventional Treatment | Details: Removal of uranium by conventional treatment processes can be very effective (up to 95 percent) under appropriate treatment conditions. Conventional treatment has been identified by the EPA as a Best Available Technology (BAT) for uranium removal.
Coagulation/ filtration processes are commonly used for uranium removal. The mechanism involves adsorption and co-precipitation of U(VI) to a coagulant precipitate. Both alum and ferric coagulants have been shown to successfully remove uranium below the maximum contaminant level (MCL) during the coagulation/ filtration process.  Both alum and ferric coagulants may require pH adjustment to achieve optimal uranium removal for most water sources.  A pH in the range of 6.0 was shown to be ideal for uranium removal.

Contaminant Name: Uranium | Function: GAC Isotherm | Details: Based on isotherm studies, adsorption of uranium in water by granular activated carbon (GAC) can be very effective.  One study showed that treating the GAC with hydrophobic aerogels would enhance GAC adsorption.  The type of GAC used in the studies was not mentioned so no conclusions could be drawn about the effectiveness of the GAC material type.

Contaminant Name: Uranium | Function: Ion Exchange | Details: Removal of uranium from water by ion exchange can be very effective (greater than 99 percent removal in most cases). Ion exchange is a Best Available Technology for the control of uranium. It is dependent upon the resin type, regeneration frequency, competing ions, and initial concentration. The most common resin used was an anionic resin (Dowex or Purolite). Limited regeneration studies were available, but based on the limited data, uranium removal appears to be unaffected by multiple regenerations.  Most studies did not differentiate between which form of uranium was being removed.

Contaminant Name: Uranium | Function: Membrane Filtration | Details: Membrane filtration alone is generally not very effective for the removal of uranium (less than 60 percent depending on the membrane type and pH).  One study found 0.45 micron membrane filtration to remove 50 to 60 percent of uranium between pHs 6.5 and 9.  The article suggested that the hydrolyzed uranyl complexes were polymerized and thus were retained on the membrane.  Typically, however, membrane filtration followed coagulation/flocculation which were suspected to be responsible for most of the uranium removal.

Contaminant Name: Uranium | Function: Membrane Separation | Details: Removal of uranium from water by membrane separation (specifically reverse osmosis) can be very effective (greater than 90 percent in most cases). Reverse osmosis is a Best Available Technology for control of uranium. Contaminant removal by reverse osmosis is dependent upon membrane type, pH, recovery, and initial contaminant concentration.

Contaminant Name: Uranium | Overall: The following processes are considered effective for the removal of uranium: adsorptive media (greater than 99 percent removal), conventional treatment (up to 95 percent removal), ion exchange (greater than 99 percent removal), membrane separation using reverse osmosis (greater than 90 percent removal), and precipitative softening (15 to 99 percent). Biological treatment (25 to 88 percent) and membrane filtration (less than 60 percent) were also shown to remove uranium under specific conditions in small-scale studies.  The level of control for conventional treatment (enhanced coagulation/filtration), ion exchange, membrane separation, and precipitative softening (often greater than 95 percent removal), is consistent with their designations as Best Available Technologies for uranium removal. Mechanisms for removal by iron-based adsorptive media was a combination of adsorption, co-precipitation, and reduction. Performance of these technologies can vary based on water quality and treatment characteristics.
Membrane filtration and biological treatment provided limited removal based on specific water chemistry.  Removal by membrane filtration was largely dependent on membrane characteristics and pH. The mechanism for removal by biological treatment was not fully understood, but likely the result of reductive precipitation and adsorption.
Research was identified evaluating the following treatment technologies for the removal of uranium:

Contaminant Name: Uranium | Function: Precipitative Softening | Details: Removal of uranium from water by precipitative softening can be very effective (up to 99 percent). Removal rates were largely dependent on the equilibrium pH, chemical doses and concentrations, charge of the uranium species, and competing ions.  The presence of free carbonate ions appeared to shift the optimum pH for removal and reduce uranium removal 20 percent or more for a given pH.  Lime sotening is a Best Available Technology for uruanium control.

Contaminant Name: V. cholerae | Description: Vibrio cholerae (V. cholerae) is a bacterium that causes cholera in humans [1678]. V. cholerae is a facultative anaerobic, gram-negative bacterium species that belongs to the family Vibrionaceae and the genus Vibrio. V. cholerae includes more than two hundreds of recognized serogroups; however, only two serogroups O1 and O139 are found responsible for epidemic and pandemic cholera so far [1675]. Serogroup O1 can be further categorized into two serotypes (Ogawa and Inaba) and two biotypes (classical and El Tor) [1675]. All four combinations of serotype and biotype exist [1678].
The natural habitat of V. cholerae as well as other Vibrio species is the aquatic ecosystem [1678]. They are often associated with phytoplankton and crustaceans [1678]. The optimal growth conditions are 37 °C and pH 7.6 [1677]. However, V. cholerae may enter a viable but non-culturable survival form under unfavorable conditions [1675]. El Tor serogroup can survive in food for two to five days at room temperature, and as long as nine days at 5 °C to 10 °C [1683]. The survival period of El Tor in water vary according to water type, source and presence of chlorine as a disinfectant [1683]. In raw groundwater and seawater without chlorine, El Tor can survive for ten to thirteen days at room temperature [1683].
Cholera is an illness caused by toxin produced by V. cholerae, and is characterized by sever watery diarrhea [1675]. While most people infected with V. cholerae do not develop symptoms, a small percent of people can develop acute watery diarrhea with severe dehydration, which can lead to death if untreated [1682]. The infectious dose of cholera is estimated to be 108 organisms in buffered saline, which can be affected by various factors, for example, stomach acidity [1669]. V. cholerae can be killed by acid (pH below 4.5) and high temperature [1677, 1668].
Cholera pandemics occurred in 19th century killed millions of people across all countries, and it is still endemic in many countries. In the United States, cholera infections are well controlled by improvements in sanitation, quality of water supplies and food hygiene. 114 cholerae cases were reported during 1991 to 2002; five cases were reported in 2008 [1679, 1680]. Foreign travel, contaminated seafood and wound exposure to contaminated water accounted for most cholerae cases in the United States [1680, 1681].
V. cholerae is currently not regulated by United States Environmental Protection Agency in drinking water.  It was considered for, but not added to, Contaminant Candidate List 3.
No occurrence data for V. cholerae were found for source water currently used for drinking water in the United States.
Date of Literature Search: August 2010

Contaminant Name: V. cholerae | Function: Chlorine | Details: Chlorine can effectively inactivate V. cholerae. One study reported 100 percent inactivation of V. chlorae in tap water and distilled water with a free chlorine dose of 1.0 mg/L and contact times of 10, 20 and 30 minutes [1666]. Two other studies on point-of-use water treatment products containing chlorine-based disinfectant reported more than 7.5 log V. cholerae inactivation [956, 957].
The CDC recommmends disinfection with 30 min contact with 2 drops of household bleach in a liter of water [2008].

Contaminant Name: V. cholerae | Function: Direct Filtration | Details: Direct filtration is expected to remove V. cholerae based on data for similar microbiological contaminants, such as E. coli or heterotrophic bacteria; however, few data specific to V. cholerae are available [1678]. The results of one study investigating the removal of V. cholerae attached to plankton using cloth, such as Nylon net or cotton Sari worn by women, as filter material suggest more than 95 percent removal of V. cholerae by filtration only (i.e. no other treatment) [1684].  A second, similar study in Bangledesh reported a 48 percent reduction in cholera infections by using the same simple filtration method [1685].

Contaminant Name: V. cholerae | Overall: The following drinking water processes were found to be effective for the inactivation or removal of V. cholerae: chlorine, ultraviolet (UV) irradiation, and direct filtration (greater than 95 percent removal for V. cholerae attached to plankton). The performance of chlorine and UV technologies vary based on chemical dose, contact time and water quality characteristics.
As the bacterium is susceptible to heat, the Centers for Disease Control and Prevention recommend one minute of vigorous boiling for inactivation. 2008
Research was identified evaluating the following treatment technologies for removal or inactivation of V. cholerae:

Contaminant Name: V. cholerae | Function: Ultraviolet Irradiation | Details: Ultraviolet (UV) irradiation is effective for V. cholera inactivation. One study evaluating low-pressure UV lamps showed one to six log inactivation of V. cholerae was achieved with UV doses ranging from 0.65 to 4.0 mWsec/cm2 [951]. Another study tested V. cholerae inactivation by a point-of-use water treatment system, which consisted of an activated carbon filter followed by a UV reactor [955]. The test system achieved >99.9999 percent (six log) V. cholerae inactivation [955]. A field trial study in Kenya suggested drinking water exposed to solar disinfection helped to protect young children from cholera [1670].

Contaminant Name: Vinclozolin | Description: Vinclozolin is a synthetic organic compound of the imide group of the dicarboximide class of fungicides. Its IUPAC name is 3-(3,5-dichlorophenyl)-5-methyl-5-vinyl-1,3-oxazolidine-2,4-dione. Vinclozolin has been registered for use in the United States since 1981, and is manufactured by BASF Corporation. Prior to 2000, it was used to control various diseases on raspberries, lettuce, kiwi, canola, snap beans, dry bulb onions, ornamental plants, and turf. [1443] However, in order to mitigate risk associated with the use of vinclozolin, USEPA accepted a proposal submitted by the registrant which included a phase out of all domestic food uses of vinclozolin except for use on canola, and revocation of all import tolerances except for wine grapes. [1443]
Vinclozolin is not regulated by USEPA in drinking water, but is an unregulated chemical drinking water contaminant listed on the USEPA's Contaminant Candidate List 3. [620]
Vinclozolin generally has been shown to have low acute oral/dermal/inhalation toxicity and that the principal toxic effects induced by vinclozolin and/or its metabolites are related to its antiandrogenic activity. [1443] Studies show that vinclozolin may have minimal antiandrogenic activity at relevant dose levels but that at least two vinclozolin metabolites occurring in mammals, plants, and soil and are responsible for much of the antiandrogenic activity attributable to vinclozolin. Vinclozolin exerts its effects most dramatically during the developmental stages of animals ultimately resulting in negative reproductive effects. Since the androgen receptor is widely conserved across species lines, antiandrogenic effects would be expected in humans. [1443] However, the human consequences are unknown. There is also evidence in published literature that vinclozolin may affect the development and function of the neuroendocrine system. It has been determined that vinclozolin's terminal metabolite, 3,5-dichloroaniline (3,5-DCA), should be regulated based on potential carcinogenic concerns. [1443]
Vinclozolin degrades in the environment by microbial-mediated hydrolysis, abiotic degradation, and transport with water. The principal degradation products of vinclozolin are metabolite B, metabolite E, which is believed to be a degradation product of parent and metabolite B, and 3,5-dichloroaniline (3,5-DCA), which is a degradation product of metabolite E. [1443] Experimental evidence has shown 3,5-DCA to be resistant to degradation processes. Vinclozolin and its principal degradates are potentially very mobile to slightly mobile in soil. Metabolites B, E and 3,5-DCA may be transported with water through the soil profile or with surface runoff. Residues are likely to be most mobile in sandy soils low in organic matter. [1443]
Date of Literature Search: June 2009
