Contaminant Name: Vinclozolin | Function: Biological Treatment | Details: Removal of vinclozolin by biological treatment was found to be effective. One study reported 90 percent removal by an isolated strain of Rhodococcus sp. bacteria named T1-1. The study reported that strains V21 and V1 were able to remove 75 percent and 67 percent of the vinclozolin present, respectively, however the T1-1 strain of bacteria was selected because of its ability to also degrade 84 percent of vinclozolin’s more toxic metabolite, 3,5-dichloroaniline.
June 2009

Contaminant Name: Vinclozolin | Function: Membrane Separation | Details: Reverse osmosis is effective for the removal of vinclozolin. Based on data from one study, vinclozolin was removed to below detection limits in the RO effluent stream.  It was suggested this removal was aided by vinclozolin’s large molecular weight (288), which was the largest of the pesticides in the study.
April 2009

Contaminant Name: Vinclozolin | Overall: Although data are limited, the following processes were found to be effective for the removal of vinclozolin: biological treatment (90 percent removal), membrane separation (> 90 percent), ozone (> 99 percent), and ultraviolet irradiation (10 to 93 percent removal).
Research was identified evaluating the following treatment technologies for the removal of vinclozolin:

Contaminant Name: Vinclozolin | Function: Ozone | Details: Based on the limited data available, ozone appears to be effective for the removal of vinclozolin in drinking water applications. One study demonstrated removal of vinclozolin to below detection limits by ozone doses typically required for disinfection. It was noted that pH and temperature have a strong effect on the ozone decay rate.
June 2009

Contaminant Name: Vinclozolin | Function: Ultraviolet Irradiation | Details: Data are suggestive that ultraviolet (UV) irradiation is somewhat effective for the removal of vinclozolin. Based on data from one study, 10 and 11 percent photodegradation were observed with the addition of low concentrations of humic and fulvic acid, respectively. Also, the addition of Fe2O3 resulted in 60 percent removal of vinclozolin, while adding the photocatalyst TiO2 gave a 93 percent removal of the contaminant.
April 2009

Contaminant Name: Vinyl Chloride | Description: Vinyl chloride is a monomer by-product from the production of polyvinyl chloride (PVC) pipes. Vinyl chloride can also be produced from microbial decomposition or organochlorides, thermal decomposition of dichloroethane and production of acetylene. Prior to the 1970's, vinyl chloride was used as an aerosol propellant and refrigerant. 
This colorless gas has a sweet odor, is flammable and highly toxic. EPA has classified vinyl chloride as a Group A, human carcinogen. Acute (short-term) exposure to high levels of vinyl chloride in air has resulted in central nervous system effects (CNS), such as dizziness, drowsiness, and headaches in humans. Chronic (long-term) exposure to vinyl chloride through inhalation and oral exposure in humans has resulted in liver damage. Cancer is a major concern from exposure to vinyl chloride via inhalation, as vinyl chloride exposure has been shown to increase the risk of a rare form of liver cancer in humans. 
EPA has set a maximum contaminant level of 2 parts per billion (ppb) of vinyl chloride for public drinking water supplies.
 Date of literature search: June 2021

Contaminant Name: Vinyl Chloride | CAS Number: 75-01-4 | DTXSID: DTXSID8021434 | Synonyms: Chloroethene, Ethylene monochloride, Monochloroethylene, Vinyl chloride monomer (VCM) | Contaminant Type: Chemical

Contaminant Name: Vinyl Chloride | Ref#: 2808 | Treatment Process: Granular Activated Carbon | Author: Kempisty, D.M., Summers, R.S., Abulikemu, G., Deshpande, N.V., Rebholz, J.A., Roberts, K. and Pressman, J.G. | Year: 2019 | Title: Granular activated carbon adsorption of carcinogenic volatile organic compounds at low influent concentrations | Source: AWWA Water Science Contaminant Name: Vinyl Chloride | Ref#: 2805 | Treatment Process: Ultraviolet Irradiation | Author: Liu, X., Yoon, S., Batchelor, B., and Abdel-Wahab, A | Year: 2013 | Title: Photochemical degradation of vinyl chloride with an advanced reduction process (ARP)–effects of reagents and pH | Source: Chemical Engineering Journal Contaminant Name: Vinyl Chloride | Ref#: 2806 | Treatment Process: Ultraviolet Irradiation | Author: Liu, X., Yoon, S., Batchelor, B., and Abdel-Wahab, A | Year: 2013 | Title: Degradation of vinyl chloride (VC) by the sulfite/UV advanced reduction process (ARP): Effects of process variables and a kinetic model | Source: Science of the total environment

Contaminant Name: Vinyl Chloride | Function: Granular Activated Carbon | Details: GAC is not a best available technology (BAT) for vinyl chloride. U.S. EPA (1998) identified GAC as an SSCT for vinyl chloride but did not consider affordability in the evaluation. In 2013, EPA conducted rapid small-scale column tests of GAC to treat groundwater spiked with various volatile organic contaminants. In these tests, the GAC columns achieved 90 percent removal of vinyl chloride, but maintained this performance for only 2,610 bed volumes. Vinyl chloride in effluent exceeded average influent at 5,860 bed volumes [2808]. This result suggests that, although it may be feasible to remove vinyl chloride to low levels using GAC, the achievable bed life is unlikely to be cost-effective at full scale.

Contaminant Name: Vinyl Chloride | Overall: Three sources were identified for treatment of vinyl chloride in drinking water. Granulated activated carbon (GAC) and ultraviolet irradiation (UV) were the two treatment methods identified. GAC is not considered a best available technology (BAT) for vinyl chloride. The contact times in the two UV studies were significantly longer than those typically used in full-scale UV treatment of drinking water (100-150 minutes).

Contaminant Name: Vinyl Chloride | Function: Ultraviolet Irradiation | Details: Although a bench-scale study observed high removals (88 to 92 percent depending on pH) of vinyl chloride by UV irradiation, this level of performance required contact times on the order of 150 minutes. Removals were much lower (14 to 34 percent) at 30 minutes contact time [2805]. A similar study found vinyl chloride degradation by UV irradiation was enhanced with the addition of sulfite, but substantial removal still required long contact times (e.g., 100 minutes) [2806]. The contact times in these studies were significantly longer than those typically used in full-scale UV treatment of drinking water.
